LatestDailyWeeklyMonthly

Daily Briefs

114 briefs

  • US

    • May nonfarm payrolls are the key release of the day, with consensus expecting an 88,000 gain — a three-year low — and unemployment steady at 4.3%; the print is awaited as a critical test for bond markets.
    • S&P 500 futures are down 0.4–0.5% ahead of the open, on track to snap a historic weekly winning streak, as the AI trade continues to unwind.
    • Fed's Logan flagged rising concern that rates may need to move higher; Treasury's Bessent rebrands fiscal package as "Working Families Tax Cuts" amid deep consumer pessimism.
    • South Korea's Kospi shed 5.5% overnight on $2.3 billion of foreign outflows, reinforcing a risk-off tone ahead of the jobs data.

    Macro and Policy

    The May payrolls report looms as the session's defining data point. Consensus sits at 88,000 new jobs — the softest reading expected in three months — against an unchanged 4.3% unemployment rate. Bond markets are positioned asymmetrically: a strong print would nudge yields modestly higher, but a weak result carries greater rally potential given that Fed rate hike expectations have been building since the US-Iran conflict drove oil prices sharply higher in late February. Fed Governor Logan reinforced the hawkish baseline this week, saying she is "increasingly concerned" that rates may need to rise. Treasury Secretary Bessent, meanwhile, has quietly retired the "One Big Beautiful Bill" branding in congressional testimony, pivoting to "Working Families Tax Cuts" — a messaging shift that reflects polling showing more than three-quarters of Americans view the economy negatively and 73% disapprove of the administration's handling of living costs.

    Markets

    Equities are under pressure across the technology complex, with semiconductor names off 3–4% in premarket trading after a sustained AI-driven rally. Lululemon declined 12% premarket after cutting revenue guidance. The 10-year Treasury yield holds at 4.47%. S&P Dow Jones Indices rejected a rule change that would have expedited S&P 500 inclusion for large private companies post-IPO, preserving existing methodology despite pressure tied to the anticipated SpaceX listing. ---

    Asia

    • China's Caixin manufacturing PMI slowed in May, adding to evidence that economic momentum is softening; Bloomberg Economics holds its 2026 China GDP forecast at 4.5%.
    • South Korea's Kospi fell 5.5% on Friday — foreigners sold more than $10 billion of Korean equities this week — the steepest weekly foreign exit on record for the index.
    • China intensified its capital outflow crackdown, with HSBC and AIA shares tumbling after reports that some banks suspended mainland clients' Hong Kong account openings for overseas investment.
    • India's rupee fell to a record low, prompting coordinated RBI and government measures including eased foreign access to bonds and reduced capital gains taxes on FII bond investments.
    • Xi Jinping is scheduled to travel to Pyongyang on June 8–9 for his first visit to North Korea since 2019.

    China: Capital Controls and Trade Tensions

    Beijing's crackdown on cross-border capital flows is deepening in scope. Regulators have threatened to confiscate "illegal gains" from unlicensed overseas brokers operating on the mainland, while tax authorities are pursuing wealthy individuals on unreported offshore investment income. Citic Securities estimates the campaign could affect up to HK$250 billion ($32 billion) of Hong Kong-domiciled assets. Private banks including UBS have postponed mainland events; non-essential travel by Hong Kong-based private bankers is being discouraged. The backdrop is striking: resident capital outflows reached an estimated record $807 billion in 2025, partly a consequence of the domestic property slump reducing onshore investment appeal. Separately, the US proposed a 12.5% tariff rate on Chinese goods under a forced-labor import investigation — a development Beijing dismissed as "political manipulation" and analysts at Bloomberg Economics say is unlikely to derail China's export engine in the near term, though further stacked duties from a parallel overcapacity probe could test Beijing's patience ahead of an expected Xi-Trump White House meeting in September.

    Korea and India

    The Kospi's 5.5% single-session decline caps a week in which foreign investors liquidated over $10 billion of Korean equities — a pace that appears without recent precedent and reflects broad de-risking from AI-linked names globally. In India, coordinated RBI-government action to stabilize the rupee — down roughly 6% against the dollar year-to-date — drew comparisons to the 2013 taper-tantrum response playbook. ---

    Europe

    • Eurozone Q1 GDP was revised to show a contraction following a slump in Irish output, missing prior estimates of flat growth.
    • Despite the GDP revision, ECB rate increases remain on track, with markets pricing two additional hikes.
    • UK firms may raise prices by 4% over the coming year; major retailers have shed nearly 18,000 jobs over the past 12 months.
    • Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham confirmed he will challenge Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the Labour leadership.

    Eurozone: Growth Revision and ECB Path

    The downward revision to eurozone Q1 GDP — now showing a contraction rather than stagnation — principally reflects a sharp decline in Irish national accounts, which are prone to distortion from multinational profit-shifting activity. The revision does not appear to have materially altered the ECB's rate trajectory: policymakers appear committed to two further hikes, supported by still-elevated services inflation and wage dynamics across core economies. The tension between a contracting growth print and a tightening bias warrants monitoring; whether Irish distortions genuinely mask underlying weakness or flatter the regional picture in other quarters remains to be seen.

    UK

    The UK domestic picture is deteriorating at the margin. Survey evidence points to firms planning 4% price increases over the next year, suggesting consumer price pressures remain sticky even as the broader disinflation trend continues. The labour market is adjusting visibly: large retailers have cut nearly 18,000 positions in the past 12 months, consistent with the impact of higher employer National Insurance contributions enacted earlier this year. Politically, Andy Burnham's formal entry into the Labour leadership contest introduces a degree of governing uncertainty, though markets have not yet priced a meaningful political risk premium into sterling or gilts. ---

    LatAm

    • No significant macro data releases for the region in this period.
    • Venezuela appeared in diplomatic context, with India signaling interest in deeper energy ties as it seeks to diversify oil sourcing amid Strait of Hormuz disruption.
    • The Bank of Ghana governor — while not LatAm — noted that rate cuts across frontier markets could resume once the Iran war ends, a signal relevant to the broader commodity-exporting EM complex including Latin America.

    Regional Macro Context

    Source material for this period contains limited direct coverage of Latin American macro data, central bank decisions, or market developments. The dominant external variables bearing on the region — elevated oil prices stemming from Strait of Hormuz disruption, the trajectory of the US dollar, and Federal Reserve policy expectations — are addressed in the Global section. The anticipated US payrolls print will be consequential for EM currencies and credit spreads broadly, including across Latin America, given its implications for Fed rate timing. ---

    Global

    • WTI crude holds near $93/barrel as markets await signs of progress in US-Iran interim peace negotiations; the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed.
    • Pakistan purchased its most expensive LNG cargo in four years, a direct consequence of Hormuz disruption; Oman's main crude export terminal briefly halted loadings after an explosion Friday.
    • US-Iran talks made little progress this week, with the worst military clashes since an April ceasefire began; Iran insists on a Lebanon ceasefire before extending the truce.
    • Ukraine's Zelenskyy sent an open letter proposing peace talks to Putin; the US House voted to advance Ukraine aid and Russian sanctions.

    Energy Markets and the Hormuz Conflict

    Oil markets are in a holding pattern around $93/barrel, with direction contingent on diplomatic signals from US-Iran negotiations. The talks appear to have stalled materially this week, with Iran conditioning any extension of the April truce on a resolution of the Israel-Hezbollah conflict in Lebanon — a linkage that introduces significant complexity and timeline uncertainty. The economic consequences of prolonged Hormuz closure are accumulating: Pakistan's record LNG purchase illustrates the supply-chain stress cascading through energy-import-dependent economies. The Asia energy shock appears to be accelerating clean energy investment decisions, with governments treating the disruption as a structural prompt to reduce fossil fuel dependence.

    Geopolitics and Monetary Divergence

    The US-Iran conflict continues to function as the dominant macro variable globally, having suppressed Fed rate-cut expectations, elevated inflation readings, and weighed on consumer sentiment across developed markets. Xi Jinping's planned Pyongyang visit and the US's new forced-labor tariff proposal — targeting dozens of countries with China at 12.5% — suggest the geopolitical backdrop will remain complex into the second half of 2026. The World Cup opening on June 11 across the US, Canada, and Mexico introduces climate stress as a visible theme, with heat exposure now a quantifiable competitive variable and a marker of the broader physical-risk environment facing outdoor mass events.

  • US

    • ISM services activity beat expectations, with input costs rising at the fastest pace in nearly four years — a significant inflation signal echoing the 2021 price surge warning.
    • The Fed's Beige Book described prices increasing "at a moderate to strong pace," with energy-related costs from the Middle East conflict driving broad inflationary pressure across shipping, groceries, and packaging.
    • US petroleum inventories including strategic reserves have fallen for 10 consecutive weeks to their lowest level since 2004, intensifying medium-term oil price risks.
    • Broadcom shares fell approximately 14% in premarket trading after issuing AI chip revenue guidance of $16 billion for the current quarter, below Wall Street expectations.

    Economic Data and Inflation Signals

    The ISM services survey showed activity expanding even as input costs accelerated to the fastest pace in nearly four years — a combination that complicates the Federal Reserve's policy calculus. The parallel Beige Book reinforced this picture: activity remains broadly firm, with the Oxford Economics word-count index of Fed district conditions indicating resilience, but price pressures are broadening. Energy costs tied to the US-Iran conflict were cited as the primary driver, with spillovers into freight, packaging, fertilizers, and groceries. Non-labor input costs are rising faster than selling prices, generating margin compression particularly among consumer-facing firms. UCLA's Anderson School projects core CPI peaking at 4.5% by year-end, up from May's 3.8%, with the oil shock having now displaced tariffs as the leading economic risk.

    Logistics and Supply Chains

    The Logistics Managers' Index transport price gauge rose to 96, the highest reading in the report's 10-year history, with capacity contracting and utilization elevated. Overall logistics costs reached their highest level since March 2022. Regional Fed contacts reported fuel surcharges of up to 50% on domestic freight, compressing retailer margins. The report noted that supply-driven inflation is harder for the Fed to counter with rate increases than demand-driven price pressures.

    Markets and Corporate News

    Equity markets pulled back — S&P 500 futures were off 0.4%, with Nasdaq 100 futures down 1.2% — as Broadcom's guidance miss weighed on AI-linked hardware names broadly. Vanguard's VOO S&P 500 ETF became the world's first trillion-dollar exchange-traded fund. SpaceX launched investor briefings ahead of a $75 billion IPO scheduled for June 12, with Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and JPMorgan competing aggressively for the lead role despite razor-thin fee arrangements. ---

    Asia

    • The Bank of Japan is set to consider a 25 basis point rate increase at its June meeting, with officials seeing the possibility of a further hike later in 2026 — a significant shift for a central bank that spent decades battling deflation.
    • The yen weakened back through 160 per dollar despite hawkish BOJ signaling and prior Ministry of Finance intervention warnings, highlighting persistent structural pressures on the currency.
    • South Korea's KOSPI fell 1.8%, with Broadcom's guidance miss weighing on tech-heavy indexes in Seoul and Taiwan.

    Monetary Policy and Currency Stress

    The BOJ's apparent readiness to raise its benchmark rate toward 1% marks a remarkable inflection for Japanese monetary policy. Yet yen weakness persists near 160 per dollar — a level that previously triggered intervention in April — suggesting markets remain skeptical that rate increases alone can resolve Japan's structural imbalances. Brookings' Robin Brooks attributes the weakness to Japan's debt overhang; others point to growth remaining too fragile to sustain meaningfully higher rates. The yen's depreciation against the Chinese yuan is also deepening bilateral tensions. Across the broader region, currency guardians in Seoul and Jakarta stepped up interventions as dollar strength, rising oil prices, and expectations of higher US rates pressured regional exchange rates. India is reportedly weighing capital inflow measures to support the rupee without tightening borrowing costs.

    Corporate and Political Developments

    TSMC's CEO stated AI chip supply will remain constrained for years despite ongoing capacity expansion, confirming a structural bottleneck in AI infrastructure buildout. Indonesia faces compounding headwinds: the rupiah is at record lows, an MSCI investability review is pending, and a corruption investigation has reached President Prabowo Subianto's flagship $15 billion free-meals program. South Korea's ruling Democratic Party won broadly in local elections, further pressuring the opposition People Power Party following former President Yoon's impeachment. ---

    Europe

    • SoftBank announced plans to invest up to €75 billion in AI data centers in France, reinforcing Paris's emerging position as Europe's primary AI infrastructure hub.
    • Germany, France, and the UK are working with Kyiv on a framework to engage Russia in Ukraine peace negotiations, citing a perceived shift in battlefield momentum toward Zelenskyy.
    • UK financial institutions are exploring retail savings deposits — totaling £2 trillion — as a potential long-term buyer base for gilts, where retail ownership currently stands below 4%.

    France's AI Positioning

    SoftBank's commitment builds on a deliberate strategy by President Macron, who personally lobbied founder Masayoshi Son, and extends France's early-mover advantage in AI policy dating to its 2018 national AI strategy. France's combination of technical talent and substantial nuclear power capacity has made it an attractive destination for data center investment relative to European peers. The announcement is a meaningful near-term legacy achievement for Macron, who faces term limits next year and a nationalist right polling well ahead of the 2027 presidential election. Execution risks remain: SoftBank's financing capacity is uncertain, and public opposition to data center land use is rising across Europe.

    Gilt Market and Fiscal Dynamics

    UK financial institutions are advocating for a retail gilt distribution push, citing the gap between the 4%–5.5% yield on government bonds and the 1.7% average savings rate. Proponents argue household savings represent an underleveraged source of long-duration domestic demand at a time when pension funds are reducing gilt exposure, increasing the UK's reliance on hedge funds and overseas buyers. The gilts market is on track for its third annual loss in five years, which may complicate efforts to build retail investor confidence. ---

    LatAm

    • Mexico's former President López Obrador issued a rare public statement backing President Sheinbaum and criticizing US pressure on her government, framing Washington's migration and narcoterrorism posture as an attack on the ruling Morena party.

    Political Economy

    López Obrador's intervention — his most prominent since leaving office — signals rising domestic political tension around Mexico's relationship with Washington. His characterization of US pressure as politically motivated may complicate the Sheinbaum administration's diplomatic positioning, particularly as the new tariff framework exempts USMCA-compliant goods, giving Mexico a degree of insulation from the broader forced-labor levy. The exemption mirrors an earlier Liberation Day carve-out and suggests the bilateral trade relationship retains structural anchors despite rhetorical friction. Broader regional dynamics were not significantly covered in the available source material for this period. ---

    Global

    • US petroleum inventories including strategic reserves fell for a 10th consecutive week to their lowest since 2004, raising the risk of a price shock if summer demand or hurricane disruptions reduce the buffer further.
    • A fragile Israel-Lebanon ceasefire was announced contingent on Hezbollah withdrawal from southern Lebanon, pushing oil modestly lower, but Iran stated there has been "no tangible progress" in broader peace talks with Washington.
    • Washington's new tariff framework — framed around forced labor — raises the effective US tariff rate by approximately 0.5 percentage points, covering economies representing 99% of US imports in 2024.
    • The Iran war is accelerating structural energy transitions in Asia and Europe, with more than 20 nations fast-tracking renewables deployment in response to fossil fuel supply disruptions.

    Oil Markets and Hormuz

    Brent crude remains just below $100 per barrel, with December futures turning upward even as near-term prices dipped on ceasefire news. The underlying supply picture is deteriorating: US diesel stocks are near 2003 lows and risk falling to a critical 20-day supply threshold by August, according to Goldman Sachs. Vitol confirmed that gasoline inventories globally are well below seasonal norms. With hurricane season approaching and the strategic reserve buffer largely depleted, the margin for error in global oil markets is narrow. The ceasefire announcement provided only modest relief given Iran's explicit statement that broader negotiations have stalled.

    Energy Transition Acceleration

    The Iran war is functioning as a structural catalyst for clean energy deployment across Asia and Europe, analogous to the role Russia's Ukraine invasion played for European gas diversification. The Philippines fast-tracked more than 30 renewable projects; Pakistan's LNG-fired generation fell 80% year-on-year in April. China's exports of solar panels, EVs, and related electrotech reached record levels in March and April. The IEA identifies more than 20 nations making irreversible structural shifts in their energy mix. In parallel, soaring US power demand from AI data centers is straining PJM Interconnection — the nation's largest grid operator — with federal officials now openly discussing a potential breakup if governance reforms fail.

    Monetary Policy Divergence

    The global monetary picture is increasingly asymmetric. The BOJ appears set to hike toward 1%, Australia's central bank is seeing early evidence its hikes are transmitting, and the Fed faces mounting pressure from energy-driven inflation — with FOMC communications under new Chairman Kevin Warsh potentially becoming more concise. Subdued readings in Switzerland, Sweden, and the Czech Republic support holds in those economies. The dollar index reached its highest level in nearly a month on tariff announcements and geopolitical uncertainty, reinforcing the dollar's safe-haven status despite the US being the proximate source of both trade and geopolitical disruption.

  • US

    • April JOLTS job openings surged to their highest pre-pandemic level, with vacancies again exceeding the number of unemployed workers — a clear beat relative to recession fears.
    • The Trump administration proposed new tariffs of at least 10% on imports from roughly 60 trading partners, including the EU, UK, Canada, and Mexico, and 12.5% on China, Japan, and others, reviving the protectionist agenda the Supreme Court had struck down.
    • The S&P 500 extended its winning streak to nine consecutive sessions, driven almost entirely by AI-related technology names.
    • Real personal income has declined 0.6% over the past year, a pace Goldman Sachs describes as rarely seen outside of recession, even as headline labor market data remains resilient.

    Labor Market

    April JOLTS data released Tuesday showed job openings surging back to their highest pre-pandemic level, with the US once again registering more than one vacancy per unemployed worker. The print stands in stark contrast to recession-adjacent signals elsewhere in the data. Real personal income has fallen 0.6% over the past year on Goldman Sachs estimates — a threshold historically associated with downturns — while gasoline costs have risen roughly 44% since late February, adding approximately $200 per month to household fuel bills. Treasury Secretary Bessent acknowledged the burden while arguing it is transitory, a framing that will face scrutiny at Senate and House hearings Wednesday and Thursday. The May payrolls report, due Friday, is forecast to show average hourly earnings growth of 3.4% year-over-year, below prevailing inflation readings.

    Trade Policy

    The administration's new tariff proposal, structured under a Section 301 forced-labor investigation, calls for a 10% levy on most major partners and 12.5% on China, India, Japan, South Korea, Brazil, and Switzerland. Bloomberg Economics estimates the effective US tariff rate would rise by only 0.5 percentage point from its current 10.7% level if implemented after the expiring Section 122 tariffs lapse in late July — a relatively modest increment. A second 301 probe targeting industrial overcapacity is expected shortly, with analysts flagging potential stacking of duties as the key risk.

    Equity Markets

    The S&P 500's nine-session winning streak is attributable almost entirely to the technology sector, which has outperformed the ex-tech S&P 500 by 33% over the past three months — the strongest relative quarter for tech on record. Marvell Technology extended its recent rally in premarket trading following Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's prediction the company would reach a $1 trillion valuation. SpaceX separately announced plans to raise $75 billion in what would be the largest IPO on record, pricing shares at $135 ahead of any formal roadshow. ---

    Asia

    • Indonesia's Jakarta Composite Index fell as much as 5.7% to its lowest level since May 2021 after prosecutors raided the headquarters of President Prabowo Subianto's flagship free meals program.
    • Bank of Japan Governor Ueda reiterated that interest rates need to keep rising to contain inflation, reinforcing the BOJ's tightening trajectory.
    • Japan's fertility rate fell to a fresh record low in 2025, adding long-run structural pressure to the economy.
    • Foreign investors have sold nearly $70 billion of Korean equities year-to-date while directing $73.6 billion into Japanese stocks, even as Goldman Sachs lifted its targets for both the Kospi and Taiwan's benchmark index.

    Indonesia

    Indonesia dominated Asian market news. The benchmark Jakarta Composite fell as much as 5.7% intraday, touching its lowest since May 2021, after anti-corruption prosecutors raided the operational headquarters of President Prabowo Subianto's free meals program — a signature campaign pledge. The raid followed the abrupt removal of the agency's chief amid food-poisoning incidents and operational failures. The rupiah weakened approximately 0.6% against the dollar, leading regional currency losses. The episode raises reputational risk for Prabowo's administration and may complicate investor confidence in a market already under pressure from the global commodity shock.

    Capital Flows and Japan

    Foreign investors have rotated sharply away from Korea and Taiwan despite those markets' AI-driven gains, redirecting capital toward Japan. Overseas selling of Korean equities stands at nearly $70 billion year-to-date, with Taiwan flows also turning negative, while Japan attracted $73.6 billion of inflows through late May. The preference for Japan reflects its market depth, breadth, and corporate governance reforms rather than superior earnings momentum. India separately moved toward reducing taxes on foreign bond investors and removing ownership caps on long-tenor sovereign notes, a potential catalyst for incremental inflows into its fixed income market. ---

    Europe

    • The Stoxx Europe 600 declined 0.4%, weighed by fresh US tariff proposals and rising oil prices.
    • The EU is preparing to warn citizens and companies about a potential trade conflict with China as the bloc considers new restrictive measures against Beijing.
    • Akzo Nobel shares fell 19% in Amsterdam after Nippon Paint and Sherwin-Williams abandoned acquisition efforts; Inditex gained 5.7% in Madrid on stronger second-quarter sales momentum.

    Trade and Policy

    The Trump administration's new tariff proposal — imposing at least 10% duties on EU imports — poses a direct challenge to European exporters, even if Bloomberg Economics estimates the near-term increment to effective tariff rates is modest. Brussels is simultaneously bracing for a separate trade confrontation with China, with the EU weighing new restrictive measures and preparing to communicate the risks to businesses. The EU also confirmed it will allow fiscal flexibility for green energy investments, providing some offset to the broader tightening of trade conditions.

    Markets and Corporate News

    European equities came under modest pressure as rising oil — Brent near $98 — squeezed import-dependent economies, with the continent's deep exposure to Persian Gulf energy a persistent vulnerability. The corporate picture was mixed. Akzo Nobel's 19% decline was among the sharpest single-session moves in European equities this year after two potential acquirers walked away simultaneously. Inditex offered a contrasting signal: the Zara owner's acceleration in second-quarter sales growth suggests consumer spending in its key markets retains some resilience despite the inflationary backdrop. UBS raised $1.5 billion in Additional Tier 1 capital — its first such issuance since Switzerland paused plans to tighten AT1 rules — signaling selective reopening of bank capital markets. ---

    LatAm

    • The US proposed 25% tariffs on Brazil specifically for unfair trade practices, hours after the broader 12.5% proposal covering most major emerging markets was announced.
    • Brazilian President Lula moved immediately to weaponize the tariff threat politically, branding it "TariFlávio" in a direct attack on electoral rival Flávio Bolsonaro.
    • Mexican teachers threatened to disrupt World Cup matches over wage demands, adding operational risk to a tournament already facing logistical scrutiny.

    Brazil

    Brazil faces a dual tariff exposure: the 12.5% levy proposed under the forced-labor investigation and a separate, more punitive 25% rate tied to a distinct unfair trade practices finding. President Lula's rapid political reframing of the tariff threat — linking it to Flávio Bolsonaro's recent White House meeting — illustrates how trade policy has become embedded in Brazil's pre-election dynamics. The episode adds uncertainty to an already complex macro picture in which energy cost inflation, driven by the Hormuz closure, is pressuring the current account.

    Mexico

    Mexico faces the 10% baseline tariff under the new US proposal, a meaningful development for an economy whose export sector is deeply integrated with US supply chains. Separately, the domestic political environment is generating its own noise, with teachers' unions threatening disruptions to World Cup host-city operations. On a constructive note, investment in Mexico's electricity sector is showing early signs of revival, with new deals in power generation, renewables, and grid infrastructure offering support to President Sheinbaum's modernization agenda — a potential offset to near-term trade headwinds if momentum is sustained. ---

    Global

    • The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed for the fourth consecutive month; US and Iranian forces exchanged strikes Tuesday, with Iran launching ballistic missiles toward Kuwait and Bahrain.
    • Brent crude rose for a third consecutive session to approximately $98 per barrel, with WTI at $95.37, as ceasefire prospects on prediction markets continued to fade.
    • The OECD warned that if Middle East hostilities persist into 2027, global growth could slow to 1.8%, pushing several economies into or near recession.
    • The Trump administration's new tariff proposals affecting 60 trading partners add a second structural shock to a global economy already absorbing the energy disruption.

    Hormuz and Energy

    The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to most tanker traffic for approximately three months, and Tuesday's exchange of strikes — including Iranian ballistic missiles targeting Kuwait and Bahrain, intercepted by US and allied defenses — diminished already-faint hopes for a near-term resolution. Saudi Arabia and the UAE are maximizing pipeline bypass capacity, but those routes face binding physical constraints and remain within range of Iranian missiles and drones. Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain lack viable alternatives. The capacity gap means the supply disruption is structural rather than temporary for as long as hostilities persist. A MSC container vessel was separately struck at Iraq's Um-Qasr port, illustrating the widening geographic footprint of maritime risk.

    Global Growth and Trade

    The OECD's warning of 1.8% global growth under a prolonged conflict scenario frames the macro stakes clearly: the energy shock is already transmitting through inflation and real income compression, while the simultaneous re-escalation of US trade barriers threatens to compound the demand drag. Bloomberg Economics estimates China's potential deployment of rare-earth supply restrictions could put $4.4 trillion in global GDP at risk — a latent vulnerability that US-China trade tensions keep relevant. The interaction between an energy supply shock, renewed tariff uncertainty, and tightening monetary policy across Asia represents the dominant risk cluster for global growth in the second half of 2026.

  • US

    • ISM Manufacturing PMI rose to its highest level in four years in May, beating consensus and signaling a meaningful acceleration in factory activity, with new orders and production both firming.
    • Alphabet announced an $80 billion equity offering — including a $10 billion Berkshire Hathaway investment — to fund AI capital expenditure, representing one of the largest equity raises in history for a public company.
    • Anthropic filed confidentially for an IPO as early as this fall, with OpenAI reported to be preparing its own filing; combined with SpaceX's imminent listing, the three transactions could raise approximately $200 billion.
    • Bitcoin fell below $70,000 for the first time in two months, extending a decline of nearly 45% from its $120,000-plus peak, amid Iran ceasefire uncertainty and capital rotation into semiconductor equities.

    Manufacturing and Economic Momentum

    May's ISM Manufacturing PMI printed above the expansion threshold at its strongest reading in four years, driven by firmer new orders and production. The White House cited this as validation for its industrial policy, simultaneously announcing targeted reductions in steel and aluminum tariffs — cutting rates on select agricultural and industrial goods to 15% from 25% — to incentivize domestic investment. However, the same ISM report showed manufacturing employment has remained in contraction since September 2023, illustrating a familiar tension: output is recovering while labor market gains in the sector remain elusive. Non-farm payrolls and CPI data for May, due imminently, will be pivotal in determining whether economic momentum is broad-based or concentrated.

    AI Capital Markets

    The AI fundraising cycle has reached a scale that is beginning to test conventional capital market assumptions. Alphabet's $80 billion equity offering is exceptional for an established public company; Goldman Sachs estimates corporate buybacks will reach $1.3 trillion this year, providing some offset to the estimated $675 billion in new equity supply from IPOs and follow-ons. Near-term dilution risk appears manageable, though history suggests lockup expiries in 2027–2028 could generate a more significant supply overhang. Marvell Technology surged 26% in premarket trading after Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's endorsement, and Hewlett Packard Enterprise rallied 24% on a bullish AI-driven sales outlook.

    Federal Reserve

    A Brookings Institution paper by Christina and David Romer offered a nuanced assessment of the Powell Fed, praising its institutional independence while warning that cutting rates since 2024 — with inflation persistently above 2% and unemployment near historic lows — risks losing sight of the broader inflation picture. With 30-year Treasury yields having recently topped 5% and a strong ISM print arriving, the risk of renewed upward pressure on rates is real. ---

    Asia

    • South Korea CPI accelerated to a two-year high, signaling renewed inflationary pressure in the region's fourth-largest economy.
    • Global funds have pulled out a decade's worth of India equity inflows, representing a material deterioration in foreign investor sentiment toward one of Asia's most closely watched emerging markets.
    • Hang Seng Index gained 2.5% and Tencent jumped on reports of an imminent WeChat AI agent launch; South Korea's Kospi added 0.1%.
    • At least seven Chinese universities with defense ties are seeking access to Nvidia's H200 chips, including two US Commerce Department-blacklisted institutions, intensifying export control concerns.

    Inflation and Capital Flows

    South Korea's CPI accelerated to a two-year high, complicating the Bank of Korea's policy calculus at a moment when global rate paths remain divergent. The print appears to reflect both energy pass-through — a direct consequence of the Strait of Hormuz disruption — and underlying demand pressures. Separately, the scale of foreign equity outflows from India is notable: global funds have reversed a decade's worth of accumulated inflows, suggesting a combination of valuation concerns, currency risk, and competition from AI-linked US equities that are attracting capital globally.

    China Technology and AI

    Chinese defense-affiliated universities seeking Nvidia H200 chips — including Beihang University and Northwestern Polytechnical University, both Commerce Department blacklist entities — underscore the persistent tension between US export controls and China's military-civil fusion strategy. Tencent's rally on WeChat AI agent reports reflects investor optimism around China's domestic AI commercialization, even as the country remains constrained in accessing frontier US semiconductor technology. SK Hynix announced plans to double wafer capacity to ease memory chip supply tightness, a move that reflects the extraordinary demand being generated by AI infrastructure globally.

    Philippines Political Risk

    Political gridlock in the Philippines Senate deepened following the arrest of a majority member, creating deadlock in Vice President Sara Duterte's impeachment proceedings and disrupting normal legislative business. The development introduces near-term political uncertainty into one of Southeast Asia's more dynamic economies. ---

    Europe

    • Eurozone CPI rose above 3% year-on-year for the first time in more than two and a half years, meaningfully above prior readings and cementing expectations for an ECB rate hike at next week's meeting.
    • UniCredit secured commitments taking its Commerzbank stake above 30%, adding significant momentum to its €38.6 billion acquisition bid for the German lender.
    • Stoxx Europe 600 gained 0.7%, outperforming US futures, as markets digested the inflation print and corporate deal flow.
    • Denmark's Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen secured a third term, forming a four-party minority coalition to navigate strained US relations.

    Inflation and ECB Policy

    Eurozone inflation breaching 3% year-on-year is the most consequential European data point of the period. The acceleration — driven in meaningful part by energy costs linked to the Strait of Hormuz disruption — moves the ECB into territory where it has limited flexibility to remain accommodative. Market pricing has shifted decisively toward a rate hike at next week's meeting. The European Commission is reportedly considering granting member states fiscal flexibility to absorb elevated energy costs, creating a potential tension between tighter monetary policy and looser fiscal conditions.

    UniCredit-Commerzbank

    UniCredit crossing the 30% ownership threshold in Commerzbank is a structural development in European banking consolidation. The move effectively forces a formal takeover offer process and increases pressure on Commerzbank's board and German regulators to engage substantively with the €38.6 billion bid. Cross-border European bank mergers have historically faced political resistance in Germany, and the path to completion remains uncertain, but the momentum shift is real.

    UK

    Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey appeared before parliament, with markets attentive to any signals on the rate path given persistent domestic inflation. Research from Tax Policy Associates highlighted that UK tax complexity has doubled over three decades and is approaching levels last seen in the post-Napoleonic era — a structural drag on growth that complicates the government's fiscal consolidation agenda. London Underground strike action began, adding to near-term economic disruption. ---

    LatAm

    • Chilean President José Antonio Kast used his first State of the Nation address to acknowledge administration setbacks on security, with new public safety measures promised ahead of his self-imposed 90-day reform deadline.

    Chile

    Kast's State of the Nation address came at a politically sensitive moment — his 90-day deadline for delivering meaningful change in one of Latin America's wealthiest economies is imminent, and the acknowledgment of setbacks on public security is a notable concession for an administration that campaigned heavily on law and order. The broader macro environment for Chile remains shaped by copper price volatility, itself a function of both AI-driven demand growth — copper is central to data center and grid infrastructure — and uncertainty around global growth. No significant central bank policy decisions or major macro data releases for the broader region were reported in the period covered. ---

    Global

    • Commodities are in what HSBC describes as a "super-squeeze," with the Strait of Hormuz disruption running into its fourth month and threatening to push markets past inventory tipping points across energy, metals, and agricultural commodities.
    • QatarEnergy and ADNOC have begun "going dark" — disabling vessel tracking signals — to move LNG through the Strait of Hormuz, adopting tactics previously associated with Russia's shadow fleet.
    • Oil steadied near $94/bbl (Brent) after its largest single-day gain in a month, with price action oscillating on conflicting signals from Washington and Tehran about the status of peace negotiations.
    • El Niño has an 82% probability of forming by end of July, according to the US Climate Prediction Center, adding a potential weather shock to an already stressed global commodity complex.

    Strait of Hormuz and Energy

    The decision by QatarEnergy and ADNOC — together responsible for roughly one-fifth of global LNG exports in 2025 — to adopt shadow fleet navigation tactics marks a qualitative escalation in the global energy disruption stemming from the Iran conflict. While some shipments are moving again, the volume remains limited, and the practice introduces new counterparty, regulatory, and insurance risks into LNG markets that previously operated with near-complete transparency. HSBC's warning about commodity "tipping points" reflects genuine concern: the longer the strait remains effectively closed, the greater the risk that inventory drawdowns in oil, gas, and derivative commodities reach levels that are difficult to reverse quickly even after a diplomatic resolution.

    Geopolitics

    US-Iran peace talks remain in a fragile and uncertain state, with Tehran threatening to suspend negotiations over Israeli military activity in Lebanon while Washington asserted talks were continuing "at a rapid pace." The contradictory signals from all parties — including divergent accounts from Trump and Netanyahu of a bilateral call — suggest the diplomatic process is not close to resolution. Russia's war in Ukraine is simultaneously creating fiscal strain in Moscow, with senior Russian finance officials warning Putin that defense spending is on an unaffordable trajectory, though there is no current evidence this has altered Putin's strategic calculus.

    AI Infrastructure and Energy

    The intersection of AI capital expenditure and energy demand is emerging as a structural theme. Data centers are projected to require 2 million tons of cement through 2030 in the US alone, and approximately 30% of power input is currently lost to cooling and transmission inefficiency. Microsoft is reportedly reconsidering clean energy commitments under AI buildout pressure. The scale of AI-driven electricity demand — with next-generation chip racks approaching 1 megawatt each — is forcing a fundamental redesign of data center architecture and raising legitimate questions about the compatibility of AI expansion timelines with carbon commitments.

  • US

    • ISM Manufacturing PMI and S&P Global manufacturing PMI due today; payrolls report on Friday expected to show +89,000 jobs with unemployment steady at 4.3%.
    • US PCE inflation has risen to near 4% YoY on a headline basis, with core measures diverging: trimmed-mean PCE declining while conventional core is rising.
    • Fed funds rate expectations have surged ~75 basis points since the Iran conflict began, as rate-cut hopes fade; US money fund assets hit a record $8.3 trillion.
    • S&P 500 futures point to another record open, with equity allocators expecting multiple expansion despite historically elevated Shiller CAPE ratios.

    Inflation and Monetary Policy

    The inflation picture remains the dominant domestic macro debate. Headline PCE has converged with CPI near 4% YoY in April — well above the Fed's 3% upper band — driven substantially by energy costs tied to the Iran conflict. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has indicated he follows trimmed-mean PCE, which is actually declining, potentially preserving optionality for easing later in 2026 if Middle East conditions improve. Compounding the complexity, one- and two-year inflation swaps have retraced roughly half their war-driven spike in the past two weeks, suggesting some moderation in near-term price expectations. Asset allocators nonetheless expect core PCE to rise further over the next year, a view that is difficult to reconcile with anything other than eventual rate hikes. The tension between a resilient real economy and persistent inflation will likely define Fed policy through year-end.

    Equity Markets and Valuations

    US equities are approaching another record despite a strategic stalemate in the Gulf. The AI investment boom — particularly in semiconductors — has been the primary driver, with Berkshire Hathaway's $6.8 billion acquisition of homebuilder Taylor Morrison adding a note of confidence in the broader economy. The Shiller CAPE ratio has exceeded its September 2000 peak, and sales multiples are the highest on record. A majority of global allocators now expect multiples to expand further — the first such consensus since the pandemic — a view that implicitly depends on profit forecasts materializing and the Iran disruption remaining contained. ---

    Asia

    • South Korean exports surged 61% YoY in May on a working-day-adjusted basis, with chip exports jumping a record 169.4%, sharply beating consensus expectations.
    • China's May Caixin manufacturing PMI showed broad-based deceleration, with revenues and margins weakening across most sectors — a clear miss relative to prior momentum.
    • The Kospi rose 3.7% to a record; Nikkei 225 gained 0.9% to a new high; Taiwan equity markets also hit all-time levels, all driven by AI semiconductor demand.
    • SoftBank overtook Toyota to become Japan's most valuable company, with shares up over 90% year-to-date.

    China: Energy Drag and Deflationary Pressure

    China's May activity data presented a deteriorating picture. The China Beige Book found broad weakness in revenues and margins, with decelerating output, soft retail sales, and fresh disappointment in housing. Services offered a partial exception. Despite producer prices rising at the fastest pace since the pandemic, consumer inflation remains subdued — reflecting demand constraints that limit firms' pricing power. Capital Economics notes a drawdown in Chinese oil inventories, which eases near-term price pressure but suggests the economy's energy buffer is eroding. The yuan has strengthened to a three-year high, partly reflecting exporters repatriating foreign currency receipts, though financial institutions are trimming appreciation forecasts in response to apparent central bank signaling. Export orders remain robust for now, supported by US tariff easing, but the durability of this channel depends heavily on Strait of Hormuz resolution.

    Asia Semiconductor Boom

    South Korea and Taiwan are the clearest beneficiaries of the AI capital expenditure cycle. Korean semiconductor exports at +169.4% YoY represent a record print; Taiwan has revised its full-year growth forecast upward, projecting export performance at a five-decade high. Nomura frames AI hardware positioning as a structural fourth variable in Asia's growth model, alongside labor, capital, and China integration. HSBC's Frederic Neumann cautions that the export boom remains narrow, and any pullback in AI hardware demand would expose underlying fragilities — a risk that remains difficult to quantify but worth monitoring as valuations extend. ---

    Europe

    • Euro-area CPI release due Tuesday; the ECB's Isabel Schnabel said the central bank can no longer "look through" the inflationary impact of the Iran war.
    • UK political uncertainty deepened as Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham signaled he could call an early general election if he replaces Keir Starmer.
    • SoftBank announced a €75 billion commitment to AI data center capacity in France, designating the country as a top European AI infrastructure hub.
    • UK gilts remain under pressure, with bond markets "sounding the alarm" amid fiscal and political concerns; EasyJet shares fell on soaring fuel costs.

    ECB Policy and Inflation

    The ECB faces a shifting calculus. Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel's statement that the bank can no longer look through war-driven inflationary pressures represents a meaningful rhetorical shift, suggesting the dovish case for further easing has weakened. Euro-area CPI due Tuesday will be the next data point to watch. Separately, the EU is considering a temporary freeze on its Russian oil price cap — currently $60 — ahead of a July review, with the ceiling likely to rise to at least $65 given prevailing market prices. This would limit Russia's windfall while acknowledging that the cap's original calibration is no longer consistent with current market realities.

    UK Political Economy

    UK political risk is rising. Andy Burnham, widely seen as a potential successor to Prime Minister Keir Starmer, has left open the possibility of an early general election — a statement that raises constitutional and market uncertainty. Meanwhile, the Starmer government faces pressure over leaked diplomatic exchanges involving former US ambassador Peter Mandelson. UK house prices fell at their fastest pace in nearly a year, compounding the political headwinds. EasyJet's board rebuffed a takeover approach from Castlelake as "highly opportunistic," though the airline's share weakness reflects genuine fundamental stress from elevated fuel costs. ---

    LatAm

    • Colombia's first-round presidential election produced a surprise: right-wing outsider Abelardo de la Espriella won and heads into the June 21 runoff as the favorite.
    • Canada entered recession last quarter, a data point with spillover implications for North American trade flows and EM risk sentiment.
    • Mexico's President Sheinbaum escalated rhetoric against US political interference, reflecting tensions that have intensified since the deaths of two CIA agents in April.
    • Peru faces its own June 7 presidential runoff amid political uncertainty and declining mining competitiveness.

    Colombia: Political Realignment

    The Colombian election result is the region's most consequential political development of the period. De la Espriella — a celebrity attorney running on a pro-Trump, law-and-order platform — demolished the traditional Uribista conservative movement while positioning himself against the left's Iván Cepeda. Investors consider Cepeda more radical than outgoing President Gustavo Petro, meaning a de la Espriella victory would likely be market-positive at the margin. The result extends a regional rightward pattern seen previously in Brazil, Argentina, and Chile. Immediate post-election rhetoric was inflammatory on both sides, underscoring the political fragility. A de la Espriella win on June 21 would strengthen US hemispheric alignment and may ease some of the investor concern around the Andean left's policy trajectory.

    Mexico-US Tensions

    President Sheinbaum's hardening stance toward Washington introduces a new variable for Mexican asset markets. Her framing of US intelligence activities as destabilization — since the April CIA deaths — carries political risk for near-shoring investment sentiment, even if the immediate macro impact is limited. The World Cup in Mexico City provides a brief moment of economic stimulus context, but the underlying bilateral relationship appears to be deteriorating at the margins. ---

    Global

    • Brent crude rose 3.4% to $94.18 and WTI gained 2.7% to $89.75 as US-Iran peace talks over the weekend showed no meaningful progress on a draft agreement.
    • Prediction markets have nearly abandoned hope of Strait of Hormuz reopening in June; long-dated oil futures suggest a lasting but not catastrophic supply disruption.
    • The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is on pace for its best quarter ever, up 69% in two months, raising legitimate questions about AI bubble dynamics.
    • A "super El Niño" with 82% probability of forming by end-July and 67% chance of becoming a strong or very strong event threatens to compound existing inflationary and commodity pressures.

    Strait of Hormuz and Oil Markets

    Three months into the US-Iran conflict, the strategic picture has not improved. Iran's forces retain the ability to constrain Strait of Hormuz traffic despite the destruction of its formal military assets, and this weekend's skirmish near the waterway confirms the ceasefire remains unstable. An interim deal — likely involving a roughly two-month extension with Iranian reopening of the strait in exchange for US lifting its port blockade — has been discussed, but exchanges over the weekend indicated no meaningful progress. Qatar has signaled openness to a temporary transit toll as a negotiating mechanism. The EU's consideration of a Russian oil price cap freeze is a direct consequence of elevated oil prices, and reflects how the Middle East conflict is reshaping European energy policy. Capital Economics suggests China's inventory drawdown may delay global oil price pressure until Q3, providing a temporary buffer.

    AI and the Global Capital Cycle

    The AI investment boom is functioning as a near-complete offset to war-related macro headwinds in financial markets. The semiconductor complex — Micron up more than 3x, SK Hynix +260%, Samsung +165% year-to-date — now includes three companies each with market capitalizations above $1 trillion. SpaceX is preparing to launch a $75 billion IPO at a $1.8 trillion valuation, large enough that index providers are rewriting benchmark entry rules in anticipation. HSBC's Neumann warns that the AI-driven export boom in Asia is narrow and concentration risk is rising. Separately, a looming LNG supply wave from 2026–2030, partly delayed by Hormuz closures, is expected to result in a prolonged glut — adding a deflationary counterforce to near-term energy inflation.

  • US

    • No significant US macro data releases are identified in the source material for this period.
    • Russia's GDP contraction and the Iran war's impact on crude prices carry indirect implications for US energy markets and Federal Reserve inflation assessments.
    • Reform UK's rise in Britain and climate policy rollback represent a political parallel to ongoing US domestic energy debates.

    No significant US-specific macro data, Federal Reserve commentary, equity market, or corporate news coverage is present in the source material for this period. The briefings below reflect what is available from the provided sources.

    Asia

    • Taiwan overtook India as the world's fifth-largest stock market, a milestone reflecting sustained equity inflows and the island's dominant position in global semiconductor supply chains.
    • China has begun restricting overseas travel for its top AI professionals, a signal of tightening state control over strategic human capital amid intensifying technology competition with the West.
    • Kazakhstan is positioning itself as a transit and trade hub, making a strategic bet on its geographic centrality as supply chains continue to fragment along geopolitical lines.

    Equity Markets

    Taiwan's ascent to the fifth-largest equity market globally is a notable structural development. The move reflects both the market's absolute performance and a relative derating of Indian equities, which have faced headwinds from elevated domestic valuations and intermittent foreign outflows. The milestone underscores how the global semiconductor investment cycle — anchored in Taiwan — continues to concentrate capital in a geographically concentrated and geopolitically exposed node. Whether that premium is sustainable in the context of cross-strait tensions remains an open question for investors with long duration exposure.

    Technology and Capital Controls

    China's travel restrictions on leading AI professionals represent an escalation in Beijing's effort to prevent talent diffusion to Western firms and research institutions. The policy appears consistent with a broader pattern of treating advanced technology expertise as a strategic national asset, analogous to export controls on semiconductor equipment. The near-term effect may be to slow cross-border knowledge transfer, though enforcement efficacy and second-order effects on China's ability to attract and retain global talent domestically remain uncertain.

    Europe

    • A severe food price shock from climate events could raise eurozone headline inflation by up to 0.6 percentage points annually, according to Oxford Economics modeling, adding a structural upside risk to the ECB's inflation outlook.
    • A heat wave roasted much of Europe this week, consistent with the accelerating climate trends identified across the region.
    • Reform UK, currently Britain's most popular party by polling, is planning to dismantle decades of climate legislation, creating a significant policy discontinuity risk for UK energy transition investments.

    Climate and Inflation Risks

    Oxford Economics published analysis this week quantifying the inflationary pass-through from climate-driven food supply disruptions. Under a severe shock scenario, eurozone food prices could rise by 1.6 percentage points annually, translating into a 0.6 percentage point increase in headline inflation — a materially larger effect than for other G7 economies. The asymmetry likely reflects the eurozone's greater agricultural exposure and the structure of food price indexation within the bloc. For the ECB, this represents a medium-term upside risk to the inflation path that is difficult to address through conventional monetary policy tools, given its supply-side origin. The finding arrives as Europe experiences an active heat wave, lending immediate salience to what might otherwise appear an abstract modeling exercise.

    UK Political Economy

    Reform UK's position as Britain's most popular party carries concrete policy implications. The party's deputy leader has outlined plans to end net-zero commitments — a posture described internally as opposition to what it calls "net stupid zero." Were Reform to translate polling strength into electoral power, the UK's legally binding climate targets and associated green investment frameworks would face material revision. For investors in UK renewable energy, carbon markets, and ESG-linked instruments, the political trajectory warrants monitoring. The tension between Reform's platform and existing statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act would likely generate significant legal and regulatory uncertainty before any legislative change could take effect.

    LatAm

    • Brazil's President Lula is actively stimulating the economy ahead of elections, according to Bloomberg Economics, raising questions about fiscal sustainability and medium-term inflation dynamics.
    • More than half of Venezuelans support dollarization as a response to inflation running at approximately 600%, highlighting the continued macro deterioration in that economy.

    Brazil: Fiscal Stimulus and Election Dynamics

    With elections approaching, President Lula's administration is deploying fiscal stimulus to support economic momentum. Bloomberg Economics characterizes this as a deliberate effort to boost growth ahead of the vote. While the near-term demand impulse may support activity data, the strategy risks widening Brazil's already elevated fiscal deficit and adding to inflationary pressures at a point when the Banco Central do Brasil has been working to anchor expectations. The tension between electoral fiscal expansion and monetary credibility is a recurring dynamic in Brazilian political economy, and the current episode appears consistent with that historical pattern. Markets will likely price additional risk premium into Brazilian rates and the real if the stimulus trajectory extends beyond what primary balance targets can accommodate.

    Venezuela: Dollarization Debate

    Majority public support for dollarization in Venezuela reflects the severity of an inflation problem running at roughly 600%. While popular sentiment is a necessary but not sufficient condition for a formal currency change, the data point illustrates how completely the bolivar has lost its store-of-value function. Full dollarization would eliminate the inflation tax but would also remove the government's ability to monetize deficits — a constraint that Venezuela's fiscal structure has historically been unable to absorb without external support.

    Global

    • Russia's GDP contracted in the most recent quarter for the first time in three years, with the non-oil private sector — roughly three-quarters of the economy — stalling under the weight of tight monetary policy and a severe labor shortage.
    • The Iran war has lifted crude prices and temporarily eased US sanctions on Russian oil, yet the windfall has proven insufficient to offset structural domestic headwinds facing the Russian economy.
    • Swiss alpine communities face an accelerating climate adaptation challenge following the May 2025 collapse of the Birch glacier and destruction of the village of Blatten, illustrating the physical and fiscal costs of glacier retreat.

    Russia: Macro Discipline and Economic Fragility

    Russia's economy presents an unusual configuration: a wartime state running relatively orthodox macroeconomic policy. The Bank of Russia's benchmark rate, now at 14.5%, implies real rates above 9% given sub-6% consumer price inflation — among the highest real rates globally. The Russian Economy Ministry has cut its 2026 growth forecast to 0.4% from 1.3%, and the budget deficit has reached a record in nominal terms, though at 2.5% of GDP it remains modest by developed-market standards. The core problem is structural: military spending crowds out civilian investment, a labor force shortfall of approximately 1.5 million workers constrains capacity, and high borrowing costs suppress private sector activity. The oil price windfall from the Iran conflict has provided incremental revenue but cannot resolve these supply-side constraints. Bloomberg Economics assesses that the model is unsustainable in the long run, though the timing of any more acute deterioration remains highly uncertain.

    Climate: Physical Risk and Adaptation Costs

    The one-year anniversary of Blatten's destruction by a glacier collapse serves as a marker for the accelerating pace of Alpine glacier loss. Switzerland has experienced average warming of 3°C since pre-industrial times — more than double the global average — and more than 1,000 glaciers have disappeared since 1973. The fiscal dimension of this process is becoming politically contentious, as urban Swiss taxpayers increasingly bear the cost of subsidizing infrastructure and insurance in high-risk mountain communities. The Oxford Economics analysis of climate-driven food price inflation adds a further transmission channel: physical climate risk is increasingly a macroeconomic and monetary policy variable, not merely an environmental one.

  • US

    • Dell shares surged 40% in premarket on a blowout sales outlook driven by AI server demand, lifting the broader technology complex.
    • Fed's Neel Kashkari signaled no imminent rate moves while keeping a hike on the table, citing persistent inflation and Middle East energy risk.
    • The inaugural Bessent-Warsh breakfast produced no pressure on rates; the new Fed chair appears to have near-term political cover to hold policy steady.
    • The S&P 500 is on pace for a ninth consecutive weekly gain, up 4.9% in May — the "sell in May" seasonal pattern failing decisively.
    • Treasury Secretary Bessent defended placing Trump's likeness on a proposed $250 bill, a measure currently barred by US law.

    Equity Markets

    US equities held at all-time highs heading into the final session of May, with the S&P 500 up 4.9% for the month and the Nasdaq 100 up 10%, following a 16% gain in April. The rally is being led by mega-cap technology, with Dell's premarket surge of 40% — after its AI server revenue outlook far exceeded consensus — lifting Hewlett Packard Enterprise (+20%) and Super Micro Computer (+12%) simultaneously. The AI infrastructure theme continues to dominate earnings surprises, and strategists at Citadel Securities note the market conversation has shifted from hedging downside to identifying remaining sidelined cash as the next marginal buyer.

    Monetary Policy

    The first Bessent-Warsh breakfast yielded a carefully calibrated public signal: the Treasury secretary did not request rate cuts, framing his expectations around Warsh doing "the right thing for inflation and growth" — language that pointedly invokes the Fed's statutory dual mandate rather than political objectives. Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari, speaking in Seoul, reinforced the cautious stance, warning that elevated energy prices and the risk of unanchored inflation expectations preclude declaring victory. Markets have incrementally repriced toward a Fed hike by early 2027, providing modest support to the dollar, though the Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index's monthly gain of 0.7% is attracting skepticism about follow-through.

    Corporate and Political

    Beyond technology, Gap fell 15% after cutting its sales outlook, underscoring uneven consumer spending. IBM gained 6% on a $10 billion quantum computing commitment. In Texas, a little-known hard-right candidate defeated an oil-industry-backed incumbent in the Railroad Commission primary, unsettling Permian Basin operators who had mobilized backing from Occidental, ExxonMobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips — a result that introduces genuine regulatory uncertainty into the US's most prolific producing basin. ---

    Asia

    • South Korea's Kospi surged 3.6% on the day and 24% for the month, driven by a Samsung and SK Hynix rally so large that some funds are being forced to sell positions that have breached concentration limits.
    • Taiwan raised its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 9.6% from 7.7%, a meaningful beat citing AI-driven demand for semiconductors and tech goods.
    • Huawei unveiled "LogicFolding," a chip architecture it claims can enable 1.4nm-equivalent performance by 2031 without EUV lithography, sending SMIC and Hua Hong shares sharply higher.
    • Japan's two-year bond yield rose after a lukewarm auction, extending the pressure on JGB markets that has drawn renewed intervention warnings from Finance Minister Katayama.
    • China's export prices rose at the sharpest pace in three years, reflecting both commodity pass-through and the tech export boom.

    Huawei Semiconductor Breakthrough

    The most consequential technology development of the period is Huawei's public disclosure of its LogicFolding architecture at a chip conference in China. Presented by semiconductor chief He Tingbo, the technology seeks to improve chip performance by shortening internal data-transmission distances rather than shrinking transistors — an approach designed explicitly to circumvent China's inability to procure ASML's EUV machines. The company targets 1.4nm-equivalent chip production by 2031, roughly three years behind TSMC's current roadmap. Bloomberg Intelligence notes that if commercially viable, this would help Chinese AI firms maintain competitiveness despite Nvidia export restrictions. Material engineering hurdles remain — heat dissipation, manufacturing complexity, and economic scale — but the market reaction was immediate, with SMIC and Hua Hong shares soaring. The disclosure matters geopolitically as well: it bolsters the argument, previously advanced by Nvidia's Jensen Huang, that export controls may accelerate rather than prevent Chinese semiconductor self-sufficiency.

    Korea and Taiwan Equity Surge

    Samsung and SK Hynix have driven South Korea's Kospi to a 24% monthly gain — an extraordinary move for a major index — on the back of the global AI infrastructure buildout. The rally has become self-reinforcing for MSCI's emerging-markets index, which gained 7.8% in May to an all-time high, with AI component makers as the primary engine. Taiwan's upward GDP revision to 9.6% for 2026 reinforces that the semiconductor cycle remains in full expansion, and Lenovo's Hong Kong-listed shares gained 105% in May on AI server growth prospects.

    China Capital Controls and Geopolitics

    Beijing launched what sources describe as its biggest crackdown on offshore capital flows in decades, which directly hit online brokerage Futu: its founder lost $1.7 billion in a single day, representing a quarter of his net worth. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi visited Ottawa — the first such visit in a decade — reflecting a deliberate diplomatic realignment as Canada diversifies away from US dependency, exchanging EV tariff reductions for lower Canadian agricultural levies. In the South China Sea, ship-tracking data reveal intensifying Chinese coast guard activity around the Spratly Islands, raising the risk of confrontation with Vietnam. ---

    Europe

    • French and Spanish CPI accelerated to the fastest pace since 2024, above consensus, reinforcing the case for an ECB rate hike.
    • France's GDP unexpectedly contracted at the start of the year, creating a direct tension with the inflation overshoot that complicates ECB calibration.
    • Sweden's economy contracted for the first time in a year, in line with expectations, while Finland posted broad Q1 growth.
    • The EU is reviving its Chips Act with a rebooted framework requiring an estimated €120 billion in public-private investment by 2035.
    • A Russian drone entered Romanian airspace and struck an apartment building in Galați, injuring two — the most serious such incident for the NATO member since the start of the Ukraine war.

    Inflation and Growth Divergence

    The most significant European data of the period presents a genuine policy tension: CPI accelerated in France, Italy, and Spain to the fastest rates since 2024, providing clear support for ECB rate tightening, while France's economy simultaneously contracted in Q1. This combination — inflation overshoot alongside weak growth — is the precise scenario that most complicates the ECB's task. The inflation data appears to reflect energy price pass-through from the Middle East conflict, which means any ceasefire extension that reduces oil prices could ease pressure more quickly than the underlying demand picture would suggest. Sweden's contraction, in line with expectations, adds to the picture of uneven European growth momentum; Riksbank officials may interpret it as justification to pause before moving rates higher.

    EU Industrial Policy and Security

    The European Commission's rebooted Chips Act, targeting €120 billion in investment by 2035, reflects a strategic response to the semiconductor race being driven by US and Chinese capital — and by the Huawei LogicFolding disclosure specifically. Separately, EU officials are seeking access to Anthropic's Mythos AI model over cybersecurity concerns, placing Brussels at the frontier of AI governance debates. The Romanian drone incident underscores that NATO's eastern flank remains under direct kinetic pressure, with Bucharest calling the intrusion a "serious and irresponsible escalation." Russia-Armenia tensions were on display at the Eurasian Economic Union summit, where Armenian Prime Minister Pashinyan declined to attend as Yerevan pursues EU membership aspirations — a geopolitical fracture within Moscow's own institutional architecture.

    UK and Corporate

    Labour's likely future Prime Minister Andy Burnham signaled a tougher regulatory stance on big tech and AI, a development worth monitoring for European platform operators. Rachel Reeves retains credibility with bond markets despite political pressures, with Bloomberg Opinion noting her indispensability to Labour's economic narrative heading into the next election cycle. ---

    LatAm

    • The US announced it will designate Brazil's two main organized-crime groups — Primeiro Comando da Capital and Comando Vermelho — as terrorist organizations, a move likely to reignite tensions between Washington and President Lula.
    • Venezuela opposition leader María Corina Machado expressed willingness to negotiate with the interim government on a path to presidential elections, a potential opening backed by Washington.
    • Industrial metals posted their best monthly performance since January, benefiting commodity-exporting economies across the region.

    Brazil-US Political Friction

    The US designation of Brazil's two largest criminal organizations as terrorist entities is the most consequential LatAm development of the period. The move gives US law enforcement expanded tools to pursue international financial flows linked to PCC and CV, but carries significant diplomatic cost: Lula has consistently resisted framing Brazilian organized crime through a counterterrorism lens, and any perception of US interference in domestic security matters is politically toxic in Brasília. The relationship between Trump and Lula was already strained by trade policy and ideological divergence; this designation adds a new source of bilateral friction at a moment when Brazil is deepening its own commodity export relationships with China. The macro implications are indirect but real — political turbulence in Brazil tends to widen sovereign spreads and weaken the real, though neither move has been confirmed in available data for this period.

    Venezuela and Regional Politics

    Machado's signal of willingness to negotiate on a path to elections is a meaningful shift in tone, suggesting Washington's pressure campaign may be producing some movement at the margins. The broader regional picture is shaped by the commodity cycle: industrial metals' best monthly performance since January benefits Chile, Peru, and Colombia through terms-of-trade improvement, providing some offset to the tighter global financial conditions implied by higher US rates. The China-Canada rapprochement, including reduced EV tariffs, is a reminder that Beijing's commercial outreach in the Western Hemisphere is broadening, with potential implications for trade competition in agricultural and resource markets where LatAm producers compete. ---

    Global

    • The US and Iran reached a tentative 60-day ceasefire extension, pending Trump's approval; Brent crude fell 1.6% and is on track for a 19% monthly decline — its largest since 2020.
    • Anthropic raised $65 billion at a $965 billion valuation, surpassing OpenAI; Apollo and Blackstone are assembling a $36 billion debt package to finance Google TPUs leased to Anthropic.
    • The Pentagon's Indo-Pacific command is seeking billions in new ship-denial and hypersonic weapons to deter China over Taiwan by 2027.
    • SpaceX trimmed its IPO valuation target to at least $1.8 trillion from $2 trillion-plus, with a roadshow potentially beginning next week.
    • Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded on its Florida launchpad, dealing a significant setback to Jeff Bezos's effort to compete with SpaceX.

    Oil Markets and the Hormuz Truce

    The tentative 60-day US-Iran ceasefire extension is the dominant macro event of the period, and its impact on energy markets is stark: Brent crude has fallen approximately 19% in May, its steepest monthly decline since 2020, as traders price in the potential resumption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz. The reversal matters globally — elevated energy prices since the conflict began have been a primary driver of inflation reacceleration across the G7, and a sustained reduction in oil prices would reduce pressure on central banks currently contemplating hikes rather than cuts. However, Trump has not yet formally agreed to the terms, and the deal remains tentative; premature dismissal of Hormuz risk appears unwarranted. Meanwhile, the US oil system shows early strain from export demand that has helped plug the Hormuz supply gap, with American crude shipments falling to a near two-month low.

    AI Capital Flows

    The scale of capital mobilizing around artificial intelligence infrastructure is approaching macro-relevant magnitudes. Anthropic's $65 billion fundraise at a $965 billion valuation — eclipsing OpenAI for the first time — was accompanied by a $36 billion private credit facility assembled by Apollo and Blackstone to finance AI chip procurement, effectively transforming advanced semiconductors into a financeable asset class. Taiwan's tech firms are separately borrowing a record $14.5 billion. These flows are compressing risk premia in private credit, concentrating equity gains in AI-adjacent equities globally, and creating feedback loops between chip demand, energy infrastructure, and sovereign growth forecasts.

    Geopolitics and Maritime Risk

    The Pentagon's 121-page Indo-Pacific command report to Congress strikes a notably harder tone on China than the broader Trump administration, warning that the PLA is targeting Taiwan readiness by 2027 and requesting billions for asymmetric sea-denial capabilities. The Hormuz conflict has sharpened Washington's focus on maritime chokepoint control, with the Taiwan Strait now explicitly framed through the same lens. Separately, Russia's drone strike into Romanian territory — a NATO member — represents a qualitative escalation on Europe's eastern flank, while the China-Canada diplomatic rapprochement and Beijing's intensifying South China Sea operations reflect a global order in active realignment.

  • US

    • S&P 500 near flat (+0.2%) as WTI crude fell sharply intraday before reversing; no major macro data releases beat/missed consensus on the day, with equity sentiment dominated by earnings and geopolitical cross-currents.
    • Snowflake surged ~36% premarket after reporting a stronger-than-expected revenue outlook and a $6 billion AWS partnership; Salesforce fell ~2.8% on a disappointing revenue forecast.
    • Micron Technology reached a $1 trillion market capitalization in 48 trading days — the fastest on record — underpinned by analyst expectations for ~$105/share in fiscal 2027 EPS, a more than 1,200% increase from 2025 levels.
    • Food inflation continuing to build: ground beef reached a record $7.06/lb in April, with economists flagging the Iran conflict and a potential El Niño as additional upward pressures into 2027.

    Equity Markets

    US equities remained largely range-bound on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 gaining a marginal 0.2% despite a sharp intraday decline in crude prices — a decoupling that reflects the degree to which AI-driven earnings momentum has eclipsed energy as the dominant market narrative. Ed Yardeni's "FEMO" framework — fabulous earnings momentum rather than fear of missing out — appears to have genuine empirical support: the S&P 500's forward P/E of 20–22x looks defensible if the economy avoids recession, and Yardeni maintains the highest 2026 target on the Street at 8,250. JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon and Bank of America's Brian Moynihan both projected strong second-quarter trading revenues at the Bernstein Strategic Decisions Conference, reinforcing near-term earnings optimism.

    Corporate Earnings and AI Spending

    Micron's sprint to $1 trillion is the standout corporate story of the week.
