Revenue nearly tripled in its most recent quarter — the fastest growth in the company's recorded history — yet the stock trades at only ~10x forward earnings, a valuation that reflects investor skepticism about the durability of the memory cycle. Snowflake's blowout guidance and its $6 billion Amazon infrastructure agreement underscore the continued build-out of enterprise AI infrastructure. Meta's launch of paid consumer subscriptions for its Meta AI chatbot represents a meaningful step toward monetizing its substantial AI capital expenditure.

    Monetary Policy and Macro Backdrop

    No Federal Reserve speakers or macro data releases materially moved markets Thursday. The VIX remains suppressed, and DataTrek's Nicholas Colas argues that robust labor market readings — initial claims and the unemployment rate both staying contained through last year's trade war and the current Iran conflict — have shifted the implicit "Trump Put" strike price well below current market levels. This macroeconomic resilience, paradoxically, may be reducing pressure on the administration to resolve the Hormuz standoff quickly. ---

    Asia

    • Asian equities slid from record highs as Brent crude approached $97/barrel following renewed US strikes on Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz; South Korea's Kospi fell 0.5%.
    • The yuan is on course for a sixth consecutive quarterly gain against the dollar — its longest winning streak since 2013 — driven by AI-related export demand and a structural shift in China's trade composition.
    • Bank of Korea's new governor signaled a hawkish shift, pointing to rate hikes ahead in a notable departure from recent dovish policy.
    • Nio shares rallied after unveiling the ES9, China's largest electric SUV to date, a six-seat model targeting the premium domestic market.

    Monetary Policy

    The Bank of Korea's new governor delivered a hawkish pivot, signaling rate increases ahead — a significant shift in tone from the prior easing bias. The move appears consistent with persistent inflationary pressures from elevated energy costs tied to the Hormuz disruption, and may reflect concern that a weakening won would amplify imported inflation. Goldman Sachs's Korea desk separately flagged leveraged ETFs tied to Korean equities as potential volatility accelerators in a market that has already reached record highs.

    China: Currency and Trade

    The yuan's six-quarter rally against the dollar — a streak not seen since 2013 — reflects a structural evolution in China's export mix. AI-related capital goods and technology exports have surged alongside the global investment boom in data center infrastructure, generating sustained dollar inflows and making Beijing more comfortable tolerating a stronger currency. This is a materially different dynamic from prior yuan strength episodes, which were typically driven by manufacturing trade surpluses alone.

    Geopolitics and South China Sea

    China expelled a Dutch naval frigate, the HNLMS De Ruyter, from waters near the Paracel Islands, citing violations of Chinese airspace by the ship's helicopter. The incident is a rare direct confrontation between Chinese and European naval forces and underscores the contested nature of South China Sea boundaries. The De Ruyter had recently participated in joint exercises with Philippine forces — a detail likely to amplify Beijing's objections. Separately, the US has deepened military coordination across the western Pacific, deploying additional missile systems, fighter jets, and expanding joint training, even as European NATO allies explore greater strategic autonomy.

    Corporate

    Nio's ES9 launch — China's largest electric SUV — drove a share price rally and signals the company's ambition to compete at the premium end of the domestic EV market, where margins are more defensible than in the mass segment. ---

    Europe

    • Stoxx Europe 600 fell 0.5% as renewed Middle East attacks weighed on risk sentiment and pushed Brent crude higher; UK assets faced additional pressure from domestic cost-of-living and labor market concerns.
    • UK youth unemployment is rising sharply enough to prompt a government-commissioned review warning of a "lost generation," with systemic failures cited across employment, education, and mental health support.
    • British food and drink manufacturers signaled broad-based price increases: more than four-fifths of producers surveyed by the Food and Drink Federation plan to pass higher costs — partly energy-conflict-related — through to consumers.
    • Soitec gained 19% in Paris, extending its year-to-date advance to 683%, after quarterly revenue surpassed expectations — illustrating the degree to which European chip-adjacent names are capturing AI infrastructure tailwinds.

    UK Labor Market and Cost Pressures

    The UK's youth unemployment surge is emerging as a structural concern rather than a cyclical one. The government-commissioned review cited a "whole-system failure" spanning entry-level job creation, mental health provision, and educational outcomes, with AI-driven reductions in graduate and junior roles compounding existing weaknesses. Separately, the Food and Drink Federation's survey showing more than 80% of producers planning price increases is a forward-looking inflation signal that the Bank of England will need to weigh carefully — particularly given that much of the cost pressure traces to the Middle East conflict and elevated energy prices rather than domestic demand.

    Political Economy: Hungary

    With the Champions League final taking place in Budapest this weekend, the occasion spotlights the political legacy of Viktor Orbán, who was ousted in Hungary's election last month. Football served as a vehicle for patronage and subsidy under Orbán's 16-year tenure, with clubs, academies, and stadiums channeling public funds toward political allies. The incoming government now faces the fiscal and institutional cost of unwinding a system built around a single leader — a transition with broader implications for Hungary's relationship with EU institutions and the structural integrity of its public finances.

    Monetary Policy

    The ECB made no new policy announcements in this period. However, the combination of sticky food and energy inflation driven by the Hormuz disruption, and softening demand in core eurozone economies, maintains the central bank in a difficult calibration exercise between supporting growth and containing embedded price expectations. ---

    LatAm

    • No significant macro data releases from Brazil, Mexico, Argentina, Colombia, or Chile were reported in this period.
    • A Russian tanker carrying approximately 240,000 barrels of diesel abandoned its voyage to Cuba, redirecting course amid uncertainty — a development that worsens an already acute fuel crisis on the island.

    Cuba Energy Crisis

    The diversion of the Russian diesel tanker is a material setback for Cuba, which is navigating one of its most severe fuel shortages in years. The cargo was intended to provide meaningful relief to an energy system under sustained stress, and its failure to arrive tightens an already constrained supply picture. The episode illustrates the degree to which smaller, sanctions-adjacent economies remain exposed to the secondary effects of geopolitical disruption in global energy markets — including shipping route uncertainty amplified by the US-Iran conflict. The broader regional implication is that countries with limited access to spot energy markets face disproportionate vulnerability when supply chains are disrupted. No other significant macro or market developments for Latin America were reported in this period. ---

    Global

    • Brent crude surged toward $97/barrel after US forces shot down Iranian drones and struck a launch unit near the Strait of Hormuz, reversing earlier peace-deal optimism that had pushed WTI down 5.55% to $88.68.
    • Gold remains approximately 3% higher year-to-date — well below its late-January record high — as higher real yields, a stronger dollar, and reduced safe-haven demand weigh on the metal; UBS cut its year-end forecast to $5,500/oz from $5,900.
    • The US-Iran conflict has entered a period of strategic stasis: the US has conducted strikes twice this week while officially describing the ceasefire as intact, with Trump caught between Iranian demands for financial relief and Republican hawks pressing for a more decisive outcome.
    • Global AI investment is generating measurable macro effects: Chinese export composition is shifting toward technology goods, supporting yuan strength; US chip earnings are decoupling equity markets from energy price signals.

    US-Iran Conflict and Hormuz

    The Strait of Hormuz remains the central axis of global macro risk. US forces shot down four Iranian drones targeting a commercial vessel and struck a drone launch unit near the strait — actions described as defensive with the ceasefire nominally intact. Iran targeted the US base from which the strikes originated, and Kuwait separately reported responding to missile and drone threats. Trump's public framing — "they're negotiating on fumes" and "maybe we have to go back and finish it" — signals negotiating posture, but the gap between Iranian conditions and Republican hawk expectations remains wide. The longer this stasis persists, the greater the cumulative economic drag from elevated energy prices, shipping disruption, and uncertainty premia across financial markets.

    Oil and Market Decoupling

    The most striking market dynamic of the period is the near-complete decoupling of equity markets from oil prices. A 5.5% single-day decline in WTI produced virtually no movement in the S&P 500 — a stark contrast to historical patterns. The mechanism is straightforward: AI-driven earnings growth has become the dominant driver of equity valuations, and analysts' profit forecasts have improved materially since the conflict began. Treasuries remain somewhat more sensitive to crude — the 10-year yield continues to track forward oil prices as a proxy for embedded inflation expectations — but even this relationship appears to be attenuating as the market prices in a higher probability of conflict resolution.

    Gold and Precious Metals

    Gold's inability to reclaim its late-January record high despite ongoing extreme geopolitical risk reflects a confluence of structural pressures: rising real yields (gold generates no income), a stronger dollar since the conflict began, central bank liquidity needs prompting marginal selling, and speculative position reduction following the peak. UBS's reduction of its year-end target to $5,500/oz from $5,900 is consistent with this diagnosis, though the bank maintains a recovery scenario to prior highs. Cyclical commodity strategists, including Longview Economics, argue the ongoing industrial upswing favors copper over gold — a view supported by gold's elevated valuation relative to lumber and copper on historical comparisons.

  • US

    • S&P 500 closes at a fresh all-time high, driven almost entirely by a semiconductor surge; Micron gains 19.3%, joining the $1 trillion market-cap club
    • Goldman Sachs raises its S&P 500 target to 8,000, citing AI tailwinds and earnings momentum
    • Ken Paxton defeats John Cornyn decisively in the Texas Republican Senate runoff after securing Trump's endorsement
    • University of Michigan consumer sentiment hits its lowest reading since the survey's 1978 inception, underscoring a widening divergence between financial markets and household confidence

    Semiconductor Rally Drives Equity Records

    The S&P 500 reached another record close on Tuesday, a move that owes nearly everything to the semiconductor complex rather than broad-based economic optimism. Micron Technology surged 19.3% — catalyzed by UBS doubling its price target, implying a potential $1.8 trillion valuation within a year — and joined a now ten-member club of US companies valued above $1 trillion. The divergence between the chip sector and the rest of the market is striking: the SOX index has contributed roughly 369 points to the S&P 500's 672-point year-to-date gain, against a more modest 79 points from the remaining Magnificent Six. Goldman Sachs' decision to lift its year-end S&P 500 target to 8,000 reflects confidence in AI-driven earnings upgrades, though the concentration of returns in a narrow set of chip names warrants scrutiny.

    Valuation and Earnings Uncertainty

    Micron's forward price-to-earnings multiple remains below the S&P 500 average, a legacy of the market's historical treatment of chipmakers as cyclical commodities. The more consequential question is whether the explosive upward revisions to forward earnings estimates — which only began materializing this year — are durable. Micron and its peers now count the world's largest hyperscalers as captive, deep-pocketed customers, which justifies a structural re-rating. Yet the precision of any forecast in a period of this growth velocity is inherently limited, and the risk of estimate disappointment is non-trivial.

    Consumer Sentiment Disconnect

    The record equity prints stand in sharp contrast to a University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading that, by Bloomberg's account, is the weakest since the survey's inception. That divergence — financial asset inflation alongside deteriorating household confidence, in part reflecting elevated energy prices from the unresolved Strait of Hormuz situation — represents a tension the market has thus far chosen to set aside. ---

    Asia

    • SK Hynix surpasses $1 trillion in market capitalization, following Samsung's similar milestone earlier this month; the company controls 57% of the high-bandwidth memory market
    • Hong Kong overtakes Switzerland as the world's largest cross-border wealth hub, with $2.9 trillion in international assets under management, fueled by mainland Chinese inflows
    • ByteDance is in discussions over capital expenditure of up to $70 billion for 2026, a significant escalation of China's AI infrastructure build-out
    • Taiwan prosecutors allege Nvidia chips were smuggled to China via Japan, marking the first public enforcement action targeting that diversion route
    • Hong Kong banks are tightening scrutiny of mainland Chinese clients as Beijing cracks down on illegal cross-border capital flows

    Semiconductor Wealth Creation

    Asia's chipmakers are among the principal beneficiaries of the current AI investment cycle. SK Hynix's crossing of the $1 trillion threshold — following Samsung's earlier milestone — reflects the company's dominant position in high-bandwidth memory, a component for which demand is expected to outstrip supply through at least 2027. That pricing power translates directly into earnings revisions that analysts are still catching up to. Samsung's workforce, meanwhile, accepted a wage agreement providing an average bonus of approximately $340,000 per chip worker, averting a strike that would have threatened near-term production.

    Hong Kong's Wealth Management Resurgence

    Hong Kong's emergence as the world's leading offshore wealth hub is a notable reversal from the narrative of decline that dominated commentary on the city several years ago. The $2.9 trillion in cross-border assets managed there — up 11% year-on-year — reflects both mainland Chinese capital seeking diversification and the recovery in the city's IPO market, which saw the Hang Seng rise 28% last year. Boston Consulting Group projects the gap between Hong Kong and Switzerland will widen to $600 billion by 2030, underpinned by Asia's wealth accumulation trajectory and China's manufacturing base.

    China's AI Infrastructure Ambition

    ByteDance's reported discussions around $70 billion in capital expenditure for 2026 signal a more aggressive posture from Chinese technology companies in the global AI race. This comes alongside Huawei's announcement of a "Logic Folding" breakthrough aimed at producing competitive AI processors without access to leading-edge Western equipment. Whether these initiatives translate into genuine competitive parity with US semiconductor leaders remains to be seen, but the investment commitment is substantial and has been interpreted by markets as further validation of the AI infrastructure supercycle thesis. ---

    Europe

    • The ECB's semi-annual Financial Stability Review warns that asset prices remain "stretched by historical standards," with geopolitical and macro-financial risks appearing underestimated by investors
    • BP Chairman Albert Manifold disputes the company's account of his abrupt dismissal, deepening uncertainty around the UK oil major's leadership
    • Former Greek Prime Minister Alexis Tsipras launches a new left-wing party ahead of elections, adding to Europe's increasingly fragmented political landscape
    • Switzerland's June 14 referendum on a 10-million population cap is generating alarm among multinationals; polls show the electorate evenly divided
    • European car sales rose for a third consecutive month, with EV demand cited as a driver

    ECB Financial Stability Warning

    The ECB's Financial Stability Review struck a cautious tone, noting that despite some recent price adjustments, asset valuations remain elevated relative to historical norms. The central bank identified geopolitical developments — explicitly including the Iran conflict — fiscal trajectories, and macro-financial imbalances as risks that markets appear to be discounting. The Bank of England's Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden offered a similar assessment last month, observing the tension between high asset prices and the breadth of underlying risks. These warnings do not imply imminent policy action, but they signal that European regulators are increasingly attentive to the possibility of a disorderly market correction.

    BP Leadership Turmoil

    The dispute between BP and its ousted chairman Albert Manifold threatens to become a protracted public controversy. Manifold has stated he was dismissed without warning or explanation and intends to challenge the company's version of events, which cited concerns over "governance standards, oversight and conduct." People close to the company have pointed to allegations of aggressive behavior toward employees and mishandling of sensitive information. The episode adds to a period of significant strategic and leadership instability at BP, and the public nature of the disagreement complicates the board's position.

    Swiss Immigration Vote

    The June 14 referendum on capping Switzerland's population at 10 million — requiring a sharp reduction in immigration — has become a source of material concern for businesses operating in the country. Companies including Roche, Google, and Logitech have drawn attention to their dependence on international talent, and Roche's chairman has characterized the initiative as dangerous for both society and the economy. With polls showing the electorate closely divided, the outcome carries genuine uncertainty for Switzerland's position as a hub for global business. ---

    Global

    • US-Iran ceasefire talks are progressing, though Secretary of State Rubio indicated a final deal on the Strait of Hormuz may require several more days
    • Emerging market resilience masks significant fragility: strip out semiconductor proxies and the MSCI EM index is near Liberation Day lows
    • Russian ballistic missile attacks on Kyiv are intensifying as the battlefield reaches stalemate; Moscow is also reportedly considering restricting diesel and jet fuel exports
    • Colombia's presidential election approaches with a leftist frontrunner, adding to a cluster of EM political risk events in Latin America

    Strait of Hormuz: Unresolved

    The US-Iran ceasefire is holding, but the Strait of Hormuz remains closed pending a broader agreement. President Trump characterized talks as progressing while Secretary Rubio cautioned that closure of a comprehensive deal could take several additional days. The prolonged uncertainty continues to weigh on energy-importing economies, with elevated oil prices feeding inflationary pressures across emerging markets. The four largest MSCI EM constituents — China, South Korea, India, and Taiwan — are each among the economies most exposed to an extended Strait disruption, illustrating the systemic nature of the risk.

    Emerging Market Fragility Beneath the Surface

    Emerging market assets have broadly stabilized since the initial shock of the Iran conflict, and capital flows to EM bond markets have turned modestly positive. However, this resilience is heavily concentrated: Oxford Economics analysis suggests that stripping out the three largest AI proxies leaves the MSCI EM index essentially flat relative to its post-Liberation Day trough. Political risk has re-emerged as a concern, with Indonesia's Prabowo Subianto drawing scrutiny for authoritarian policy moves, Turkey's opposition leader facing a court-ordered ouster, and Colombia heading into a first-round presidential vote with a leftist candidate polling as frontrunner. Oxford Economics' quarterly EM survey recorded its sharpest deterioration in conditions since late 2022, with growth risks skewed to the downside and inflation expected to remain elevated.

    Russia-Ukraine and Global Energy

    Russia's escalation of ballistic missile attacks on Kyiv, combined with reports that Moscow is considering restricting diesel and jet fuel exports in response to Ukrainian drone strikes on refinery infrastructure, introduces an additional layer of complexity to global energy markets. Canada's agreement to supply Germany with LNG from a planned west-coast facility represents a meaningful step toward European energy diversification, though the facility's timeline leaves near-term supply balances unaffected.

  • US

    • US equity futures extended gains (+0.6% for S&P 500 futures) even as fresh US strikes on Iranian targets injected renewed uncertainty into ceasefire negotiations.
    • The Fed's first full week under Chair Kevin Warsh began with markets pricing rate hikes by early 2027, reversing expectations from just three months ago when cuts were the consensus.
    • The PCE price index — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — is due Thursday, with consensus expecting a reading approaching 4%, driven primarily by energy costs.
    • MSCI momentum stocks are on track for their strongest two-month outperformance versus the broad market on record, with AI mania identified as the dominant driver.

    Federal Reserve and Monetary Policy

    Kevin Warsh was sworn in as Fed Chair on Friday, and markets have wasted little time pricing the implications. Traders now assign high probability to the first rate hike arriving by March 2027, a complete reversal from the rate-cut expectations that prevailed in late February. The 5s30s Treasury spread has compressed to its tightest level in a year, reflecting a "higher for longer" repricing at the front end. Warsh received rare license from President Trump at his swearing-in — "don't look at me, just do a great job" — though whether that deference persists under political pressure remains to be seen. Thursday's PCE reading, expected near 4%, will be the week's pivotal data point: a significant upside surprise could accelerate the timetable for tightening.

    Equity Markets and Momentum

    US equity futures held gains despite the Iran-related volatility in crude, suggesting markets are treating the overnight strikes as tactically defensive rather than strategically escalatory. The more consequential story may be the momentum trade itself: MSCI's global momentum gauge has outperformed the ACWI by 17 percentage points since end-March, the best two-month run in three decades of data. Bank of America's Michael Hartnett characterized the environment as "bubbly," noting retail mania and compressed volatility. The risk is straightforward — crowding in a narrow set of AI-linked winners leaves the market highly sensitive to any inflation or earnings shock that forces the Fed's hand more aggressively than current pricing implies. ---

    Asia

    • Taiwan's stock market overtook India in total market capitalization, driven primarily by TSMC's sustained rally on AI semiconductor demand.
    • South Korea's Kospi surged 2.6%, bucking the cautious tone in broader Asian markets.
    • China moved to restrict overseas travel for top AI professionals at firms including Alibaba and DeepSeek, in what appears to be a material escalation of technology-security controls.
    • Japan's net external assets rose to an all-time high of ¥561.8 trillion, yet the country slipped to third-largest creditor nation as China's net external position grew faster.

    China: AI Controls and Physical Robotics

    Beijing's decision to impose overseas travel restrictions on strategically important AI personnel represents a notable shift in posture — less an isolated measure than part of a broader effort to prevent talent and intellectual property from migrating abroad. The move coincides with growing recognition, articulated by analysts at Alpine Macro and Barclays, that China's advantages in physical AI — manufacturing scale, rare-earth dominance, and actuator supply chains — may be more durable than its current gap in large language models. Barclays estimates that the "brawn" component, dominated by actuators reliant on magnetic rare earths, accounts for roughly 50% of humanoid production costs, a segment where China's industrial base is difficult to challenge in the near term. Chinese IT stocks have now outperformed even the US tech sector under Trump's second term, which appears to partly reflect this reappraisal.

    Japan and South Korea

    Japan's net external asset position reached a record ¥561.8 trillion at end-2025, yet the country now ranks third globally after Germany and China. The structural shift reflects the faster accumulation of China's overseas net position rather than any deterioration in Japan's. Separately, South Korea announced plans to develop nuclear-powered submarines under the "Jangbogo-N" program, with initial deployment targeted for the late 2030s. Officials framed the initiative as both a response to North Korea's submarine-based nuclear capabilities and a long-term industrial anchor for shipbuilding, nuclear energy, and defense manufacturing — sectors that stand to benefit from sustained government procurement. ---

    Europe

    • Bund yields and inflation breakevens fell sharply on Monday as prospects of a Hormuz deal raised hopes of easing energy-cost pressures, though fresh US strikes on Iran have since partially reversed the move.
    • ECB rate-hike expectations moderated, with markets paring back bets on three hikes this year following the shift in the oil outlook.
    • ECB's Isabel Schnabel told Reuters the ECB should hike rates in June, while Governing Council member Villeroy noted second-round inflation effects have not yet materialized.
    • Italy's governing coalition claimed an unexpected mayoral victory in Venice, a modest but politically meaningful win for Prime Minister Meloni two months after a referendum defeat.

    ECB Policy and the Hormuz Effect

    The potential Hormuz deal created an abrupt reassessment of European macro risks on Monday, given that Europe — as a large net oil importer — has been disproportionately exposed to the supply disruption. Citi's economic surprise indexes showed a striking divergence between European and US macro performance over the past three months, consistent with the asymmetric impact of the conflict. Bund yields and inflation expectations both moved sharply lower, and ECB rate expectations softened accordingly. The tension within the Governing Council is now visible: Schnabel's call for a June hike sits uncomfortably alongside Villeroy's observation that second-round effects have yet to materialize, and the uncertainty over whether any Hormuz deal will hold complicates both positions. European banks are also in focus after a report that lenders have offloaded risk on $500 billion of corporate loans — a transaction volume drawing regulatory scrutiny.

    UK and Political Landscape

    UK markets were closed for the Monday holiday, limiting the immediate domestic reaction to the oil-market moves. Political commentary centered on speculation over the next Treasury Secretary, with former energy secretary Ed Miliband identified by some in Westminster circles as a possible candidate — a suggestion that met with notable skepticism from political analysts who view the field as stronger elsewhere. In Spain, a developing graft probe involving former Prime Minister Zapatero has introduced fresh uncertainty around the governing coalition's stability ahead of elections, as the investigation unsettles the alliance supporting current Prime Minister Sánchez. ---

    Global

    • Reports of a potential US-Iran deal to extend the ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz drove Brent crude down more than 7% on Monday, but fresh US airstrikes on Iranian missile sites and mine-laying vessels pushed prices back above $98 per barrel in Asian trading.
    • China appears to be playing a quiet but consequential mediation role, with Pakistani Army Chief Asim Munir shuttling between Beijing and Doha and Iran reportedly considering sending enriched uranium to China as part of a final agreement.
    • Russia's Foreign Minister Lavrov called Secretary of State Rubio to advise the evacuation of US personnel from Kyiv, as Moscow signaled continuation of systematic strikes on Ukrainian decision-making infrastructure.
    • The Quad — US, Japan, India, Australia — launched a maritime surveillance initiative for the Indo-Pacific and committed up to $20 billion in support for critical-minerals supply chain development.

    Strait of Hormuz: Deal or Delay?

    The sequence of events over the past 48 hours illustrates the gap between headline optimism and operational reality in the Iran negotiations. Trump's assertion that talks are "proceeding nicely" was followed within hours by US Central Command strikes on Iranian missile launch sites and vessels attempting to lay mines — characterized by US authorities as defensive. Brent, which fell sharply Monday on thin holiday volumes, recovered to approach $99.50 a barrel. Prediction market odds of a swift return to normal in Hormuz declined on the strike reports, though financial markets appeared to treat the incidents as tactical rather than strategic, with equity futures and bond markets broadly holding Monday's moves. Republican hawks, including Senator Lindsey Graham, have argued the emerging terms concede too much to Tehran. Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei, meanwhile, declared that US military bases in the region will no longer be safe — language that complicates any clean resolution. Rubio acknowledged talks could take several more days.

    China's Mediation Role and Geopolitical Architecture

    Beijing's positioning in the Iran talks merits attention beyond the immediate deal mechanics. President Xi met Pakistani Army Chief Munir in Beijing, thanking him for his mediation efforts, while an Iranian delegation traveled to Doha for consultations that included discussion of frozen Iranian funds. The reported possibility of Iran's highly enriched uranium being transferred to China — however uncertain — would represent an unusual expansion of Beijing's direct involvement in Middle Eastern security arrangements. China has strong economic incentives: its export-dependent economy is sensitive to both demand destruction abroad and elevated input costs from high oil prices. The episode is consistent with a broader pattern of Beijing seeking to shape outcomes in regional conflicts where its economic interests are materially affected, without assuming visible diplomatic leadership.

  • US

    • S&P 500 futures rose 1.0% and Nasdaq 100 futures gained 1.5% on US-Iran deal optimism, while WTI crude fell 5.9% toward $90.93.
    • Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is expected to prioritize inflation-fighting credibility over White House pressure for rate cuts; rising real yields remain a structural headwind for long-term rates regardless of Hormuz resolution.
    • Trump administration's effective halt on onshore wind permits is putting roughly $50 billion in investments and 150,000 jobs at risk, according to industry groups.
    • Trump's upcoming medical examination ahead of his 80th birthday is drawing renewed scrutiny of his health.

    Equity and Rate Markets

    US equity futures rallied sharply as diplomatic signals around the Strait of Hormuz improved sentiment and oil prices fell, lifting risk appetite broadly. However, the rate picture remains more complicated. Investors appear to be looking through near-term energy price relief: rising real yields — rather than inflation expectations alone — explain much of the move higher in long-term US Treasury rates. ING's analysis suggests that even a full Hormuz reopening may leave long-duration rates "a tad stranded at elevated levels," as the structural driver of higher real yields is unlikely to reverse quickly.

    Monetary Policy

    At the Federal Reserve, markets appear to be pricing Chair Kevin Warsh as resistant to political pressure from the White House on rate cuts. Kevin Hassett, the president's chief economic adviser, argued that a Middle East deal would create room for the Fed to ease, but the market's working assumption is that Warsh will not preempt the data. The tension between a potential cyclical oil-price disinflationary impulse and persistently elevated real yields leaves the Fed's near-term path genuinely uncertain.

    Energy Policy

    The Trump administration's effective freeze on onshore wind permitting is creating measurable economic consequences, with a trade group estimating $50 billion in stranded investment risk. The policy diverges sharply from energy transition momentum elsewhere, and comes at a moment when Middle East supply disruptions are reinforcing the strategic case for domestic renewables diversification. ---

    Asia

    • Global equities climbed toward record highs and Japanese government bonds rallied as Asia markets opened with optimism on US-Iran diplomacy; the dollar weakened.
    • Beijing launched its most forceful crackdown on cross-border equity trading, triggering an estimated $1.7 billion single-day wealth loss for Futu Holdings founder Leaf Li; broader capital outflow concerns are intensifying.
    • Singapore flagged a weakening 2026 outlook despite a strong Q1, citing Middle East energy disruption, tariff risks, and potential AI capex pullback.
    • Huawei's semiconductor chief outlined a new pathway to narrow the gap with TSMC, claiming potential breakthroughs without access to advanced equipment.

    China Capital Controls

    Beijing's crackdown on illicit cross-border stock trading — described as the most forceful in recent years — sent an unambiguous signal about the authorities' intent to arrest capital outflows that reached an estimated $1 trillion in 2025, the largest annual figure since records began in 2006. The immediate market casualty was Futu Holdings, whose US-listed shares fell sharply, cutting Leaf Li's fortune by more than half from its October peak. The episode illustrates a structural tension: Beijing wants financial engagement with global markets on its own terms, but retail investors have been voting with their capital.

    Singapore and Regional Macro

    Singapore's Ministry of Trade reported Q1 GDP growth of 1.0% quarter-on-quarter, well ahead of the 0.2% consensus, driven substantially by AI-related investment flows. However, the government's forward guidance struck a notably cautious tone, identifying the Middle East energy disruption, US tariff escalation risk, and a possible correction in AI capital spending as meaningful downside scenarios. Malaysia separately reported tangible economic fallout from the Iran conflict. The contrast between strong first-quarter momentum and deteriorating forward visibility is becoming a recurring theme across the region.

    Huawei Semiconductors

    Huawei's semiconductor chief He Tingbo, in a rare public appearance at an industry conference, outlined a strategy to close the manufacturing gap with TSMC — currently estimated at approximately five years — through a new developmental pathway that does not rely on leading-edge lithography equipment. The claim warrants scrutiny, as similar assertions have historically preceded more gradual progress than initially suggested, but the directional intent reinforces that US export controls have accelerated rather than halted China's domestic chip ambitions. ---

    Europe

    • The ECB is edging toward a rate hike at its June meeting unless a durable US-Iran peace agreement is reached and sustained; President Lagarde confirmed the bank will revise its inflation outlook higher.
    • UK political paralysis deepens as Starmer's leadership position weakens, with government decision-making slowing and the left of the Labour party pressing wealth tax proposals.
    • Turkish police stormed the main CHP opposition party headquarters in Ankara using pepper spray, seizing control of the building in a politically charged escalation.
    • A Bloomberg investigation found companies across at least nine European countries purchased Chinese carbon credits from projects that may not exist, exposing significant governance failures in the EU's emissions offset framework.

    ECB Policy

    Two Governing Council members indicated the ECB is moving toward a rate increase at its June meeting, conditional on the Strait of Hormuz situation remaining unresolved. President Lagarde separately confirmed the bank will revise its inflation projections upward next month. The policy pivot is notable given the ECB's recent easing cycle: the energy shock from Middle East hostilities has effectively overridden the disinflationary forces that had been guiding the institution toward neutral. The conditionality on a "lasting peace deal" introduces genuine two-way risk to the rate path in coming weeks.

    UK Political Risk

    The UK's political situation continues to deteriorate. Commentary from Westminster suggests Starmer's administration is entering a period of effective policymaking paralysis as the leadership question remains unresolved. Market participants appear to be watching whether fiscal policy credibility is maintained during the transition — bond markets have thus far been relatively stable, though the risk of a leftward policy shift during any leadership contest is a live consideration. Andy Burnham's potential candidacy is drawing both attention and some skepticism from fixed-income investors.

    Carbon Market Integrity

    A Bloomberg investigation found that companies in at least nine European countries — including BP, Shell, TotalEnergies, EDF, ExxonMobil, and OMV — purchased carbon credits from Chinese projects in the EU's upstream emissions reduction scheme that appear to have been fraudulent or non-existent. German authorities have already voided credits from 30 projects claiming 2.1 million tons of CO₂ savings. No criminal penalties are expected for the purchasing companies, as acquisitions were made in good faith, but the episode raises fundamental questions about the governance architecture of European carbon markets ahead of the EU's planned expansion of credit-based mechanisms toward its 90% emissions reduction target for 2040. ---

    Global

    • US and Iran are inching toward a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz; Brent crude fell below $100 and WTI dropped nearly 6%, though significant obstacles remain including disagreements over asset unfreezing.
    • Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei — who has not appeared in public since being injured in the war's opening strikes — faces the defining decision of his nascent leadership: accept an interim ceasefire or hold out for terms Washington will reject.
    • Russia launched a major missile-and-drone assault on Ukraine, including a hypersonic Oreshnik ballistic missile strike near Kyiv, in one of the conflict's larger single-day attacks.
    • Ebola may have killed more than 200 people in the Democratic Republic of Congo, with flights suspended and cross-border containment at risk.

    Hormuz Diplomacy

    The most consequential development across global markets remains the trajectory of US-Iran negotiations. Secretary of State Rubio struck an upbeat tone in New Delhi, and some vessel movement — including an Iraqi crude supertanker crossing the US blockade line into the Arabian Sea — has added practical weight to diplomatic optimism. ADNOC has reportedly been moving oil and gas shipments through the Gulf using its own fleet, suggesting informal clearances on both sides. However, Iran's Foreign Ministry explicitly stated that "no one can claim that the signing of an agreement is imminent," and Tehran's demand to unfreeze assets remains a blocking issue. The gap between market pricing and diplomatic reality appears to be narrowing but not yet closed.

    Geopolitical Crosscurrents

    Beyond Hormuz, the geopolitical backdrop remains unsettled. Russia's use of a hypersonic Oreshnik missile in a mass strike involving 90 missiles and 600 drones against Ukrainian cities represents a continued escalation in the quality of munitions deployed, even as the front lines remain relatively static. In China, Xi Jinping reportedly expressed sharp criticism of Japan's Prime Minister Takaichi over Tokyo's defense buildup during the Beijing summit with Trump, a signal of persistent Sino-Japanese friction. In Turkey, the forced entry into CHP opposition headquarters marks a further erosion of political norms under the current government. These developments collectively suggest the global geopolitical risk premium, while partly priced through energy markets, has additional dimensions that remain underweighted in equity valuations.

  • US

    US Daily Briefing — May 23, 2026

    Coverage relevant to US markets and policy is thin on this Saturday, with Bloomberg's weekend distribution focused primarily on global thematic analysis. Two storylines nonetheless carry implications for US investors.

    Gulf capital recycling under strain

    Bloomberg Economics highlights how the ongoing Iran conflict, now in its third month, is materially altering the petrodollar recycling mechanism that has historically supported US asset prices and Treasury demand. With the Strait of Hormuz largely closed, Gulf sovereigns are confronting simultaneously lower hydrocarbon revenues and higher domestic outlays — Saudi Arabia's Q1 budget reflects roughly $17 billion in war-related costs, equivalent to 1.5% of GDP. The Public Investment Fund had already cut its targeted foreign asset allocation to 18% from a prior ambition near 50%, and recent withdrawals from LIV Golf and a $200 million Metropolitan Opera deal suggest a continued pivot inward. For US markets, the implication is incremental rather than acute: a structurally smaller Gulf bid for Treasuries and US equities at the margin, and diminished credibility around the multi-trillion-dollar investment pledges made to the Trump administration in 2025. The magnitude and persistence of the effect remain to be seen, particularly given uncertainty around the conflict's duration.

    Subnational climate policy diverges from federal

    Berkeley, California has become the first US municipality to require fossil-fuel appliance replacement or equivalent green upgrades as a condition of single-family home sale, under its Building Emissions Saving Ordinance effective January. The move is notable less for its direct economic footprint than as a marker of the widening gap between municipal climate action and federal rollback under the Trump administration, which has canceled grant programs including a Groundswell-administered residential energy resiliency initiative in Alabama and Georgia. For homebuilders, appliance manufacturers and residential energy retrofitters, the patchwork of state and local mandates increasingly substitutes for the federal incentive architecture being dismantled. No major US macro, Fed, or corporate developments were reported in today's distribution.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific Daily Briefing — May 23, 2026

    Coverage for the region is thin in today's flow, with the bulk of newswire material focused on the ongoing Iran war's disruption of Gulf petrodollar flows and US domestic climate policy. We highlight the Asia-relevant threads below.

    China growth momentum fades, reviving stimulus debate

    Bloomberg Economics flagged that China's economy is slowing across the board, reigniting expectations for additional policy support. The signal is consistent with the softer prints seen through the spring, though specifics on the composition of any forthcoming package — fiscal, monetary, or property-targeted — remain unclear. Markets will look for clarity from Beijing in the weeks ahead, particularly given that external demand is being further pressured by the Hormuz disruption and elevated energy costs.

    India digests a sharp equity drawdown tied to AI underexposure

    Indian equities have seen a notable rout, with commentary framing it as the cost of "missing the AI wave." The narrative marks a meaningful shift from the bullish India-as-alternative-to-China positioning that dominated 2024–25 flows, and warrants scrutiny as to whether this reflects a durable rerating of sector composition or a tactical rotation. We would caution against extrapolating either way until earnings revisions confirm the trend.

    Indirect Asia channel: shrinking Gulf capital pools

    The most consequential cross-regional development remains the contraction of petrodollar recycling. Gulf sovereign wealth funds, historically large allocators to Asian tech, infrastructure, and EM debt, are turning inward to fund war-related spending and domestic projects such as Saudi Arabia's $35 billion Aramco privatization push. Pakistan, which has previously relied on Gulf bilateral support to avert default, sits among the more exposed sovereigns; Egypt-style rescues will be harder to replicate. Asian beneficiaries of GCC FDI — including Indian infrastructure platforms and Southeast Asian data-center projects — may face delayed or scaled-back commitments, though the magnitude remains to be seen and will depend on the war's duration. No material flow today on Japan, Korea, or Australia.

    Europe

    European Daily Briefing — Saturday, May 23, 2026

    Weekend coverage of European markets and policy is light, with no major eurozone data releases or ECB communications crossing the wires. We focus on two structural themes with material implications for European assets and policy.

    Gulf capital retrenchment poses a headwind for European assets

    The ongoing Iran conflict, now in its third month, has largely shut the Strait of Hormuz and is reshaping the petrodollar recycling mechanism that has long supported European markets. Gulf sovereign wealth funds — collectively managing assets approaching multiple trillions of dollars — have historically deployed capital across European equities, real estate and prestige assets including English football clubs. With Saudi Arabia's first-quarter budget showing roughly $17 billion (1.5% of GDP) in war-related costs, and Qatar facing potential losses of $20 billion from damaged gas infrastructure that could take five years to restore, the pool of Gulf capital available for foreign investment is contracting. Saudi Arabia had already pivoted inward before the conflict, with PIF cutting its targeted international allocation to 18% from a previous goal approaching 50%, and is now pursuing a $35 billion Aramco privatization to court domestic capital. The implications for European luxury, sports franchises, prime real estate and sovereign debt markets warrant monitoring, though the magnitude of impact remains to be seen.

    UK political shift complicates climate and fiscal agenda

    Reform UK has overtaken the Conservatives as the principal party of the British Right, introducing meaningful uncertainty over the durability of UK climate policy. Against a backdrop of sluggish growth, local authorities — illustrated by Cardiff's combined cycle-lane and flood-mitigation projects — are increasingly designing infrastructure to qualify for multiple funding streams, a pragmatic response to fiscal constraints. Reform's stated opposition to net-zero policy, articulated by deputy leader Richard Tice, suggests a potential reordering of UK energy and infrastructure investment priorities should current polling translate into electoral outcomes. Eurozone-specific catalysts resume Monday with the standard data calendar.

    Global

    Gulf capital flows reverse as Hormuz disruption enters third month

    Nearly three months into the US-Iran conflict, the largely shuttered Strait of Hormuz is reshaping not only energy markets but the global capital cycle that has long depended on recycled petrodollars. Bloomberg Economics estimates Saudi Arabia's first month of fighting alone cost roughly $17 billion, or 1.5% of GDP, with defense, logistics and economic-support outlays climbing across the six GCC states. Qatar reports that one damaged gas facility could take up to five years to restore, with associated losses potentially reaching $20 billion. The UAE is accelerating construction of pipelines that bypass Hormuz. The combination of compressed export revenues and elevated domestic spending implies a meaningful contraction in the pool of Gulf capital available for deployment abroad — a flow that, over decades, helped absorb US Treasury supply and suppress global borrowing costs. The magnitude and persistence of that effect on US rates remains to be seen, but the directional implication is clear.

    Pledges to Washington increasingly in question

    The trillions in investment commitments made to the Trump administration last year look progressively less deliverable. Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund had already cut its targeted share of foreign assets to 18% from an earlier ambition near 50%, and the kingdom shifted from net lender to net borrower in 2024. The war is accelerating an inward turn: LIV Golf is preparing for possible bankruptcy after PIF withdrew funding, a $200 million Met Opera deal has been abandoned, and Riyadh is now focused on the $35 billion Aramco privatization to court capital domestically rather than export it. Recipients of Gulf largesse — Egypt, Pakistan, Jordan, and prospective reconstruction in Gaza, Lebanon and Syria — face a tighter funding environment.

    Climate policy bifurcation

    With the Trump administration unwinding federal climate action, sub-national actors are filling gaps: Berkeley's new ordinance mandating emissions-reducing upgrades at point of sale is the first US measure of its kind, joining disclosure regimes in Austin, Minneapolis and Portland.

  • US

    Markets: AI Concentration Sustains the Rally as Bond Markets Reprice Inflation Risk

    US equity futures pointed modestly higher overnight, with the S&P 500 on track for an eighth consecutive weekly gain — the longest streak since 2023. The breadth picture, however, remains notably narrow: an equal-weighted version of the S&P 500 is unchanged since late February, while the cap-weighted benchmark has risen 8.2% since the start of the US-Israel war against Iran. Ten technology names — led by Nvidia, Amazon, Apple, Alphabet and Broadcom — account for the entirety of the index's gain over that period. Workday rose roughly 11% premarket on results that eased concerns about AI-driven disruption to enterprise software, while Take-Two and Zoom also gained. US-listed shares of Futu and Up Fintech fell more than 35% on China's cross-border brokerage crackdown. Behind the equity advance, the longer end of the Treasury curve continues to signal discomfort. G7 long yields touched a two-decade high this week as investors demand greater compensation for inflation risk tied to the Strait of Hormuz disruption. Pimco's Dan Ivascyn noted that prior energy shocks have historically preceded recessions, a view that, if it gains traction, points to lower equities, wider credit spreads and eventually lower yields. Rapidan warned that a Hormuz disruption persisting through August could push Brent toward $130 and produce a demand shock comparable to 2008.

    Fed: Warsh Sworn in as Chair

    Kevin Warsh takes the Fed chair today, with markets focused less on imminent rate action than on potential changes to the communications framework. ABN Amro analysts suggest the SEP and press conference cadence could be scaled back within 6–12 months, and that Warsh may commission an external review of the Fed's framework — a step pointedly not taken after the post-Covid inflation overshoot.

    Fiscal and Political

    Senate Republicans rebuffed Trump's $1.8 billion request for a "weaponization" account to compensate political allies, a notable intra-party setback. Separately, Trump reversed course and announced 5,000 additional US troops for Poland. US markets are closed Monday for Memorial Day.

    Asia

    China retains structural leverage as Xi-Trump dynamic stabilizes

    Following this week's Xi-Trump summit, both sides notably downplayed rare earths in their public remarks — a shift that itself signals where leverage now sits. Bloomberg Economics estimates roughly $1.2 trillion of US GDP touches rare-earth-dependent industries, and despite some $10 billion in global public capital flowing into alternative mines and refineries this year, even optimistic forecasts see Chinese dominance persisting through 2030. The next Xi-Trump meeting, likely at the White House in September, precedes the expiration of the current trade truce, which paused Beijing's threatened extraterritorial export controls. Separately, capital flows continue to favor China: domestic banks received $61.9 billion in net inflows on behalf of clients in April, the sixth month of inflows in seven, supporting both the yuan and equities. However, China's so-called "national team" is reportedly cutting ETF holdings by roughly 90% in H1, and exchanges are scrutinizing AI-fueled rallies — suggesting officials are managing froth rather than encouraging it.

    Japan faces a widening policy tension

    Core inflation slowed to a four-year low, ostensibly giving the BOJ room to pause, yet PM Takaichi reversed course to unveil a roughly $19 billion stimulus package including renewed energy subsidies — a response to Hormuz-driven fuel costs and yen weakness. The combination complicates the BOJ's hiking path while adding pressure to already restive JGB markets.

    India's rupee approaches record lows near 97/USD,

    with the RBI reportedly weighing measures echoing its 2013 taper-tantrum playbook, including rate hikes, FX swaps and steps to attract offshore dollars. With global yields elevated and outflows persistent, the efficacy of such measures remains to be seen.

    Southeast Asia bears collateral damage from the Iran conflict

    Indonesia's plan to route coal, palm oil and nickel exports through a Danantara-controlled entity drew a Moody's warning over centralization risks. Thai and Philippine fishing fleets are idled by diesel costs, and DeepSeek is reportedly raising up to $10 billion at a $45 billion valuation, signaling continued investor appetite for Chinese AI despite commercialization risks the founder himself flagged.

    Europe

    Macro and inflation dynamics dominate as Iran-war energy shock filters through European economies

    Brent climbed back above $104/bbl, reigniting concerns that the closure of the Strait of Hormuz — if sustained through August — could push crude toward $130 and deliver a stagflationary impulse comparable in scale to past oil-driven downturns, according to Rapidan Energy Group. ECB President Christine Lagarde maintained that medium-term inflation expectations remain anchored at target, though commentary from at least one Governing Council member kept the door open to a further rate hike, and the EU's economic affairs chief said the ECB "will have to respond." Long-end G7 sovereign yields touched two-decade highs this week as investors price in the risk that persistent supply shocks shift 2% targets from ceiling to floor. The tension between Lagarde's anchored-expectations message and bond-market positioning bears watching.

    UK data and politics weighed on sentiment

    Retail sales fell at the sharpest pace in nearly a year, and a closely-watched survey showed consumer confidence among lower earners deteriorating markedly as the energy shock erodes household budgets. Chancellor Rachel Reeves moved to close a corporate tax loophole used by oil and gas producers, projecting roughly £300 million annually to fund cost-of-living measures — a modest sum relative to BP and Shell's combined $3.7 billion in UK tax payments last year, but one that risks compounding the post-2022 windfall-tax drag on North Sea investment. Poland is contemplating a similar windfall levy that could raise 6 billion zloty from Orlen, while France's budget minister flagged TotalEnergies' "exceptional profits" without committing to new taxation. On the data calendar, Germany released final Q1 GDP and France issued business confidence figures; the German Ifo print earlier in the week showed a first improvement in business sentiment since hostilities began, suggesting some resilience. In corporate news, the Estée Lauder–Puig tie-up around Charlotte Tilbury collapsed over compensation terms, sending Puig shares sharply lower. Shell faces Dutch supreme court arguments today from climate activists seeking court-mandated emissions cuts aligned with a 2050 net-zero pathway.

    Global

    Iran-US Standoff Keeps Energy Markets on Edge

    Iran said the latest US proposal "partly bridged the gap" in negotiations, but the diplomatic opening was immediately complicated by Supreme Leader comments insisting Tehran retain its uranium stockpile, and by a dispute over a proposed Iran-Oman permanent toll regime in the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump reiterated threats of renewed strikes if talks collapse. Brent rose 2.6% to above $105 a barrel, with WTI near $98, reflecting two-way risk pricing rather than directional conviction. Rapidan Energy warned that if Hormuz disruptions persist through August, Brent could approach $130, with macroeconomic consequences comparable to 2008. The conflict has also reportedly cost US forces more than two dozen MQ-9 Reaper drones — close to $1 billion in equipment — underscoring that escalation risk remains material.

    Inflation Risk Reprices the G7 Bond Complex

    Long-dated yields across the $50 trillion-plus G7 sovereign market reached two-decade highs this week as investors demanded greater compensation for inflation persistence. Memories of the post-Covid "transitory" misjudgment are shaping central bank reaction functions: the RBA has already delivered three hikes this year, and ECB officials including Lagarde have signaled vigilance, with at least one policymaker leaning toward a hike. TS Lombard's Dario Perkins characterized the environment as potentially heralding "rate hikes everywhere," with 2% targets increasingly functioning as a floor rather than a ceiling. Pimco's Daniel Ivascyn cautioned that prior energy shocks have historically culminated in recession — a tension worth monitoring as tightening bias collides with demand destruction risk.

    Divergence and Second-Order Effects

    Japan provides a counterpoint: core inflation slowed to a four-year low, complicating the BOJ's tightening path even as PM Takaichi announced a ~$19 billion stimulus package with revived energy subsidies. India's RBI is reportedly revisiting its 2013 taper-tantrum toolkit as the rupee nears 97/USD. The shock is rippling unevenly through real economies — Southeast Asian fishing fleets are idling on diesel costs, while European governments from the UK to Poland eye windfall taxes on oil majors' trading profits, a move that may further erode regional upstream investment.

    Rare Earths: A Slower-Moving Constraint

    China's dominance over rare earth separation and magnet production — underpinning roughly $1.2 trillion of US GDP-linked activity — appears likely to persist well into the next decade despite roughly $10 billion in global public funding for alternatives this year. The asymmetry will continue to shape US-China engagement ahead of the September trade truce expiry.

  • US

    A new Fed era begins amid a hawkish pivot

    Kevin Warsh will be sworn in as Federal Reserve Chair on Friday, inheriting an FOMC that has grown markedly more hawkish since the outbreak of the Iran war redrew the inflation landscape. Minutes from Jerome Powell's final meeting indicate "almost all participants" expect upward pressure on inflation from energy and supply-chain disruptions to persist, while "many" preferred dropping the easing bias from the statement altogether. A majority signaled some firming may be appropriate if inflation runs persistently above 2%. Markets have repriced accordingly: fed funds futures now imply rates topping 4% next summer, versus 2.8% priced in late February, with traders assigning roughly 70% odds to a quarter-point hike by December. Warsh, nominated as a dove, appears positioned to cap rates rather than cut them — Standard Chartered's Steve Englander notes he may lean on trimmed-mean inflation and unit labor costs to delay hikes, though a credible case for cuts remains elusive given the energy shock. Jamie Dimon, speaking to Bloomberg Television, warned rates "could be much higher than they are today."

    Nvidia delivers, but SpaceX steals the narrative

    Nvidia reported 85% revenue growth, 62% gross margins, and authorized an additional $80 billion buyback — among the largest capital return commitments on record. The stock nonetheless dipped in extended trading as the forward guidance underwhelmed elevated expectations. Attention shifted to SpaceX's public IPO filing, targeting up to $75 billion in proceeds via Nasdaq under ticker SPCX. The prospectus disclosed $18.7 billion in 2025 revenue and a $4.94 billion loss, alongside a super-voting structure preserving Elon Musk's control and performance milestones tied to a Mars colony. OpenAI is reportedly preparing its own filing within weeks, with Anthropic potentially following in October. The 30-year Treasury yield touched levels last seen in 2007, and the 2-year reached its highest since February 2025, complicating the equity backdrop.

    Microeconomic strain from immigration enforcement

    Brookings data show 82% of Los Angeles County businesses report negative impacts from ICE operations, with 44% losing more than half their revenue; in Chicago, 95% of businesses in affected corridors report declining sales. While the aggregate GDP effect remains modest, the concentrated damage to small firms may serve as an early indicator of broader policy-driven disruption. Separately, Moody's downgraded Mexico to one notch above junk, citing fiscal deterioration — a development with implications for North American credit and trade exposures.

    Asia

    AI optimism drives historic Asian tech rally

    Asian equities staged a powerful advance following Nvidia's earnings and SpaceX's IPO filing, with the gains concentrated in semiconductor and hardware supply chains where the region holds structural advantages. South Korea's Kospi surged 8.4%, while Taiwan's Taiex rose 3.4%. Samsung climbed 8% after reaching an eleventh-hour deal with its union — averting a potentially crippling strike at the world's largest memory-chip maker — that could distribute roughly ₩40 trillion ($26.6 billion) in AI-linked bonuses, averaging around $340,000 per chip employee. SK Hynix jumped nearly 8%, lifting its market capitalization to $910 billion. Samsung and TSMC have together added approximately $1.2 trillion in market value year-to-date. South Korean export data for May 1-20 reinforced the narrative, with working-day-adjusted shipments up 53% year-on-year and chip exports surging 202%. Japan posted a third consecutive trade surplus as semiconductor exports rose 44%, partly offset by a 64% collapse in crude imports reflecting Hormuz disruptions.

    Indonesia's policy turn unsettles markets

    Bank Indonesia delivered a surprise 50bp hike as the rupiah traded near record lows and the Jakarta Composite extended its year-to-date decline to roughly 26% — the world's worst-performing major equity market. The move followed President Prabowo's announcement of a new Danantara-controlled state entity to oversee exports of palm oil, coal, and ferro-alloys (collectively over $65 billion in 2025 shipments), a step that reportedly blindsided senior officials. While Jakarta frames the overhaul as targeting under-invoicing, investors read it as deepening economic nationalism. The hike may provide only temporary relief absent a shift in policy direction.

    Regional pressures mount elsewhere

    The Reserve Bank of India is reportedly weighing all options — including a rate hike, expanded FX swaps, and overseas dollar issuance — after the rupee touched nearly 97/USD. Trump's renewed suggestion of a direct call with Taiwan's Lai Ching-te introduces fresh tail risk to recently stabilized US-China ties. A BOJ official signaled support for a near-term hike, with June increasingly in play.

    Europe

    Eurozone activity contracts again under energy shock

    The composite PMI fell to 47.5 in May from 48.8, holding below the 50-threshold for a second consecutive month and marking the sharpest contraction in two and a half years. The data confirm that the Iran war and accompanying surge in energy costs are weighing materially on activity. The divergence within the bloc is notable: Germany's composite reading was broadly stable, while France's plunged to its lowest level since 2020. The European Commission separately projected the fastest euro-area inflation since 2023, leaving the ECB in an increasingly uncomfortable position as it weighs further rate increases against deteriorating growth. French political risk eased somewhat with parliament's confirmation of Emmanuel Moulin as Bank of France governor, removing one source of uncertainty around the ECB's policy debate.

    UK fiscal response and Foreign Office retrenchment

    Chancellor Rachel Reeves is preparing a package to ease cost-of-living pressures stemming from the Iran conflict, including free summer bus travel for children and potential cuts to food tariffs. The UK also concluded a long-awaited free-trade agreement with the Gulf Cooperation Council, deepening commercial ties with oil-rich states at a sensitive moment. Less constructively, the Foreign Office is undertaking job cuts that will roughly halve the team handling Iran-related fallout — a curious resource decision given the conflict's persistence. UK May PMI data also showed waning momentum and mounting price pressures.

    Corporate and political notes

    Spanish construction group ACS, chaired by Florentino Pérez, has emerged as a significant beneficiary of the AI capex cycle through data-center contracts with Meta and a $2 billion partnership with BlackRock. Ubisoft fell 14% in Paris after guiding to declining sales on a thinner release slate. In Hungary, the ruling Tisza party plans to use its two-thirds majority to impose an eight-year prime ministerial term limit, effectively barring Viktor Orbán's return. Chancellor Merz proposed associate EU membership for Ukraine, though Kyiv has previously been cool to the formulation.

    Global

    Oil, Hormuz, and the Diplomatic Track

    Crude prices remained the central macro variable, with Brent trading near $106 after a 5.6% decline Wednesday on hints of progress in US-Iran talks. President Trump told reporters Washington was in the "final stages" with Tehran, and Iran's Foreign Ministry confirmed it was reviewing the US response to its 14-point proposal, which envisions a short-term arrangement reopening the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for lifting the blockade on Iranian ports, followed by deeper nuclear negotiations. The diplomatic signals, however, sit alongside continued physical disruption: Goldman Sachs estimates visible global crude and product inventories are drawing down at a record 8.7 million barrels per day in May, nearly double the conflict's running average. Bloomberg Intelligence's latest survey shows market participants increasingly converging on oil capped near $100 over the next year, implying demand destruction rather than supply restoration does the rebalancing work. A resolution remains far from certain, and reports that Iran's supreme leader directed retention of near-weapons-grade uranium underscore the gap still to bridge.

    Monetary Policy Divergence Widens

    The Fed minutes revealed a markedly more hawkish FOMC than previously understood, with "almost all" participants expecting persistent inflation pressure from elevated energy prices and "many" preferring to drop the easing bias entirely. Futures now imply fed funds above 4% next summer, versus 2.8% pricing pre-conflict. Kevin Warsh, sworn in Friday as Fed Chair, inherits a committee whose consensus has shifted beneath him; the realistic ceiling on his dovishness appears to be holding rates rather than cutting. Bank Indonesia, by contrast, was forced into a surprise 50bp hike as the rupiah traded near record lows, compounded by President Prabowo's announcement that palm oil, coal, and nickel exports will be routed through a state entity under sovereign wealth fund Danantara — a move that blindsided even senior officials and sent the Jakarta Composite down 26% year-to-date. The Reserve Bank of India is reportedly weighing similar measures, including a rate hike, after the rupee touched nearly 97 per dollar.

    Asia's AI Cushion

    Korean and Japanese trade data suggest the semiconductor complex is partially offsetting the energy shock for net importers, with Korean chip exports up 202% year-on-year in the first 20 days of May and Japan's trade balance holding in surplus despite a 64% collapse in crude import volumes. Oxford Economics frames the resulting external surpluses as a narrower echo of the 2000s Asian savings glut, with capital recycling indirectly supporting US asset prices even as Treasury yields press higher.

  • US

    Bonds Reprice the Long End; Equities Diverge

    The 30-year Treasury yield rose to 5.19% on Tuesday, the highest since June 2007, as persistent oil-driven inflation pressures from the Hormuz closure continued to push the term premium higher. Bloomberg's gauge of average global sovereign yields is now at its highest since July 2008. The selloff is increasingly bifurcating the equity market: large-cap AI names remain bid, while options activity in the Russell 2000 shows the first meaningful pickup in hedging demand in six weeks, reflecting concern that smaller, rate-sensitive companies will absorb the brunt of tighter financial conditions. Futures markets are now pricing roughly a three-in-four probability of a Fed hike by December — a notable shift for a committee nominally still in a cutting cycle. Barclays' Ajay Rajadhyaksha argued that, absent a reopening of Hormuz, only weaker macro data is likely to arrest the move in yields.

    Fed Posture and the Housing Channel

    Fed official Anna Paulson reiterated a preference for holding rates steady, with FOMC minutes due later in the session. TD Securities now expects 30-year mortgage rates to end the year near 6.15%, leaving housing affordability at levels not sustained since the 1980s and pointing to continued stagnation in existing sales and new construction. HSBC's base case now contemplates a 2026 Fed hike as a tail risk that can no longer be dismissed.

    Nvidia and the AI Capex Question

    Nvidia reports after the close, with consensus looking for 79% revenue growth to $78.9 billion. Hyperscaler capex projections for 2026 and 2027 have been revised to $812 billion and $968 billion respectively — up 89% year-to-date among the top four buyers. Equity holders have rewarded the spending; credit markets have not, with hyperscaler IG spreads now 25% wider than the broader index, having been 15% tighter a year ago. The Shiller CAPE stands at 39.6, a level exceeded only during the late stages of the dot-com bubble.

    Trade and Corporate

    The EU finalized the text of its US trade deal, clearing a procedural hurdle ahead of the July 4 tariff deadline; the pact caps EU export tariffs at 15% in exchange for removal of EU duties on US industrial goods. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are reported to lead the SpaceX IPO, with the filing potentially landing today. The Pentagon confirmed a permanent reduction of roughly 4,000 US troops from Europe.

    Asia

    Xi anchors Beijing diplomacy as Putin visit underscores axis

    President Xi Jinping welcomed Vladimir Putin to Beijing with full state honors, the Russian leader's 25th visit, as the two convened against the backdrop of escalating tensions over Iran and the ongoing Ukraine conflict. Xi explicitly cautioned Washington against resuming strikes on Iran, calling a "comprehensive ceasefire" imperative—remarks that followed President Trump's renewed threats of military action. Talks centered on the long-discussed Power of Siberia 2 pipeline and deepening energy ties, with Russia now reliant on China for over 90% of sanctioned technology imports. The visit caps a year in which Xi has hosted leaders from the UK, Canada, South Korea, and Vietnam, positioning Beijing as an alternative diplomatic hub amid Washington's more transactional posture.

    Samsung labor breakdown threatens chip supply

    Negotiations between Samsung Electronics and its largest union collapsed, with a general strike set for Thursday after management rejected a government-mediated proposal. Shares fell as much as 4.4% before paring losses. The dispute reflects broader political tensions over AI-era wealth distribution: Samsung's semiconductor arm posted a 48-fold quarterly profit increase and is projected to generate nearly $200 billion in operating profit this year. President Lee Jae Myung's government has intervened urging direct talks, though the underlying question—how AI windfalls are shared—remains unresolved and increasingly politicized.

    Indonesia tightens monetary and commodity controls

    Bank Indonesia raised rates 50bp to 5.25%, double consensus expectations, in a defensive move against rupiah weakness amid Iran-driven volatility. Separately, President Prabowo announced a state-owned entity to centralize exports of palm oil, thermal coal, and nickel—a sweeping reassertion of state control echoing earlier authoritarian-era practices and likely to unsettle foreign commodity buyers.

    Other developments

    Indian rupee weakness continues, with funds positioned for a move toward 100/USD as New Delhi prepares vessels to transit the Strait of Hormuz pending government approval. Taiwan's President Lai used his second-anniversary address to cast Taipei as a stabilizing actor, comments aimed at reassuring Trump following last week's Xi summit. Japanese 30-year yields approach 3%, though Pimco has turned constructive on the long end.

    Europe

    EU finalizes US trade deal

    After months of negotiation, EU lawmakers and member states reached agreement on the text of the long-delayed trade pact with Washington, clearing a major hurdle to ratification ahead of President Trump's July 4 deadline for higher tariffs. The framework removes EU tariffs on American industrial goods in exchange for a 15% ceiling on the bloc's exports — critically averting a threatened increase to 25% on European automobiles. The outcome offers near-term relief for European exporters, though it formalizes a meaningfully less favorable trading arrangement than the status quo ante. Eurozone export data underscored the stakes: EU shipments to the US fell 37% year-on-year in March, the sharpest decline in records dating to 2003, following last year's pre-tariff frontloading.

    ECB and Banque de France leadership

    Belgian Governor Pierre Wunsch indicated the ECB will likely hike rates in June, with the Iran-driven oil shock reshaping the inflation trajectory. Emmanuel Moulin, appearing before French lawmakers voting on his nomination to lead the Banque de France, reinforced that the ECB must be "ready to act." The shift toward a tightening bias marks a notable repricing from earlier in the cycle and aligns with broader developed-market dynamics, where global long-bond yields have reached their highest levels in nearly two decades.

    UK inflation and politics

    UK CPI fell to its lowest reading in more than a year, prompting traders to scale back expectations for further BoE hikes — a divergence from euro-area pricing worth monitoring. Separately, Reform UK deputy leader Richard Tice warned investors against participating in the AR7 offshore wind auction, pledging the party would unwind Labour-era renewables contracts. Given Reform's recent local election performance, the policy risk premium attached to UK green infrastructure assets warrants reassessment, though the legal basis for retroactive contract intervention remains untested.

    Corporate

    Marks & Spencer guided full-year profit above pre-cyberattack levels, gaining 4.7% in London. Mercedes-Benz unveiled an electric AMG GT positioned against rivals navigating the EV transition.

    Global

    The Hormuz closure approaches an inflection point

    With the Strait shut for nearly three months following the outbreak of the US-Iran conflict, HSBC's global economics team argues that even a swift reopening would now leave durable scars on inflation and growth. The bank's revised base case envisions gradual restart of Hormuz traffic from mid-June, though NATO is reportedly discussing intervention to escort vessels through the waterway if it remains closed by early July. India has finalized plans to send tankers through the strait pending government approval, and a South Korean-flagged supertanker appears to be attempting a similar crossing. WTI traded near $107.77, easing modestly as markets weighed President Trump's renewed threat to resume strikes against Iran — a threat now meeting some resistance within the Republican-led Senate. Barclays' Ajay Rajadhyaksha has lifted his 2026 Brent average to $100; in the options market, one trader paid for a position that would pay up to $129 million on a sharp Brent decline by early next week.

    Monetary policy is tilting hawkish across regions

    Bloomberg's gauge of average sovereign yields has reached its highest level since July 2008, with 30-year US Treasuries hitting 5.19% — a 19-year high — and Japanese long yields approaching 3%. Bank Indonesia surprised with a 50bp hike to 5.25% to defend the rupiah; Iceland also tightened. The ECB is widely expected to hike in June, and HSBC argues a 2026 Fed hike "can't be ruled out." UK inflation, by contrast, fell to a one-year low, prompting markets to pare BoE hike expectations — a rare instance of divergence.

    Energy security is reshaping policy
