    Germany's gas storage sits below 30% full with the inverted forward curve discouraging injections; traders increasingly expect Berlin to intervene. The UK quietly loosened Russia sanctions to permit imports of diesel and jet fuel refined from Russian crude in third countries — a pragmatic concession to wartime supply constraints. Indonesia announced state centralization of palm oil, thermal coal, and nickel exports, citing fiscal pressure and currency weakness. The combination of equity valuations reminiscent of 2000 and bond yields reminiscent of 2007 remains the defining tension, with AI capex the principal counterweight to deteriorating financial conditions.

  • US

    Bond Market Pressures Mount as Warsh Prepares to Take Fed Helm

    The selloff in US Treasuries deepened, with 10-year yields reaching 4.63% — the highest since February — and 30-year yields approaching levels not seen since 2007. Citigroup's Jim McCormick flagged 5.5% as the next focal point for the long bond. The move reflects a confluence of pressures: war-driven inflation concerns following the Strait of Hormuz disruption, with April headline CPI at 3.8% year-over-year (the firmest reading since 2023); persistent fiscal supply; sustained AI-related capex; and continued labor market resilience. The timing is awkward for Kevin Warsh, whose Fed swearing-in is scheduled for Friday. UBS's Jonathan Pingle suggested Warsh would likely prefer lower rates but faces credibility constraints given the inflation backdrop, and may instead frame policy around protecting AI investment rather than aggressively easing. S&P 500 futures fell 0.3% and Nasdaq 100 futures dropped 0.6% in pre-market trade, with tech leading declines.

    Trump Defers Iran Strike; Trade Deadline Looms

    President Trump said he called off planned strikes on Iran after Saudi Arabia and other Gulf allies requested additional time for diplomacy, though he provided no specifics on targets or terms beyond requiring "no nuclear weapons for Iran." Brent slipped below $111. Separately, EU officials are racing to finalize legislation for the US trade deal ahead of Trump's July 4 deadline, with European auto tariffs potentially rising to 25% from 15% if implementation slips.

    Corporate: NextEra-Dominion, Google-Blackstone, Meta Restructuring

    NextEra announced a $67 billion acquisition of Dominion Energy — the largest US utility deal on record — positioning the combined entity across Florida and Virginia to capture data-center power demand, with battery storage central to the strategy. Google and Blackstone agreed to form an AI cloud business with an initial $5 billion equity commitment from Blackstone, scaling to roughly $25 billion including leverage; shares of CoreWeave, Nebius and Applied Digital fell at least 2.5% pre-market. Meta is reassigning 7,000 employees to AI-related roles ahead of broader cuts later this week. A jury rejected Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI, finding he had waited too long to sue.

    Positioning Signal

    BofA's latest fund manager survey showed a record monthly jump in equity allocations and the sharpest cash drawdown since February 2024 — readings strategist Michael Hartnett characterized as near-complete "bull capitulation," with early June flagged as ripe for profit-taking contingent on yield trajectory.

    Asia

    Putin arrives in Beijing as energy leverage shifts

    Vladimir Putin landed in Beijing today for his first foreign trip of the year, accompanied by an unusually heavyweight delegation including five deputy prime ministers, central bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina, and the heads of major state enterprises. Moscow is hoping that Middle East-driven energy turmoil — with Brent near $110 and the Strait of Hormuz constrained — will soften Beijing's position on pricing for the long-stalled Power of Siberia 2 pipeline. The optics, however, favor Xi. Putin arrives weakened by a record Ukrainian drone strike on Moscow over the weekend and fresh from Xi's own summit with Donald Trump, at which Xi reportedly suggested Putin may come to regret the invasion. With Russian gas largely shut out of Europe and the economy heavily dependent on Chinese demand, Beijing has room to play hardball, though both sides will likely emphasize their "no-limits" partnership.

    Asia equity divergence widens sharply

    Korea's Kospi fell 3.3% today, paring some of an extraordinary 73% year-to-date gain that has been driven almost entirely by Samsung and SK Hynix — together accounting for roughly two-thirds of the index's advance. Retail margin debt has climbed to a record ₩36.5 trillion ($24bn), concentrating risk further. The Jakarta Composite, meanwhile, slumped 3.5%, extending its position as 2026's worst-performing major benchmark (-26%). Pressures include MSCI's warning of a possible frontier-market downgrade, a widening fiscal deficit, and speculation that Jakarta will centralize commodity exports to defend the rupiah. Indonesia subsequently confirmed plans to tighten control over coal and palm oil exports.

    Japan growth surprises, but bond market signals strain

    Q1 GDP expanded an annualized 2.1%, accelerating from a revised 0.8%, on stronger consumption and trade. However, PM Takaichi's supplementary fiscal package pushed super-long JGB yields to fresh record highs, prompting Finance Minister Katayama to flag markets as a policy risk. The global bond selloff — with US 30-year yields at 5.14% — threatens to compound funding pressures across weaker Asian economies and could weigh on the AI-driven equity rally.

    Other notable items

    President Marcos reiterated that the Philippines would likely be drawn into any Taiwan conflict, a stance that will irritate Beijing. Indian carriers including Air India and IndiGo have asked state refiners to defer jet fuel price hikes until the Iran conflict subsides. Australia secured supplementary jet fuel from China and urea from Brunei, while the RBA flagged elevated risk of inflation expectations becoming unanchored.

    Europe

    European Daily Briefing — May 19, 2026

    European equities advanced modestly against an otherwise risk-off global backdrop, with the Stoxx Europe 600 rising 0.8% even as US and Asian markets retreated on inflation concerns linked to the Middle East conflict. Brent crude eased to around $110 a barrel after President Trump deferred a planned strike on Iran at the request of Gulf allies, though sovereign bond markets across the region remain under pressure. UK gilts extended Monday's gains following Andy Burnham's commitment to preserve existing fiscal rules, while G7 finance officials are reportedly acknowledging that the energy-driven inflation shock they had hoped to avoid now appears likely to persist. EU officials convene today in a critical push to finalize legislation implementing the trade framework agreed with Washington nearly a year ago, with Trump's July 4 deadline approaching and the threat of auto tariffs rising from 15% to 25% should Brussels fall short. The timing is unfavorable: European industry is already absorbing the energy shock from the Iran conflict, and Ryanair's Michael O'Leary warned of potential corporate casualties among European airlines if Hormuz disruptions continue. In parallel, the EU is weighing new defensive measures against Chinese import volumes ahead of a June leaders' summit, with Beijing already signaling it would retaliate against the pending Nuctech subsidy probe. UK political dynamics continue to shape gilt pricing. Burnham, the frontrunner to succeed Keir Starmer, ruled out any loosening of self-imposed borrowing limits — a signal aimed at reassuring markets wary of a leftward fiscal shift, though it may disappoint Labour's left flank. Separately, IFS data showed only 50.6% of UK 16-24 year-olds in payrolled work at end-2025, down from 54.9% in late 2022, while April recorded the sharpest employer job cuts since the pandemic's onset. In corporate news, Standard Chartered announced plans to eliminate roughly 8,000 support roles over four years as it integrates AI, with CEO Bill Winters framing the move as capital substitution rather than pure cost reduction. Germany formally launched the privatization of Uniper, opening what could become Europe's largest utility transaction this year. LVMH's Marc Jacobs disposals and reported Gucci-Alpine F1 sponsorship talks underscore continued repositioning across the luxury sector.

    Global

    Iran Standoff and Energy Markets

    President Trump said he called off a planned strike on Iran after Saudi Arabia and other Gulf allies asked for more time to pursue diplomacy, though he offered no detail on targets or terms beyond a demand for "no nuclear weapons." Brent slipped 1.8% to around $110/barrel and WTI eased to $108 on the news, but the relief was modest given that the Strait of Hormuz remains restricted. Washington issued a fresh monthlong waiver permitting sales of Russian crude already loaded on tankers, an unusual concession aimed at containing fuel costs. Knock-on effects are widening: Singapore's state LNG buyer has secured non-Middle East cargoes through year-end, Indian carriers are lobbying refiners to delay jet fuel hikes, and Ryanair's CEO warned of corporate casualties among European airlines if the chokepoint persists.

    Bond Markets Buckle Globally

    The energy shock is compounding a synchronized sovereign bond selloff. US 10-year yields touched 4.63%, the highest since February, with 30-year yields approaching post-2007 peaks; Citi flagged 5.5% as the next technical level. Japanese super-long yields hit fresh records following PM Takaichi's supplementary fiscal package, and UK gilts are pricing political risk around a potential Burnham premiership. The drivers extend beyond oil — sticky inflation, AI-related capex, deteriorating fiscal trajectories and aging demographics are reinforcing one another. Kevin Warsh's Fed swearing-in Friday adds another variable: any dovish tilt risks accelerating the selloff rather than easing financial conditions.

    Putin-Xi in Beijing

    Putin arrived in Beijing seeking to leverage Middle East energy disruption into better terms on the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline. The asymmetry is pronounced — Sunday's record Ukrainian drone strike on Moscow underscored Russia's vulnerabilities, while Xi, fresh from his summit with Trump, can afford to negotiate firmly. Progress on gas pricing remains unconfirmed.

    Climate

    Scientists have formally retired the RCP8.5 worst-case warming scenario as implausible given policy progress and clean-tech maturation, a technical revision Trump characterized as vindication. BloombergNEF's New Energy Outlook now projects 2.4C warming by 2050 under current trajectories, with solar set to become the largest single power source by 2032.

  • US

    Bond Rout Reshapes Rate Expectations

    US Treasuries extended last week's selloff as Brent pushed above $111/barrel amid the unresolved Iran conflict and continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The 10-year yield held at 4.60% in early trade, while the long end remained the focal point of stress — Federated Hermes' Karen Manna framed the move as a market "reckoning with renewed inflation," with 30-year yields approaching levels not seen in two decades. Rate-cut pricing has shifted materially: traders that earlier in the year were positioned for Fed easing in 2026 are now beginning to discount the tail possibility of a hike. DoubleLine's Jeffrey Gundlach said a June cut is off the table, and Ed Yardeni publicly urged the Fed to abandon its easing bias to avoid losing control of the long end. The FOMC minutes due Wednesday will be parsed carefully for any acknowledgment of the inflation impulse from energy.

    Equities Test the AI Thesis Against Higher Yields

    S&P 500 futures declined 0.6% overnight following Friday's drop of more than 1%. The episode appears so far to resemble a wobble rather than a regime change, though Morgan Stanley's Michael Wilson — who recently raised his S&P target — cautioned that sustained yield volatility could produce the first meaningful correction since the late-March lows. The week's earnings calendar is consumer-heavy (Home Depot, Lowe's, Walmart) and culminates in Nvidia on Wednesday, a key test of whether AI-driven earnings momentum can offset the discount-rate headwind.

    Corporate and Deal Flow

    NextEra is reportedly in talks to acquire Dominion Energy at roughly $66 billion, which would rank as the largest power-sector transaction on record — notable given accelerating data-center load growth. UnitedHealth fell 5.6% premarket after Berkshire disclosed it had exited the position; Berkshire built a $2.6 billion Delta stake and added Macy's. Regeneron dropped 12% on a failed late-stage melanoma trial. SpaceX's S-1 may surface as soon as Wednesday, setting up a potential June roadshow at a valuation reportedly approaching $2 trillion.

    Policy Backdrop

    The post-summit US-China framework remains thin on specifics beyond a $17 billion annual agricultural commitment through 2028. With Section 232-style overcapacity and forced-labor investigations queued to replace the tariffs struck down in February, a fresh round of duties is likely this summer — potentially colliding with gasoline prices that have risen toward $4.50 and consumer affordability concerns ahead of the midterms.

    Asia

    China growth disappoints across the board

    April data delivered a uniformly weak reading that has reopened the debate over Beijing's stimulus restraint. Retail sales rose just 0.2% year-on-year — the softest print since the post-Covid reopening of December 2022 — with auto sales down 15%, jewelry off 21%, and previously subsidy-supported categories such as appliances and furniture declining at double-digit rates. Industrial production grew 4.1%, the weakest in nearly three years, while fixed-asset investment unexpectedly contracted 1.6% year-to-date. No economist surveyed by Bloomberg had anticipated a downside surprise of this breadth. The figures, following last week's contraction in new loan demand, suggest the booming export channel is no longer offsetting domestic weakness. They also complicate the post-summit narrative: while Beijing agreed to purchase at least $17 billion of US agricultural products annually through 2028 and to establish new trade and investment boards, the agreements remain vague, and the trade board's mandate appears to cover only around $30 billion in flows. The "placeholder summit" framing leaves meaningful scope for friction when Trump's tariff replacement architecture is unveiled this summer.

    Japan's fiscal pivot and JGB stress

    Prime Minister Takaichi instructed the finance ministry to compile a supplementary budget after weeks of insisting none was needed, citing commodity-driven inflation pressures from the Iran conflict. The 30-year JGB yield surged as much as 20bp to a post-1999 high before paring gains, contributing to a broader Asian bond selloff that extended to Indonesia and India, both heavily energy-import dependent. HSBC's Frederic Neumann noted that with Gulf flows unlikely to be restored quickly, regional inflation pressures will continue building.

    Elsewhere

    Thailand is preparing a 200 billion baht ($6.1 billion) cost-of-living package. Taiwan's President Lai invoked the island's semiconductor centrality as questions linger over US commitments post-summit. Samsung shares rose on resumed union talks, and Kospi gained modestly on AI sentiment. Malaysia's Anwar raised the prospect of a snap election amid coalition strains. Putin's upcoming Beijing visit revives Power of Siberia 2 talks, though pricing leverage remains with Xi.

    Europe

    UK politics dominates as bond markets test European nerves

    Wes Streeting, in his first public appearance since resigning as health secretary, used the platform to lay groundwork for a potential Labour leadership bid and argued Britain should rejoin the EU. Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, who intends to contest a June by-election as a route to challenging Keir Starmer, echoed the case for EU re-engagement. Hedge funds and asset managers responded by piling into bearish sterling option bets last week, reflecting concern that a Burnham-led Labour could pursue looser fiscal policy. Separately, *The Sun* reported Chancellor Rachel Reeves is set to abandon a planned fuel-duty increase, a politically expedient move given oil-driven cost pressures but one that complicates the fiscal arithmetic at a sensitive moment for gilts.

    Bond stress framed the G-7 finance ministers' meeting in Paris

    A renewed selloff—led by Japanese government bonds but extending across global curves—overshadowed the agenda of addressing structural imbalances. French Finance Minister Roland Lescure characterised the move as "correcting, but I wouldn't go as far as to say it's collapsing," while ECB President Christine Lagarde acknowledged the gyrations fell within her remit. German Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil pressed partners to deepen cooperation on raw materials, energy and supply chains. With Brent above $110 and the Strait of Hormuz closure persisting, inflation expectations are repricing higher across the bloc, complicating the ECB's policy path even as Lescure maintained the eurozone faces slowdown rather than recession or stagflation.

    UK M&A activity stands in marked contrast to political dysfunction

    Bids targeting UK companies have risen more than 250% year-to-date to roughly $150 billion—the strongest pace since 2015—with Unilever, Schroders, Beazley, Intertek and Tate & Lyle among the names in play, as overseas acquirers exploit depressed sterling valuations. In corporate news, Standard Chartered named former banking analyst Manus Costello as CFO, ending a months-long search following Diego De Giorgi's February departure. Publicis rose 2.4% in Paris after agreeing to acquire LiveRamp for approximately $2.5 billion. In Spain, the ruling Socialists suffered their worst-ever result in the country's largest region. Germany's climate advisory council warned the country is likely to miss its 2030 emissions targets by up to 100 million tons of CO2 following the coalition's decision to dilute the buildings-sector heating law.

    Global

    Iran stalemate drives the global macro picture

    Brent crude rose above $111 a barrel and WTI traded near $107 as the US-Iran impasse over the Strait of Hormuz entered its third month with no diplomatic breakthrough in sight. President Trump warned Tehran the "clock is ticking" hours after drones targeted a UAE nuclear power plant, while Iran's Mehr agency characterized US terms — including transfer of uranium stocks, no reparations, and release of less than a quarter of frozen assets — as offering "no tangible concessions." Commercial shipping through the strait remains largely frozen. The UAE is accelerating construction of a pipeline to Fujairah to double bypass capacity by next year, though such workarounds will not substitute for restored Gulf flows at scale.

    Bond markets are doing the repricing the Fed has not

    A global sovereign selloff deepened, with Japan's 30-year yield surging as much as 20bp to its highest since the tenor's 1999 debut, and US 30-year yields approaching 5%. Traders have flipped from pricing US cuts this year to entertaining the prospect of a hike — a striking reversal that reflects how energy-driven inflation is overwhelming the disinflationary forces central banks had been counting on. Equity futures slipped but the AI-led rally has so far proven resilient; Morgan Stanley's Wilson warned a sustained yield grind higher could trigger the first meaningful correction since March.

    Policy divergence is widening under a common shock

    Japan's PM Takaichi reversed course and instructed the finance ministry to compile a supplementary budget; Thailand is readying a roughly $6.1 billion cost-of-living package. China's April data — retail sales up just 0.2% y/y, industrial production at 4.1%, fixed-asset investment contracting 1.6% YTD — undercut Beijing's reluctance to add stimulus and complicate the afterglow of last week's Trump-Xi summit, which yielded $17 billion in annual US agricultural purchases through 2028 but little operational detail. G-7 finance ministers convening in Paris confront these imbalances against a backdrop French Finance Minister Lescure called "correcting, but not collapsing."

    Tensions to watch

    Trump's July 24 tariff deadline could collide with elevated US pump prices and a still-closed Hormuz, testing the durability of the Beijing détente. Putin arrives in China this week with more leverage on Power of Siberia 2 than he has had in years, though Xi retains the structural upper hand given diversified supply options.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    China's posture toward global supply chains has shifted meaningfully, from defensive adjustment to proactive coercion. A report commissioned by the US Chamber of Commerce and prepared by Rhodium Group, released this week, characterized Beijing's recent regulatory moves as a transition from "reactive to proactive, future-looking economic coercion strategies." New regulations unveiled over the past several weeks allow China to retaliate against foreign governments or firms that assist in actions deemed to harm Chinese industrial and supply chain security — a legal architecture constructed explicitly to deter the kind of sourcing diversification that Washington and Brussels have been encouraging. Natixis chief Asia Pacific economist Alicia Garcia Herrero framed the implication bluntly: foreign governments seeking to coax companies out of Chinese supply relationships now face a Beijing that can characterize such interference as a violation of Chinese law. The strategic intent appears clearly articulated at the highest levels of party leadership. Xi Jinping's latest Five-Year Plan, released in March, replaced the long-standing framing of "peace and development" as the defining feature of the global environment with language centered on geopolitical rivalry as the primary driver of an uncertain and unstable future — a wording shift that JPMorgan economists Jahangir Aziz and Tingting Ge describe as elevating national security to a "central and over-arching principle for economic policies." Consistent with that framing, Beijing is also screening outbound investment to ensure Chinese firms' overseas operations serve to enhance exports rather than displace domestic production, which may increasingly discourage the offshoring of higher-value manufacturing. The Rhodium report warns of a potential self-reinforcing dynamic: as China's share of global manufacturing continues to rise, eroding margins for competitors in developed economies, the capacity of Western firms to reinvest in R&D diminishes, and private capital increasingly avoids sectors where Chinese state-backed competition is entrenched. For industrial communities in Germany, Japan, and Italy, the adjustment pressure is structural rather than cyclical. Separately, Trump confirmed following his May 13–15 visit to Beijing that China has not approved purchases of Nvidia H200 AI chips, with the president noting that China intends to develop its own alternatives — consistent with a broader strategy of reducing dependence on the US technology stack.

    Europe

    The EU's industrial policy ambitions are coming into direct conflict with Beijing's regulatory counter-offensive. China's new supply chain coercion framework appears calibrated in part as a warning to Brussels, which has been advancing the Industrial Accelerator Act — the so-called "Made in Europe" proposal — that would mandate preference for domestic suppliers in public procurement and target 20% of GDP from manufacturing by 2035. Separately, Chinese authorities warned the EU this week that countermeasures would follow if Brussels proceeds with its probe into Nuctech, the Chinese security-devices manufacturer whose state subsidies the European Commission is investigating for providing an unfair competitive advantage over local firms. The pairing of these moves suggests Beijing is prepared to use both regulatory and diplomatic levers to resist European supply chain reorientation. The broader structural concern for Europe — particularly for manufacturing-intensive economies such as Germany — is that China's industrial dominance is no longer merely a competitive challenge but an actively managed strategic position. Rhodium Group's analysis highlights specific vulnerabilities for German automotive communities, Italian machinery districts, and Japanese electronics cities, where prolonged margin compression and revenue erosion may prove difficult to reverse without coordinated policy responses across advanced economies. Whether the EU can maintain the political cohesion required for such coordination, given member-state divergence on China exposure, remains an open question. Turkey's decision to scrap its inflation target in the face of an energy-price shock points to additional stress within emerging European economies, though details remain limited from available coverage.

    Global

    The Iran conflict is reshaping infrastructure risk calculus well beyond the immediate theater of operations. The vulnerability of centralized desalination infrastructure — plants supplying water to tens of millions across the Middle East — has been exposed by the conflict, according to Atoco CEO Samer Taha, accelerating interest in decentralized atmospheric water harvesting technology. Atoco, founded by 2025 Nobel chemistry laureate Omar Yaghi, is preparing to bring to market a shipping container-sized unit capable of producing 4,000 liters daily using metal-organic frameworks, with applications ranging from data centers and hospitals to off-grid communities. The commercial logic is straightforward: centralized water systems, like centralized energy infrastructure, carry concentration risk that conflict and climate stress are making increasingly apparent. The water scarcity dimension extends well beyond the Middle East. In the American West, record-low Rocky Mountain snowpack — described by experts as potentially signaling a more permanent aridification — is prompting a measured revival of large-scale water storage infrastructure. The Chimney Hollow Dam, the largest built in the US in twenty-five years, began filling in April, with several additional reservoir projects underway in Colorado and California. Meanwhile, some 500,000 residents of Corpus Christi face potential water shortfalls within a year. The intersection of these trends with AI infrastructure is notable: data centers are placing significant new demand on already-stressed aquifers, creating a tension between the energy transition's digital requirements and the physical resource constraints of water-scarce regions. Separately, China's green technology firms — battery, solar, and EV suppliers — are reported to be positioning to capture demand generated by the energy shock flowing from the Iran conflict, illustrating how geopolitical disruption continues to create asymmetric commercial opportunities across regions.

  • US

    US equity and bond markets came under significant pressure on Friday, as surging oil prices and renewed inflation concerns brought a sharp halt to the AI-driven equity rally that had carried stocks to successive record highs in recent weeks. S&P 500 futures fell 1.0% and Nasdaq 100 futures declined 1.5%, while the 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.54%, its highest level in approximately a year, extending a rise that has persisted across every session this week. The two-year yield climbed to 4.06%, a level last seen in March 2025. Fed futures markets now price roughly a two-thirds probability of a rate hike by December, a notable repricing that reflects Fed Governor Michael Barr's assessment earlier in the week that inflation remains "the overwhelming risk facing the economy." Stronger-than-expected US producer price data reinforced that concern. On technology and corporate news, Nvidia's market capitalisation approached $6 trillion — a valuation no publicly traded company has previously reached — as AI-related demand for semiconductors continues to drive earnings upgrades that outpace share price gains. Cerebras Systems debuted in what is described as the year's largest IPO, surging 68% to a $67 billion market cap. Memory-chip names Sandisk and Micron also stand out: despite gains of 482% and 172% respectively this year, both trade at roughly nine times forward earnings as analyst estimates have risen faster than prices. Separately, Apple's relationship with OpenAI has deteriorated to the point where the startup is reportedly preparing potential legal action. Microsoft pared intraday losses after Bill Ackman disclosed a new stake in the company. On the fiscal and political side, Trump's financial disclosures revealed personal equity purchases in the first quarter, including positions in Nvidia, Oracle, Microsoft, Boeing and Costco. With midterm elections less than six months away, Republicans have yet to coalesce around a credible response to voter concerns about the cost of living — a political vulnerability that the ongoing energy shock is amplifying.

    Asia

    The dominant story across Asian markets on Friday was a broad and sharp risk-off move, with a gauge of Asian equities falling 2.2%, the steepest single-day decline since March. South Korea's Kospi led the selloff, dropping 6.1%, as the market's heavy concentration in semiconductor names — the very stocks that have driven its world-beating rally in recent months — left it acutely exposed to a repricing of rate expectations. The move appeared to reflect exhaustion following an exceptionally rapid run-up rather than a fundamental deterioration in earnings, though the distinction may offer limited comfort in the near term. The Trump-Xi summit in Beijing concluded after roughly 36 hours of meetings, pageantry and a state dinner at Zhongnanhai. The headline deliverable was a Chinese commitment to purchase 200 Boeing aircraft — a meaningful symbolic win, though below the upper-end expectation of 500 jets — and an agreement to maintain the trade truce reached in South Korea last autumn under a newly coined framework of "constructive strategic stability." Agricultural purchase commitments are anticipated but not yet finalised. On Iran, both sides expressed convergence in wanting the conflict to end and the Strait of Hormuz reopened, though no actionable mechanism emerged. The most pointed moment came when Xi warned that mishandling Taiwan could push bilateral relations into a "highly dangerous situation" — his bluntest language on the subject to date. A delayed $14 billion US arms package for Taiwan now represents the most immediate test of whether the summit's goodwill proves durable. Beneath the summit headlines, China's credit quality issue warrants attention. Estimates from independent analysts suggest true non-performing loan ratios may be closer to 10% — versus the official 1.5% — implying as much as $3 trillion in loans that should technically be classified as past due. Regulatory forbearance has so far contained systemic risk, but the policy traps capital in unproductive firms and is visibly slowing overall loan growth. Japan's corporate goods prices rose by the most in 12 years, adding to pressure on the Bank of Japan to tighten. India raised retail fuel prices for the first time in four years, absorbing a fraction of the roughly 50% increase in Brent crude since the Iran conflict began. Thailand convened an emergency meeting between the prime minister and leading business figures to address what officials described as converging crises of inflation, unemployment and energy costs.

    Europe

    UK political risk moved sharply to the foreground on Friday after Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham secured a credible path to challenge Keir Starmer for the Labour leadership, following the decision by the MP for Makerfield to stand aside in his favour. Burnham, characterised in domestic media as a figure with genuine popular appeal, is now considered the frontrunner to unseat the prime minister. Markets responded promptly: the pound fell, gilt yields surged — with the 10-year reaching 5.14%, up 15 basis points on the day — and UK assets underperformed materially relative to already-weak European peers. The political anxiety is compounded by investor concern that a leadership transition could produce a more expansive fiscal posture at a time when the gilt market is already absorbing significant supply pressure from oil-driven inflation. The broader context is one of European political fragility. Romania's government has fallen, Latvia's prime minister resigned over a drone-related controversy, and Germany's chancellor faced trade union protests. The analysis that "few places appear governable" may be rhetorical in framing but captures something real about the compression of political time horizons across the continent. Italy under Giorgia Meloni stands as a notable exception to this pattern of rapid leadership attrition. On green finance, the UK has halved its pledge to the Green Climate Fund — reducing its 2024-2027 contribution from £1.6 billion to £815 million — a further withdrawal from multilateral climate commitments that follows the US cancellation of a $4 billion pledge last year. Separately, an analysis of London commercial property suggests that as much as 78% of Westminster offices and 71% of City of London offices will fail to meet Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards expected to take effect in the early 2030s, raising material stranded-asset risk across the sector. In eastern European bond markets, Hungary's borrowing costs have fallen below Poland's for the first time since 2020, reflecting investor optimism surrounding new Prime Minister Peter Magyar's push to align Budapest with EU mainstream policy and revive the country's euro accession path.

    Global

    The defining macro theme on Friday was the intensifying feedback loop between the Iran war, oil prices, and global bond markets. With the Strait of Hormuz remaining largely closed under a fragile ceasefire, Brent crude has risen more than 60% over three months to approximately $109 a barrel — far enough to inflict broad demand destruction, but not yet resolved in either direction. The US EIA now projects global oil demand growth of only 200,000 barrels per day in 2026, against a pre-war forecast of 1.2 million; the IEA goes further, projecting an outright contraction of 420,000 barrels per day. Down years for oil demand are rare and historically coincide with severe economic stress. TD Securities warns that depleted inventories could drive a fresh surge toward $150 a barrel absent a Hormuz resolution; BloombergNEF estimates $160 if closure persists through July. The Trump-Xi summit produced a shared rhetorical commitment to reopening the strait and ending the Iran conflict, but no operational mechanism. Trump's comment to Fox News that the US does not need the strait open "at all" may complicate diplomatic signalling, even if intended for a domestic audience. The UAE, frustrated by Gulf neighbours' refusal to join a coordinated military response to Iranian strikes earlier in the conflict, announced plans to double its Hormuz-bypass export capacity via the Fujairah pipeline by 2027 — a structural adaptation to a prolonged disruption scenario. Sovereign bond markets are now pricing a coherent inflation narrative across jurisdictions: US 10-year yields at 4.54%, UK gilts at 5.14%, and Japan's 20-year rate at its highest level since 1996. The correlation of losses across US, UK, eurozone and Japanese government bond indices this year reflects a common driver in energy prices, layered over country-specific fiscal and political factors. Producer price indices in the US, China and Japan all surprised to the upside this week, suggesting pipeline inflation pressure has not peaked. Fed rate hike expectations for December have moved from a tail risk to a base-case probability for a meaningful share of market participants — a repricing with implications for equity valuations, credit spreads, and emerging market capital flows that have not yet fully resolved.

  • US

    The Senate confirmed Kevin Warsh as Federal Reserve chair by the narrowest margin on record, completing one of the most consequential and contentious leadership transitions at the central bank in decades. The confirmation sets up an immediate test of institutional independence, with markets already attentive to how Warsh may approach monetary policy at a moment of acute inflationary pressure. That pressure was on full display in Wednesday's producer price data. The PPI rose 6% year-on-year in April — its highest reading since 2022, excluding the post-pandemic surge — driven by war-related energy costs and compounded by freight transportation. More striking still, electronics components inflated 27% year-on-year as AI-driven memory chip shortages ended decades of persistent technology deflation. Oxford Economics now projects the PCE deflator reaching 3.8%, its hottest reading since May 2023, effectively foreclosing near-term rate cuts. Against that macro backdrop, equity markets continued to push higher. The Nasdaq 100 closed at yet another all-time record, with Cisco adding 19% after results signaled its AI pivot is gaining traction. Cerebras Systems raised $5.55 billion in the year's largest IPO, valuing the AI chipmaker at roughly $40 billion. Ford surged 13% after Morgan Stanley argued its CATL battery partnership positions its energy storage business as a potential hyperscaler supplier worth $10 billion. The simultaneous rise in the VIX alongside stock prices — a historically rare configuration — may signal that some investors are hedging aggressively into the rally rather than expressing genuine conviction. The concentration debate sharpened further. The top ten S&P 500 constituents — almost exclusively technology — now account for approximately 40% of index market capitalization, a level that invites comparisons to the Nifty Fifty era of the early 1970s. Unlike then, today's leaders post genuine earnings, but critics including Jeremy Grantham and Seth Cogswell argue that hyperscale AI infrastructure spending economics remain opaque and self-referential, and that competition among AI platforms will ultimately compress margins.

    Asia

    The Trump-Xi summit in Beijing dominated regional attention, delivering a day of carefully choreographed pageantry alongside sharper-edged substance. Xi greeted Trump with a full state welcome at the Great Hall of the People, and both leaders struck cooperative tones in public remarks. Trump called Xi "a great leader" and invited him to the White House in September. Xi declared that common interests "outweigh differences" and pledged China's door would "open wider," delivering the message directly to a delegation of US executives representing roughly $20 trillion in market capitalization, including Elon Musk, Tim Cook, Jensen Huang, and Kelly Ortberg. Beneath the cordiality, Xi delivered a pointed warning that mishandling Taiwan could push the bilateral relationship into a "highly dangerous situation," with Chinese state media publishing the remarks while the two leaders were still in the room. The Taiwan message appeared calibrated to establish a firm boundary without derailing trade discussions. On the economic side, China renewed import licenses for hundreds of US beef plants as an early gesture, while Treasury Secretary Bessent outlined discussions on a potential board of investment to allow Chinese capital into non-sensitive US industries, and suggested possible tariff relief on roughly $30 billion of non-critical trade. The question of whether the summit produces durable progress on semiconductors, rare earths, and agricultural goods will crystallize on day two. Elsewhere across the region, Asian central banks are facing rising strain. The Philippines, India, and Indonesia are burning foreign-exchange reserves to defend weakening currencies as oil import costs mount, with shrinking import cover pushing policymakers toward rate hikes. A Bank of Japan board member called for tightening "as early as possible," citing inflation risks from the Iran war, sending the yen higher and lifting June hike expectations. South Korea's equity market continued to exhibit speculative characteristics, with retail borrowing at record levels and a YouTuber with 1.3 million subscribers describing the mood as "nearly maniacal" — even as underlying chip earnings at Samsung and SK Hynix remain robust.

    Europe

    The UK economy delivered its strongest quarterly growth in a year, with GDP rising 0.6% in Q1 2026. The IT sector contributed meaningfully, and activity held modestly positive even in the early weeks of the Iran conflict. However, virtually all forecasters expect the pace to prove unsustainable. Bloomberg Economics projects near-stagnant growth over the remainder of the year as higher energy costs, tighter borrowing conditions, and political uncertainty compound one another. The political situation deteriorated further. Health Secretary Wes Streeting is expected to resign from Cabinet to mount a leadership challenge against Prime Minister Keir Starmer, while former deputy prime minister Angela Rayner signaled she is preparing her own bid following clearance from a tax investigation. Markets have long discounted a degree of Starmer instability — Polymarket has placed the odds of his departure before year-end above 50% for months — which limits the marginal selloff from fresh headlines. Nevertheless, UK 30-year gilt yields rose to levels last seen in 1998, and the political noise is beginning to concern overseas bond buyers. The spread between gilts and Treasuries has tracked the oil price since the outbreak of hostilities, suggesting the Iran war may be a more proximate driver of funding costs than domestic politics alone. The ECB's policy path also became less certain. Governing Council member Martins Kazaks warned that the data are pointing toward stagflation risks, and stated that a rate hike would be warranted if oil price increases feed through to inflation expectations. Two weeks after ECB President Lagarde signalled a tightening bias, a June move is now described as "less obvious" rather than imminent — reflecting the tension between above-target inflation and softening growth, a tension that Oxford Economics estimates could add 0.6 to 0.7 percentage points of additional inflation pass-through given elevated public inflation awareness.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz remained the most consequential single geographic flashpoint in global markets. US efforts to resolve the Iran war were set back after a commercial vessel was apparently seized by unauthorized personnel near the UAE, injecting fresh uncertainty over who effectively controls the waterway. A Japanese supertanker emerged in the Gulf of Oman after what appears to have been a covert transit, while two India-bound LNG cargoes also appeared to cross the strait — highlighting that flows are occurring, but under conditions of elevated risk rather than normalized passage. The energy disruption is rippling into global price data with increasing clarity. US PPI accelerated to 6% year-on-year, led by freight costs and energy, while US jet fuel exports surged to a record 455,000 barrels per day as airlines in Europe and Asia scrambled for supply. OPEC+ members are targeting a completion of quota restoration by end-September, though whether that alleviates tightness depends substantially on Hormuz conditions. WTI futures held near $101 per barrel. The summit's energy subtext was notable. Xi reportedly opposes any toll regime for Hormuz transit and expressed interest in purchasing more US oil to reduce Gulf dependency — an alignment of interests that could, at the margins, create an opening for US LNG as China's Qatari supply has been disrupted. The UN climate chief, speaking at Tsinghua University during Trump's visit, praised China's clean energy leadership explicitly as a counter-model to US withdrawal from Paris Agreement commitments. Big Tech's global debt issuance added a separate cross-regional theme: Alphabet has raised nearly $60 billion over four months in dollars, euros, pounds, Swiss francs, Canadian dollars, and yen, with banks warning the scale of AI-driven corporate borrowing risks crowding out local issuers in foreign-currency bond markets. Investors snagged 5% yields on 30-year Treasuries for the first time in nearly two decades, marking a notable level for long-duration pricing globally.

  • US

    US inflation data for April delivered an uncomfortable surprise, with headline CPI rising to 3.8% year-on-year — the highest reading in three years and meaningfully above the Federal Reserve's upper tolerance band of 3%. While the Strait of Hormuz closure remains the primary driver through elevated energy prices, the picture beneath the surface is more troubling: core inflation excluding energy ran at 2.8%, median and trimmed-mean measures are turning upward, sticky prices have re-crossed 3%, and the Fed's "supercore" services measure — services inflation minus shelter — rose sharply in the month. The breadth of the acceleration suggests the oil shock is generating second-order effects rather than remaining purely a relative-price disturbance. The affordability dimension compounds the political difficulty. Consumer prices rose faster than average hourly earnings in April, the first such occurrence since 2022 and a dynamic that did not materialise during Trump's first term. The Atlanta Fed's wage tracker recorded 3.9% wage growth through March, leaving real compensation in marginally negative territory on the latest reading. Markets adjusted quickly to the new information. Two-year Treasury yields touched 4%, their highest since June 2025, as the probability distribution for the fed funds rate shifted decisively: the next move is now priced more likely to be a hike than a cut. The S&P 500 dipped only 0.16% before recovering, with semiconductor names initially falling sharply before regaining much of the intraday loss — a sign that AI-driven earnings momentum continues to provide a partial buffer against the rate repricing. Kevin Warsh was confirmed as a Federal Reserve governor on the same day, with his elevation to chair expected to follow. The timing is notable: he inherits an institution where the easing cycle that markets anticipated at the start of the year has effectively been suspended, and where the risk profile is shifting from a familiar dilemma of slow growth versus sticky inflation toward a harder stagflationary configuration if the energy shock persists. The Trump administration separately faces a significant fiscal development, processing more than $35.5 billion in tariff refunds following the Supreme Court ruling that struck down the president's broad tariff authority. ---

    Asia

    The Trump-Xi summit in Beijing dominates the regional agenda. Trump arrived Wednesday evening for a 36-hour meeting that encompasses trade, tariffs, critical minerals, Taiwan, and the Iran war. The delegation's composition is itself a signal: Jensen Huang of Nvidia was a last-minute addition to a group that already included Apple's Tim Cook, Tesla's Elon Musk, Boeing's Kelly Ortberg, and Goldman Sachs' David Solomon. Huang's presence thrusts AI and semiconductor access into the heart of the agenda — he has characterised the Chinese market as a $50 billion opportunity. Pre-summit working-level talks between Treasury Secretary Bessent and Vice Premier He Lifeng began in Seoul, the first face-to-face contact since Paris two months ago. One structural outcome receiving attention ahead of the meeting is the possibility of a currency accord — what TS Lombard characterises as a "Beijing Accord" under which the yuan would be allowed to appreciate. Goldman Sachs estimates the yuan is more than 20% undervalued; a stronger currency would serve China's domestic demand agenda while satisfying Washington's manufacturing competitiveness objectives. The question is whether either side has sufficient trust to formalise such an arrangement. China's economic data continue to present a contradictory picture. April exports rose 14% year-on-year, well above the 8% consensus, with technology the primary driver. At the same time, the bond market reflects subdued domestic growth and inflation expectations, with 10-year yields below 2% — now below Japanese government bond yields, a striking inversion. One analyst estimates hidden non-performing loans in the banking system at roughly 10% of total credit, implying approximately $3 trillion in under-classified bad debt. Elsewhere in the region, India more than doubled gold and silver import tariffs to approximately 15%, pairing Prime Minister Modi's appeal for citizens to curb bullion purchases with formal policy tightening in an attempt to defend the rupee and shore up foreign exchange reserves. The move adds to pressure on already weak Indian equity markets. In Japan, 20-year JGB yields touched their highest level since 1997, driven by elevated energy import costs feeding domestic inflation. SoftBank reported a surprise jump in quarterly profit, largely attributable to valuation gains on its OpenAI stake. ---

    Europe

    UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer survived another day under intense pressure, though his political position remains precarious. Four junior ministers have resigned in an attempt to force his departure, and market pricing implies roughly a one-in-five chance he remains in office through year-end. Traders are at their most bearish on sterling in five weeks. The King's Speech, scheduled to lay out a packed legislative agenda, provides a near-term test of whether Starmer can project governing authority while managing internal Labour dissent. The constitutional and procedural difficulty of removing a sitting Labour leader has so far protected him, but the parliamentary arithmetic and cabinet cohesion continue to deteriorate. UK government bond markets reflected the political and fiscal anxiety. Thirty-year gilt yields hit a 28-year high, a move that underscores investor concerns about fiscal credibility at a moment when the prime minister's ability to deliver on spending commitments is in question. Jamie Dimon added an external dimension, warning that any post-Starmer government that raises taxes on banks would cause JPMorgan to abandon plans for a multi-billion-pound new headquarters at Canary Wharf. In Germany, Chancellor Friedrich Merz's coalition held a six-hour working session aimed at narrowing policy rifts and restoring public confidence, but produced few concrete agreements. A year into office, Merz's approval ratings have fallen to historic lows, and the AfD has extended its polling lead. The absence of coalition cohesion at a moment requiring fiscal and industrial policy decisions carries macro consequences for the eurozone's largest economy. ECB policy is shifting in tone. Bundesbank President Nagel told Handelsblatt that rate hikes are increasingly likely — a notable pivot given that rate cuts had been the dominant expectation for the eurozone earlier this year. France provided additional cause for concern, with unemployment unexpectedly rising to its highest level in five years, adding to evidence that the euro area's second-largest economy was already losing momentum before the Iran war began. The EU is also working to join a US-led initiative to secure AI and semiconductor supply chains, signalling Brussels' recognition that industrial and geopolitical alignment with Washington may be necessary regardless of ongoing trade tensions. ---

    Global

    Oil markets remain the principal transmission mechanism for the Iran conflict. WTI crude traded above $100 per barrel during the session, having gained approximately 8% over the prior three days, before pulling back roughly 1% on the day. The IEA reported that global inventories are falling at a record pace and will continue declining for months. Satellite imagery suggests Iranian crude exports from the Kharg Island terminal have halted entirely over the past several days — the first sign of a prolonged stoppage since the conflict began — while a Chinese supertanker operated by Cosco units was tracked traversing the Strait of Hormuz, a rare attempted crossing that attracted immediate attention given the waterway's contested status. The conflict's global economic fallout is becoming more visible across multiple dimensions. Hapag-Lloyd reported approximately $250 million in additional monthly operating costs since the war began on February 28, though its CEO noted that demand outside the Persian Gulf has been "surprisingly" resilient, with Asian export volumes holding firm through April and into the near term. The IEA's inventory assessment implies continued upward pressure on prices absent a resolution, while US crude export capacity — currently elevated by strategic reserve drawdowns — faces finite limits. The asymmetric warfare dynamic is increasingly relevant to geopolitical risk pricing. Iran has deployed midget submarines to maintain partial Hormuz control despite significant US naval superiority, a tactical approach that has sustained the stalemate now entering its eleventh week. Trump dismissed Iran's most recent peace proposal as "garbage," a development that pushed Brent December futures back above $90 and further compressed expectations for near-term resolution. The summit's energy dimension extends to Beijing's own exposure. China depends on Hormuz for a substantial share of its energy imports, and the prolonged closure is disrupting chipmaker supply chains at TSMC and Samsung. Whether Xi is willing to use Chinese influence on Tehran — and at what price — may be the summit's most consequential undisclosed negotiation. India's currency defence measures, Japan's bond yield spike, and Malawi's fertiliser access crisis each represent distinct manifestations of the same underlying shock propagating through the global economy.

  • US

    The dominant US market theme on May 12 centers on the recalibration of Federal Reserve expectations as Kevin Warsh's nomination clears an initial Senate vote. Rather than delivering the rate-cutting cycle that investors had initially priced around his appointment, bond market positioning is shifting toward tighter-for-longer policy. Bank of America strategists have explicitly flagged the possibility of rate increases as underpriced, recommending short positions in two-year Treasuries. The pivot in sentiment reflects two forces: April's solid employment report and war-driven inflation pressures. Economists surveyed by Bloomberg expect Tuesday's CPI print to show annual inflation of 3.7%, the highest reading since 2023 — a figure that would materially constrain Warsh's room to maneuver regardless of his stated preferences. The US 10-year yield held near 4.43%. Equity futures softened, with S&P 500 futures down 0.3% and the Nasdaq 100 off 0.7%, following back-to-back record highs in the prior session. The pullback appears driven by oil price uncertainty and the inflation print rather than a fundamental reassessment of the tech rally, though Michael Burry publicly warned that Nasdaq valuations have reached unsustainable levels following a "parabolic" surge. At the individual stock level, ZoomInfo plunged 32% after cutting its profit outlook and announcing layoffs, while Hims & Hers fell 17% on a profit miss. On the fiscal and policy front, the Trump administration nominated former Navy SEAL Cameron Hamilton to lead FEMA ahead of the Atlantic hurricane season, which begins June 1. Hamilton's record is notably ambiguous — he previously authored a memo titled "Abolishing FEMA" before publicly contradicting that position before Congress. Roughly 5,000 staffers have departed the agency since January, raising questions about operational capacity precisely as wildfire and hurricane seasons converge. The administration separately asked a federal trade court to pause a ruling that declared the president's 10% global tariffs unlawful while appeals proceed.

    Asia

    South Korea's equity market experienced its sharpest single-session dislocation in recent memory when presidential policy chief Kim Yong-beom floated, via a Facebook post, the idea of a "citizen dividend" funded by taxes on AI profits. The Kospi shed more than $300 billion in market capitalization within 97 minutes, falling as much as 5.1% before recovering a portion of losses after Kim clarified he meant tapping excess tax revenues rather than imposing a new windfall levy on corporate profits. The episode nonetheless exposed fragility beneath a rally that had lifted the index roughly 86% year-to-date through Monday. The underlying tension is genuine: Samsung Electronics posted a 755% profit increase in Q1 2026, its market capitalization recently crossed $1 trillion after shares more than quadrupled over the past year, and the company is forecast to generate 330 trillion won in operating profit this year — a figure that would rank it second only to Nvidia globally. Samsung's labor union is threatening an 18-day strike from May 21, demanding 15% of operating profit for chip-division employees. The convergence of redistribution politics and labor unrest around AI-driven windfall profits appears likely to persist. The Trump-Xi summit scheduled for Thursday in Beijing adds a consequential layer to the Asia picture. Trump will arrive under domestic strain, having characterized the US-Iran ceasefire as on "massive life support," and the US moved to sanction a dozen entities over Iranian oil sales to China in the days immediately preceding the meeting — a gesture of economic pressure that complicates the diplomatic atmosphere. The consensus expectation is modest: a possible extension of the 12-month trade-war ceasefire agreed at October's South Korea summit, with bilateral investment accommodation representing the principal upside surprise scenario. Elsewhere, Japan's finance minister confirmed close coordination with US Treasury Secretary Bessent on foreign exchange policy, with Bessent characterizing excess FX volatility as undesirable — a tacit endorsement of Japan's recent market intervention. Japanese household spending fell for a fourth consecutive month, and the Bank of Japan signaled a possible rate increase in June.

    Europe

    The UK's political situation deteriorated materially on May 12, with Prime Minister Keir Starmer publicly defying cabinet-level calls for him to set a departure timetable. Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood is among those privately urging him to consider a transition, according to people familiar with the matter. Starmer's response — telling cabinet that "the country expects us to get on with governing" — did little to stabilize investor sentiment. Thirty-year gilt yields rose as much as 12 basis points to 5.80%, the highest level since 1998, while the pound fell 0.5% to $1.35. Schroders disclosed it is actively avoiding UK bonds, citing the risk that Starmer's replacement could pursue a more expansionary fiscal stance. The market reaction reflects a well-established pattern: UK assets are sensitive not just to who governs, but to the uncertainty of transition itself, and Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham is already being discussed as a potential successor. In Germany, Chancellor Friedrich Merz convened coalition talks between the CDU-led conservatives and the Social Democrats aimed at advancing tax and pension reform, with public support for his government having declined sharply since taking office. Investor confidence readings did improve marginally, though Germany's fiscal watchdog cautioned against excessive spending, illustrating the tension between the reform ambition and the consolidation constraints the new government has imposed on itself. At the European Commission level, reporting suggests Ursula von der Leyen's management style — relying on a tightly centralized group of advisers while sidelining broader institutional structures — is generating internal friction and diluting focus on the Commission's core mandate of strengthening the single market. The EU did reach unanimous political agreement to sanction violent settlers in the West Bank, its first such consensus since the height of the Gaza crisis.

    Global

    The Iran conflict remains the most consequential cross-cutting variable in global markets. Trump's characterization of the US-Iran ceasefire as on "massive life support" — after he rejected Tehran's latest peace offer as "a piece of garbage" — drove Brent crude above $107 a barrel, with WTI near $98.93. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, with one commodity shipping major already planning for the scenario where it stays shut through year-end. BloombergNEF estimates disruption across 27 Middle Eastern oil fields has reached 9 million barrels per day. A supertanker that exited the Persian Gulf over the weekend appeared to reverse course toward the Gulf of Oman, illustrating the operational paralysis affecting physical oil flows. Adding to supply-chain stress, Qatar has directed vessels near the Ras Laffan LNG export facility to disable their AIS transponders, with at least nine tankers going dark from May 11 — ostensibly a safety measure, though the opacity itself adds uncertainty. The short-term buffer preventing a more severe oil price spike rests on two factors that analysts at Morgan Stanley and Mercuria both characterize as unsustainable: record US crude and product exports, running above 14 million barrels per day with emergency stockpile drawdowns at record rates, and a sharp reduction in Chinese crude purchases as state refiners draw on commercial inventories. Morgan Stanley estimates neither dynamic can persist, and that a prolonged Hormuz closure into late June or July would push prices materially higher. The supply-chain stress from the energy crisis is registering in indicators that previously flashed red during the pandemic period. The Iran war has squeezed global jet fuel supplies ahead of peak driving season. Separately, the race for critical minerals — particularly rare earths and antimony — continues, with Europe struggling to mobilize capital for projects such as a Cold War-era antimony mine in Slovakia, while concern grows in Brussels that any US-China investment accommodation reached at this week's summit could prioritize American companies at European expense.

  • US

    US equity markets continue to trade at all-time highs, with the S&P 500 blowing through first-quarter earnings estimates in a fashion that appears qualitatively different from typical beat-and-raise cycles. The semiconductor sector is the primary driver: demand from AI-related data center buildout is surging while supply remains tighter than anticipated. Brokers now forecast that next year's earnings for the SOX semiconductor index will approach double last year's levels. The broader market concentration story has intensified alongside this rally — the cap-weighted S&P 500 is widening its gap over the equal-weighted version, and the SOX index has now outperformed the Magnificent Seven since the ChatGPT launch. Alphabet, meanwhile, is closing on Nvidia as the world's largest company by market cap, with a market capitalization of $4.8 trillion against Nvidia's $5.2 trillion following a 34% gain in Alphabet shares in April alone. The macroeconomic backdrop is more complicated. April non-farm payrolls rose 115,000 and the unemployment rate held steady, a reading that most analysts characterized as Goldilocks-adjacent but consistent with a labor market entering what Macquarie's Viktor Shvets described as an "unstable equilibrium" — no hiring but also no firing. The more pressing concern is inflation. Tuesday's CPI print is expected to show headline inflation approaching 4%, driven by elevated oil prices stemming from the Hormuz closure. Oxford Economics pushed its next Fed cut forecast from June to December, and futures markets price the Fed on hold into the second half of 2026 at the earliest. The combination of strong earnings and restrictive-for-longer monetary policy represents an awkward but, for now, equity-supportive equilibrium. Separately, a federal trade court declared Trump's 10% global tariffs unlawful, a fresh legal setback for the administration's trade agenda, though the practical market implications remain to be seen given ongoing appeals.

    Asia

    China's inflation data for April provided a significant data point: producer prices rose 2.8% year-on-year, the fastest pace since July 2022 and well above consensus, while consumer inflation climbed unexpectedly to 1.2%. Several analysts declared that China has formally exited its deflationary spiral, though the drivers are partly imported — higher energy and raw material costs resulting from the Iran war and the Hormuz closure. The yuan has strengthened to a more than three-year high on the back of these dynamics, with Goldman Sachs estimating the currency remains over 20% undervalued against the dollar and projecting a move to 6.50 within twelve months. Chinese crude imports fell 20% year-on-year in April to their lowest since July 2022, a reflection of the supply disruption rather than weakening demand. The geopolitical calendar is heavily loaded. A Trump-Xi summit in Beijing is scheduled for Thursday and Friday, the first US presidential visit to China in nearly a decade. Senior US officials indicate Trump intends to press Xi on Beijing's economic relationship with Iran, including revenue flows and potential weapons exports. Pre-summit talks between Treasury Secretary Bessent and Vice Premier He Lifeng are set for Seoul on Wednesday. In Tokyo on Monday, Bessent meets Prime Minister Takaichi and Finance Minister Katayama amid continuing tensions over yen intervention and Japan's monetary policy trajectory; US Treasury yields have on more than one occasion seen spillover from Japanese bond selloffs. Goldman economists separately flagged that AI-driven chip booms in South Korea and Taiwan are on track to push both economies' current-account surpluses to record levels, likely pressuring their central banks toward rate increases later this year. In India, Prime Minister Modi appealed to citizens to conserve fuel and avoid gold purchases as the rupee trades near record lows.

    Europe

    UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer faced the most acute political pressure of his tenure as he delivered a speech aimed at resetting his government following Labour's sweeping defeat in local elections. Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner publicly called for an urgent change in direction, and Reform UK's Nigel Farage — who polled as the biggest winner from last week's voting — is consolidating his position as a serious political force, though his path to power remains uncertain given his divisiveness and the prospect of opponents coalescing against him. Labour's predicament reflects a broader public frustration with the government's economic management against the backdrop of Iran-war-induced inflation pressures. On the economic front, German industrial production unexpectedly fell in March for a second consecutive month, reinforcing concerns about the eurozone's largest economy amid rising energy costs. A Bloomberg survey of economists showed the ECB is expected to hike rates twice in 2026 as inflation accelerates, a marked shift in the policy outlook. Separately, demand for affordable electric vehicles is rising sharply across Europe: inquiries for EVs priced below €30,000 on Germany's Mobile.de surged 87% since early March, with Renault's Zoe among the leading models. This reflects a meaningful consumer behavioral response to the fuel price shock. British employers meanwhile continued shifting toward temporary hiring in April, with permanent-role hiring falling at its fastest pace in three months, extending a downturn that began in 2022, according to the Recruitment and Employment Confederation. US troop drawdowns from Europe remain a live concern among European defense planners, with allies anticipating further announcements potentially including reductions in Italy and the cancellation of plans to station long-range missiles in Germany.

    Global

    The central tension in global markets remains the Iran conflict and the sustained closure of the Strait of Hormuz, now entering its tenth week. Donald Trump rejected Iran's latest peace proposal, sending Brent crude up as much as 4.6% toward $106 a barrel before settling above $103. Bond yields rose globally on renewed inflation fears, though equity markets showed limited reaction to the diplomatic impasse. Morgan Stanley warned that the oil market is in "a race against time" — the buffers that contained prices through the initial weeks of the conflict, including elevated US crude exports and a demand slowdown in China, may not hold if the strait remains closed into June. In a counterintuitive development, physical oil cargo prices fell sharply as buyers retreated from last month's bidding frenzy, though traders cautioned this calm may be short-lived. The Hormuz disruption is reshaping trade and energy flows in structural ways. Iran is boosting rail freight to China via Kazakhstan and Turkmenistan, with train frequency rising from roughly one per week to one every three to four days and container rates along the route climbing 40% above typical levels. A Qatari LNG tanker made a rare passage through Hormuz to Pakistan — the result of Islamabad's mediation between Washington and Tehran — but this remains an exception rather than a trend, with Qatari export volumes still far below pre-conflict levels. China's clean technology manufacturers are explicitly leveraging the global energy shock as an export opportunity: EV exports rose 112% year-on-year in April, solar panel shipments doubled month-on-month in March to a record, and battery exports climbed over a third. Chinese firms are actively targeting new markets in Europe, Africa, and Southeast Asia. Against this backdrop, more than 40 nations were convening Monday to plan a European-led naval escort mission for ships transiting Hormuz once a ceasefire is established.

  • US

    The Federal Reserve's path toward rate cuts appears increasingly constrained by the ongoing US-Iran conflict, with surging oil and fertilizer prices sustaining inflationary pressure that pushes accommodation further out of reach. This represents a direct tension with President Trump's publicly stated desire for lower interest rates — a policy goal that the administration's own military engagements in the Gulf may be actively undermining. The self-reinforcing dynamic between energy prices and monetary policy expectations deserves close attention from investors pricing near-term Fed action. On the dollar, the question of reserve currency durability has moved to the forefront at this week's Milken Institute conference in Los Angeles, where a range of institutional investors and policymakers expressed growing unease about dollar-asset concentration. EQT's Per Franzén, overseeing roughly €270 billion in private-market assets, noted that clients are actively reassessing their historical overweight to dollar-denominated holdings — a sentiment that, while not new, appears to be gaining institutional traction. Critically, the source of dollar vulnerability in this framing is not competitive displacement by the renminbi or another currency, but rather the accumulation of US policy errors. As Gateway Partners' V. Shankar observed, the dollar's demise, if it comes, will be self-inflicted rather than externally imposed. The Gulf conflict is creating specific transmission mechanisms worth monitoring. Iran's reported collection of Hormuz transit tolls in Bitcoin represents a de minimis but symbolically notable bypass of dollar payment infrastructure. More consequentially, China's instruction to private refiners to disregard US sanctions on Iranian oil directly challenges the enforcement architecture that gives dollar primacy its coercive utility. Treasury Secretary Bessent has identified dollar supremacy as the administration's top financial objective; the current operating environment is testing the internal coherence of that goal against other policy choices. Whether these strains prove transitory or structural remains genuinely uncertain.

    Asia

    China's posture on Iranian oil sanctions has emerged as one of the week's more significant geopolitical-financial developments. Beijing's directive to private refiners to disregard US secondary sanctions targeting Iranian crude purchases represents a deliberate challenge to American financial enforcement mechanisms, and carries implications beyond energy supply alone. It signals that China is prepared to absorb diplomatic friction with Washington in exchange for discounted oil access and a principled demonstration that US sanctions architecture has limits — a message with resonance across emerging markets. On the dollar-alternative infrastructure front, China remains a central participant in Project mBridge, the BIS-coordinated cross-border settlement system developed alongside Hong Kong, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Thailand. The system's design explicitly routes around traditional US-dominated financial channels. While mBridge remains nascent in transaction volume relative to SWIFT-based flows, its development alongside elevated geopolitical tensions and rising oil prices creates conditions under which adoption could accelerate faster than baseline projections assume. Wall Street analysts have reportedly begun calling a potential turn in China's property market, according to Bloomberg's New Economy coverage this week — a development worth monitoring given the sector's outsized role in domestic credit dynamics and household wealth. Chinese truck operators are also reportedly accelerating electric vehicle adoption as fuel costs rise sharply in the wake of the Hormuz disruption, illustrating how the Gulf conflict is producing second-order demand shifts with relevance for EV manufacturers and battery supply chains across the region. India warrants a forward-looking note: Bloomberg New Economy signals an editorial pivot toward more sustained India coverage ahead of the New Economy Forum in New Delhi this October, reflecting the economy's growing prominence in global investor and policy discussions. Near-term India-specific data was limited in this period's coverage.

    Europe

    European corporate and regulatory developments this week centered on the intersection of sustainability obligations and industrial strategy. Polestar, the Gothenburg-based EV manufacturer spun out of Volvo, published its annual sustainability report indicating a 7.3% reduction in carbon footprint per vehicle in the prior year, driven in part by its Polestar 4 SUV. The company maintains a stated objective of producing a net-zero vehicle by 2035 — a target that stands in contrast to the broader industry trend of deprioritizing emissions commitments amid margin pressure, rising tariff costs, and competition from lower-cost Chinese EV producers. The European regulatory backdrop provides structural context. The EU's forthcoming digital product passport requirement will mandate that automakers compile detailed sustainability and recyclability data for EVs and their batteries by February, creating compliance obligations that differentiate producers who have invested in carbon accounting infrastructure from those who have not. Polestar's multi-year head start in this area may translate into competitive advantage as the regulatory timeline tightens. Meanwhile, the European Commission's partial retreat from its earlier aggressive phase-out timeline for internal combustion engines reflects the political difficulty of maintaining ambitious climate commitments under simultaneous energy price and industrial competitiveness pressures. The broader European macro picture is shaped significantly by the Hormuz disruption's energy price pass-through, which complicates the ECB's rate trajectory in ways that parallel the Fed's dilemma — elevated energy costs sustaining inflation at a moment when growth headwinds would otherwise argue for accommodation. No specific ECB communications or eurozone data releases were covered in this period's source material, and UK-specific developments were absent from available coverage.

    Global

    The defining cross-regional theme this week is the compound effect of the US-Iran conflict on energy markets, trade flows, and the architecture of dollar-denominated global finance. The joint American-Israeli operation against Iran has produced consequences that extend well beyond the immediate theater: retaliatory attacks on Persian Gulf infrastructure, paralysis of Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic, and sharp increases in oil and fertilizer prices. Even if hostilities were to cease in the near term, the supply chain and inflationary aftereffects appear likely to persist for months, with meaningful implications for global growth trajectories and central bank sequencing across multiple jurisdictions. The Hormuz disruption is simultaneously accelerating certain structural transitions. Chinese commercial truck operators are reportedly accelerating electrification in response to surging diesel costs — a concrete illustration of how energy price shocks can compress the adoption timeline for alternatives. More broadly, the Iran conflict has elevated the perceived security premium of domestic clean energy capacity, reinforcing investment rationales for renewables and EVs that had faced headwinds from US policy reversals and subsidy reductions. De-dollarization dynamics, long discussed but slow-moving, appear to be gaining modest momentum through specific, observable channels: Iran collecting Hormuz tolls in Bitcoin, China defying secondary sanctions, Gulf sovereign wealth funds reassessing their US asset allocations as American security guarantees prove less reliable, and central banks across emerging markets increasing gold holdings while reducing dollar reserves. The long-run dollar story remains contested — stablecoin growth creates new Treasury demand, and many smaller economies have no practical payments alternative — but the accumulation of stress points is becoming harder to dismiss as purely episodic. The more salient risk, as several Milken conference participants noted, is not displacement by a rival currency but erosion through sustained US policy inconsistency.

  • US

    The dominant financial news of the day centers on growing anxiety over US sovereign debt sustainability. DoubleLine Capital's Jeffrey Gundlach disclosed he is repositioning portfolios for an extreme scenario in which Washington restructures its obligations — potentially cutting coupons to as low as 1% — though he places the probability below 30%. The disclosure is notable less for its immediacy than for its direction: Gundlach joins Apollo's Marc Rowan and Bridgewater's Ray Dalio in issuing unusually direct warnings about the trajectory of US fiscal and financial conditions. US public debt held by the public has now surpassed $31 trillion, exceeding annual GDP, though five-year CDS-implied default probabilities remain below 1%, underscoring the gap between market pricing and investor rhetoric.
