On trade and fiscal policy, a US federal trade court declared Trump's 10% global tariffs unlawful in a fresh legal setback, following the Supreme Court's earlier vacating of prior levies. The administration has given the EU until July 4 to ratify a trade deal or face higher duties, signaling continued assertiveness despite the judicial headwinds. The April non-farm payrolls report, expected to show a solid 65,000 increase, will be closely watched for signs of labor market resilience. In equity markets, S&P 500 futures edged higher on a technology-led rally, with Nasdaq 100 futures up 0.7%. Cloudflare fell as much as 17% in premarket trading after announcing job cuts alongside a disappointing revenue forecast, while Trade Desk sank 15% on a guidance miss. Block advanced 7.3% after AI-driven cost restructuring improved its profit outlook. Consumer stress remains a subtext: Kraft Heinz's CEO warned that lower-income shoppers are "literally running out of money at the end of the month," with elevated gasoline prices — a direct consequence of the Iran conflict — compounding pressure on household budgets. New York Governor Kathy Hochul moved to soften the state's landmark climate law, pushing the emissions-enforcement deadline to 2028 from an immediate court-ordered timeline, citing energy affordability concerns. The decision reflects a broader pattern across US states where Iran war-induced fuel price increases are weakening political support for near-term climate mandates.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets recorded one of their strongest weekly performances since late 2024, with the MSCI Asia gauge rising 5.6%. South Korea's Kospi led globally with a 14% weekly gain, driven by a surge in semiconductor names — SK Hynix rose 30%, Samsung 18% — as easing Middle East tensions and AI optimism redirected investor attention toward regional growth assets. Goldman Sachs raised its Kospi target for the fourth time this year, projecting an additional 20% gain and citing forecast earnings growth of 300% for 2026, a figure the bank describes as the strongest for any Asian market since the 1999 post-crisis recovery. Taiwan's benchmark gained approximately 7% and Japan's Nikkei 225 rose 5.8%. Gains were partially checked on Friday as renewed US-Iran clashes near the Strait of Hormuz reintroduced energy risk. Toyota issued a notably cautious earnings outlook, projecting ¥3 trillion in operating income for fiscal 2027 against a consensus of ¥4.6 trillion, citing elevated raw material costs stemming from the Iran conflict. The stock fell as much as 3.5%, illustrating how the energy disruption continues to weigh on Japan's industrial exporters even as equity indices broadly recover. The yen remains under pressure despite apparent Bank of Japan interventions that moved the exchange rate from roughly ¥160 to below ¥157 per dollar; Vanguard remains bearish on the currency, arguing that higher oil prices are eroding Japan's terms of trade and that intervention has limited medium-term efficacy. Asian governments are actively intervening in energy markets to cushion the oil shock. Japan committed $10 billion to assist regional nations with procurement and stockpiling. Australia provided A$30 million to Fiji for fuel purchases and repurposed export financing to support domestic importers, where March import costs for diesel and aviation fuel ran more than 40% above February levels. Separately, a Bangkok-based company — OBON — has been identified in US prosecutorial filings as the suspected conduit for smuggling Super Micro servers containing Nvidia chips to China, with Alibaba named as a potential end customer. China's labor market showed continued deterioration in early-career cohorts, with the jobless rate for workers aged 25-29 rising to 7.7% in March, the highest since data revisions two years ago. Chinese courts have begun siding with workers displaced by AI automation, signaling that authorities are attempting to manage social tensions arising from rapid AI adoption — even as Beijing pursues AI development as a strategic priority.

    Europe

    Reform UK's performance in English local elections emerged as the day's defining political development in the region. With counts approaching completion in a third of councils, Reform had secured a net gain of 275 seats while Labour's tally fell by 204 — Labour losing roughly half of the seats it was defending. Prime Minister Keir Starmer ruled out stepping aside despite the results. The outcome suggests a meaningful fragmentation of Britain's traditional two-party political structure, with both Reform on the right and the Greens on the left gaining ground at the expense of Labour and the Conservatives simultaneously. The degree to which this represents durable realignment rather than a mid-term protest vote remains to be seen, but the breadth of Reform's gains gives Nigel Farage a credible platform heading into the national electoral cycle. On energy security, European utilities and governments are accelerating hardening of electricity network infrastructure. Poland's grid operator PSE has deployed helicopter-mounted sensor arrays to monitor for tampering with transmission lines, with similar steps being replicated across Central and Eastern Europe's eastern flank. The shift reflects a recognition that power infrastructure was not designed to withstand the range of threats — missile strikes, cyberattacks, drone assaults — that have become operationally relevant since Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Transmission operators are already facing funding shortfalls, making the additional capital requirements politically and financially difficult. In climate policy, the ECB's Christine Lagarde cautioned that even euro-denominated stablecoins would create risks to financial stability. In France, EU prosecutors are investigating possible misappropriation of funds linked to media training for National Rally figures including Jordan Bardella, a development the party denied. The Venice Biennale opened amid political controversy over the participation of both Israel and Russia, with Finland scaling back its presence and the EU withdrawing more than $2 million in funding from the Russian pavilion — now reopening for the first time since 2022.

    Global

    The most consequential cross-cutting development remains the US-Iran conflict and its implications for global energy markets. US forces struck Iranian missile and drone launch sites near the Strait of Hormuz after Iran attacked three American warships transiting the strait — a significant escalation even as President Trump maintained that the broader ceasefire remains in effect. WTI crude moved higher on the clash but retreated intraday, reflecting market uncertainty about whether the engagement represents a contained incident or the beginning of a new escalation cycle. Iran is expected to respond to a US-proposed peace framework via Pakistan within two days; Trump has threatened intensified strikes if Tehran refuses terms. China's role in the energy disruption is multifaceted and worth disaggregating. Beijing slashed April oil imports by approximately one quarter from pre-war levels, with state-owned companies reselling cargoes to European and Asian buyers — a dynamic that has helped rebalance global supply despite the Hormuz closure. Simultaneously, China's top diplomat Wang Yi urged Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz quickly, driven both by Beijing's dependence on Middle Eastern oil (approximately two-fifths of its imports) and the desire to position Xi Jinping as a peacemaker ahead of his summit with Trump. Iran, meanwhile, is expanding rail trade through Kazakhstan and Turkmenistan to China as a partial workaround to the US port blockade, deepening its economic dependence on Beijing. The Trump-Xi Beijing summit, scheduled for next week, will be the first visit by a sitting US president to China since 2017. Senior business figures from Blackstone, Citigroup, Nvidia, Apple, Exxon, and Boeing are expected to accompany Trump, signaling a commercial dimension to the visit. Expectations for substantive deliverables are low — Taiwan, tech curbs, and Hong Kong remain areas of significant divergence — but both sides appear to have strategic incentives to maintain a stable bilateral appearance. On artificial intelligence and energy infrastructure, the power demands of data centers are increasingly a government-level concern across Australia, the UK, Kenya, and the United States, with BloombergNEF projecting US data center power consumption to exceed 106 gigawatts by 2035, more than double current levels.

  • US

    US equity markets closed at record highs on Wednesday and extended those gains in overnight futures, with the S&P 500 approaching 7,400 as investors increasingly look through the energy shock toward a potential US-Iran deal. The market's resilience is notable: having largely decoupled from oil's gyrations in recent weeks, equities are drawing support from robust high-tech investment and an earnings season that continues to outperform expectations — roughly 80% of S&P 500 companies reporting thus far have beaten estimates. Wall Street bonuses are projected to rise for a third consecutive year as volatility-driven trading revenues and a recovery in dealmaking reinforce the sector's performance. The more contested debate in fixed income markets centers on whether 30-year Treasury yields can sustain a push through 5%. The long bond briefly breached that level this week, driven by persistent fears that Middle East-linked energy inflation will delay Federal Reserve easing further. Market pricing remains relatively hawkish by some estimates, though analysts at Evercore ISI argue a durable deal bringing oil lower would render rate cuts delayed rather than derailed. Fed Governor Collins this week aligned herself with last week's policy dissent on easing language, suggesting internal divisions at the FOMC have not fully resolved. The K-shaped nature of the oil shock is increasingly well-documented. New York Fed research finds that households earning under $40,000 annually reduced real gasoline consumption by 7% even as nominal spending rose 12%, while higher-income cohorts barely adjusted consumption at all. This dynamic complicates the Fed's task: the inflation impulse is real but its incidence is uneven, limiting the demand-destruction signal from aggregate data. On corporate news, Fortinet surged 15% in premarket trading after raising its revenue outlook, while Snap fell sharply on advertising revenue weakness attributed directly to the Middle East conflict. The Trump administration began processing refunds for $166 billion in tariffs declared unlawful by the Supreme Court, a development with meaningful fiscal implications that has received relatively limited market attention amid the broader geopolitical noise.

    Asia

    China's financial regulators have quietly directed the country's largest banks to halt new lending to five refiners recently sanctioned by the US for their involvement in Iranian oil trade. The directive leaves existing loans intact, stopping short of a full clampdown, but the signal is deliberate and well-timed: President Trump is expected to press President Xi Jinping on China's continued purchases of Iranian crude at their upcoming Beijing summit. Beijing is simultaneously invoking its "blocking" rules to project defiance against US secondary sanctions while shielding its major banks from the dollar-access risks that aggressive enforcement would entail. The financial retreat is cautious but unmistakable. The legal exposure for global banks operating between the two systems is crystallizing separately. JPMorgan and Citigroup are being sued in China after freezing tens of millions of dollars in payments tied to a firm subsequently sanctioned by the US — with the plaintiff alleging funds were blocked and routed to US authorities before sanctions formally applied. The case illustrates the narrowing corridor available to institutions caught between competing legal jurisdictions. Japan's Nikkei 225 surged 5.6% to a record high as deal optimism surrounding the Strait of Hormuz lifted risk sentiment broadly across the region. The yen, however, continues to struggle near 155 per dollar despite repeated suspected interventions by Japanese authorities estimated to have deployed around $30 billion in recent days. Analysts broadly characterize intervention as increasingly ineffective level-targeting absent a meaningful shift in Bank of Japan policy, given persistent structural headwinds from elevated energy import costs and still-accommodative monetary settings. In Southeast Asia, the Philippines faces its most severe economic stress since the pandemic, with growth slowing sharply, inflation surging, and consumer spending at its weakest in over a decade — a direct consequence of the country's heavy reliance on Middle Eastern crude. Malaysia's central bank kept its benchmark rate unchanged for a fifth straight meeting, judging that contained inflation and a relatively resilient ringgit provide sufficient buffer for now.

    Europe

    UK local elections took place Thursday, with control of the Scottish and Welsh parliaments and more than half of England's councils at stake. The vote functions as a verdict on Prime Minister Keir Starmer's tenure of under two years, during which a combination of policy missteps, tax increases, and questions over judgment — notably the appointment of Peter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington — have eroded Labour's standing significantly. The political arithmetic is complicated: Labour holds a large parliamentary majority and faces no general election until 2029, but market attention has focused on whether leadership pressure could push successor candidates toward looser fiscal commitments, which gilt markets would view unfavorably. That gilt market backdrop is already under strain. The spread of UK gilts over US Treasuries has widened beyond levels seen at the peak of the Liz Truss episode in 2022, reflecting a combination of the UK's negative net international investment position, twin current account and fiscal deficits, and the perception that the Bank of England faces a more constrained path than the Federal Reserve. Overnight index swaps imply significantly more BoE tightening than was priced before the Iran conflict, a divergence that has widened materially from the Fed's expected trajectory. Bank of England officials this week joined private forecasters in questioning the reliability of recent UK economic data, adding further complexity to rate-setting. On the European corporate front, Shell reported stronger-than-expected first-quarter earnings as elevated energy prices and increased trading volatility — both direct consequences of the Hormuz closure — more than offset production declines. Campari sank 11% in Milan after disappointing results. Looking further ahead, China's newly issued five-year plan, with its emphasis on upgrading traditional industries and cultivating frontier technologies including robotics and biomedicine, is generating significant anxiety in European industrial circles, particularly in Germany. Machinery association VDMA describes the level of corporate concern as unprecedented, as Beijing's shift from scale toward quality and sustainability directly threatens sectors that have historically underpinned European export competitiveness.

    Global

    The central geopolitical and market-moving development of the day is the tentative US-Iran diplomatic framework that has circulated in the form of a one-page memorandum of understanding. The proposal calls for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for Washington ending its blockade of Iranian ports and lifting some sanctions, with Iran agreeing to a moratorium on uranium enrichment. Broader nuclear questions — the duration of the moratorium and disposition of Iran's highly-enriched uranium stockpile — would be negotiated subsequently. Brent crude fell sharply on the news before paring losses after Tehran indicated a deal was not imminent; the commodity remains above $99 per barrel, having declined roughly 12% over the prior two sessions but still elevated relative to pre-conflict levels. Market participants appear to be pricing something between a near-term transit reopening and a comprehensive settlement — a distinction that matters considerably for the path of energy prices, inflation expectations, and central bank policy globally. Analysts note that even a narrow deal restoring shipping flows would meaningfully relieve pressure on fuel, fertilizers, plastics, and a range of intermediate goods now experiencing shortages. European industrial sectors — aerospace, autos, broader manufacturing — stand to benefit disproportionately given the continent has borne the heaviest economic burden from the disruption. Supply chain infrastructure is adapting in parallel with diplomacy. DP World has introduced end-to-end war risk insurance covering ocean freight, air freight, inland haulage, and storage for cargo traversing the region — an acknowledgment that perceived risk will persist even if a deal materializes. The New York Fed's global supply chain pressure index reached its highest reading since July 2022 in April. Meanwhile, the Iran conflict is accelerating green bond issuance in emerging markets, with first-quarter volumes rising 53% year-on-year to $24 billion as energy-import-dependent economies seek to accelerate renewables deployment — a structural consequence of the shock that may outlast any near-term diplomatic resolution.

  • US

    US equity markets extended their rally to yet another all-time record for the S&P 500, though the composition of the advance warrants scrutiny. Factor analysis shows quality stocks — characterized by stable earnings and strong balance sheets — have been consistent underperformers since Liberation Day, while momentum and liquidity factors continue to dominate. Goldman Sachs strategist commentary flags "yellow flags" in the thinness of the rally. The advance appears driven by animal spirits and abundant liquidity rather than fundamental re-rating, leaving it potentially vulnerable to any deterioration in the macro or geopolitical backdrop. On the technology earnings front, AMD surged approximately 17% in premarket trading after raising its sales forecast, and Super Micro Computer gained 19% on a strong report. The AI investment thesis received further validation as Alphabet raised nearly $17 billion in debt to fund data center buildout. Nvidia, by contrast, has fallen 9% over the past six sessions and is the worst performer in the Philadelphia semiconductor index year-to-date, as customers including Alphabet, Amazon, and Intel develop credible competing chip architectures — though there is limited evidence yet of material market share loss. On fiscal and data integrity matters, two consequential developments emerged. Former Treasury Secretary Mnuchin stated plainly there is no "break-the-glass" solution to the US debt problem, pushing back on Hank Paulson's call for emergency planning, while acknowledging rising risk premiums in long-dated Treasuries. The CBO projects federal debt reaching 108% of GDP by 2030. Separately, new research suggests the politically motivated dismissal of Bureau of Labor Statistics chief Erika McEntarfer correlated with approximately $20 billion in foregone industrial investment — a figure 25 times the BLS annual budget — as executives reportedly grew less willing to commit capital amid eroding confidence in government data. The SEC's proposal to end mandatory quarterly earnings reporting remains in a 60-day comment period, with multiple alternative frameworks under consideration.

    Asia

    Asian equities delivered one of the more striking single-session moves in recent memory. A gauge of Asian shares rose 2.6% to a record high, led by South Korea's Kospi, which surged more than 6% as Samsung Electronics rallied 14-16% to cross a $1 trillion market capitalization — only the second Asian company to achieve that threshold. The re-rating reflects a market judgment that memory's role in the AI infrastructure stack is structural rather than cyclical, with Samsung sitting at the intersection of chipmaking and data infrastructure expansion. AI-related names across the region followed suit, with the liquidity and momentum factors particularly pronounced. The yen rallied as much as 1.8% against the dollar, reigniting speculation of Japanese official intervention — the second potential episode since April 30. Goldman Sachs analysts estimate Japan retains sufficient reserves for roughly 30 intervention episodes at last week's scale, though officials are expected to conserve firepower for more strategically effective moments. The yen remains approximately 9% weaker against the dollar over the past twelve months. Geopolitically, Japan's deployment of 1,400 combat troops to the Philippines for joint Balikatan drills — including a ship-sinking exercise in the South China Sea — marks the first such deployment since World War II and signals a meaningful shift in Tokyo's willingness to project military power proximate to China. In China, Foreign Minister Wang Yi hosted Iran's Abbas Araghchi and urged Tehran to continue negotiations, ahead of the Trump-Xi summit expected next week where the Iran war will be a central agenda item. Indonesia tightened foreign currency purchase limits to $25,000 from $50,000 to support a beleaguered rupiah. In India, a five-week administrative halt on gold and silver imports has pushed domestic bullion prices higher and raised shortage concerns in the world's second-largest gold market.

    Europe

    European markets processed a complex set of signals on May 6. UK 30-year gilt yields reached 5.78% — the highest level since 1998 — as inflation and fiscal concerns keep the UK in the crosshairs of sovereign debt markets. Thursday's UK local elections carry meaningful political risk for Prime Minister Keir Starmer; Labour is on course to record its lowest share of London voters in over a century, with Reform and the Greens gaining ground. Analysts note that poor results could sharpen intra-party challenges to Starmer's leadership, adding another layer of uncertainty to an already strained fiscal picture. In Germany, Chancellor Friedrich Merz — who entered office one year ago with sweeping ambitions to revitalize the economy and anchor European leadership — is, by most assessments, struggling on all three fronts. His coalition faces questions about longevity, and he has already ruled out forming a minority government should it collapse. The diplomatic backdrop worsened after Trump announced US troop withdrawals from Germany in response to Merz's public criticism, underscoring how transatlantic tensions constrain European policy space. On corporate earnings, Novo Nordisk surged approximately 9% after its new Wegovy pill drove quarterly sales growth and the company guided for a smaller-than-expected revenue decline. Infineon's revenue outlook beat expectations on AI-driven power chip demand. BMW's earnings declined, reflecting intense pricing competition in China. In central bank governance, French President Macron nominated Emmanuel Moulin, a close adviser, to head the Banque de France and take a seat on the ECB's Governing Council. Separately, the EU reiterated its demand that Washington adopt the main elements of their trade deal by July and respect prior commitments, including a 15% tariff ceiling, as US Ambassador Puzder warned 25% tariffs on European vehicles could arrive "relatively soon."

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme remains the US-Iran conflict and the tentative signals pointing toward a negotiated resolution. President Trump suspended "Project Freedom" — the US-led naval operation to escort stranded vessels through the Strait of Hormuz — citing a request from mediator Pakistan and what he described as "great progress" toward a comprehensive agreement. Axios reported the two sides are working toward a one-page memorandum of understanding to frame subsequent nuclear negotiations. The operation had produced the opposite of its intended effect: while two ships were escorted out on Monday, US forces repelled multiple Iranian drone, missile, and fast-attack boat strikes, and Iran struck an oil terminal in Fujairah, UAE. The path to a durable settlement remains uncertain. Oil markets reacted sharply. WTI crude fell nearly 10% on the day, dropping below $100 a barrel from a peak near $112 a month ago, though December 2026 WTI futures remained around $77 — approximately 8% above early April levels — suggesting markets do not yet price a near-term, sustained reopening of the Strait. US shale producers are nonetheless responding: Diamondback Energy's CEO signaled an "inflection point," with the Permian Basin expected to add as many as 30 rigs. Exxon plans a 12% production increase. US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon for the first time since July 2022. Chinese aluminum and metal exports are rising materially, benefiting from the Middle East supply disruption and clean-tech demand. Microsoft is reportedly weighing abandonment of its 2030 hourly clean-energy matching target — a significant retreat from commitments made before the AI era — as data center power demand and natural gas's appeal grow. This dynamic is not isolated to Microsoft: across major hyperscalers, reported carbon emissions have risen 23-64% versus pre-ChatGPT baselines, underscoring a widening tension between AI infrastructure expansion and corporate climate commitments that has yet to find a credible resolution framework.

  • US

    US equity markets remain remarkably resilient in the face of a deteriorating geopolitical backdrop. S&P 500 futures edged higher by roughly 0.3%, with the index holding near record highs despite the renewed Iran-related tensions that sent bond yields climbing on fresh inflation concerns. The durability of that performance owes much to the ongoing AI capital expenditure cycle: first-quarter earnings from the Magnificent Seven — with Nvidia still to report — have been broadly robust, with AI-driven investment in information-processing equipment rising 64% in real terms and software spending up 46% in Q1. All other capex declined 17%, underscoring that the AI trade remains the dominant force in US business investment. Corporate buybacks are providing a structural floor. S&P 500 companies announced $665 billion in authorized repurchases in the first four months of the year, the highest pace on record to start any year, with full-year authorized repurchases projected at $1.55 trillion — potentially eclipsing last year's record. The buyback surge partially offsets a notable compression in shareholder returns via dividends and free cash flow, as hyperscalers redirect cash toward data-center buildouts. Wall Street projects free cash flow for the largest cloud platforms to fall more than 70% from peak by end-2026, even as earnings continue to climb — a tension that reflects accounting treatment of capex rather than underlying deterioration. On the corporate front, Amazon's launch of Amazon Supply Chain Services — packaging its freight, trucking, air, ocean, and last-mile capabilities into a single commercial offering — represents a potentially significant competitive incursion into freight markets. Shares in FedEx, UPS, and Expeditors declined on the announcement, though Bloomberg Intelligence analysts characterize the near-term threat as incremental rather than structural. Elsewhere, Palantir shares fell after disappointing US commercial revenue, Duolingo tumbled on a soft bookings outlook, and Pinterest surged after results beat expectations. The Fed left rates unchanged last week, with internal divisions over the inflation outlook deepening as two-year inflation swaps have risen above 3%.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets pulled back modestly from recent highs in thinned holiday trading, with the Hang Seng declining 0.8%, as the renewed Iran-US exchange of fire rekindled inflation concerns and prompted risk reduction across the region. The most immediate transmission mechanism is through oil-dependent currencies: the Indonesian rupiah set a new record low, the Indian rupee also touched historic lows, and the Philippine peso approached all-time weak levels as investors shed exposure to economies most vulnerable to the energy shock. Despite the macro headwinds, Indonesia delivered a notably strong first-quarter GDP print of 5.61% year-on-year — a three-year high — supported by record government spending. That pace ranks Indonesia among Asia's fastest-growing large economies, though it trails Taiwan's AI-fueled 13.7% expansion. Hong Kong's economy also expanded at its fastest pace in nearly five years in Q1. The bifurcation between AI-leveraged and oil-exposed economies is becoming a defining feature of the regional growth landscape. Japan's diplomatic posture during the holiday week merits attention. Prime Minister Takaichi conducted overseas visits to Vietnam and Australia — deepening what both sides characterize as a quasi-alliance — while Defense Minister Koizumi deployed to the Philippines to observe Japanese troops exercising there for the first time since World War II. The conspicuous absence of any substantive China engagement, set against the backdrop of a ruling-party delegation visiting Taiwan and prior statements on potential Japanese military involvement in a Taiwan Strait scenario, suggests the bilateral relationship remains under meaningful strain. A senior LDP lawmaker visited China briefly but met no officials. The Reserve Bank of Australia delivered a third consecutive rate hike in an 8-1 vote, unwinding all of last year's easing. Governor Bullock acknowledged the hardship the cycle is imposing on households but signaled the board has little alternative given pre-existing inflation pressures, a loose fiscal stance, and weak productivity. The RBA now leads developed-market peers in the hawkish response to the energy shock.

    Europe

    European markets showed modest resilience on Tuesday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 0.6%, though the region faces a complex macro backdrop that warrants careful monitoring. The European Commission's Dombrovskis described the continent as facing a stagflationary shock — a characterization that captures the uncomfortable combination of higher energy costs and sluggish growth, even if European electricity markets have remained calmer than oil markets might imply. ECB President Lagarde, speaking at a climate conference, reinforced the message that Europe's dependency on imported fossil fuels is "clearly unsustainable," adding institutional weight to the energy transition narrative. HSBC reported first-quarter profits that missed consensus estimates, weighed by an unexpected UK fraud-related charge and provisions related to Middle East conflict risks. Shares fell 5.9% in London. The result partially obscures a more constructive picture in wealth management and Hong Kong, and the bank upgraded its net interest income outlook. By contrast, UniCredit delivered a record quarterly profit of €3.22 billion, up 16% year-on-year and well ahead of the €2.68 billion consensus — illustrating the continued divergence in European banking fortunes. UK political dynamics are evolving in ways that may complicate the government's policy agenda. Labour's perceived failure to deliver on election commitments has revitalized Scottish nationalist sentiment, adding a domestic pressure point for Prime Minister Starmer. Meanwhile, the EU-US trade relationship remains tense: EU finance ministers reiterated that the bloc retains retaliatory tools should the US follow through on proposed 25% auto tariffs, while French officials emphasized European options against threats to strategic industries. Romanian politics added to continental uncertainty, with a no-confidence vote against Prime Minister Bolojan backed by both the far-right and the Social Democrats — a coalition that, if successful, could deepen a fiscal crisis around the EU's widest budget deficit.

    Global

    The defining event of the past 24 hours is the breakdown of the month-old US-Iran ceasefire, which now appears increasingly fragile. US warships escorted American-flagged vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, prompting an exchange of fire and an Iranian missile and drone attack that caused a large fire at the UAE's Fujairah port — a facility Tehran has now explicitly designated within its "zone of maritime control." Hundreds of vessels clustered near Dubai as shipowners continued to avoid the strait. Iraq is offering substantial discounts to term buyers for crude loaded this month, but tankers would need to transit Hormuz to collect barrels, creating a commercial impasse. The oil market implications are compounding daily. With the conflict entering its tenth week, analysts estimate a cumulative supply loss now approaching or exceeding one billion barrels, with a minimum of 10 million barrels per day not reaching global markets via the strait. Brent futures for end-2026 have moved above $90 per barrel — the highest since the conflict began — while WTI spot trades above $104. Prediction markets now assign less than 50% odds to a return to normal Hormuz traffic by end of June, down from around 90% two weeks ago. Two-year inflation swaps in the US have crossed 3%, their highest since 2022. The geopolitical calculus points toward a negotiated resolution structured around something resembling the 2015 JCPOA framework, though the political costs for both sides remain high. Analysts at Macquarie characterize a return to a JCPOA-like arrangement as the only "modestly rational" outcome, while BCA Research notes that continued economic resilience — underpinned by the AI investment cycle — paradoxically reduces the pressure on either party to compromise. The Trump-Xi summit remains scheduled for May 14-15 in Beijing, with trade, Taiwan, and Iran all on the agenda. The US has also become the world's largest crude exporter by volume, shipping more than 250 million barrels in the past nine weeks to help offset the supply shortfall — though US domestic inventories are drawing down rapidly, raising questions about the sustainability of that role.

  • US

    US equity markets are sustaining record highs even as the underlying economic picture grows increasingly bifurcated. The S&P 500 remains near all-time highs with volatility subdued, supported by a resurgence in the AI trade following strong earnings from Alphabet and other large-cap technology companies. That resilience is proving a strategic asset in the standoff over the Strait of Hormuz: financial conditions remain accommodative, undermining Iran's central weapon of economic coercion. However, the calm at the index level obscures genuine stress at the household level. Gas prices have exceeded $4 per gallon, and Pantheon Macro estimates the energy shock could translate into roughly a 1% annualized decline in real after-tax income in Q2. With the savings rate already at 3.9%, the buffer for lower-income households is thin. Bank of America estimates the full-year "gas tax" amounts to roughly 0.5% of GDP — approximately equal to the projected boost from either the One Big Beautiful Bill or AI capital expenditure. The K-shaped character of US consumption is becoming more pronounced. New York Fed data show retail spending concentrated among households earning above $125,000 annually, while consumer confidence has fallen to fresh lows. Spirit Airlines ceased operations, a pointed illustration of the energy shock's asymmetric impact on businesses serving lower-income consumers. The temporary income boost from tax refunds is now fading, leaving that cohort increasingly exposed. On the corporate front, GameStop has proposed a $56 billion all-cash-and-stock acquisition of eBay, offering a roughly 20% premium to Friday's close — a striking bid from a company with a $12 billion market capitalization. Separately, data-center startup Fermi ousted its CEO after failing to sign a single client despite a $19 billion IPO valuation, serving as a cautionary reminder that AI capital formation enthusiasm does not guarantee commercial execution. The April nonfarm payrolls report, due Friday, is expected to show approximately 62,000–65,000 jobs added, with the labor market yet to register the energy shock's full impact.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets climbed to fresh record highs, led by AI-linked semiconductor names in South Korea and Taiwan, whose benchmark indices surged 5.1% and 4.6% respectively. The rally reflects strong earnings momentum from chipmakers supplying the global AI infrastructure build-out, and appears to be shrugging off near-term headwinds from the Hormuz disruption. Morgan Stanley's chief Asia economist Chetan Ahya has characterized the dynamic as the beginning of a multi-year industrial super-cycle, arguing that structural forces — AI infrastructure, energy transition, and defense spending — will broaden from a narrow semiconductor rally into wider capital expenditure, job creation, and ultimately consumption growth across the region. Taiwan's first-quarter GDP expansion and South Korea's export boom are cited as early evidence. The cycle is expected to last three to four years, with Taiwan, South Korea, China, and Japan identified as the primary beneficiaries. China's posture on US sanctions hardened materially over the weekend. Beijing ordered its companies to disregard US secondary sanctions — an explicit act of defiance that represents a meaningful departure from its historically cautious approach of quiet compliance. The immediate trigger was Washington's decision to sanction Hengli Petrochemical, one of China's largest private refiners, over Iranian crude purchases. Energy security appears to be the key threshold: private refiners now account for roughly a third of China's processing capacity and have been integral to maintaining fuel supply during the Hormuz disruption. The directive likely signals that Beijing views energy access as a non-negotiable red line ahead of the Trump-Xi summit expected in approximately ten days. Japan's Ministry of Finance intervened in foreign exchange markets, spending approximately $34 billion to buy yen after the currency breached ¥160 against the dollar following the Federal Reserve's meeting last week. The intervention produced the yen's largest single-day gain since 2022. However, analysts are skeptical of its durability given that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is perceived as holding a structurally dovish fiscal and monetary stance, the Bank of Japan remains cautious about committing to near-term rate hikes, and Japan's dependence on Hormuz-transiting oil continues to weigh on the currency structurally. The Bank of Korea, meanwhile, signaled that rate increases may be warranted, citing stronger-than-expected economic resilience and mounting inflationary pressures.

    Europe

    European assets continued to underperform relative to US markets, a divergence that reflects the continent's greater structural exposure to the Strait of Hormuz disruption given its reliance on Middle Eastern energy flows. Euro Stoxx 50 futures fell 1.7%, in contrast to modest gains in US and Asian futures. That vulnerability has been compounded by two fresh US actions: President Trump announced a tariff increase on European automobiles to 25%, and the Pentagon confirmed a drawdown of US troops from Germany beyond the initial 5,000 previously disclosed. Both moves appear connected to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz's public criticism of US Iran policy. The European Commission signaled it is prepared to respond to the auto tariff threat, though the much-delayed US-EU trade deal — initially reached last July but still unratified by EU lawmakers — remains a complicating backdrop. The diplomatic calendar offered some counterweight. Dozens of European leaders convened in Yerevan, Armenia, for the European Political Community summit, with the gathering underscoring the EU's push to expand its strategic footprint in the Caucasus — a region that offers an alternative trade corridor bypassing Russia. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced Britain's intention to join the EU's €90 billion Ukraine loan plan, a notable step in post-Brexit alignment on European security matters. NATO Secretary-General Rutte and Canadian Prime Minister Carney also attended, the latter marking the first non-European leader at such a summit. In Swiss financial regulation, lawmakers convened to begin a legislative process that could impose up to $20 billion in additional capital requirements on UBS — a proceeding with significant implications for the institution's capital allocation and European banking sector sentiment. UK local elections on May 7 are expected to reveal further erosion of support for both Labour and the Conservatives, with polling suggesting Nigel Farage's Reform UK and other insurgent parties stand to benefit.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz remains the central fault line in global markets. Three weeks into the double-blockade, President Trump announced "Project Freedom," a plan to guide neutral vessels through the waterway — but the initiative has left shipping executives confused given a lack of operational detail and the US Navy's stated non-involvement. The head of Iran's parliamentary National Security Commission characterized any US interference as a violation of the existing ceasefire. Oil prices fluctuated sharply on the mixed signals, with WTI touching $106 per barrel in some trading windows. Brent futures for end-2026 reached new highs, and 2027 contracts remain elevated, suggesting markets are not pricing a near-term resolution. Global LNG exports fell to their lowest level in nearly two years in April, while Dubai International Airport reported a 66% year-on-year drop in March passenger traffic. The US has emerged as the world's largest crude exporter once again, shipping over 250 million barrels in the past nine weeks to compensate for constrained Middle Eastern supply. Analysts are beginning to flag limits to this cushion. OPEC+ major producers agreed on a modest symbolic increase of 188,000 barrels per day for June, largely a signaling exercise following the UAE's surprise exit from the bloc. Abu Dhabi's ADNOC simultaneously announced $55 billion in accelerated project awards. The geopolitical contest for influence in the Caucasus is intensifying, with the US signing a 99-year agreement with Armenia for management of a transit corridor linking Azerbaijan to Nakhchivan — a move that directly challenges Russian influence in a region Moscow has historically dominated. Turkey, Iran, and the EU are also asserting competing interests. For middle powers globally, the dilemma is acute: the Hormuz shock is delivering an immediate, visible economic hit through energy prices, inflation, and disrupted supply chains, while the longer-run challenge of Chinese industrial overcapacity feels slower and more diffuse — even as Beijing's sanctions defiance raises the stakes of the structural US-China contest ahead of their anticipated summit.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    India's trade policy underwent a notable shift this week, with New Delhi signing a free trade agreement with New Zealand — the latest in a sequence of deals that collectively represent a meaningful departure from the protectionist posture Prime Minister Modi embraced between roughly 2017 and 2020. During that period, India's average tariff rate rose from approximately 13% to 18%, accompanied by an expansion of quality control orders that functioned as non-tariff barriers directed primarily at Chinese imports. The strategic logic at the time rested on nurturing domestic demand and building national champions through protection and production incentives. That template now appears to be under revision. The New Zealand agreement follows what negotiators have described as a landmark deal with the European Union — ending roughly two decades of intermittent talks — as well as a trade agreement with the UK concluded last July. Negotiations on an interim arrangement with the United States remain ongoing. The proximate catalyst for the pivot appears to be the pressure generated by the Trump administration's own protectionist turn, which has made securing alternative market access more urgent. But policymakers also appear to have absorbed a structural lesson: manufacturing's share of Indian GDP has declined from 16% to 13% over the past decade, and export performance has consistently correlated with India's strongest growth periods. The recalibration has practical limits worth noting. India has not moved to reduce most-favored-nation tariff rates broadly, retaining concerns about Chinese overcapacity and a widening bilateral trade deficit with Beijing. Quality control orders are being scaled back selectively rather than dismantled. Agriculture and dairy remain shielded. And structural constraints — unpredictable tax enforcement, slow land and labor reform, regulatory complexity — remain unaddressed by trade agreements alone. Whether the current liberalization momentum is durable or proves contingent on external pressure remains an open question.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    Two cross-cutting themes warrant attention this week. First, India's trade policy shift — detailed in the Asia section — carries global implications that extend beyond the bilateral deals themselves. In a world where the United States has retreated from its role as an open, anchor market, advanced economies in Europe, Australasia, and potentially elsewhere face renewed incentive to deepen commercial ties with large emerging markets. India's historical underperformance as an exporter means its export capacity can expand substantially without immediately displacing incumbent suppliers, a feature that makes it a relatively low-friction partner for trade liberalization. The EU deal in particular may signal a broader reorientation of global trade architecture away from the US-centric model that prevailed for much of the post-war era. Second, climate science is producing increasingly granular evidence of how global warming is altering seasonal weather patterns in ways that carry economic consequences beyond headline temperature records. Research published in Nature Communications found that more than 60% of the globe has experienced more frequent, intense, and rapid temperature reversals since 1961. Separate work projects that extreme day-to-day temperature changes could grow in frequency and intensity by approximately 20% this century. The mechanisms identified include earlier seasonal warming, a more thermally active Gulf of Mexico driving warm air northward, and a wobblier jet stream permitting greater intrusion of Arctic air into temperate latitudes. For investors, the relevance extends to agricultural commodity markets — an intense drought in the US Great Plains is currently threatening wheat harvests — as well as to insurance, retail seasonality, and infrastructure resilience. These effects are incremental rather than abrupt, but the cumulative drift in weather volatility appears to be accelerating.

  • US

    Apple delivered a stronger-than-expected revenue forecast for the third quarter, sending shares up roughly 2.8% in early trading. The company acknowledged headwinds — higher memory-chip costs and persistent Mac shortages — but the guidance nonetheless represented the standout positive earnings event of the week. Alphabet's blowout results the prior session added $421 billion to its market capitalization in a single day, the second-largest one-day market cap gain on record for any stock. Reddit climbed 16% on a sales beat, while Atlassian rallied 24% after results eased concerns about AI's disruptive impact on enterprise software. On the other side, Roblox tumbled 22% after safety features for children constrained user growth, and Sandisk and Western Digital fell sharply despite a strong run year-to-date. Equity futures were broadly steady, suggesting a natural pause following what appears to have been the best monthly performance for US stocks since late 2020. In credit markets, investors are showing early signs of fatigue after a $300 billion AI-related debt binge, with demands emerging for better pricing and stronger covenant protections. On monetary policy, the departure of Stephen Miran — who cast the lone public vote for a rate cut across the entire G-7 this week — highlights the unusual position the US may soon occupy. With Kevin Warsh expected to assume the Fed chair role, the prospect of continued dovish pressure from the White House sits in direct tension with inflation dynamics being stoked by the Iran conflict and elevated energy prices. Fitch separately warned that the US debt burden remains "far above" other AA-rated sovereigns, a reminder that fiscal vulnerabilities persist even as near-term growth holds. The S&P Dow Jones Indices consultation on accelerating index inclusion for mega-cap IPOs drew attention, with SpaceX cited as a potential beneficiary that could join the S&P 500 in six months rather than twelve.

    Asia

    Japan intervened in foreign-exchange markets for the first time since 2024, purchasing yen and selling dollars after issuing a "final" warning to investors. The move triggered an intraday strengthening of as much as 0.7%, with dollar-yen reaching 155.5 before partially retracing to around 156.5. Analysts at Rabobank cautioned that intervention can stabilize markets only briefly, noting that Japan's underlying structural dependence on imported energy — now made substantially more expensive by the Strait of Hormuz disruption — continues to exert downward pressure on the currency. The Bank of Japan's recent policy meeting produced a split vote, with dissenting members favoring an outright rate hike, raising the probability of a June move. Japan's top currency official also indicated readiness to intervene in crude oil futures markets, where speculative positioning has been amplifying yen weakness, an unusual extension of currency-management posture into commodity markets. South Korea's export data for April showed continued strength, with semiconductor demand anchoring growth despite the mounting cost pressures associated with elevated energy prices. The resilience of Korean tech exports provides some offset to the broader regional drag from the Iran conflict. In China, an IMF official noted nascent signs of inflation returning as energy costs rise, though the official cautioned that more durable price gains are needed to fully reverse entrenched deflationary pressures. China's upscale automotive brands, including BYD's luxury segment, face pointed criticism for pricing strategies in European markets — charging multiples of domestic prices — that appear poorly calibrated for competitive penetration. Taiwan's main opposition leader, Kuomintang chair Cheng Li-wun, sought a meeting with President Trump during a planned US visit, positioning herself as a potential channel for dialogue with Beijing — a rare move that risks complicating cross-strait dynamics. India's crisis deserves separate attention: every one of the world's 50 hottest cities this week was located in India, with temperatures exceeding 40°C across densely populated states. The combined pressure of an unusually severe pre-monsoon heatwave and energy supply disruptions linked to the Iran war is straining power grids, with the McKinsey Global Institute estimating heat-related productivity losses could reach $250 billion. Monsoon relief is not expected until June.

    Europe

    The week's most consequential European development may be the ECB's unmistakable pivot toward a June rate hike. President Christine Lagarde disclosed that policymakers debated an immediate increase at Thursday's meeting but concluded they had "insufficient information" to act — language that, according to people familiar with deliberations, implies only a narrow path to avoiding a hike in June if hostilities at the Strait of Hormuz persist. Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel separately indicated he favors a June move absent a material improvement in the outlook. The Bank of England delivered a similarly hawkish signal: Chief Economist Huw Pill voted for an immediate increase, and Governor Andrew Bailey warned that waiting for second-round inflation effects to materialize before acting would mean responding "too late." Markets are pricing a June hike as the base case for both institutions. The White House's Kevin Hassett publicly characterized potential ECB or Fed rate hikes as "policy errors," an unusual intervention in the affairs of independent central banks. On UK trade, President Trump announced the removal of some Scotch whisky tariffs following King Charles III's visit to Washington — framed by Trump as a personal gesture to the monarch. Buckingham Palace expressed gratitude, and the concession represents a tangible, if narrowly scoped, trade gain for London. British officials privately expressed skepticism, however, that the royal visit would meaningfully alter Trump's underlying disposition toward Prime Minister Starmer or accelerate a broader UK-US trade agreement. The UK also raised its national terror threat level to "severe" following a stabbing in London's Golders Green district that left two Jewish men hospitalized. Prime Minister Starmer pledged to deploy the "full power of the state" against antisemitism. Separately, Italy's Meloni government announced housing-access measures ahead of next year's general election, while Spain's persistent child poverty — affecting roughly one-third of children, a share exceeded in the EU only by Bulgaria — drew renewed attention despite years of above-average economic growth.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz impasse remains the single most consequential variable in global markets. President Trump affirmed the US naval blockade of Iranian ports will continue, stating "their economy is crashing, the blockade is incredible." WTI crude rose to approximately $105 per barrel with Brent near $112, representing a 12% weekly gain for WTI. The forward curve tells a more nuanced story: oil for delivery later in the year trades closer to $80, reflecting market expectations that the disruption is unlikely to persist indefinitely — though the timeline for any resolution remains genuinely uncertain. Iranian officials warned the blockade will continue pushing prices higher, and traders are increasingly pricing in an extended closure rather than a near-term diplomatic breakthrough. The UAE's decision to exit OPEC adds a further structural dimension to the energy picture. The departure removes meaningful spare production capacity from the cartel's toolkit — the same firepower deployed in 2014 and 2020 to pressure higher-cost producers — while signaling that Abu Dhabi intends to pursue an independent, volume-oriented strategy once the Strait reopens. US shale producers, pumping near-record volumes of 13.6 million barrels per day, are well-positioned in the current price environment but are cautious about overextension given the forward curve's implied reversion. Exxon Mobil and Chevron both exceeded profit expectations, underpinned by elevated realized prices. Ukraine's strikes on Russian energy infrastructure reached their highest monthly level since December, with at least 21 attacks on refineries, export terminals, and pipelines in April. The campaign appears designed partly to limit Moscow's ability to benefit financially from the Iran war-driven oil price rally. Meanwhile, G-7 monetary policy is converging toward tightening across most members — with the notable potential exception of the US — as central banks in Europe, Japan, and Canada all signal that persistent energy-price inflation may require a policy response. Singapore's Prime Minister Lawrence Wong captured the broader unease, warning in a May Day address that "the pace of change will be faster than anything we have seen before."

  • US

    The Federal Reserve's April meeting, Jerome Powell's last as chair, produced the most fractious policy vote since October 1992, with four FOMC members dissenting — three regional presidents (Beth Hammack, Neel Kashkari, and Lorie Logan) objecting to any residual easing bias in the forward guidance, and Stephen Miran dissenting on separate grounds. Rates were left unchanged, as expected. The episode signals that incoming chair Kevin Warsh will inherit a committee disinclined to accommodate the rate cuts President Trump has sought: futures markets no longer price any easing in 2026, and traders now assign roughly 50% odds to a rate increase by April 2027. Two-year Treasury yields jumped 11 basis points on the day, the largest Fed-day move since January 2022, while 30-year yields reached 5% for the first time this year. Adding to the institutional complexity, Powell announced he will remain on the Fed board as a governor under Warsh until the Justice Department's investigation into the Fed's building renovation is "well and truly over." Treasury Secretary Bessent characterized the decision as a "violation of all Federal Reserve norms," though the parallel to Marriner Eccles — who remained as governor after leaving the chair — somewhat undercuts that framing. Powell's stated intention is to maintain a low profile and support the incoming chair, but the presence of a former chair with two years remaining on his board term, amid a deeply divided committee, will complicate Warsh's ability to quickly shift policy direction. On the corporate side, Wednesday's mega-cap earnings delivered a differentiated verdict. Alphabet surged more than 7% after reporting revenues and profits ahead of estimates, with its custom TPU chip architecture seen as a credible challenge to Nvidia's dominance in AI inference workloads. Meta fell roughly 7% in after-hours trade despite strong advertising revenue of $56.3 billion, after raising its 2026 capex guidance by $10 billion to potentially above $145 billion — a level investors deemed excessive relative to near-term returns. Amazon's cloud unit AWS posted 28% revenue growth, its fastest in over three years, and was received more favorably. Microsoft's Azure growth was viewed as underwhelming, with shares slipping modestly. The combined 2026 capex envelope for the four hyperscalers now approaches $725 billion, sustaining but not expanding AI-related enthusiasm.

    Asia

    China's economic data for April presented a sharply bifurcated picture. Manufacturing remained in expansion territory, supported by resilient export demand even as the Iran war drives up input costs. Services and construction, however, slipped back into contraction, with building activity registering its worst slump since the early stages of the pandemic. The divergence underscores a familiar structural tension: external demand is sustaining factory output while weak domestic consumption and a deepening property downturn weigh on the broader economy. How long export strength can carry overall growth, particularly if the energy shock worsens and global demand softens, remains an open question. Beijing's intervention in the Meta-Manus transaction represents a significant escalation in China's approach to technology governance. Chinese authorities have ordered the two companies to unwind a completed $2 billion acquisition within weeks and restore Manus's assets to their original state — an instruction whose practical feasibility is genuinely unclear. Manus co-founders have been barred from leaving China. The stated rationale centers on preventing the outflow of strategically important AI capabilities and talent to the United States. The broader signal, however, appears directed at domestic entrepreneurs: Chinese startups that restructure offshore to attract US capital and circumvent domestic regulatory review will face severe consequences. Several private firms have reportedly already been instructed by the NDRC to reject US-origin capital unless explicitly approved. The episode risks reinforcing perceptions of elevated political risk in China's technology sector, potentially complicating Beijing's simultaneous effort to attract international capital back to the private economy. China's role in global LNG markets is providing an inadvertent buffer for European buyers. Beijing's LNG imports are tracking roughly 1.3 million tons below year-earlier levels in April, enabled by years of investment in pipeline imports and domestic production capacity. However, domestic trucked LNG prices are rising in key industrial provinces, and the 30-day moving average of Chinese imports has begun to edge higher, suggesting demand destruction may be approaching its limits. The yen weakened toward levels that prompted intervention in 2024, with Japan's top currency officials issuing a "final" warning to speculators as the Bank of Japan maintains its wait-and-see posture amid the energy shock.

    Europe

    The eurozone economy unexpectedly decelerated at the start of 2026, with first-quarter GDP growth falling short of consensus estimates as surging energy costs — a direct consequence of the Strait of Hormuz blockade — weigh on industrial output and household purchasing power. The configuration increasingly resembles a stagflationary dynamic: energy-driven inflation pressures are building precisely as growth momentum fades. Germany, Italy, and Spain individually outperformed expectations, but the aggregate picture remains concerning, and the trajectory into the second quarter depends heavily on whether oil prices stabilize or continue to rise. The European Central Bank and the Bank of England both held rates steady at their respective April meetings, in line with consensus expectations. The policy stance represents a holding pattern rather than a clear directional signal: both institutions face a genuine dilemma between responding to accelerating energy inflation and cushioning economies that are more directly exposed to the Middle East conflict than the United States. Market pricing has shifted materially over recent months — traders now expect the ECB to begin hiking rates in June, with three increases priced in for the year, a dramatic reversal from February's expectations of unchanged rates throughout 2026. Whether that pricing holds will depend on how durable the oil price surge proves to be, and whether second-round inflation effects materialize in wage and services data. UK domestic politics present a separate source of uncertainty. Keir Starmer's Labour government is projected to lose approximately 1,850 seats in next week's local elections, according to expert analysis, a performance that would raise credible questions about the Prime Minister's political durability. Standard Chartered, BNP Paribas, and Société Générale each reported first-quarter results that exceeded expectations, offering some evidence that European financial institutions are navigating the energy shock more robustly than their equity valuations — which have underperformed the S&P 500 by roughly 9 percentage points since the Iran conflict began — might suggest.

    Global

    Brent crude briefly surpassed $126 per barrel on Thursday, its highest level since the aftermath of Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, after Axios reported that President Trump is scheduled to receive a briefing on new military options against Iran. The US Central Command chief is preparing fresh strike plans, suggesting the conflict may be approaching an inflection point rather than winding down. Trump separately confirmed to Axios that he will not lift the naval blockade of Iran's ports without a nuclear agreement, removing any near-term ambiguity about the conditions for Hormuz reopening. Polymarket assigns only 52% odds to the strait reopening by end-June, making a protracted blockade the probabilistic base case. The Pentagon's publicly stated $25 billion war cost estimate has drawn skepticism from analysts who argue it omits munitions replenishment, facility repair, and rising fuel costs for military operations themselves. The UAE's withdrawal from OPEC+ adds a structural dimension to energy market dynamics that extends beyond near-term price moves. The departure reflects a broader regional realignment: Abu Dhabi's posture has increasingly diverged from Riyadh's across multiple dimensions — its alliance with Israel, its commercial ambitions in Africa, and now its willingness to challenge the cartel's production architecture. Reports that the UAE is reconsidering its participation in the Arab League, the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, and potentially the Gulf Cooperation Council suggest a durable reorientation rather than a tactical maneuver. A Turkey-Saudi axis may emerge as a counterweight, with implications for the region's post-conflict order. The Hormuz closure is producing second-order logistics adaptations. Container throughput at Khor Fakkan — a UAE port outside the strait — has surged, with truck volumes rising from roughly 100 per day pre-conflict to approximately 6,000 currently. Jebel Ali's container vessel arrivals have fallen from 12 per day to approximately one. US crude exports meanwhile reached a record above 6 million barrels per day as importing nations seek alternatives to Middle Eastern supply. China's top oil producer has pledged an all-out domestic supply effort, leaning on stockpiles, coal, and electrification, but cracks are visible in refinery runs and industrial power costs. Gold has fallen roughly 13% since the conflict began, as markets price the inflation consequences of elevated energy costs into rate expectations — a reminder that the war's financial transmission channels remain complex and, in some respects, counterintuitive.

  • US

    Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's final FOMC press conference headlines a consequential afternoon for markets, with the rate decision arriving concurrently with first-quarter results from Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon — the combined weight of these events representing a significant near-term test for equity valuations. Ahead of the decision, concern over whether AI capital spending is translating into returns briefly dragged the Nasdaq 100 down 1% after a report suggested OpenAI missed internal targets for user acquisition and sales, though the company pushed back on that characterization. Tech sector earnings are nonetheless expected to have grown roughly 41% in the first quarter — the strongest gain among all S&P 500 sectors — providing some fundamental underpinning to recent market resilience. On fiscal policy, key congressional Republicans appear poised to break with the White House over a proposed 44% increase to the Pentagon budget, a rare act of intra-party defiance that may signal the administration's softening grip as midterm elections approach. Separately, Republican lawmakers are also pushing to lower taxes on capital gains. Both dynamics complicate the fiscal arithmetic at a moment when 30-year Treasury yields are pressing toward 5%, with bond traders pricing the inflationary consequences of sustained elevated oil prices. Corporate news was broadly constructive. NXP Semiconductors surged 16% premarket on an upbeat revenue forecast, suggesting the auto semiconductor cycle may be turning. Seagate rallied 18% on strong AI-related storage demand. Starbucks gained after raising its sales outlook. On the downside, Robinhood fell nearly 10% after revenue missed estimates and management flagged an additional $100 million investment requirement for its "Trump account" initiative. Brown-Forman and Pernod Ricard abruptly ended merger talks, removing a potential transatlantic deal from the table. Meanwhile, Kearney's annual Reshoring Index remained in negative territory for 2025, suggesting that while tariffs have redirected some sourcing away from China, a meaningful revival of domestic manufacturing capacity remains years away.

    Asia

    The Bank of Japan delivered a "hawkish hold" at its April meeting, keeping rates unchanged on a 6-3 vote — a record number of dissents — while signaling its intention to raise rates further, likely in June, contingent on avoiding serious escalation in the Middle East. Despite the explicit forward guidance, the yen remains stubbornly weak near 160 per dollar, reflecting persistent investor skepticism about how far normalization can proceed under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, who has historically favored a weak yen to support exporters and appointed two dovish committee members last month. Takaichi recently criticized a trade minister for suggesting rate hikes could strengthen the currency, keeping political pressure on the BOJ visible. Spring Shunto wage negotiations produced a third consecutive year of approximately 5% wage increases, and five-year inflation breakevens have reached 2.7%, suggesting bond markets believe the deflationary era is genuinely over — yet the yen's weakness persists, creating a tension between the data and market pricing. In China, regulators have suspended the issuance of new autonomous vehicle licenses after dozens of Baidu Apollo Go robotaxis abruptly stopped operating in Wuhan last month, stranding passengers. Authorities called for a full safety review by local governments, marking a meaningful setback for an industry estimated to reach 83 billion yuan by 2030. Goldman Sachs staff in Hong Kong have separately lost access to Anthropic's Claude AI model, a location-specific restriction affecting primarily software engineers. Hong Kong Exchanges reported record first-quarter net income of HK$5.2 billion, up 27%, driven by a surge in IPOs and robust trading volumes. Hong Kong also ran its widest trade deficit in at least 74 years, a likely spillover from Hormuz-related supply disruptions. In India, major airlines warned of potential service suspensions unless the government reduces jet fuel prices, with the Federation of Indian Airlines describing the sector as "on the verge of closing down." As the world's third-largest oil consumer, India is absorbing disproportionate pressure from the Hormuz closure, with Brent trading near $111 a barrel following a 2.8% gain on Tuesday.

    Europe

    European bank earnings delivered broadly constructive results. UBS reported first-quarter net income of $3.04 billion, beating all estimates, with revenue rising nearly 30% driven by equities and foreign exchange — shares jumped 4.3% in Zurich and management confirmed intentions to add to its $3 billion share buyback program. Deutsche Bank posted higher-than-expected profit and revenue, supported by trading income and stronger contributions from asset management and retail banking. Banco Santander's profit also beat expectations, with its US expansion — anchored by the recent Webster Financial acquisition — providing incremental support. Adidas gained 8.3% in Frankfurt on strong apparel momentum. In the UK, a notable split-screen moment unfolded: King Charles III addressed the US Congress with an unusually direct appeal to reject isolationism, support NATO, and maintain the Ukraine commitment — remarkable in its pointed political content for a constitutional monarch. Simultaneously, Prime Minister Keir Starmer survived a parliamentary vote related to the Peter Mandelson appointment controversy, underscoring how managing the relationship with Washington continues to consume significant bandwidth in Downing Street. A UK research group separately warned that a deepening Middle East crisis could tip the UK into recession and force aggressive rate hikes, a tail risk that may be underpriced given the country's energy import exposure. In the Netherlands, the government is advancing a plan to impose a 36% annual tax on unrealized gains from stocks, bonds, and cryptocurrencies, effective 2028. Critics warn of capital flight and reduced domestic investment appetite. The proposal adds the Netherlands to a growing list of Western governments pursuing wealth-tax measures, though the political and economic pushback appears more acute given the country's historical reputation for stable economic policymaking. Separately, the EU is considering adding conditionality to its €90 billion Ukraine loan package, linking some disbursements to an unpopular business tax reform.

    Global

    The most consequential development of the day is the US decision to prepare for an extended naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, with President Trump reportedly concluding this represents a lower-risk path than either resumed bombing or disengagement. The implication is a prolonged period of neither active hostilities nor resolution — a structural supply shock rather than an acute one. Brent traded above $114 a barrel, with December futures reaching new highs, signaling that markets are now pricing the blockade as a durable condition rather than a temporary disruption. The US also threatened secondary sanctions on banks supporting Chinese private refiners purchasing Iranian oil, adding financial pressure on Beijing at a moment when the planned Trump-Xi summit in May was already navigating tensions over technology and trade. The UAE's shock exit from OPEC after 60 years adds a further destabilizing dimension to energy markets. The decision appears driven less by frustration with Saudi Arabia's near-term output policy — Riyadh has itself embraced looser supply management recently — than by a strategic imperative to maximize production before the energy transition erodes long-run demand. Abu Dhabi's urgency to pump reflects a view that the demand plateau may arrive within the next decade, undermining OPEC's core price-defense mechanism. The departure blindsided other members and raises questions about the cartel's long-term cohesion. Chip supply chains are emerging as an underappreciated casualty of the conflict. PGMEA, a photoresist chemical derived from naphtha and critical to semiconductor fabrication, has become harder to source since the war began. Shin-Etsu Chemical, Japan's largest chemical company, withheld its full-year guidance, citing naphtha-derived supply constraints; its most recent quarterly operating income fell 13% and missed estimates. Analysts note that photoresist disruptions take months to propagate through complex supply chains and are difficult to reverse — though for now, the broader semiconductor industry appears to have sufficient inventory buffers to absorb near-term pressure. The Bloomberg agriculture spot index has also hit a two-year high, up 13% over three months, as fertilizer supply disruptions and harvest uncertainty compound food price pressures globally.

  • US

    US equity markets enter a pivotal stretch of earnings season with the fundamental picture remaining broadly constructive, even as the geopolitical backdrop grows more complex. With slightly over a third of S&P 500 companies having reported first-quarter results, the beat rate stands at approximately 80%, above the historical average of 77%, while the aggregate earnings surprise of roughly 12% materially exceeds the typical 7% premium. Societe Generale estimates that 12-month forward profits are now approximately $600 billion higher than four months ago — a 12% revision that would represent the largest upward revision on record outside a post-recession recovery. The concentration of those gains, however, is notable: semiconductors alone account for some 43% of the 2026 consensus profit upgrade, with IT hardware and energy together explaining close to 98% of the extraordinary revision. Wednesday's hyperscaler earnings reports from Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon will be consequential. Ahead of those results, shares of SoftBank fell sharply in Tokyo following a Wall Street Street Journal report that OpenAI recently missed its internal targets for new users and sales, dragging down correlated names including Oracle, CoreWeave, and AMD in pre-market trading. Microsoft and OpenAI also formally ended their exclusivity arrangement, a development that may reshape the competitive AI partnership landscape. The Federal Reserve's policy meeting, which begins today, is widely expected to produce a hold. The decision itself may be less consequential than the tone adopted by Chair Powell in his subsequent press conference, given the currency implications for the yen and the broader signal it would send about the Fed's tolerance for elevated inflation driven by the oil shock. Analysts from BCA Research note that while the US economy's structural tendency supports earnings growth, projecting continued 20% profit expansion heading into year seven of the cycle requires assumptions that have no modern precedent, AI tailwinds notwithstanding. Credit markets remain supportive, with investment-grade and high-yield spreads near their tightest since the Global Financial Crisis.

    Asia

    The Bank of Japan delivered what markets are characterizing as a hawkish hold, keeping its benchmark rate unchanged at 0.75% in a 6-3 vote — the most divided outcome under Governor Ueda's tenure. Three board members, including the usually consensus-leaning Junko Nakagawa, voted to raise rates immediately, and Bloomberg Economics now assigns high probability to a hike at the June meeting. The yen briefly strengthened through 159 per dollar before reversing as Ueda's press conference introduced caveats around the bank's baseline outlook and dollar buying resumed alongside rising oil prices. The currency's near-term trajectory now depends substantially on the tone of Powell's Wednesday remarks; a hawkish signal could push the yen through the psychologically significant 160 level. Political constraints on the BOJ remain meaningful. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's administration — aligned with the late Shinzo Abe's reflation legacy — has appointed two dovish board members, with a second joining in June, complicating the central bank's normalization path even as inflationary pressures from energy costs and a weak exchange rate accumulate. South Korea's equity market has emerged as the year's strongest performer among major indices, with the Kospi rising approximately 60% and total market capitalization surpassing the UK's at $4.04 trillion. Samsung Electronics reported a 755% year-on-year profit increase in the March quarter, and Samsung and SK Hynix together now represent 42% of Kospi capitalization, up from 28% two years ago — a concentration that amplifies both momentum and tail risk. Labor tensions are building: thousands of Samsung employees rallied over the weekend demanding higher wages, with the union threatening an 18-day strike beginning May 21. China's Politburo pledged to counter external shocks and enhance energy security, citing better-than-expected first-quarter growth. Moody's upgraded China's credit outlook to stable. Separately, Beijing is pressing Meta to unwind its $2 billion acquisition of AI startup Manus, a degree of extraterritorial commercial intervention that, if executed, would represent a significant escalation in China's assertiveness over cross-border technology deals.

    Europe

    European equity markets are finding limited upside as the war-driven energy shock intersects with deteriorating growth prospects. Euro-area consumer inflation expectations jumped across the board in March, complicating the ECB's policy calculus at a moment when the region is simultaneously absorbing higher energy costs, weak demand, and growing recession risk.
