Analysts note that European stocks' brief period of outperformance relative to the US has faded, with stronger US growth and technology-driven market gains pulling capital away from the region. UK politics remains turbulent. Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces a parliamentary vote today on whether to open a formal investigation into his assurances that due process was followed in appointing Peter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington. While Starmer's allies expect to win the vote, the prolonged controversy — which has run alongside several hours of committee hearings on Mandelson's security vetting — constitutes a costly distraction less than two weeks before local elections seen as a meaningful test of his leadership. King Charles III has arrived in Washington for a state visit, a trip freighted with uncertainty about the durability of the UK-US special relationship under the current US administration. On corporate earnings, BP reported adjusted net income of $3.2 billion in the first quarter, more than double the year-earlier figure and ahead of the $2.64 billion analyst consensus, as surging oil prices and market volatility drove strong oil trading results. Barclays' trading division, by contrast, failed to match the outsized revenues generated by Wall Street peers during the same period of elevated volatility. In European M&A, EssilorLuxottica heir Leonardo Maria Del Vecchio agreed to buy out two siblings' stakes in family holding company Delfin for approximately €10 billion. UK Lords separately called for a substantially larger fiscal headroom, adding to Starmer's domestic policy pressures.

    Global

    Oil markets continued to tighten on Tuesday, with Brent crude rising as much as 2.9% to $111.32 per barrel — a seventh consecutive day of gains and the highest level in several weeks. The proximate driver remains the US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, now approaching two months in duration. Analytics firm Kpler estimates Iran has 12 to 22 days of remaining storage capacity at current production rates before it must begin curtailing output, while US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent indicated some well shutdowns may have already begun. Six to eight supertankers are clustered near Chabahar port in the Gulf of Oman, just outside the blockade perimeter. A single LNG tanker appeared to traverse the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, the first such transit since the conflict began, though it remains unclear whether this represents a durable development or an isolated incident. Diplomatic activity continues without resolution. Iran submitted a peace proposal that the White House confirmed is under active consideration, though US officials described persistent red lines around Tehran's nuclear program. Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi completed a diplomatic tour that included a meeting with Vladimir Putin in St. Petersburg — a signal not lost on Washington. The contrast with the 2015 nuclear negotiations is sharp: those talks involved career diplomats, multilateral frameworks, and exhaustive document review; the current process is being managed by figures whose backgrounds are in real estate, and has been disrupted twice by military strikes. The economic ramifications are broadening. Oxford Economics estimates global goods trade will expand just 2.5% in 2026, roughly half the 4.9% pace recorded in 2025, with the oil shock alone potentially subtracting 0.6% to 0.9% from world trade volumes. A Dallas Fed survey found 35% of Texas manufacturers are already feeling direct negative impact from the war. Partially offsetting the macro drag, the oil shock is accelerating EV adoption: European consumers purchased 206,200 electric vehicles in March, a 44% year-on-year increase, while South Korean EV sales more than doubled. Chinese EV and hybrid exports reached a record, rising 140% from the prior year — a dynamic that simultaneously reflects the energy crisis and China's growing competitive position in global auto markets. BlackRock Investment Institute warned that elevated inflation driven by the oil shock means long-term government bond yields are likely to remain structurally higher, and that traditional equity-bond diversification relationships may no longer hold.

  • US

    The most consequential domestic development heading into this week is the resolution of the Fed leadership impasse. Senator Thom Tillis dropped his blockade of Kevin Warsh's nomination to chair the Federal Reserve after the Justice Department ended its criminal investigation of incumbent Chair Jerome Powell. The episode illustrates how institutional guardrails, however imperfect, retained enough force to produce an orderly outcome. Warsh, who carries both Wall Street and Fed credentials, is widely viewed as the most market-compatible nominee the administration could credibly advance. The remaining uncertainty concerns Powell's own intentions. His term as chair expires next month, but his governor's seat runs two more years. Whether he elects to remain as a governor — a decision that now appears less certain given the collapse of the legal pressure campaign against him — is likely to be the most closely watched subtext at Wednesday's FOMC meeting. The meeting itself is broadly expected to produce an unchanged rate decision, though markets will parse the statement for signals that sustained energy cost pressures are shifting the committee's reaction function. Equity markets enter the week at record highs, but the composition of the rally warrants scrutiny. The Philadelphia Semiconductor index has gained 47% over 18 consecutive positive sessions since markets perceived a thaw in the Iranian standoff — the longest winning streak in the index's history. At a trailing price-to-earnings multiple of roughly 60 times, valuations are elevated and appear to price in substantial margin expansion premised on chipmakers' pricing power over hyperscaler customers. That thesis faces a near-term test: Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft all report Wednesday, with Apple following Thursday. If any of these companies signal restraint in capital expenditure on chips, the feedback loop sustaining the semiconductor rally could weaken. University of Michigan consumer sentiment fell to 49.8 in April, its lowest reading since data collection began in 1978, suggesting the macro backdrop outside of equity markets remains more fragile than headline index levels imply.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets advanced approximately 1.2% on Monday following an Axios report indicating Iran had transmitted a new proposal to Pakistan — the primary mediator — suggesting willingness to reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for a lifting of the US port blockade, with nuclear negotiations deferred to a later phase. MSCI's emerging markets index reached a record level, and the regional technology gauge surged 3.3% to an all-time high, reflecting the same AI-driven semiconductor enthusiasm driving US markets. Taiwanese and South Korean indices, both heavily weighted toward chipmakers, have materially outperformed the US market over the eighteen months since the 2024 election. China featured prominently across several distinct developments. Beijing blocked Meta's $2 billion acquisition of agentic AI startup Manus, citing concerns over technology leakage to the US. The decision comes as Beijing tightens oversight of domestic AI firms and signals continued wariness about foreign acquisition of strategic technology assets. Separately, China's tax authorities launched a sweeping crackdown on circular invoicing practices in the metals trading sector, rattling copper, aluminum, and silver traders in Shanghai who rely on such invoices for bank financing. The State Taxation Administration framed the investigation as targeting fraudulent activity that has artificially inflated economic output measures. Economists have also sharply upgraded forecasts for China's import growth, now expecting it to surpass export expansion for the first time since 2021 — a development that, if sustained, could moderate political pressure over the bilateral trade balance. The US sanctioning of Hengli Petrochemical's Dalian refinery, one of China's largest private "teapot" operators, marks an escalation in Washington's effort to interdict Iranian oil revenues. Beijing's likely response — condemning the sanctions while maintaining de facto tolerance of private-sector flows — represents a calibrated posture that avoids a direct confrontation while protecting China's energy security interests. How Beijing responds formally will be watched ahead of an anticipated Trump-Xi meeting next month. In India, Sun Pharmaceutical agreed to acquire Organon for approximately $11.75 billion on an enterprise basis, marking one of the largest outbound acquisitions in Indian corporate history.

    Europe

    Germany's economic outlook continues to deteriorate, with the government last week halving its 2026 growth forecast to 0.5% — a significant downward revision from projections made when Chancellor Merz took office promising a "year of growth." The Iran war's disruption to energy markets is the proximate cause, though Germany entered 2026 already burdened by structural competitiveness challenges. The Ifo Institute's business expectations index fell to its lowest level since 2023, while eurozone PMI data slipped into contraction territory with delivery times and input price inflation rising at their fastest pace since 2022 — consistent with mounting stagflationary pressure. Thursday's first-quarter GDP release will offer an early quantitative read on the damage, though economists expect the full impact to emerge in subsequent quarters given that hostilities began mid-period. Germany's €500 billion infrastructure stimulus fund may be the primary buffer preventing outright contraction, per Ifo President Clemens Fuest. King Charles III arrived in Washington Monday for a four-day state visit, including bilateral meetings with President Trump, a White House banquet, and an address to a joint session of Congress. The visit comes as UK-US relations are under strain, against the backdrop of a weekend shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner. Prime Minister Starmer faces his own domestic pressures: Labour appears set to lose control of the Welsh Parliament for the first time since its formation in 1999, with May 7 local election results expected to be broadly unfavorable for the government. In European corporate and financial developments, Germany was reported to have sounded out European banks earlier this year to serve as potential white knights in defense of Commerzbank against UniCredit's ongoing takeover approach. Bayer is seeking a favorable Supreme Court ruling today that could limit the scope of Roundup weedkiller litigation that has cost the company more than $10 billion. S&P cut credit ratings for Belgium and Slovakia and placed Finland on negative watch, reflecting fiscal deterioration partly linked to elevated energy costs. Lufthansa has cancelled approximately 20,000 flights over the next six months in response to the surge in jet fuel prices — an early and visible sign of the war's operational consequences for European aviation.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively impassable, with Polymarket odds placing the probability of a full reopening by end-April at zero and end-of-May odds also unfavorable — implying the world's most critical oil artery could remain closed for a third consecutive month. Iran's Foreign Minister Araghchi traveled to Moscow on Monday to coordinate with President Putin after the weekend's second round of US-Iran peace talks failed to materialize despite Pakistani mediation efforts. Tehran's latest proposal — conveyed through Islamabad — calls for reopening the strait in exchange for a US lifting of its port blockade, with nuclear negotiations separated into a distinct later track. Washington's response remains to be seen. Oil markets reflect the unresolved standoff. Brent crude traded near $108 per barrel Monday, up roughly 2.5%, while WTI rose above $96. Goldman Sachs raised its fourth-quarter Brent forecast to $90 per barrel — up nearly $30 from pre-conflict projections — though spot prices remain elevated well above that level. December crude futures have settled near or above $80, suggesting markets are pricing a protracted disruption. The aviation sector is among the most acutely exposed: Delta Air Lines' fuel costs have doubled in a month, adding an estimated $2.5 billion to quarterly expenses. Lufthansa's 20,000-flight reduction is an early industry response; broader route cancellations appear likely if conditions persist into the peak summer travel season. The US escalated its campaign to isolate Iranian oil revenues by sanctioning Hengli Petrochemical's Dalian refinery, the first time Washington has targeted a major Chinese private refiner. The move is designed to raise the cost to Beijing of continued Iranian crude purchases, though China's likely response involves calibrated ambiguity rather than compliance. Separately, the US and EU announced an agreement to coordinate on critical minerals supply chains and reduce dependence on Chinese rare earths — a strategically significant step even if implementation details remain to be defined. The first international conference on transitioning away from fossil fuels convened in Santa Marta, Colombia, drawing more than 50 countries; the Iran war's disruption to energy markets has lent the gathering added urgency, though translating ambition into binding commitments remains the central challenge.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    No significant coverage for this period.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    China's tightening of oversight over US capital flows into its technology sector warrants attention, though details from the source material are limited. The move appears consistent with Beijing's broader effort to assert regulatory control over strategic industries and manage the composition of foreign investment in sensitive areas. Separately, Treasurer Bessent's signaling that Gulf and Asian nations are seeking dollar swap lines reflects continued demand for dollar liquidity mechanisms among emerging-market and commodity-exporting economies — a dynamic that, if formalized, would reinforce dollar primacy even as questions mount about US fiscal sustainability. India's Maldives are reported to be experiencing a financial-offshoring boom, suggesting capital continues to seek jurisdictions with favorable regulatory frameworks in the region. China's domestic solar industry faces capacity rationalization pressures, with Beijing reportedly moving to curb excess production as the sector grapples with structural overcapacity — a tension between industrial policy ambitions and near-term profitability that has broad implications for global clean-energy supply chains. Chinese companies meanwhile continue to dominate the EV battery supply chain globally, with competitive pressure from alternative chemistries remaining a medium-term rather than immediate threat to their market position.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    Two cross-cutting themes dominate this period. The first is the ongoing economic fallout from the US-Israel conflict with Iran, which is generating inflationary spillovers across multiple regions. Energy supply disruptions have raised fuel costs globally, including in remote Amazonian communities dependent on diesel, while central banks from Uruguay to elsewhere in the emerging-market world are navigating a more complex inflation environment as a result. Colombia is hosting fossil-fuel exit talks even as the energy crisis deepens — a genuine tension between energy security imperatives and decarbonization commitments that has no easy resolution. The second theme is a structural shift in emerging-market macro resilience. The experience of Latin American economies during the current episode illustrates how central bank credibility, built through proactive inflation-fighting during the pandemic cycle, has materially altered the transmission of external shocks. Currencies have proven more stable than historical precedent would suggest, and policymakers have retained space for counter-cyclical responses — a meaningful regime change relative to prior decades. The IMF has flagged that this dynamic partly reflects a relative deterioration in advanced-economy risk perception, with US Treasuries losing some of their traditional premium amid concerns over fiscal trajectories and questions about Federal Reserve institutional independence. The battery and energy storage theme carries genuine macro significance: Brazil's program to electrify isolated Amazon communities via solar and lithium storage represents a convergence of falling technology costs, federal subsidy rationalization, and emissions reduction, with the government projecting $171 million in subsidy savings and avoidance of 800,000 metric tons of greenhouse gas emissions by 2036. The Iran war's upward pressure on diesel prices has, counterintuitively, accelerated the economics of this transition.

  • US

    US equity markets maintained a broadly constructive tone on Friday, led by technology. Intel surged approximately 20% in premarket trading after delivering a blockbuster revenue forecast driven by AI infrastructure demand, with the stock on course to surpass its year-2000 record high. The Nasdaq 100 was on track for a fourth consecutive weekly gain. Semiconductor names benefited broadly, with AMD rising 7.2% and Arm up 6.8%. SAP's better-than-expected cloud revenue also lifted sentiment, reinforcing the narrative that AI spending is generating measurable earnings power across the technology supply chain. The macro backdrop, however, carries meaningful tension. Economists raised their US inflation forecasts and now pencil in just one Federal Reserve rate cut this year, citing elevated energy costs stemming from the Iran conflict. The Fed meets next week, and the Dallas Federal Reserve's survey of oil-and-gas firms — released Thursday as a special supplement — adds nuance to the growth picture. A majority of energy companies do not anticipate meaningful hiring or production increases despite crude prices trading above $90 per barrel for nearly a month. Executives cited extreme policy uncertainty, price volatility linked to administration social media posts, and an inability to plan capital budgets as reasons for restraint. The implication for the broader labor market is meaningful: if the energy sector fails to expand even as oil prices remain elevated, and non-energy sectors face headwinds from those same high prices, the net employment effect could be negative. Meta and Microsoft announced workforce reductions totaling as many as 23,000 positions, framed by markets as discipline around AI cost management rather than a demand signal. A US Army soldier was criminally charged with using classified intelligence on the timing of Nicolás Maduro's capture to trade on prediction markets, the first application of the so-called Eddie Murphy Rule by the CFTC. Goldman Sachs separately estimated US potential GDP growth at approximately 2.3% annually through 2028, driven by AI-related productivity gains of around 2% per year.

    Asia

    DeepSeek, the Hangzhou-based AI startup that triggered a significant market repricing in early 2025, unveiled preview versions of its V4 flagship model on Friday, introducing the V4 Flash and V4 Pro series. The company cited advances in coding benchmarks, reasoning, agentic capabilities, and a 1 million-token context window. Chinese chipmaker stocks rallied on expectations that the new model will support domestic semiconductor demand, a dynamic consistent with Beijing's broader push toward AI self-sufficiency. The announcement arrives roughly a year after DeepSeek's R1 model contributed to a sharp selloff in US technology stocks. Chinese regulators separately announced plans to restrict technology companies from accepting US capital without government approval, framed as a response to Meta's acquisition of startup Manus. The move reinforces a pattern of Beijing tightening control over strategic technology flows in both directions. On climate policy, the State Council published rules holding provincial officials directly accountable for meeting Xi Jinping's emissions targets, including carbon peaking before 2030 and clean energy consumption benchmarks. Authorities rated unsatisfactory face formal disciplinary processes; clean energy equities advanced on the news, with Huadian Energy gaining as much as 10%. TSMC shares rose as much as 5.1% to a record after Taiwan's Financial Supervisory Commission moved to relax a rule capping single-stock fund holdings at 10% of net asset value, a change JPMorgan estimated could draw more than $6 billion in inflows to the chipmaker. Tim Cook's announced departure as Apple CEO generated notable discussion on Chinese social media, reflecting his significant role in building China's technology supply chain and managing Sino-American commercial tensions. Indonesia's sovereign wealth fund Danantara confirmed it is in early-stage talks to acquire France's Eramet's 38.7% stake in PT Weda Bay Nickel, the world's largest nickel mine, as President Prabowo moves to consolidate natural resource assets under state control.

    Europe

    European equity markets edged lower, with the Stoxx Europe 600 down 0.8% even as technology-driven gains in the US provided some offset. The ECB is expected to hold policy steady until June, with Germany's business outlook deteriorating more than expected and the war-driven energy crisis complicating any straightforward easing trajectory. Forward electricity prices for the summer in major EU markets remain elevated year-on-year despite strong solar output and improved nuclear availability, as high natural gas costs keep a floor under power prices. Europe's airline sector faces a particularly acute challenge. Jet fuel equivalent to $196 per barrel has prompted Lufthansa to cancel 20,000 short-haul flights, KLM to eliminate 80 return services from Amsterdam, and EasyJet to warn of a potential first-half loss. Persian Gulf refineries that normally supply roughly 20% of EU jet fuel remain effectively cut off behind the Strait of Hormuz, and Asian refineries — constrained by reduced crude supply — have their own product shortages. India's Reliance has prioritized domestic LPG output over jet fuel exports, removing one alternative source. Shell's Pernis refinery in the Netherlands is maximizing jet fuel production, and Nigeria's Dangote refinery has raised shipments to Europe to record levels, but these measures appear insufficient to offset summer demand. The EU finalized disbursement of its €90 billion loan to Ukraine after Hungary lifted its veto, and European leaders signaled preliminary conditions are in place to begin Ukraine's formal EU membership process, though a committed timeline remains absent. Europe's ban on Russian LNG imports took effect Saturday, a poorly timed development given current energy market stress. SAP reported strong cloud revenue growth, gaining 7.3% in Frankfurt, while Siemens Energy rose 4.9% after raising its annual outlook on demand for gas turbines and grid infrastructure.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, with oil rising for a fifth consecutive day as no meaningful progress toward US-Iran de-escalation was apparent. A US-sanctioned supertanker, the Yuri, carrying 2 million barrels of Iranian crude from Kharg Island, was tracked sailing toward the strait on Friday, apparently testing Washington's resolve to maintain its naval blockade. Goldman Sachs estimated Persian Gulf oil output is running 14.5 million barrels per day below pre-war levels this month — roughly 57% below pre-conflict volumes — and assessed that any restoration would likely require several months following a full reopening of the strait and an absence of renewed strikes. President Trump announced a three-week extension of the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, which creates some space for diplomatic activity but falls well short of a broader resolution. Iranian negotiators, according to officials familiar with the matter, have grown less willing to engage in direct talks, citing Trump's social media posts as intended to humiliate Tehran's leadership rather than facilitate a deal. Pimco has privately extended more than $10 billion in loans to Gulf state-linked borrowers since the war began, a signal of the scale of wartime capital reallocation. Energy trader Pierre Andurand's largest hedge fund fell approximately 52% in the first half of April after bullish oil bets placed at the conflict's outset reversed. On the defense and technology dimensions of the broader geopolitical shift, Chinese defense contractors made their largest-ever showing at the Defence Services Asia exhibition in Kuala Lumpur, with 192 firms attending and drone and counter-drone technologies prominently featured. The event coincided with Japan lifting most restrictions on lethal weapons exports for the first time since World War II. The US unveiled measures targeting the practice of AI model distillation by Chinese developers. Xi Jinping's government also moved to exclude Taiwan's president from African airspace by pressuring island nations to revoke overflight permits. The US-China summit, tentatively planned for mid-May, is framed by Beijing as occurring on terms increasingly favorable to China, given Washington's perceived missteps in both the Iran conflict and the trade confrontation.

  • US

    US equity markets paused a record-breaking rally on Thursday as the Strait of Hormuz impasse deepened, with S&P 500 futures off roughly 0.5% and Brent crude pushing further above $100 per barrel. The pullback followed weeks of remarkable resilience: nearly 80% of S&P 500 companies reporting first-quarter results have beaten estimates, and realized volatility under the current administration has, counterintuitively, tracked levels seen during the relatively calm Biden years. Analysts at Barclays note that the market's equanimity in the face of sustained geopolitical stress mirrors the pattern seen through 2016-2018, which ultimately resolved in the sharp "Volmageddon" correction of February 2018 — a precedent worth monitoring as low-volatility conditions persist. Tesla delivered first-quarter earnings of $0.41 per share against a consensus of $0.34, but the beat failed to sustain an after-hours rally once management outlined plans to increase capital expenditure to $25 billion this year — $5 billion above prior guidance — in support of AI and robotics ambitions. Shares gave back gains as investors signaled a preference for clarity on the core automotive business over unquantified future ventures. Texas Instruments provided a sharper contrast, surging 12% after a stronger-than-expected forecast driven by data-center and industrial demand. ServiceNow fell 13% after cutting its margin outlook, citing deal delays attributable to the Middle East conflict. On monetary policy, Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh used his Senate confirmation hearing to signal interest in overhauling inflation measurement, expressing a preference for trimmed-mean gauges. The Dallas Fed's trimmed PCE currently reads 2.33% versus 3.0% for core PCE — a gap of 70 basis points that, if adopted as the policy benchmark, would materially shift the perceived distance from the Fed's 2% target. Strategists at BMO flagged the significance in the context of the Fed's current rate-hold posture. Wells Fargo economists cautioned, however, that over a full decade the trimmed measure has not painted a meaningfully different picture of underlying inflation trends. Consumer sentiment this month fell to an all-time low, with households increasingly concerned about war-driven inflation and gasoline prices approaching four-year highs — a divergence from equity market performance that several analysts describe as a growing tension for corporate earnings power.

    Asia

    South Korea delivered a strong first-quarter GDP print of 1.7% quarter-on-quarter, driven by AI-related export demand that powered semiconductor revenues. SK Hynix reported a five-fold jump in quarterly profit and reaffirmed plans to increase capital spending, underscoring the degree to which global AI infrastructure investment is supporting Korean export volumes even as energy price pressures and US tariff uncertainty weigh on broader sentiment. Hyundai, by contrast, missed earnings estimates, citing US tariff disruption, Iran war-related supply chain strain, and cooling demand in key export markets. Japan's cabinet this week approved legal changes allowing the country to export lethal military equipment — including missiles, fighter jets, and warships — for the first time since the post-war constitution enshrined pacifism. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is pursuing a strategic vision of greater defense self-sufficiency in response to rising threats from China and North Korea, with government funding earmarked for defense R&D and a deal to supply warships to Australia already concluded. China has issued warnings about revived Japanese militarism, while domestic polling shows 67% of Japanese respondents opposed to weapons exports — a political constraint that Takaichi appears willing to absorb given her current approval trajectory. Planned visits by Takaichi and her defense minister to Southeast Asian nations signal efforts to develop an early order pipeline. In China, an unexpected deterioration in the labor market has extended to early-career workers, with AI adoption cited as a contributing factor. Tencent and Alibaba are in preliminary discussions to participate in a maiden financing round for DeepSeek, which is expanding into agentic AI applications. PwC agreed to pay HK$1.3 billion in fines and compensation to settle investigations into its auditing of China Evergrande, as the firm attempts to rebuild following client and staff departures triggered by an earlier record fine. Malaysia's king confirmed the anti-corruption agency head will not have his term renewed, following Bloomberg reporting on alleged improprieties. In India, approximately 9 million names were removed from voter rolls in West Bengal two weeks before state elections began, with opposition parties alleging politically motivated targeting of Muslim and lower-income voters.

    Europe

    The eurozone economy unexpectedly contracted in April for the first time since late 2024, according to preliminary PMI data, driven by a steep decline in the services sector as the Iran war weighs on consumer confidence and energy costs. The data compounds pressure on the ECB as it navigates a difficult trade-off: energy-driven inflation is running higher — UK CPI accelerated to 3.3% in March on a 9% monthly surge in motor fuel prices, the largest since Russia's invasion of Ukraine — while growth risks are deteriorating. Analysts at TS Lombard have raised the risk of a "Trichet 2.0" scenario, in which the ECB tightens into a weakening economy, noting that China's exports and a relatively firm euro are already suppressing core goods inflation, and that household and corporate financial buffers are thinner than in 2022. European leaders gathered in Cyprus to discuss coordinated responses to the energy shock, while the ECB separately advanced the case for joint regional bonds. In Brussels, officials unveiled a plan to accelerate economic electrification. The parallel tracks of short-term crisis management and longer-term structural reform appear to be gaining political momentum, though execution timelines remain uncertain. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves received a near-term fiscal reprieve as government borrowing fell to its lowest level in three years — though analysts caution the Iran war's inflationary pass-through may prove short-lived in budgetary terms. Prime Minister Starmer faced mounting political pressure over the Peter Mandelson affair, with London Mayor Sadiq Khan offering qualified support while arguing that political stability remains a UK competitive asset. The UK and France separately concluded a £660 million deal targeting cross-Channel migration flows. UBS faces a potential $20 billion increase in capital requirements as a Swiss regulatory split widens, representing a material headwind for the bank's return targets. Trump's executive action requiring pharmaceutical companies to price US drugs at parity with other wealthy nations is creating a difficult strategic calculation for European patients: companies including Biogen and Roche face the choice of raising European prices, cutting US prices, or bypassing select European markets — with tangible implications for patient access to recently approved therapies.

    Global

    The Strait of Hormuz remains the dominant fault line in global markets. Both the US and Iran are maintaining effective blockades of the waterway, with Washington conditioning any easing on receipt of a new Iranian proposal and Tehran refusing to negotiate while the US siege remains in place. Traffic through the strait ground to a halt after Iran fired on commercial ships and seized at least two vessels. The US military separately intercepted two Iranian oil supertankers attempting to evade its blockade. Brent crude rose above $104 per barrel, its fourth consecutive daily gain, and WTI was trading near $94. European natural gas futures also climbed. Persian Gulf and Asian sovereigns have sought currency swap lines with the US Treasury, reflecting the dollar's reinforced role in global trade under stress conditions. Oil market analysts continue to debate how long strategic petroleum reserve releases and demand destruction can cushion the disruption, with a consensus that prolonged Hormuz closure would escalate supply consequences materially. Shell reported its Rotterdam refinery is operating at maximum throughput to produce jet fuel, as European airlines warn of a looming summer supply crunch. The energy shock is producing a discernible second-order effect: acceleration of clean energy policy and investment. At the Petersberg Climate Dialogue in Berlin — the opening event in the road to COP31 in Turkey this November — delegates from nearly 40 countries framed the crisis as a catalyst for reducing fossil fuel dependence. Australia's climate minister articulated the strategic logic with characteristic directness, contrasting the 150 million kilometers solar energy travels from the sun with the 150 kilometers of the Strait of Hormuz. China's EV and battery exports more than doubled year-on-year in March, in part reflecting this demand shift. On trade, approximately $300 billion in goods subject to US tariffs are estimated to be circumventing levies annually via transshipment through Southeast Asia and Mexico, according to Altana data showing a 76% year-on-year surge in suspect transactions in 2025. The pattern is sharpening USMCA renegotiation tensions, with USTR Greer signaling potential enforcement action against Canada over retaliatory trade measures.

  • US

    US equity futures advanced on Wednesday, with S&P 500 futures up roughly 0.5% and Nasdaq 100 futures gaining 0.6%, after President Trump announced an indefinite extension of the Iran ceasefire. The move reinforced what markets have come to call the TACO trade — the wager that Trump will consistently seek an off-ramp rather than permit escalation — and confirmed the pattern that has defined post-selloff behavior since the pandemic: once traders decide risk is back on, there is no retest. As Bloomberg's Longview Economics notes, the historical retest rate for major corrections stood at 87% from 1978 through 2020; it has been zero since. Whether that reflects structural changes in retail participation, tighter hedge fund risk controls, or simply extrapolation of a short behavioral sample remains genuinely uncertain. The session also brought Kevin Warsh's long-awaited Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing. The Fed chair nominee pledged independence repeatedly but declined to offer any near-term guidance on the interest rate path, leaving his views largely opaque. More substantively, Warsh signaled comfort with the administration's "economic statecraft" agenda, describing a Fed role in supporting dollar stability through Treasury Secretary Bessent and Secretary of State Rubio's framework. His comments on dollar swap lines were notably restrained — he framed the Fed's contribution as delivering stable prices rather than directly expanding liquidity facilities — but the alignment with the Bessent-Rubio posture on dollar supremacy is worth monitoring. The nomination remains complicated by Senator Thom Tillis's hold, tied to the Justice Department's probe of the Fed. On the tariff front, Trump signaled that companies seeking refunds from the Supreme Court-ordered repayment of struck-down IEEPA tariffs — a process potentially totaling more than $160 billion — might face implicit political consequences. "If they don't do that, I'll remember them," the president said of firms that forgo claims. The comment adds a chilling dimension to what CBP framed as a routine administrative process. Tesla reports quarterly earnings after the close, with Wall Street focused less on the headline figures — expected to beat lowered estimates — than on concrete progress toward robotaxi commercialization and AI/robotics initiatives that underpin the stock's 183x forward earnings multiple.

    Asia

    Japanese equity markets drew renewed interest from US investors, according to Goldman Sachs, as confidence recovers following the initial shock of the Middle East conflict. The observation aligns with a broader MSCI global gauge that has risen approximately 7% since the ceasefire was announced two weeks ago, with AI-focused benchmarks in South Korea and Taiwan rallying more than 13%. Memory chipmakers remain central to that move: Samsung shares have tripled and SK Hynix's have quadrupled since late August as AI-driven demand pushed chip prices sharply higher. The debate among investors is whether this represents a structural supercycle — with data center buildout creating a sustained demand floor — or a cyclical upturn that will eventually revert. Samsung and SK Hynix still trade at under 10x earnings, a significant discount to semiconductor peers, reflecting persistent market skepticism about the durability of memory cycles. In China, the People's Bank of China injected additional liquidity into the banking system as data revealed an unexpected deterioration in the labor market, particularly among early-career workers, where unemployment has risen as AI adoption accelerates. Separately, China's "national team" — led by Central Huijin Investment — appears to have stepped back from dominant ETF positions that had stabilized markets during the tech-led rally earlier this year, with first-quarter filings showing holdings falling below the 20% disclosure threshold. The retreat suggests authorities may be satisfied with current market levels, or are managing the risk of an overheated rally, though their current exposure remains opaque. Trump's cryptic CNBC remarks about a vessel carrying a "gift from China" — implying potential lethal aid to Iran — introduce a significant geopolitical variable. If substantiated, such transfers would test a declared US red line and could materially complicate the bilateral relationship at an already sensitive moment. Beijing has not publicly responded. Japan's March export data showed faster-than-expected growth on rebounding Chinese demand, offering a modestly constructive data point even as the Bank of Japan's expected rate hike next week faces diminished probability given war-related uncertainty.

    Europe

    Keir Starmer's political position deteriorated further on Tuesday as former top official Olly Robbins testified to a public inquiry that Downing Street was "dismissive" of concerns raised during Peter Mandelson's vetting process ahead of his appointment as UK ambassador to Washington. Mandelson's well-documented ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein were at the center of the controversy, and Starmer's decision to dismiss the Foreign Office's permanent secretary has triggered what amounts to an open revolt by senior civil servants — a damaging and distracting conflict at a moment when global crises demand coherent diplomatic capacity. Starmer's credibility appears to be eroding incrementally rather than acutely, but the trajectory is concerning for a government that entered office promising institutional restoration. On the macroeconomic front, UK inflation accelerated in March, with the surge in energy costs tied to the Hormuz closure feeding through to consumer prices. Research published this week suggests the Iran conflict could erase most of Chancellor Rachel Reeves's fiscal buffer, compounding an already narrow margin. In Germany, the Bundesbank estimated slight first-quarter GDP expansion, but the ZEW investor expectations index collapsed to -17.2 in April from -0.5 in March — its lowest level in more than three years and well below the consensus estimate of -5.8 — signaling that business confidence is deteriorating rapidly as the war weighs on Europe's industrial core. European bank lending conditions are also reportedly tightening under war-dented demand, adding to medium-term growth concerns. Hungary's incoming leader Péter Magyar has made euro adoption a top priority, which would represent the eurozone's largest single expansion since Greece joined in 2001. The ambition is real, but the timeline is long: Viktor Orbán's 16-year legacy of sluggish growth leaves the budget deficit well outside Maastricht parameters, and the convergence process typically spans several years under the best of conditions.

    Global

    The most consequential development of the day was President Trump's announcement of an indefinite ceasefire extension with Iran, delivered within 30 minutes of news that Vice President Vance's planned trip to Islamabad for renewed talks had been cancelled. The sequencing — bad news followed almost immediately by a de-escalatory action — encapsulates the dynamic that markets have priced: Trump retains escalation optionality but consistently elects the off-ramp. The Strait of Hormuz remains closed, however, and the US naval blockade continues. Iran's UN envoy indicated Wednesday morning that Tehran had received "some sign" Washington is ready to lift the blockade, suggesting backchannel communication is active even as formal talks have stalled. The UK and France convened a summit of military planners in London to discuss keeping the Strait open; separately, an Iranian gunboat fired on a container ship near Hormuz on Wednesday. The commodity dimensions of the conflict deserve attention beyond crude oil. The Bloomberg Agriculture sub-index has risen meaningfully since hostilities began, with wheat and rice both moving higher, though the moves remain below 2022 levels — possibly because markets anticipate a short conflict, or have not yet fully priced fertilizer supply disruptions from the Gulf. Bank of America flags corn as carrying the largest upside risk, given mounting pressure in nitrogen fertilizer markets. Industrial metals have also rallied sharply: copper and aluminum are each up more than 11% since mid-March, supported both by supply chain disruption and robust structural demand from AI data center buildout and grid electrification. The copper-to-gold ratio recently touched four-decade lows, a level historically associated with the onset of commodity supercycles. Commodity trading houses have been direct financial beneficiaries of the volatility. Vitol, Trafigura, and Gunvor together raised $7.5 billion in contingency liquidity facilities at the war's outset, and preliminary first-quarter figures suggest profits comparable in magnitude to the $30 billion-plus earned collectively in 2022. Russia's oil export revenues have risen to their highest since early in the Ukraine invasion, but elevated domestic borrowing costs and a military-distorted economy mean the windfall is unlikely to translate into broader growth — a meaningful constraint on any Putin-mediated diplomatic initiative.

  • US

    Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing before the Senate Banking Committee dominated domestic attention on Tuesday, though the political dynamics surrounding it may matter more than anything Warsh actually says. The nominee is expected to vow to protect the Fed's independence while simultaneously arguing that the central bank has overreached its statutory mandate in areas beyond monetary policy — a formulation carefully calibrated to reassure markets without alienating the administration that nominated him. The more consequential obstacle to confirmation remains Senator Thom Tillis's stance linking his support to the resolution of the case against incumbent Jerome Powell over Fed renovation expenditures. Warsh's opening remarks, leaked Monday, signal a preference for a narrower Fed role and a smaller balance sheet — positions that carry genuine market implications, though he appears to have avoided committing to specifics in his five-page statement. Any signal on quantitative tightening will be watched closely given the sensitivity of bond markets to balance sheet rhetoric. US equity markets continued their April advance on low conviction. The S&P 500 is up approximately 9% this month, closing above 7,000 for the first time last week, prompting JPMorgan to raise its year-end target to 7,600. However, the rally's technical underpinnings remain fragile: fewer than half of S&P 500 members traded above their 50-day moving averages at the index's record, volume has run 11% below the six-month average, and investor sentiment indicators have actually declined during the advance. The Nasdaq 100 logged a 13-session winning streak — its longest since 2013 — before pausing Monday. On the corporate front, Apple confirmed that Tim Cook will transition to executive chairman in September, handing the CEO role to hardware chief John Ternus. Amazon gained roughly 3% in premarket trading after committing an additional $5 billion to Anthropic. Alaska Air suspended profit guidance and flagged a deeper-than-expected second-quarter loss, citing rising fuel costs attributable to the Strait of Hormuz disruption. Trump invoked the Defense Production Act to direct federal funds toward energy infrastructure, including coal, LNG, petroleum and grid projects, as his administration faces pressure over elevated fuel prices.

    Asia

    China's economic narrative has shifted meaningfully in recent weeks. GDP data surprised to the upside, property indicators showed tentative green shoots, and equity markets have recovered to near decade highs. Two influential academic papers published this month examine whether Beijing has escaped the "Japanification" comparison that has shadowed it since the property downturn began. Kenneth Rogoff and Yuanchen Yang, writing for the NBER, acknowledge that China's state-dominated financial system and interventionist policy apparatus have prevented a financial breakdown, but warn that without structural reforms to rebalance growth, today's managed adjustment could evolve into stagnation — particularly if export-oriented sectors like EVs and solar encounter external constraints. Stephen Jen and Joana Freire of Eurizon SLJ Capital are more sanguine, arguing China has "turned a corner" and that fierce domestic competition, combined with US-China technological rivalry, is sustaining productivity in ways that distinguish Beijing's trajectory from Tokyo's lost decades. The debate remains unresolved, though recent data lend some weight to the more constructive reading. Asian technology exports are providing a separate source of optimism. South Korean chip exports surged 183% year-on-year in the first 20 days of April, driven by AI-related semiconductor demand, with overall exports rising 49%. Taiwan's export orders jumped 66% in March, the fastest pace in 16 years, reflecting continued strength in high-performance computing and cloud infrastructure. Victory Giant, a Chinese Nvidia supplier, surged as much as 60% in its Hong Kong debut after raising $2.6 billion. The South Korean Kospi is up 52% year-to-date, reaching a record, with Samsung and SK Hynix leading the advance. Japan's Topix Nonferrous Metals sub-index has gained over 400% since the Liberation Day tariff reversal a year ago, driven by data center component demand and accelerating corporate governance reform under Prime Minister Takaichi. Her cabinet also approved Japan's first overseas arms export framework since World War II, a significant strategic shift. The Bank of Japan is meanwhile leaning toward holding rates unchanged at its next meeting, citing uncertainties from the Iran conflict. Indonesia's equities declined after MSCI extended its market status review to June.

    Europe

    Keir Starmer faces a potentially defining political test as former senior civil servant Olly Robbins testifies before the House of Commons regarding the prime minister's handling of Peter Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador. The central question — whether Starmer was aware of Mandelson's vetting failure or pressured the Foreign Office to proceed regardless — goes to the heart of his credibility on standards in public life. Starmer has already survived the first day of parliamentary scrutiny, but Robbins's testimony carries greater evidentiary weight. UK businesses stepped up job cuts in March, a sign that the Iran war is generating fresh caution in the labour market, adding to the economic headwinds facing the government. In European financial markets, the UniCredit-Commerzbank takeover battle has entered a decisive phase. UniCredit CEO Andrea Orcel and Commerzbank counterpart Bettina Orlopp met last month but failed to find common ground despite Orcel having accumulated just under 30% of Commerzbank and tabling a €35 billion offer. The German government continues to back Orlopp's resistance, though questions about the sustainability of that defense are sharpening as Commerzbank's profit tailwinds from higher rates fade. ECB President Christine Lagarde noted that the Iran war's "double uncertainty" — simultaneously threatening energy supply and inflation — is complicating monetary policy deliberations, effectively arguing for a data-dependent approach that delays further rate action. German investor optimism fell to a three-year low, reflecting broader eurozone anxiety. Associated British Foods announced plans to spin off budget clothing chain Primark after more than three decades as part of the conglomerate, while Ukraine reported the Druzhba pipeline would resume oil flows Tuesday, potentially paving the way for a reconstruction loan.

    Global

    The imminent expiry of the two-week US-Iran ceasefire is the dominant global market risk. President Trump signaled on Tuesday that he is unlikely to extend the truce beyond its Wednesday deadline, intensifying pressure on negotiators attempting to reconvene talks in Islamabad. Iran has yet to formally confirm its participation. Three vessels — two cargo ships and a fuel tanker — appeared to attempt transit of the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday while both US and Iranian blockades nominally remained in place, a high-risk manoeuvre that may test enforcement. Approximately 800 vessels remain stranded in the Persian Gulf, and the International Maritime Organization is developing an evacuation plan. DHL's CEO warned the global economy risks a "tipping point" if the supply shortfall is not resolved. Xi Jinping broke his public silence on the standoff, calling Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman to urge restoration of Strait transit — a signal of Beijing's acute vulnerability as a major oil importer, though short of any concrete diplomatic intervention. Oil markets slipped on Tuesday as the prospect of resumed talks tempered immediate supply fears, with WTI trading near $88 per barrel. Analysts cautioned that prices do not yet fully reflect the scale of the supply disruption. US shale producers face an awkward dilemma: oil above $100 per barrel generates strong cash flows, but memories of the 2014 dash-for-growth bust have instilled discipline that resists White House pressure to ramp up output. Continental Resources remains the only major producer to confirm increased drilling plans. On a structural note, clean power surpassed coal's share of global electricity generation in 2025 for the first time since 1919, with renewables at 34% against coal's 33%, according to Ember — a milestone likely to sharpen policy focus on energy independence. In the long-duration energy storage space, China holds roughly 72% of cumulative installed capacity but its dominance appears less entrenched than in conventional lithium-ion, offering competitive openings for US and European developers.

  • US

    US equity futures pulled back modestly on Monday, with S&P 500 futures off approximately 0.5% as a turbulent weekend in the Strait of Hormuz undercut the assumptions underpinning the market's recent historic rally. The S&P 500 had risen 12.33% in just 14 trading days following Iran's initial signals of willingness to negotiate, a pace of appreciation seen only four times this century — and the first such episode to bring the index to an all-time high rather than merely recovering from deeply oversold levels. Whether Monday marks the start of a reversal or another false alarm, as last Monday proved, remains to be seen. The more consequential near-term catalyst for markets may arrive Tuesday, when Kevin Warsh appears before the Senate Banking Committee for his Fed chair confirmation hearing. Bond markets have been partially buoyed by the expectation that Warsh would favor looser monetary policy; any signal that he intends to look through the war-driven energy shock would likely reinforce wagers on rate cuts and support the short end of the Treasury curve. Any hawkish pivot on inflation, however, could trigger a reversal. Separately, Republican Senator Thom Tillis has pledged to block Warsh's confirmation until the Justice Department concludes its investigation into Jerome Powell, creating the possibility that Powell remains in his post beyond his May 15 term expiry. On the corporate front, American Airlines explicitly rejected merger interest from United Airlines, dampening speculation of a transformative combination in the industry. QXO, the Brad Jacobs vehicle, announced a roughly $17 billion acquisition of insulation company TopBuild. Energy Secretary Chris Wright contradicted Treasury Secretary Bessent by stating US gasoline prices are unlikely to fall below $3 per gallon until next year, a notable divergence within the administration that may complicate the political narrative around energy relief. The SaaS sector remains under structural pressure, with the S&P Composite 1500 Software index having shed approximately 35% over the prior six months amid AI disruption fears, though some analysts argue the selloff overstates the near-term risk to incumbent business models.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets were largely muted Monday, with the CSI 300 edging up 0.6% even as oil prices surged, reflecting a degree of investor compartmentalization between energy market disruption and Chinese domestic equity sentiment. The divergence is notable given China's acute exposure to the Strait of Hormuz closure: roughly 20% of the world's oil transits the waterway, and tanker flows had fallen to near-zero in the weeks following the outbreak of conflict. China is responding at the margin by pivoting to alternative feedstocks — US ethane imports are expected to hit a record 800,000 tons in April, approximately 60% above the monthly average — while clean technology exports surged in March, with lithium-ion battery shipments up 34% year-on-year and electric vehicles up 53%, benefiting from heightened global demand for non-fossil alternatives. At the IMF spring meetings in Washington last week, China's delegation mounted a robust defense of its record trade surpluses, arguing that imbalances reflect cultural savings preferences rather than policy distortions and pointing to rising imports as evidence of an improving trajectory. The argument drew skepticism from other delegations, including Japan's finance minister, who described the presentation as ultimately unconvincing. The episode signals a more assertive posture from Beijing on external criticism of its economic model. Elsewhere in the region, North Korea conducted a series of five tactical ballistic missile tests, variants of the Hwasong-11, which Kim Jong Un praised effusively. The launches come amid ongoing concern about Pyongyang accumulating real combat experience through its troop deployments supporting Russia in Ukraine. Japan meanwhile participated for the first time in major naval exercises with the US and the Philippines, even as China's navy conducted drills in the western Pacific — a parallel display of posturing that underscores the widening military competition west of the First Island Chain. Singapore is procuring additional LNG supplies as the Hormuz disruption curtails flows from Qatar and the UAE.

    Europe

    European equities fell approximately 1% on Monday as the weekend's events in the Strait of Hormuz rekindled concerns about the durability of any near-term ceasefire. European natural gas prices climbed as much as 11%, compounding the energy cost pressures that have already weighed heavily on the continent's industrial base. The EU is preparing measures to optimize jet fuel distribution among member states and source alternative supplies, reflecting the practical strain that seven weeks of Hormuz disruption is placing on aviation and broader energy infrastructure. In Germany, Chancellor Friedrich Merz faces an increasingly difficult political environment. His approval rating has fallen to deeply negative territory, with 80% of respondents in a recent poll expressing disapproval of his performance. Measures announced last week to cushion the blow of rising energy prices — including a €1,000 worker payment intended to be voluntarily funded by employers — were widely criticized as inadequate, with the federal government itself declining to participate. Merz has called crisis talks to address energy price impacts on German industry, but his coalition appears beset by the same infighting that characterized its predecessor, providing oxygen to the AfD, which leads in polls ahead of state elections in eastern Germany in September. UniCredit CEO Andrea Orcel pressed his case for acquiring Commerzbank, arguing the German bank requires deep structural change. In the UK, Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces a significant domestic political test, making a statement to the House of Commons on Monday over the controversy surrounding Peter Mandelson's security vetting clearance as US ambassador. Calls for Starmer to resign are growing ahead of local elections next month, which are predicted to be damaging for Labour. Separately, Bulgaria's presidential election produced a decisive victory for former President Rumen Radev, who opposes sanctions on Russia and military aid for Ukraine, adding a complicating element to European political cohesion on the war in the east.

    Global

    The central development across global markets remains the unresolved status of the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of global oil supply ordinarily flows. A chaotic weekend saw Iranian foreign minister declare the strait "completely open" on Friday, only for Tehran to reverse course within 24 hours after the US refused to lift its own blockade of Iran-linked vessels. The US Navy then fired upon and boarded an Iranian-flagged cargo ship in the Gulf of Oman — the first such seizure since the blockade began — prompting Iranian authorities to reimpose controls. Brent crude surged as much as 7.9% to near $95 per barrel in Asian trading, while European gas rose as much as 11%. The ceasefire is due to expire Tuesday, and the prospects for US-Iran talks in Islamabad appear uncertain. Vice President Vance, special envoy Witkoff, and Jared Kushner were scheduled to depart for Pakistan Monday evening, but Iran's Foreign Ministry stated no decision had been made on attendance, with a spokesman citing a lack of "seriousness" on the US side. The gap between the two parties' stated positions remains wide: the US claims Iran has agreed to surrender enriched uranium and end its nuclear program; Iranian officials deny both. Polymarket bettors place only approximately a one-in-five probability on tanker traffic returning to normal by the end of April. The economic effects are beginning to propagate more broadly: tankers face 3.5-day delays at the Panama Canal as diverted traffic surges, Pakistan is experiencing severe power and gas outages, Colombia is reckoning with energy demand disruptions, and Singapore is scrambling to secure alternative LNG supplies. The war is simultaneously accelerating the energy storage boom, with battery installations expected to jump roughly a third this year as economies seek to reduce fossil fuel dependence. China's clean tech export surge — with solar cells up 80% year-on-year in March — reflects both this structural demand shift and Beijing's ability to capitalize on a crisis not of its making.

  • US

    Utility bills are emerging as a defining issue heading into the 2026 midterm elections, with electricity costs having nearly doubled for many American households over the past several years. The confluence of AI data center buildout, grid upgrade expenditures, tariff pass-throughs, and energy market disruption linked to the Iran conflict has driven power prices higher at rates not seen in decades. Democrats who campaigned explicitly on household energy affordability swept key races in New Jersey, Virginia, and Georgia in late 2025, providing a template that challengers are now deploying against incumbent Republicans defending a narrow House majority. Pennsylvania's 7th District, where a former utility employee is running against Republican Representative Ryan Mackenzie, illustrates the dynamic: bipartisan agreement on the problem dissolves into sharp disagreement over solutions. President Trump acknowledged the issue in his February State of the Union address — the first time a president has explicitly framed rising electricity bills as a consumer concern in that forum — though his proposed remedy, requiring tech companies to build their own power plants, remains more aspiration than policy. The midterm environment appears to be shaping up around kitchen-table economics rather than ideological abstraction, a terrain historically more favorable to the opposition party. On defense and climate adaptation, a notable institutional divergence is apparent within the Trump administration. Secretary Hegseth has moved aggressively to eliminate explicit climate language from Pentagon mission statements and cancel climate-related research, yet the Defense Department continues to advance large-scale infrastructure hardening at military installations. Tyndall Air Force Base in Florida — damaged to the tune of nearly $5 billion by Hurricane Michael in 2018 — is being rebuilt with flood-resistant structures, reinforced roofing, and coastal protection features. The framing has shifted from "climate resilience" to "operational readiness," a semantic adjustment that has proved sufficient to preserve funding. Close to $400 billion in federal assets, predominantly Defense Department holdings, face elevated coastal flood risk, providing a practical rationale that transcends the political debate.

    Asia

    China's economy posted stronger-than-expected first-quarter growth, driven in significant part by an export surge as manufacturers moved to front-run US tariff escalation. While the headline figure offered reassurance, the composition of growth raises questions about durability: domestic consumption remains subdued relative to the export contribution, and the tariff environment that incentivized the front-loading may itself constrain external demand in coming quarters. Beijing's 15th five-year plan, approved in March, provided additional clarity on the government's long-term orientation, including a measured approach to climate commitments that analysts characterize as prioritizing energy security alongside emissions reduction rather than accelerating the transition. The plan signals continued state direction of industrial policy, with implications for capital allocation across technology, manufacturing, and energy sectors. India's position in the evolving US-China technology decoupling faces fresh complications. Controls on Chinese technology exports are creating friction for Indian manufacturers attempting to scale production in sectors such as electronics and battery components — industries that New Delhi has identified as central to its manufacturing ambition. India simultaneously published its own climate plan for 2035, which observers assess as cautious relative to the pace of emissions growth the country is experiencing. The broader theme across both economies is one of managed transition under significant resource and geopolitical constraint, rather than transformational acceleration. Elsewhere in the region, Japan and South Korea continue to navigate the twin pressures of dollar strength — partly a function of US tariff uncertainty — and domestic inflation dynamics that complicate central bank communication. No material policy shifts were signaled this week from either the Bank of Japan or the Bank of Korea.

    Europe

    Hungary's political transition following Peter Magyar's decisive victory in the April 12 parliamentary election carries consequences well beyond a country of fewer than 10 million people. Magyar and his Tisza party secured what appears to be a parliamentary supermajority, reducing Viktor Orbán's Fidesz to a rump opposition after 16 years in power. The practical implications for the European Union are immediate and significant: Orbán's Hungary had held up approximately €90 billion in EU support for Ukraine, repeatedly exercising veto power to block measures requiring unanimity. With Magyar signaling a sharp reorientation toward Brussels — including an aspiration to eventually adopt the euro — that obstruction may lift, potentially unlocking both the Ukraine aid package and broader EU decision-making that had been paralyzed. The scale of the shift invites comparison to earlier inflection points in European integration, though the analogy should not be overextended. Magyar inherits an economy that has consistently underperformed regional peers Poland and the Czech Republic since 2010, having been shaped by what one Macquarie strategist characterized as "unchecked executive power through erosion of institutional pillars." Hungary's dependence on Russian energy imports and its existing commercial ties to Asian battery manufacturers — a legacy of Orbán's industrial policy — will constrain the pace and character of reorientation. Magyar is unlikely to exit Russian gas supply arrangements quickly, and veto rights over EU decisions may prove too useful a tool to abandon entirely even under new leadership. Nevertheless, the political signal carries weight. Brussels now faces fewer internal obstacles to cohesion on Russia policy, Ukraine support, and the broader question of EU strategic autonomy. The episode also illustrates that the right-wing nationalist model Orbán represented — and which Washington under the current administration has sought to promote across Europe — is not electorally invulnerable when economic mismanagement becomes the dominant voter concern.

    Global

    The intersection of the US-Iran conflict and global energy markets continues to represent the most consequential near-term macro risk across regions. The source material references "war in Iran" as actively disrupting global energy markets, contributing to gasoline price increases that are feeding into household affordability stress in the United States and, almost certainly, in energy-importing economies elsewhere. The Strait of Hormuz — through which a substantial share of global oil flows — remains a focal point of supply vulnerability, though the precise operational status and risk of escalation remains unclear from available reporting. What is apparent is that energy price volatility is already transmitting into consumer prices and political dynamics with notable speed. The Pentagon's quiet continuation of climate adaptation work at military installations — rebranded as "resilience" and "readiness" rather than climate response — reflects a broader tension playing out across governments: the gap between political positioning on climate and the practical decisions driven by asset exposure and operational necessity. Close to $400 billion in US federal assets face elevated flood risk; similar pressures apply to infrastructure across coastal Asia and Europe. China and India's recently published climate plans both signal a decade of cautious incrementalism rather than accelerated transition, suggesting that global emissions trajectories are unlikely to shift materially in the near term despite the scale of physical risk accumulating. On global monetary policy, divergence across major central banks remains a structuring theme, though no fresh policy decisions were signaled this week. The combination of trade-related inflation pressure in the US, export-driven cyclical support in China, and gradually easing inflation in parts of Europe creates an environment in which rate path convergence remains distant — a condition that tends to sustain dollar volatility and complicate capital allocation across emerging markets.

  • US

    US equity markets continued their strong recovery from the war-related selloff, with the S&P 500 on track for a third consecutive week of gains exceeding 3% and closing above 7,000 points for the first time since January. The Nasdaq 100 extended its winning streak to 12 consecutive sessions. The rally has been underpinned by Iran peace optimism, robust earnings broadly, and renewed appetite for risk among retail investors. A basket of shares favored by individual investors is on course for its best month relative to mutual fund favorites since 2020, with the Roundhill Meme Stock ETF up 54% since late March — a reflection of sentiment rather than fundamentals, though the two are not entirely separable in the current environment. Netflix provided the most notable earnings disappointment of the week, with shares falling approximately 10% in premarket trading after the streamer's earnings forecast missed estimates. Co-founder and chairman Reed Hastings simultaneously announced his departure from the board after 29 years. Alcoa also disappointed despite elevated metal prices, with operational disruptions and higher costs weighing on results. QVC, the television shopping network, filed for bankruptcy as part of a plan to restructure more than $5 billion in debt — a casualty of secular shifts in retail rather than the macro environment. On fiscal and energy policy, states including California, Colorado, New York and New Jersey are accelerating large-scale renewable energy procurement to qualify for federal investment tax credits that expire on July 4. The deadline — tied to the 250th anniversary of US independence — is concentrating deployment activity, with Southern California Edison alone targeting 6,000 megawatts of additional capacity. The Trump administration, meanwhile, rejected CenterPoint Energy's request to retire a 60-year-old Indiana coal plant, a decision consistent with the administration's posture toward fossil fuel infrastructure. Former Treasury Secretary Henry Paulson, speaking on Bloomberg Television, warned that a collapse in Treasury demand remains a tail risk and urged authorities to develop contingency plans — a sobering note amid an otherwise ebullient market backdrop.

    Asia

    China's first-quarter GDP expanded 5.0% year-on-year, beating expectations, offering a measure of reassurance to policymakers navigating simultaneous headwinds from US tariffs and the Iran war. However, the Canton Fair in Guangzhou provided a more granular and cautionary picture. Middle Eastern buyers — who had increasingly substituted for absent American and European customers since the pandemic and the onset of Trump's tariffs — are now conspicuously absent due to the conflict and flight disruption. Exporters described this year as potentially the worst in memory, facing the sequential blows of tariffs and war simultaneously. High-tech exports surged nearly 30% in the first quarter, offering a partial offset, but the durability of that momentum against deteriorating external conditions remains uncertain. Xi Jinping had his most diplomatically active week in nearly two years, receiving at least five high-profile delegations including Spain's prime minister, Abu Dhabi's crown prince, Vietnam's president, and Russia's foreign minister. Xi framed China as a source of stability against what he described as a "crumbling" global order, proposing a four-point Middle East peace plan and vowing to deepen ties with Moscow. His positioning is deliberate — building on the 2023 Saudi-Iran rapprochement — but Beijing's concrete contributions to conflict resolution have so far been limited, and state media pushed back against suggestions that China nudged Tehran toward negotiations. A Trump-Xi summit in roughly a month's time looms as the next test. The yuan's strength has prompted the People's Bank of China to use its daily reference rate to temper further appreciation, as war-driven capital flows fuel bullish sentiment. On trade infrastructure, yuan-denominated crude oil transactions reached a single-day record of 1.22 trillion yuan through CIPS. Separately, Australia announced a multibillion-dollar agreement to procure 11 advanced naval frigates from Japan — the first three vessels covered by an A$15–20 billion contract — a transaction that advances both Australian defense modernization and Japan's ambitions in global arms markets. LNG supply disruptions are reshaping long-term energy strategy across the region. Asian LNG flows have fallen to their lowest since 2020, and with prices still 50% above pre-war levels, Vietnam is reconsidering gas-fired power in favor of renewables, Pakistan is reviving domestic gas production, and China — buffered by domestic gas, pipeline imports, and record coal output — has seen LNG imports fall 25% without facing a supply deficit.

    Europe

    UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces a deepening political crisis after it emerged that Peter Mandelson, his ambassador to Washington, was granted security clearance despite objections from vetting officials — a decision complicated by Mandelson's past associations with Jeffrey Epstein. Starmer responded by dismissing the Foreign Office's top civil servant, Olly Robbins, but the episode has rekindled questions about his judgment and grip on power, with calls for his resignation growing. The episode arrives at a moment when Starmer was attempting to position the UK as a constructive international actor, co-hosting with France a summit on establishing a naval force to ensure freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz. European divergences over the Hormuz mission are becoming apparent, with France and the UK convening the discussion but member states approaching the operational question from differing strategic perspectives. The London Metals Index reached a record high, driven largely by aluminum, as the Iran war disrupts supply chains and industrial metals respond to both genuine scarcity and safe-haven demand. Alstom fell 36% in Paris after withdrawing its free cash flow guidance for the full year, citing slow progress on key projects — the sharpest single-day decline for the French rail manufacturer since 2023. On monetary policy, a Bloomberg survey points to an ECB rate hike in June as 2026 inflation runs above earlier projections. ECB policymaker Madis Müller called for vigilance on inflation risks, and President Christine Lagarde used the IMF-World Bank meetings in Washington to warn of the risks posed by Anthropic's new Mythos AI model — a somewhat unusual intervention that nonetheless reflects genuine institutional anxiety about cyber vulnerabilities at a moment of elevated geopolitical stress. Bloomberg Opinion editors cautioned that European central bankers should be careful not to amplify the energy shock's economic damage through premature tightening. In France, three major mobile operators entered exclusive talks to acquire Patrick Drahi's SFR in a transaction valued at approximately $24 billion, a deal that would reshape the country's telecoms landscape if completed.

    Global

    The dominant macro narrative remains the trajectory of the US-Iran conflict and its implications for energy markets and global growth. Trump expressed optimism about a permanent ceasefire, claiming Iran had conceded on nuclear ambitions and Hormuz access — assertions Tehran has not publicly confirmed. Talks may resume this weekend, though Gulf Arab and European officials believe a durable agreement is more likely six months away. The ceasefire in Lebanon, announced this week, offers modest positive signal, but the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, with only a single Pakistan-flagged tanker completing an exit with crude cargo since the US blockade began — underscoring the gap between diplomatic tone and operational reality. Oil responded to the peace signals, with Brent falling toward $98 per barrel and WTI near $93, paring weekly gains. Price volatility has cooled markedly from the record $38 intraday range seen in mid-March, with Brent trading in approximately a $10 band this week. Industrial metals tell a different story — the London Metals Index reached a record high on aluminum supply concerns, with the six-metal LME index up roughly 12% over four weeks. The broader structural damage from the Hormuz disruption is becoming clearer. Panama Canal congestion has reached its worst since the 2023-2024 drought, with vessels facing 3.5-day waits and one ship paying $4 million to jump the queue. LNG's credibility as a long-term energy solution for emerging Asia has been materially set back — a second price shock in four years following Russia's invasion of Ukraine is accelerating diversification toward renewables, domestic gas, and coal, with implications for Big Oil's growth strategy in developing markets. Anthropic's Mythos AI model — described as highly capable of identifying system vulnerabilities and orchestrating cyberattacks — emerged as an unexpected focal point at the IMF-World Bank spring meetings in Washington. Central bank governors from the ECB, Bank of England, and New York Fed all raised the issue publicly within days of each other, reflecting institutional alarm at a moment when international coordination capacity is already strained. The dollar's safe-haven rally appears to be fading, with Wall Street banks including Deutsche Bank and Wells Fargo recommending short positions as ceasefire prospects reduce haven demand.

  • US
