    The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 reached all-time highs on April 16, completing a roughly 10% and 14% rally respectively from their March 30 closing levels — one of the fastest such moves recorded since 1950. The advance is analytically unusual: prior recoveries of this magnitude and speed have typically occurred from deeply oversold levels following prolonged bear markets or acute crises. The closest historical parallel, by earnings multiple and timing, appears to be March 2000. Analysts attribute the rally partly to behavioral conditioning from last year's Liberation Day episode, when traders who sold early missed a rapid rebound, making investors more reluctant to hold short positions through the Iran conflict. The structural shift toward passive and retail "buy-the-dip" participants reinforces this dynamic. Valuations remain stretched by most conventional measures. The 10-year Treasury yield sits at approximately 4.3%, and Robert Shiller's excess CAPE yield — which subtracts the bond yield from the equity earnings yield — implies below-average prospective decade returns. First-quarter earnings estimates have declined during the rally, not risen, making the multiple expansion harder to justify on fundamental grounds. On monetary policy, the Federal Reserve situation remains unresolved and politically charged. Trump reiterated his threat to dismiss Jerome Powell if he remains as chairman past his May term expiration. The Justice Department continues to pursue its building-renovation probe despite a court ruling characterizing it as without merit. Senator Thom Tillis has blocked Kevin Warsh's confirmation pending resolution of the legal matter. Notably, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has now signaled twice in two days that he understands the case for the Fed to hold rates steady while awaiting clarity on the war's inflationary impact — a marked shift from his prior advocacy for cuts, and one that implicitly provides political cover for Warsh to avoid an embarrassing dissent on his first FOMC vote. The Fed's Beige Book described an economy in suspension, with no near-term catalyst to move in either direction. Markets are pricing near-zero probability of a June rate change. A federal antitrust jury found Live Nation illegally monopolized the live events industry, setting the stage for a potential structural breakup of the company.

    Asia

    China's first-quarter GDP expanded 5.0% year-on-year, the strongest pace in three quarters, powered by manufacturing and exports. The result exceeded consensus expectations and suggests limited direct economic spillover from the Iran conflict so far. However, the data expose a persistent structural imbalance: industrial output grew 5.7% in March while retail sales rose only 1.7%, falling short of forecasts and signaling that domestic consumption has not meaningfully recovered. The two-speed economy — robust on the supply side, fragile on the demand side — remains a central policy challenge. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company delivered a first-quarter profit surge of 58% and raised its full-year revenue growth guidance to above 30%, reinforcing its role as the primary proxy for global AI infrastructure investment. TSMC's market capitalization now exceeds $1.7 trillion, and Taiwan's total equity market value has overtaken the UK's to become the seventh largest globally at $4.14 trillion. The Taiex has gained 17% this month, and South Korea's Kospi has rallied approximately 23%, recovering sharply from an 18% two-day decline at the onset of the Iran war. BlackRock has flagged Korean equities as a preferred position given AI-driven earnings momentum. Japan's Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, speaking after bilateral discussions with Treasury Secretary Bessent in Washington, warned she was prepared to take "bold action" to support the yen. The statement was widely interpreted as a direct intervention threat; the absence of pushback from Bessent is being read as implicit US tolerance for possible currency-market action. Macro hedge funds in China suffered notable losses in March. Bridgewater's All Weather Plus strategy fell 5.6% between late February and early April, trimming its first-quarter return to 3.9%. Shanghai Longlife's Macro Hedging No. 1 fund — 2025's top performer with a 153% return — declined 25% in March. The peer-group average loss was 6.3%, the worst on record.

    Europe

    The European Central Bank appears likely to hold rates unchanged at its upcoming meeting, according to people familiar with internal deliberations. Policymakers are choosing to defer judgment on whether the Iran conflict's economic fallout warrants a monetary policy response, with tighter financing conditions credited for keeping inflation expectations anchored in the near term. Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey struck a similar note, signaling no urgency to adjust rates given the difficulty of assessing the war's full economic impact. Eurozone inflation in March came in slightly above the preliminary estimate, but this appears insufficient on its own to shift the ECB toward tightening. Elsewhere in European markets, easyJet shares fell sharply — their steepest single-session decline since 2022 — after the airline warned of a first-half loss attributable to travel disruptions stemming from the conflict. Pimco indicated it sees value in European bonds following the war-driven selloff, suggesting some institutional buyers view current yield levels as attractive entry points. Hungary's Orbán government suffered a meaningful electoral defeat, triggering what is being described in markets as a "euro convergence trade" — investors positioning for a potential shift in Budapest's economic policy orientation toward greater EU alignment. At Monte dei Paschi di Siena, shareholders voted to reinstate ousted CEO Luigi Lovaglio, with nearly 50% of attending shareholders supporting the candidate list that included his reappointment, against roughly 39% for the board-backed alternative proposing Fabrizio Palermo. The outcome represents a significant defeat for the outgoing board. The BBC announced plans to cut up to 2,000 positions, approximately 10% of its workforce, as part of a structural transition led by incoming Director General Matt Brittin.

    Global

    The defining macro tension of the moment is the divergence between policymaker pessimism and market optimism. At the IMF-World Bank spring meetings in Washington this week, the emerging consensus among officials was that investors are materially underestimating the economic damage from the Iran war. Qatar's Finance Minister described current market conditions as "the tip of the iceberg," and participants broadly expect structural consequences — higher costs, longer trade routes, elevated geopolitical risk premiums — to persist well beyond any ceasefire. Global Trade Alert has tracked more than 250 distinct government policy responses since hostilities began on February 28, the large majority of which are liberalizing import measures, energy subsidies, or demand-reduction programs, particularly across Asia. The response is notably broad-based rather than sectoral, reflecting the diffuse nature of the supply disruption. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, now entering its third day of formal US blockade activity. Two Iran-sanctioned vessels — the LPG carrier G Summer and the VLCC Hong Lu — were observed transiting a new route between Iran's Larak and Qeshm islands into the Persian Gulf, suggesting Tehran is testing alternative corridors. The US and Iran are reportedly considering a two-week ceasefire extension to allow further negotiations. The aluminum market has entered what JPMorgan characterizes as a structural supply "black hole." Iranian strikes on smelters in Abu Dhabi and Bahrain at the end of March caused production losses that will take at least a year to recover, pushing London Metal Exchange prices to four-year highs above $3,600 per ton. JPMorgan now forecasts prices could reach $4,000 per ton — approaching the 2022 all-time high — as the market faces its largest supply deficit in over 25 years. The shortage falls disproportionately on Western manufacturers, for whom Chinese and Russian supply remains largely inaccessible due to sanctions and tariffs. Food security represents a slower-moving but potentially more consequential transmission channel. Gulf-region countries account for at least 30% of global fertilizer supply, including urea, ammonia, and diammonium phosphate. A Farm Bureau survey finds over 70% of US farmers cannot currently afford sufficient fertilizer. Price effects will emerge with a harvest-cycle lag, and a prolonged conflict risks compounding input cost inflation with yield reductions, amplifying food price pressures globally. The US Justice Department has opened a probe into fertilizer and farm input pricing practices. WTI crude was trading near $92.50, with Brent around $95.

  • US

    US equity markets are holding near all-time highs as investor sentiment has rotated decisively toward optimism over a potential end to the US-Iran conflict. The Nasdaq 100 has posted ten consecutive sessions of gains — its longest streak since November 2021 — rising approximately 13% over that stretch, while the S&P 500 futures were trading near 7,000. Software stocks, which had been the most punished by fears of AI-driven disruption, staged a sharp bounce: Oracle surged 18% in two sessions, and an iShares software ETF gained 6.4%, though it remains down 25% year-to-date and bottom-fishing investors are not yet declaring a durable turn. The Bank of America global fund manager survey is notable for the degree to which sentiment has reoriented. The proportion of respondents viewing US equities as overvalued fell to its lowest level in more than seven years. That finding warrants some skepticism — the Shiller CAPE remains elevated by historical standards and US stocks trade at a substantial premium to the rest of the world — but it does suggest that war-driven selling compressed valuations enough to bring marginal buyers back. The interaction between that valuation re-rating and a weakening dollar may prove self-limiting: if the US is perceived as cheap again, capital inflows could put a floor under the currency, but the structural drivers of dollar weakness — narrowing rate differentials and reduced foreign appetite for US assets — have not been resolved. On the fiscal and monetary policy front, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent confirmed that tariff rates may be restored to their pre-Supreme Court levels by July, after the court struck down the use of emergency powers for their original imposition. The Atlanta Fed published survey evidence suggesting that the One Big Beautiful Bill's capex provisions have had limited uptake: only 20% of firms incorporated the legislation into their planning, echoing a similar result from the 2017 tax cuts. That finding complicates the bull case for a business investment-led expansion. US consumer stress continues to accumulate beneath the equity market resilience. BNPL provider Klarna has lost more than 60% of its market value since its September IPO; nearly half of BNPL users report having paid at least one installment late in the past year, and usage is increasingly concentrated in essentials such as groceries — a sign of financial necessity rather than discretionary adoption. Prosecutors also made an unannounced visit to the Federal Reserve in Washington, adding to institutional uncertainty around the central bank.

    Asia

    Chinese and broader Asian markets recovered further on Wednesday, with the Hang Seng rising 0.3% and stock benchmarks in China and the US having now recouped all their war-driven losses since the conflict began in late February. The recovery reflects cautious optimism that US-Iran negotiations will produce a durable de-escalation, though the structural disruption to energy flows continues to impose real costs on the region's industrial base. The most consequential geopolitical signal from Beijing was Xi Jinping's meeting with Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov at the Great Hall of the People. Xi emphasized that "the stability and certainty of China-Russia relations are particularly valuable" amid global turbulence — a formulation that signals Beijing is deepening its strategic alignment with Moscow at a moment when Russia's energy exports, particularly oil and gas, have become more valuable to China as Middle East flows tighten. Chinese oil imports from Saudi Arabia are set to halve next month, and China's overall oil and gas imports declined in March as the Hormuz disruption took effect. Russia's potential to fill that gap creates a structural incentive for Beijing to reinforce the relationship regardless of the war's outcome. Southeast Asian sovereign credit quality is under pressure. S&P Global Ratings flagged Indonesia as particularly exposed to a prolonged conflict scenario, given that higher energy prices will widen its subsidy burden and current account deficit simultaneously. S&P's base case assumes Hormuz disruption peaks in April, but acknowledges that some disruption could persist for months given infrastructure damage. For Indonesia, and other energy-importing ASEAN sovereigns, the resolution timeline matters more than the headline ceasefire. India's manufacturing ambitions remain constrained by China's dominance in batteries, EVs, and critical minerals, despite substantial Make in India investment. That dependency is deepening rather than narrowing, with up-and-coming Indian sectors becoming more reliant on Chinese technology even as geopolitical logic argues for diversification. The Port of Los Angeles chief's assessment — that supply chain normalization through Hormuz will take roughly four and a half months even after the strait reopens — implies sustained disruption to container flows through Asian trade hubs, where cargo for the Middle East is already piling up at ports in China, India, Japan, South Korea, and Singapore.

    Europe

    The ECB's policy path for the April 30 decision remains genuinely unclear after ECB President Christine Lagarde declined to signal either a hike or a hold at the IMF Spring Meetings in Washington. Her comments placed the eurozone between the ECB's March baseline scenario — which itself incorporated two rate increases in 2026 — and the adverse scenario projecting 3.5% inflation this year. She explicitly stated the ECB has no tightening bias, yet simultaneously acknowledged that simply "looking through" the energy shock risks undermining the bank's credibility on price stability. Those positions are in tension, and Lagarde did not resolve them, leaving the market to price a range of outcomes ahead of the meeting. The real economy signals from Europe are deteriorating. European chemical producers are facing acute input cost pressures, with prices of fossil-fuel-derived feedstocks — essential for everything from food packaging to pharmaceuticals — surging as supply remains trapped in the Persian Gulf. SKW Stickstoffwerke Piesteritz's COO warned that the loss of basic chemical supply chains could trigger an economic collapse, language that may be directionally correct even if the extreme outcome is not the central case. The Port of Antwerp-Bruges, home to major operations for TotalEnergies, ExxonMobil, and BASF, is managing additional disruption from a fuel spill. French luxury goods suffered a notable earnings shock. Hermès shares fell as much as 14% — its largest single-day decline on record — after first-quarter sales growth disappointed, with the company attributing weakness to the confidence effects of the war. Kering also reported disappointing figures. ASML provided the counterpoint: Europe's most valuable company raised its full-year sales forecast to €36-40 billion from €34-39 billion, driven by AI-related semiconductor demand, with its CEO noting that supply cannot meet demand "for the foreseeable future." Politically, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz is pressing for rapid disbursement of a €90 billion EU loan for Ukraine following Hungarian Prime Minister Orbán's electoral defeat. Merz met with Ukrainian President Zelensky in Berlin and reinforced support for Ukraine's EU membership. Italy's Giorgia Meloni has used Pope Leo XIV's moral critique of the Iran war as a pretext to distance herself from Trump, a shift that reflects broader European recalibration as the US president's behavior becomes increasingly difficult for allied governments to manage domestically.

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme remains the US-Iran conflict and its Hormuz consequences, even as markets price in a negotiated resolution. President Trump told multiple US outlets he sees the war as "very close to over" and hinted at significant progress within days. A second round of talks is being arranged, and a ceasefire entered its second week. Markets have responded as though the conflict is effectively concluded: oil futures have fallen sharply, the Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index has retraced most of its war-driven gains, and equity markets globally have recovered their losses. However, the gap between market pricing and geopolitical reality remains meaningful. Operationally, the Strait of Hormuz remains near-closed. An Iraq-bound supertanker successfully transited the strait on its second attempt, making it the first crude carrier to do so since the US blockade on Iranian ports came into force — a data point that illustrates how thin actual traffic remains. WTI crude was trading near $91-92 per barrel, and Brent year-end futures are still priced around $80, implying that the market does not expect a full and immediate supply normalization. The Port of Los Angeles chief estimates that even after the strait reopens, full shipping normalization will take approximately four and a half months. The IMF's Spring Meetings provided a sobering macro backdrop. The Fund cut its global growth forecast and outlined a scenario in which prolonged conflict brings the world close to recession. Global trade volume growth is now projected at 2.8% for 2026, below global GDP growth of 3.1%. Brazil's new finance minister warned that a sustained conflict could force central banks worldwide to tighten to contain energy-driven inflation — a dynamic that would compound pressure on emerging market sovereigns already strained by high energy import costs. Russia's oil export position is itself under pressure: Ukrainian drone strikes on the Novorossiysk port have disrupted Black Sea flows just as Baltic exports recover, bringing Russia's four-week average shipments to their lowest since August at 3.22 million barrels per day. That constrains Russia's ability to fully capitalize on elevated prices and limits the degree to which it can substitute for lost Gulf supply. Meanwhile, the broader energy security debate is accelerating the case for geothermal power in Europe, where more than 60 companies and investors have written to EU leaders urging stronger support for a technology that is domestic, dispatchable, and — as one advocate noted — significantly harder for adversaries to attack than surface infrastructure.

  • US

    US equities continued their recovery on Monday, with the S&P 500 rising more than 1% to erase all losses recorded since the outbreak of the US-Iran conflict on February 28 — a milestone that has surprised many market observers given the scale of economic disruption since. The recovery appears driven less by geopolitical optimism than by structural momentum: momentum-driven strategies that were performing well ahead of the conflict never suffered a serious reversal, and investor appetite to resume the prior bull run has proven resilient. As SocGen's Andrew Lapthorne noted, the market does not appear to be pricing elevated tail risk so much as reflecting an investor base "conditioned to buy the dip" after three years and $35 trillion in MSCI All-World gains. Still, the macro backdrop has deteriorated materially since February 28. The 10-year Treasury yield has climbed from 3.94% to 4.28%, Brent crude has risen from roughly $72 to near $99 per barrel, and the IMF is expected to downgrade its global growth projections in its World Economic Outlook release. First-quarter earnings season began with disappointments from Goldman Sachs and industrial bellwether Fastenal, though bank earnings from JPMorgan, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo were due before Tuesday's open. The White House used the IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings in Washington to publish its annual Economic Report of the President, projecting 3.5% growth this year and an average of 3% through 2036 — figures well above Fed and IMF estimates. The administration attributed its bullish outlook to deregulation and immigration controls, and projected 10-year Treasury yields falling to 3.7% this year, against a current rate of approximately 4.28% and a CBO forecast of 4.4% for the early 2030s. The gap between White House projections and independent forecasts remains substantial. Soaring electricity costs are emerging as a notable political risk ahead of November midterm elections, with rapid AI data center buildout, tariff-related cost pressures, and grid upgrade spending pushing utility bills higher at rates not seen in decades. The Iran conflict's impact on gasoline prices has compounded the affordability strain.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets followed Wall Street higher on Tuesday, with the Nikkei 225 gaining 2.4% and South Korea's Kospi rising 2.7%, as investors focused on the possibility of resumed US-Iran negotiations rather than the formal initiation of a US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Taiwan's Taiex closed at a record high, posting a year-to-date gain of 25%, driven heavily by Taiwan Semiconductor as investor enthusiasm for AI supply-chain names reasserted itself. China faces a complex set of pressures. March trade data showed export growth slowing sharply while imports surged at the fastest pace since late 2021, as elevated energy commodity purchases reflected the war's disruption to global supply. The divergence underscores the dual squeeze: higher input costs weighing on margins while external demand softens. Chinese President Xi Jinping, meeting with Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez in Beijing, described the international order as "crumbling into disarray" — language observers noted was notably stronger than his customary characterizations of global turbulence. China's independent oil refiners — the so-called teapots, accounting for roughly a fifth of national refining capacity — remain under acute pressure. Heavily reliant on discounted Iranian crude, the sector is now exposed to both US sanctions enforcement and the blockade. Floating stockpiles of approximately 38 million barrels of Iranian crude in Asian waters provide a temporary buffer of perhaps two months, and Beijing has released strategic reserves and granted additional import quotas to sustain output. Nevertheless, if the blockade persists, the cushion will narrow. China's accelerating shift toward domestically-generated electricity from coal and renewables provides some structural insulation, but not enough to offset near-term refining stress. Singapore became the first Asian central bank to tighten monetary policy settings in response to rising inflation driven by higher energy prices — a move that may signal broader policy divergence across the region if crude remains elevated. Meanwhile, China Evergrande founder Hui Ka Yan pleaded guilty to fundraising fraud and bribery in a Chinese court, closing a significant chapter in the country's property sector restructuring.

    Europe

    Financial markets in Europe reacted positively to the prospect of resumed US-Iran talks, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 0.9%. The more consequential European story of the day, however, was political: Viktor Orbán's landslide defeat in Hungary's parliamentary elections, which markets had increasingly priced in as polls pointed toward opposition leader Péter Magyar. The Hungarian forint rose to a four-year high — surpassing its level at the time of Orbán's 2022 re-election — reflecting currency traders' relief at the prospect of restored access to frozen EU funds and a more conventional economic policy framework. The market judgment was on economic rather than political grounds: Hungary's growth has lagged peers Poland, Czechia, and Slovakia through the Orbán years, and forex markets consistently penalized the self-imposed isolation from EU financing. Magyar has indicated he intends a swift transition of power, supports eventual euro adoption, and wants to reintegrate Hungary into the EU's institutional framework. Orbán called a Fidesz party congress for April 28 to review the defeat. Elsewhere in Europe, the Iran conflict is generating tangible economic fallout. LVMH reported that sales at its fashion and leather goods division — home to Louis Vuitton — fell in the first quarter, with the war's dampening effect on demand cited alongside broader consumer caution. HSBC's CEO told Bloomberg Television that Middle East uncertainty is beginning to dent client confidence. The Bank of England's Andrew Bailey warned that private credit funds face heightened risks from one-off loss events that could threaten broader confidence in the sector. Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni called Trump's remarks about Pope Leo XIV "unacceptable," signaling a growing rift with Washington that reflects wider European discomfort over the Iran conflict's management. ECB President Christine Lagarde was due to speak at the IMF Spring Meetings, with markets attentive to any shift in guidance given the competing pressures of elevated energy-driven inflation and slowing regional growth.

    Global

    The central tension in global markets on April 14 was the coexistence of a formal US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and active diplomatic engagement aimed at avoiding a full rupture. President Trump's blockade, cutting off vessel traffic to and from Iranian ports, represents a significant escalation; yet simultaneous reports of prospective face-to-face negotiations before the April 7 ceasefire expires next week gave markets a constructive signal to focus on. WTI crude fell to approximately $97 per barrel as diplomacy dominated sentiment, though Brent remains near $99, roughly $27 above pre-war levels. The blockade's enforcement is already being tested. A US-sanctioned tanker linked to China — the Rich Starry — transited the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, creating a direct challenge to the mechanism's credibility and raising the possibility of US-China friction over energy access. The episode underscores a point made by several analysts: the blockade is, in part, a pressure tactic directed at Beijing as much as Tehran, given that China absorbs the overwhelming majority of Iranian crude exports. Iran, for its part, warned it would target ports across the Persian Gulf if its own shipping infrastructure is threatened. The International Energy Agency issued a striking forecast: global oil demand is expected to decline in 2026 for the first time since the pandemic, as the price surge caused by the conflict offsets underlying demand growth. The agency warned that "energy markets and economies around the world need to brace for significant disruptions in the months to come." The conflict is accelerating several longer-run shifts. China's lithium battery exports rose 50% year-on-year in the first quarter as buyers sought alternatives to oil-dependent energy, while the petroyuan concept has gained renewed traction after Iran accepted yuan payments for Strait transit. Delta Air Lines quietly downgraded its net-zero emissions goal to an "aspiration" and removed a 2030 sustainable aviation fuel target, illustrating how corporate climate commitments are being re-evaluated under the combined pressure of energy market disruption and political headwinds. Lebanon and Israel began the first direct US-hosted talks in decades, though Hezbollah's refusal to participate in or abide by any agreement reached limits the realistic scope of what can be achieved.

  • US

    Goldman Sachs kicks off the first-quarter US reporting season on Monday against a backdrop of deteriorating macro conditions. Analysts still project approximately 12% earnings growth for S&P 500 companies, though that estimate faces meaningful headwinds: March CPI printed at its highest level in more than two years, briefly pushing the headline rate back above the Fed's effective upper-range target of 3%. Core CPI and the trimmed mean remained below that threshold, and goods prices have not yet reflected the tariff shock as severely as many anticipated. However, the Fed's "supercore" services inflation ex-shelter ticked higher, and all four main CPI components are now rising — a configuration that appears to preclude near-term rate cuts. The Federal Reserve, which is due to install a new chairman next month pending Senate confirmation, appears firmly on hold. Bond markets have absorbed the inflation data poorly, with yields drifting higher and mortgage rates remaining elevated. Mortgage applications fell more than 10% through March, and existing home sales appear set to hold near 4.1 million annualized — roughly flat with last year. The pent-up demand that might have driven a housing recovery remains sidelined by financing costs. Consumer sentiment warrants attention in its own right. The University of Michigan's preliminary April index fell to 47.6, the weakest reading in the survey's history dating to 1978 — surpassing troughs reached during the 1980 hostage crisis, the 1990 Gulf War, and the Covid pandemic. Year-ahead inflation expectations rose a full percentage point to 4.8%. The political polarization embedded in sentiment data provides some analytical comfort, but even Republican respondents now report conditions worse than at any point during the Biden years. For an administration sensitive to the political costs of energy-driven inflation, the imperative to resolve the Hormuz situation quickly is substantial. S&P 500 futures were down roughly 0.6-0.7% as markets reopened Monday.

    Asia

    Asian markets absorbed the collapse of US-Iran talks and the subsequent US blockade announcement with moderate rather than acute selling pressure. A gauge of Asian equities fell around 0.8%, while Japan's Topix and South Korea's Kospi pared intraday losses; Taiwan's Taiex notably ended the session higher, supported by Taiwan's export data, which surged to an all-time high as global AI chip demand more than offset supply chain disruptions from the Strait closure. The more consequential dynamic may involve China. Iran is a major oil supplier to China, and Saudi Arabia's crude shipments to Chinese customers are reportedly set to halve in May — to approximately 20 million barrels from roughly 40 million in April — as Aramco raised official selling prices to a record. The US blockade raises a direct question about whether US warships will interdict vessels carrying Iranian oil to China, which would constitute a significant escalation in US-China tensions. Former US Ambassador to Saudi Arabia Michael Ratney flagged this risk explicitly. China has thus far condemned the US-Israeli strikes on Iran as "unacceptable" but President Xi has not publicly commented on the blockade. Spain's Prime Minister Sánchez, speaking at Tsinghua University during a Beijing visit, called on China to leverage its influence to end the Iran and Ukraine conflicts. Xi is scheduled to host Trump next month. In Japan, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda signaled a more cautious stance on rate hikes ahead of the next policy meeting, a notable pullback given that markets had anticipated tightening as recently as this month. Japan's 10-year government bond yield climbed to 2.49%, its highest level since 1997, as global bonds sold off on inflation concerns. Supply chain effects are becoming visible in the real economy: toilet manufacturer Toto suspended new prefabricated bathroom orders citing naphtha shortages, joining a growing list of Japanese manufacturers cutting production or raising prices as Persian Gulf oil feedstock supplies tighten.

    Europe

    Viktor Orbán's 16-year tenure as Hungarian Prime Minister ended Sunday when opposition leader Péter Magyar's Tisza party secured a supermajority in parliament, representing the most significant shift in Hungarian politics in over a generation. Magyar's victory carries immediate European implications: the incoming prime minister has pledged to dismantle Orbán's "illiberal" governance structures, reorient Hungary toward the EU and NATO, and unlock approximately €90 billion in EU funding that Orbán had blocked, including funds critical to Ukraine's defense. Leaders across Europe, including Starmer and Macron, offered congratulations. The forint surged to a four-year high following the result. The transition is not without complication — Magyar inherits real economic challenges that contributed to Orbán's political vulnerability — but the geopolitical dividend for Kyiv, Brussels, and the broader EU project appears substantial. The result is simultaneously a setback for both Trump, who had endorsed Orbán, and Putin, who had relied on Budapest to fracture EU sanctions consensus. Elsewhere in Europe, the macro environment is deteriorating under energy price pressure. UK household energy costs are tracking roughly £500 higher for a typical household, and the RICS homebuyer appetite gauge fell to its lowest since August 2023. Germany's coalition agreed on €1.6 billion in fuel-price relief measures for consumers; the EU is separately preparing a recommendation to lower energy taxes across member states. Ireland announced a fuel price relief package following public protests. Moody's warned that French elections risk stalling budget consolidation. Lufthansa shares fell 4.4% after pilots announced a two-day strike over pensions. LVMH reports quarterly sales after the Paris close Monday, serving as the first read on European luxury demand amid deteriorating consumer confidence.

    Global

    The collapse of 21 hours of US-Iran talks in Islamabad over the weekend and President Trump's subsequent announcement of a full naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz represent a meaningful escalation of the conflict that has disrupted global energy markets since the US and Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28. Brent crude rose as much as 9.1% to approach $104 a barrel on Monday's Asian open, with European natural gas futures spiking nearly 18% at one point. Aluminum reached a four-year high on concerns about long-run supply disruption from Persian Gulf producers. The strategic logic of the blockade, as articulated by advisers close to the situation, is to close Iran's toll-booth system while simultaneously working to reopen the mined half of the Strait through demining, escorts, and insurance backstops — thereby pressuring Tehran to concede politically. This is not an incoherent strategy, but it carries substantial execution risk: US warships would operate in closer proximity to Iranian drone and missile capabilities, and any vessel hit could trigger a rapid escalation cycle. Iran responded Monday by threatening that "no port in the Persian Gulf and Sea of Oman will be safe" if its own facilities are targeted. The UK government confirmed it will not participate in the blockade. The broader economic consequences are coming into sharper focus. Bloomberg Economics' base case now projects global growth of 2.9% in 2026 — the weakest since 2020 — with a downside scenario of 2.2% if oil averages $170 a barrel. The IMF will publish its downgraded World Economic Outlook on Tuesday, the fourth consecutive year its Spring Meetings have opened under conditions of acute global stress. Economist Carmen Reinhart argues the world is less prepared to respond to a crisis than at any point in the past two decades, constrained by a $348 trillion global debt burden, central banks still contending with inflation, and a fragmented multilateral order. Coordinated policy response appears unlikely. For now, the singular variable dominating the global outlook remains the duration and severity of the Hormuz disruption.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    China's economic policy framework is undergoing a structural shift that carries significant implications for global trade and capital allocation. Rather than deploying another round of demand-side stimulus in response to the energy-cost surge triggered by the Iran conflict, Beijing appears to be firmly committed to a supply-side reorientation articulated in its latest Five-Year Plan. The message, as Dong Tao of Springs Capital characterizes it, is unambiguous: fiscal and monetary policy will no longer serve as the primary levers of growth management. State support is being directed toward technology firms and the advancement of what Beijing terms "new productive forces," rather than infrastructure or consumer transfer payments. The consequences for household incomes are notable. Urban private-sector wage growth fell below 1% by end-2025, according to Natixis economist Alicia Garcia-Herrero, against a pre-pandemic rate of approximately 8%. The absence of specific numerical targets for consumption growth in the Five-Year Plan reinforces the assessment that domestic demand will remain structurally subdued. That, in turn, implies China's already-record trade surplus is likely to persist or widen — an outcome that sits in direct tension with calls from Washington, Paris, and others for external rebalancing. What is perhaps most consequential is the tone shift. Andrew Polk of Trivium China notes that at last month's China Development Forum, Premier Li Qiang rejected criticism of China's surpluses while signaling no meaningful course correction. China's plan, Polk argues, is to double down on industrial upgrading, which by definition requires export markets to absorb excess production. Separately, China ended more than three years of factory-gate deflation this week, a development that may signal early upstream price recovery, though the durability of that turn remains to be seen. India, meanwhile, roiled its $149 billion offshore rupee market by imposing a trading ban, adding a layer of regional financial uncertainty.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    The Iran conflict, which began with US and Israeli strikes on February 28, continues to reverberate through global energy markets. Oil and gas prices have risen materially since the onset of hostilities, with considerable volatility accompanying each new operational development. Even as the prospect of a Strait of Hormuz reopening provides intermittent relief to markets, prices remain elevated relative to pre-conflict levels, and the transmission to local economies — particularly across Asia — is becoming increasingly visible. The energy-cost surge is already compressing purchasing power in import-dependent economies and complicating the policy calculus for central banks navigating the growth-inflation tradeoff. The structural implications may prove more durable than the near-term price moves. Bloomberg's David Fickling argues the conflict may reshape Asia's energy systems over decades, potentially accelerating the reorientation of supply chains, LNG contracting, and energy infrastructure investment across the region. How quickly and completely the Strait reopens — and whether any resolution proves stable — remains a central uncertainty for commodity markets. On the climate and financial innovation front, prediction markets for weather and climate events are gaining traction, with trading volume on short-term weather contracts demonstrating measurable growth. Research by Patrick Brown at Interactive Brokers found that prediction markets outperformed US National Weather Service forecasts on at least some near-term weather events, lending credibility to the commercial application of these instruments. Institutional use cases — including reinsurance companies seeking better medium-term cyclone or El Niño forecasts through bespoke expert markets — represent a more targeted and arguably more defensible deployment of the prediction market framework. Analysts caution, however, that the complexity of longer-horizon climate projections may limit the applicability of popular market-based mechanisms beyond near-term meteorological events.

  • US

    US equity markets continued their recovery, with the S&P 500 recording its seventh consecutive day of gains — the longest winning streak since October — as the Iran ceasefire appeared to hold heading into weekend peace talks in Islamabad. US junk bond spreads have returned to pre-war levels, and the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index closed at a record high, reflecting a meaningful easing of financial conditions. The market's durability through a turbulent geopolitical period has been notable, though investors remain alert to weekend headline risk. The day's macro focus shifted to the March CPI release, which analysts expect to show the fastest monthly headline increase in nearly four years, driven by the energy price surge following the US-Iran conflict. That inflationary impulse is complicating the Federal Reserve's path, with Bloomberg Economics now projecting only two cuts this year, down from four forecast in January. The outlook was further complicated by an unusual joint action from Fed Chair Jerome Powell and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who summoned Wall Street bank CEOs to an urgent meeting over concerns that Anthropic's new AI model, Mythos — released to a limited set of firms — could enable a new generation of cyberattacks. Officials stressed that banks need to harden their defenses against AI-enabled threats, a signal that financial sector regulators are increasingly treating advanced AI as a systemic risk issue. In corporate developments, an index of SaaS stocks fell 4.8% on Thursday to its lowest level in over three years, extending a roughly 40% decline in 2026, as Anthropic's release of its Claude Managed Agents product and a new Meta model renewed fears that AI agents will erode the competitive moats of incumbent software vendors. Optical component maker Lumentum rose nearly 5% in premarket trading on accelerating demand from large technology companies, while Nike slipped after a downgrade citing athleisure market saturation. First-quarter S&P 500 earnings growth is now estimated at 12.5%, up from 10.9% prior to the war, with results from Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase due next week.

    Asia

    The dominant story across the Asia-Pacific region Friday was Xi Jinping's meeting in Beijing with Cheng Li-wun, chairwoman of Taiwan's Kuomintang opposition party and the first KMT leader to visit mainland China in a decade. Xi called for "cooperation and peace," while Cheng echoed the message and expressed hope that the Taiwan Strait would not become a "chessboard for external interference" — language calibrated to appeal to Beijing's longstanding opposition to what it views as foreign meddling. Xi simultaneously reaffirmed China's sovereignty claim over Taiwan, making the diplomatic warmth conditional rather than unconditional. The meeting carries explicit geopolitical signaling: with President Trump due to visit Beijing for a summit with Xi in mid-May, Beijing appears to be demonstrating to Washington that Taipei contains significant constituencies favoring engagement over deterrence. The KMT has stalled a $40 billion Taiwanese defense expansion and a $14 billion US arms deal has not received Trump's approval, reportedly to avoid jeopardizing the planned summit. On the economic front, China's factory-gate prices exited deflation in March, with PPI rising 0.5% year-on-year after a 0.9% decline in February — an inflection partly attributable to the global energy price surge following the Iran war. Consumer prices, however, continued to cool as holiday-season demand faded. Capital Economics cautioned the PPI upturn remains modest and may not persist. In a separate positive development for Chinese industry, EV and hybrid exports surged 140% year-on-year in March to a record 349,000 units, with BYD, Geely, and Chery leading shipments. Domestic auto sales remain under pressure — BYD's home sales fell more than 40% — underscoring the continued bifurcation between China's export competitiveness and subdued internal demand. Alibaba disclosed ownership of the "Happy Horse" AI video model, which debuted atop global rankings and displaced ByteDance's Seedance 2.0. Alibaba's cloud unit also led a $293 million funding round for ShengShu Technology, further intensifying competition in AI video generation. In India, the central bank's aggressive currency support effort — the boldest in a decade — risks deterring the foreign investors it has sought to attract, a tension that remains unresolved.

    Europe

    European markets traded higher Friday, with the Stoxx 600 gaining 0.6%, following Asian equities upward as the Iran ceasefire appeared to hold ahead of weekend talks in Islamabad. The macro backdrop, however, carries growing tightening implications. Bloomberg Economics now sees the European Central Bank shifting to rate hikes rather than cuts, reflecting the persistence of energy-driven inflationary pressure from the Middle East conflict. UK gilt markets remain under particular strain: the Bank of England, previously expected to cut, is now seen holding, with one fixed-income specialist noting that "for about a year gilts have been looking standout cheap" but that the war has upended the prior consensus. Hungarian politics commanded significant attention as Prime Minister Viktor Orbán faces his most serious electoral test in Sunday's vote. The outcome may hinge on older voters who consume primarily state-controlled media — outlets Orbán has shaped into government mouthpieces over 16 years in power — and who remain largely unaware of corruption allegations or reports of Russian influence. Should Orbán fall, it would mark the end of a political era in Central Europe, though the result remains genuinely uncertain. In Italy, Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni ousted the CEO of state-backed aerospace and defense contractor Leonardo while retaining the heads of Eni and Enel, a recalibration of executive leadership at state firms that follows a referendum defeat last month. The opposition is energized: Silvia Salis, a 40-year-old former Olympic hammer thrower and progressive mayor of Genoa, is emerging as a potential challenger, with some commentators already labeling her the "anti-Meloni." In corporate news, Sodexo plunged 20% in Paris after issuing weaker-than-expected revenue and margin guidance, and UBS confirmed it will not hand over privileged documents to the Credit Suisse Nazi-linked accounts investigation, citing legal exposure risks following its court loss earlier in the week.

    Global

    The defining macro theme this week has been the recalibration of global monetary policy expectations in response to the energy shock produced by the US-Iran conflict. Bloomberg Economics' aggregate measure of advanced-economy interest rates now sits approximately 35 basis points higher at the end of 2026 compared with projections from three months ago — a meaningful upward shift reflecting widespread recognition that damage to Middle East energy infrastructure may prove durable. The Fed, ECB, and Bank of England are all navigating asymmetric pressures: growth headwinds on one side, energy-driven inflation on the other. Oil markets remain central to the picture. WTI held near $99 per barrel Friday, on track for its steepest weekly loss in nine months as ceasefire optimism tempered the conflict premium, though Saudi Arabia's disclosure that Iranian attacks have impaired its own production capacity provided a partial floor. Strait of Hormuz traffic remains severely disrupted — only a handful of vessels transited on April 8-9 versus a pre-war norm of roughly 135 per day — and reopening the waterway is the critical near-term variable. Trump demanded Iran reopen the strait ahead of weekend talks in Islamabad, where Vice President JD Vance will lead the US delegation. Pakistan, which brokered the ceasefire, has gained significant diplomatic capital from hosting the talks, though it faces risks as a Saudi treaty ally should hostilities resume. The geopolitical implications extend well beyond the Hormuz. China's role in persuading Iran to accept a ceasefire — credited by Iranian officials to a last-minute Beijing push — illustrates the growing influence Beijing derives from its deep financial footprint across the Gulf, where it has outcompeted the US as a regional financier. Xi's $270 billion in Middle East investments constrain how openly China can back Iran, creating a nuanced position: quietly facilitating de-escalation while avoiding overt alignment with either side. Separately, the US-China space competition took a concrete step forward with the successful Artemis II mission, which broke the distance record for human spaceflight and puts a crewed lunar landing as early as 2028 on the table — approximately two years ahead of China's current timeline. The IPCC's funding and data challenges following the US withdrawal represent a quieter but consequential long-term development, with China projected to surpass the US in publicly funded research within two years.

  • US

    The dominant US market story on April 9 remains the aftermath of the US-Iran ceasefire, though the initial euphoria is already giving way to more measured assessment. Equities rallied sharply on Wednesday — S&P 500 futures have since eased, trading around 6,800 — while the rates market has not followed suit. Fed funds futures remain priced for zero cuts in 2026, a stark reversal from the two reductions that had been fully priced before hostilities began. Swaps markets now imply inflation above 3% over the next twelve months, against sub-2.25% at the start of the year, reflecting the accumulated energy shock rather than any geopolitical resolution. A growing number of Fed officials have reportedly flagged the risk that the Iran war stokes prices further, and with February core PCE data due, the inflation picture is unlikely to clarify in the near term. Earnings season arrives at an unusually fraught moment. FactSet projects S&P 500 earnings growth of roughly 13% year-on-year, which would mark a sixth consecutive quarter of double-digit gains, and Bloomberg Intelligence estimates full-year growth of approximately 16%. Energy and AI-linked technology are the principal contributors; the broader market's earnings trajectory has, if anything, deteriorated. Analysts at Morgan Stanley note the Magnificent Seven now trade at multiples comparable to consumer staples, a dislocation that has historically preceded multiple expansion, though the thesis requires confirmation that AI capital expenditure is translating into broad profitability — a question investors expect to dominate first-quarter conference calls. Separately, a Democratic congressman has written to the SEC and CFTC requesting an investigation into unusual trading activity in oil and equity futures markets prior to the postponement of US military strikes against Iran last month. Morgan Stanley launched a Bitcoin-tracking ETF under the ticker MSBT — the first such product from a traditional Wall Street investment bank — underscoring the asset class's institutional entrenchment even as Bitcoin has fallen roughly 40% from its October peak, trading near $71,000. On the corporate front, Constellation Brands guided below expectations on beer volume growth, while Marvell Technology and Texas Instruments gained on analyst upgrades.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets slipped modestly on Thursday as ceasefire optimism faded and oil prices partially rebounded, with WTI futures recovering toward $98-99 a barrel. The China CSI 300 fell 0.6%, while broader Asian sentiment remains fragile given the region's acute energy import dependence. China occupies a structurally ambiguous position in the current environment. While Bruegel warns that Beijing's reliance on Iranian crude represents a "severe test" for its energy strategy, Deutsche Bank's Jacky Tang argues China is the war's principal economic beneficiary given its status as the world's dominant clean-technology producer. As Japan, South Korea, and India accelerate energy diversification, the equipment required — solar panels, batteries, and associated infrastructure — will largely originate from Chinese manufacturers. Beijing is also weighing a substantial bailout for its state-run airlines, with options including subsidies, tax breaks, cheap state-backed loans, and potentially mergers, as elevated fuel costs push the "Big Three" carriers deeper into losses. Two fully laden Chinese supertankers are positioned at the mouth of the Strait of Hormuz, potentially among the first vessels to test the fragile transit arrangement. Japan's consumer confidence fell in March by the most since the early stages of the Covid pandemic, compounding concerns about a security vacuum in Asia as US military assets remain concentrated in the Persian Gulf. Seven & i Holdings delayed the planned US listing of its convenience store operations, sending shares down 4.6%, citing insufficient progress in turning around the business. Fast Retailing, by contrast, raised its outlook on strong Japan sales and global demand. In India, foreign investors have sold $17.8 billion in equities over 23 consecutive sessions — a record outflow pace — as higher crude prices fan inflation fears and squeeze margins in an import-dependent economy. State elections are running concurrently, functioning as a de facto referendum on Prime Minister Modi's handling of the energy crunch, with fuel shortages visible on the campaign trail. Sri Lanka, meanwhile, is nearing a $700 million IMF disbursement after clearing a program review, with growth and reserves tracking ahead of plan despite ongoing vulnerability to oil price volatility. TPG is exploring a sale or IPO of Asia OneHealthcare, with a potential valuation of up to $7.5 billion.

    Europe

    European markets opened with mixed futures on Thursday as ceasefire optimism gave way to renewed uncertainty over whether the Iran truce will hold. The Stoxx Europe 600 was down 0.7%, reflecting the broader retreat from Wednesday's risk-on surge. The EU's senior economy official warned that Europe remains at risk of stagflation despite the ceasefire, a concern reinforced by data showing German industrial production declined even before the outbreak of hostilities — suggesting structural weakness predates the energy shock. UK mortgage rates rose above 5% in March, according to RICS data, with homebuyer demand falling to its lowest since August 2023, demonstrating that the Middle East conflict is transmitting through borrowing costs into domestic property markets. Labour has slipped to fourth place in UK national polling ahead of May local elections, a notable deterioration in Prime Minister Starmer's political standing that may complicate the government's fiscal and policy choices. On geopolitics, the US has formally requested that European NATO allies submit concrete plans within days to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, marking a shift from rhetorical commitment to operational demand. Trump publicly berated the alliance again, raising the Greenland issue, after NATO members declined to participate in the Iran operation or offer basing rights. This friction is creating political space for alternative European alignments: former Italian Prime Minister Conte called for deeper engagement with China following repeated US tariff threats, while Hungary's Viktor Orbán faces the most serious electoral challenge of his sixteen-year tenure ahead of Sunday's vote — an outcome with ramifications extending from Brussels to Kyiv. Aldi and Lidl's expansion rivalry has become a minor illustration of continued UK consumer bifurcation, with both German discounters competing for a site in the affluent Norfolk town of Holt.

    Global

    The central market event of the week remains the fragile US-Iran ceasefire announced Tuesday, which has delivered a sharp but already wavering relief rally. World equities gained 3.26% on Wednesday; oil fell 12.1% — its largest single-day decline since April 2020 — before rebounding approximately 4% on Thursday as Tehran alleged ceasefire violations and Israeli strikes in Lebanon intensified. Vice President Vance will lead US-Iran talks in Islamabad on Saturday, and Trump has pledged to keep US forces in the Persian Gulf pending a final agreement. The Strait of Hormuz remains functionally closed. Only three vessels transited on Wednesday following the ceasefire announcement. Physical crude markets remain acutely stressed: Dated Brent struck a record above $140 per barrel on Tuesday before falling to around $120, still commanding a spread of more than $20 above benchmark for North Sea grades. The EIA estimates the conflict has removed approximately 314 million barrels from global supply to date — roughly 80% of the IEA's record emergency reserve release. Goldman Sachs has flagged Brent averaging above $100 through 2026 if the Strait remains closed for another month. Japan is reportedly considering releasing the equivalent of twenty days of consumption from strategic reserves. The structural consequences of the episode are becoming clearer across several dimensions. First, the economics of modern warfare have shifted materially: cheap attritable drones costing $20,000 are being neutralized by $4 million interceptors, creating an unsustainable cost asymmetry that is drawing US missile inventories away from Indo-Pacific theaters and raising concerns about a security vacuum in Asia — a point explicitly raised by Japan's defense minister. Second, the energy transition is being accelerated by necessity rather than policy, with Asian economies now facing urgent pressure to reduce Middle East import dependence; China's position as the dominant clean-technology supplier makes it a structural beneficiary of this reorientation. Third, emerging market capital flows, which had reached near two-decade highs earlier in 2026, suffered approximately $60 billion in equity outflows during the crisis — a concentrated rather than systemic shock, differentiated primarily by energy import dependence. Wednesday's rally partially reversed those outflows, but the durability of the recovery hinges on whether the Strait reopens in practice, not merely in principle.

  • US

    US equity futures staged a sharp rally on April 8 as markets responded to the US-Iran ceasefire announcement, with S&P 500 futures gaining 2.6% and Nasdaq 100 futures up 3.3% in early trading. The immediate beneficiaries were technology and semiconductor stocks — Micron rose nearly 10% and Western Digital gained 8.3% on expectations that ceasefire conditions would help unlock helium supplies critical to chip manufacturing — while energy names moved sharply in the opposite direction, with Exxon Mobil down 5.8% and Occidental Petroleum falling 8.1% in premarket. Fertilizer stocks, which had risen on supply disruption fears, also gave back gains. Levi Strauss provided a brighter note, with shares rallying 12% on better-than-expected results driven by direct-to-consumer strength. On the monetary policy front, Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson acknowledged that the Iran conflict will push near-term US inflation higher while characterizing current policy as appropriate. The Fed's March meeting minutes are due later Wednesday. The more pressing near-term data catalysts arrive Thursday and Friday, with PCE for February followed by the March CPI print. Both are expected to confirm headline inflation has risen materially, with gasoline's year-on-year increase moving from roughly zero to more than 25% in barely a month, likely pushing the headline rate back above 3%. The ISM non-manufacturing survey offered an additional caution signal: more than 70% of purchasing managers reported rising input prices, the largest monthly increase in 13 years, which some analysts characterize as increasingly consistent with stagflationary conditions, though strong new orders provide a partial offset. Falling oil prices have revived rate-cut expectations, with bond markets rallying and the 10-year Treasury yield declining to 4.24%. Longer-term inflation expectations remain relatively anchored — the five-year/five-year breakeven rate sits below 2.2% — suggesting markets are treating the energy shock as transitory. Kevin Warsh's confirmation as incoming Fed chair remains pending in the Senate, with an expected bias toward rate cuts if inflation data permit.

    Asia

    Asian markets registered broad gains following the ceasefire announcement, with the Topix recovering to its highest level since the war began on February 28 and the China CSI 300 Index advancing 3.5%. The relief rally reflected the unwinding of war-premium positioning rather than any fundamental improvement in regional economic conditions, which remain under strain from the energy shock. China has been one of the most consequential actors in managing the supply disruption. Beijing granted independent teapot refiners additional crude import quotas to allow them to maintain fuel production at mandated levels amid the Persian Gulf disruption — a practical measure that reveals the extent to which China's energy security was stretched. On the LNG side, however, the picture appears more structurally challenging. Hopes for a strong rebound in Chinese LNG demand are fading even with the ceasefire in place; BloombergNEF projects imports will fall to 62.3 million tons in 2026, down from 68.4 million tons last year — a rare decline in nearly two decades of near-uninterrupted growth. Supply risks and elevated prices are expected to persist regardless of ceasefire terms. The US-China relationship remained in focus as Trade Representative Jamieson Greer promoted the concept of a bilateral board of trade ahead of the anticipated Trump-Xi summit next month. Greer downplayed a parallel investment-focused group and indicated his pre-summit agenda would center on rare earths flows. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi is scheduled to visit North Korea this week for the first time in over six years, a visit that underscores Beijing's effort to consolidate relationships amid shifting geopolitical alignments. The Reserve Bank of India held rates unchanged, with Governor Sanjay Malhotra acknowledging the ceasefire while cautioning that persistent supply chain disruptions continue to pose risks to growth and inflation. The rupee strengthened 0.5% following his remarks, partially reversing a roughly 7% decline over the past year. In Japan, real wages rose at the fastest pace since 2021, supporting the case for continued Bank of Japan normalization.

    Europe

    Europe's market reaction to the ceasefire was broadly positive, with equity futures and bond prices both rallying as WTI crude fell approximately 16% on the day. European natural gas and diesel prices briefly slumped around 20%, reflecting the intensity of the war premium that had built up over the prior six weeks. The relief, however, is tempered by genuine uncertainty about how quickly energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz will normalize, and European industry remains more directly exposed to the supply shock than its US counterpart. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer traveled to the Gulf Wednesday for meetings with regional leaders, seeking to leverage Britain's role in organizing multilateral diplomatic efforts around Hormuz — the UK chaired a meeting of approximately 35 nations to coordinate pressure on Iran, notably in the absence of the US. Starmer has also used his government's early divergence from Washington's position on the war as a domestic political asset ahead of local elections, criticizing both Reform UK's Nigel Farage and Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch for their initial support of the US-Israeli campaign. Britain faces its own economic pressures: real wages are stagnating, consumer prices are elevated, unemployment appears set to rise, and the Bank of England's rate is expected to remain well above the fed funds rate by year-end as UK inflation stays comparatively sticky. In Germany, measures to alleviate surging fuel costs have centered on regulatory adjustments rather than fiscal outlays. European inflation dynamics differ somewhat from the US: the euro zone's five-year/five-year inflation breakeven has risen but remains below 2%, though the gap has widened relative to the US since the war began. France moved to fast-track emergency agricultural legislation to address a deteriorating food trade balance, while EDF pledged €240 million to accelerate heat pump and electric truck adoption as subdued electricity demand and continued renewable buildout have depressed power prices. Moody's cut the outlook on Blue Owl Capital's flagship fund to negative, adding to broader signals of stress in private capital markets.

    Global

    The dominant development of April 8 is the agreement between the US and Iran on a two-week ceasefire, brokered with Pakistan serving as an intermediary. Under the terms as understood, the American-Israeli military campaign is to halt in exchange for Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping. The oil market's response was immediate: Brent crude fell as much as 16% to approximately $91.70 a barrel, and WTI tumbled nearly 19% — among the largest single-day declines in crude oil history, comparable only to the Covid demand collapse in 2020 and the allied entry into Kuwait in January 1991. The historical parallel to the Kuwait crisis is instructive: oil more than doubled during that conflict before returning to pre-crisis levels within twelve months. The current shock has traced a broadly similar arc, though it appears to be resolving more rapidly. Important caveats remain. Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz showed little immediate change on Wednesday, with more than 800 vessels still trapped in the Persian Gulf. Shipowners, insurers, and industry bodies including Bimco are awaiting technical clarity on transit procedures before committing vessels to re-enter. Iran's intent to levy fees on passing ships — a form of de facto toll on the waterway — introduces a novel geopolitical dynamic that Bloomberg Opinion's Javier Blas characterizes as transforming what was a free international waterway into a controlled one. Iran and the US are each characterizing the ceasefire as a victory, while analysts describe the arrangement as a fragile starting framework. Several Arab Gulf states reported continued Iranian missile attacks in the immediate aftermath of Trump's announcement, and both Kuwait and the UAE subsequently reported fresh strikes. Pakistan's emergence as the key mediating channel — leveraging ties with Saudi Arabia, Iran, the US, and China — reflects a meaningful realignment of diplomatic influence in the region. The broader geopolitical fallout for US alliances appears significant: not a single NATO member or Gulf ally directly joined the US-Israeli campaign, and French President Macron publicly called on medium powers to assert greater independence from Washington. The IMF is poised to cut its global growth forecasts as a result of the conflict, while the World Bank cautioned governments against worsening fiscal positions through poorly targeted stimulus, urging that any measures be transparent, temporary, and targeted.

  • US

    Financial conditions in the United States remain taut ahead of President Trump's 8 p.m. Eastern Time deadline for Iran to agree to a ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with S&P 500 futures oscillating in a narrow range—erasing overnight losses before settling near flat—as traders weigh the possibility of resolution against the risk of further escalation. Oil's 50% surge since the conflict began in late February is now transmitting clearly into the domestic economy: retail diesel prices have risen nearly 50% to $5.62 per gallon, gasoline has crossed $4 per gallon for the first time since 2022, and the Logistics Manager's Index climbed to 65.7 in March—its highest reading since May 2022—as transportation costs surge and inventories remain lean relative to the prior shock cycle. Bloomberg Economics projects March CPI will show the largest month-over-month headline increase since June 2022, penciling in a 0.9% gain, while core PCE for February—due Thursday—is seen rising 0.4%, keeping the year-over-year rate above 3%. The parallels with 2022 are becoming difficult to dismiss, though Bloomberg Economics does not currently project a recession absent a significant further widening in credit spreads. In markets, health insurers rallied sharply after CMS proposed a 2.5% increase in Medicare Advantage payment rates for 2027, with Humana gaining 11% and UnitedHealth rising 7%. Broadcom advanced 3.6% in premarket trading following a chip development agreement with Google. Private credit continues to draw attention after JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon's annual shareholder letter reiterated concerns about weakening credit standards across leveraged lending, while stopping short of declaring systemic risk—though Blue Owl Capital shares have now retraced to their 2020 IPO price. SpaceX has begun investor meetings for a potential IPO that could value the company above $2 trillion. The Supreme Court's earlier ruling invalidating emergency-powers tariffs and a subsequent Court of International Trade order mandating $160 billion in refunds to importers represent a meaningful fiscal and legal development that has received less attention than geopolitical headlines.

    Asia

    Asian markets displayed tentative resilience on Tuesday, with the MSCI Asia index gaining close to 1% overnight as investors assigned some probability to a diplomatic outcome before Trump's deadline, though conditions remained fragile given Iran's continued strikes across the Gulf in the hours preceding the cutoff. Samsung Electronics reported a striking eightfold increase in quarterly profit, driven by robust AI memory chip demand that has thus far defied the market disruption generated by the war; shares rose as much as 4.9% in Seoul. The result underscores the degree to which AI-related semiconductor demand has become a stabilizing force for the Korean technology complex even as broader market sentiment deteriorates. India's macro position warrants close attention. The Reserve Bank of India is expected to hold rates at its Wednesday meeting, caught between the need to support slowing growth and the currency pressure generated by India's acute dependence on Middle Eastern energy—the country sources roughly half its crude and most of its cooking gas from that region. The rupee has declined more than 3% year-to-date, prompting what the RBI describes as some of its most aggressive intervention steps in over a decade. State Bank of India separately disclosed approximately $5 billion in positions against the rupee that were caught by the regulator's crackdown on currency speculators, with estimated losses of around $32 million from forced unwinds. China's housing stress deepened in focus, with lenders increasingly confronting underwater mortgages as property values continue to fall, raising the risk of borrower defaults and complicating banks' efforts to preserve asset quality without triggering a broader credit deterioration. Separately, China's central bank purchased the most gold in more than a year in March, a signal of continued reserve diversification. In Taiwan, the Kuomintang's chair began a six-day visit to Beijing—the first by a sitting KMT leader in a decade—raising cross-strait engagement questions at a sensitive geopolitical moment.

    Europe

    European equity markets showed modest gains on Tuesday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 rising 0.7%, though the region faces a more challenging medium-term backdrop than the headline number suggests. Earnings estimate revisions have turned negative for European equities even as US estimates hold in positive territory—a reversal from the earlier-year narrative in which European stocks were outperforming on the back of fiscal stimulus expectations and ECB easing. The European Central Bank's next policy move now appears likely to be a rate increase rather than a cut, potentially as early as this month, as energy-driven inflation reasserts itself. Britain and France are identified by BloombergNEF as the most exposed European markets to diesel and kerosene supply disruption, given their reliance on refined product imports from Kuwait and Saudi Arabia respectively. Several Italian airports have already issued limited fuel supply advisories. ASML declined 4.7% in Amsterdam after US lawmakers introduced legislation to tighten chip tool export restrictions to China, a move with significant implications for one of Europe's most strategically important technology companies. Universal Music Group shares surged 11% in Amsterdam after Bill Ackman proposed a deal valuing the company at approximately $65 billion. H&M remains under pressure, with the retailer's CEO facing investor skepticism over a turnaround strategy squeezed between Inditex at the premium end and Shein and Primark at the value end—a credibility problem compounded by broader consumer confidence concerns as the conflict sustains elevated fuel costs. Hungary's election on Sunday carries outsize geopolitical weight for the EU: polls suggest Viktor Orbán's grip may be loosening, with JD Vance campaigning alongside him in Budapest and the outcome carrying direct implications for EU Ukraine policy and the bloc's Russia sanctions architecture.

    Global

    The defining macro variable of April 2026 remains the Strait of Hormuz, effectively closed since the US-Israeli offensive against Iran began on February 27. The waterway normally channels approximately one-fifth of global oil and LNG supply; its sustained blockage has driven Brent crude up roughly 50% since the conflict began, with WTI prompt spreads trading near record backwardation levels—close to $15.50 per barrel at one point Monday—signaling acute near-term supply anxiety. Trump's 8 p.m. Tuesday deadline for Iran to agree to a deal including Hormuz reopening represents the latest in a series of ultimatums that have each produced market volatility without resolution; Iran continued missile and drone attacks across the Gulf in the hours before the deadline, clouding the outlook. The secondary effects are now widespread. The Fed Reserve Bank of New York's Global Supply Chain Pressure Index rose in March to its highest since January 2023. Asia is bearing the greatest burden: multiple countries have reported fuel shortages, and some have implemented four-day working weeks. The conflict is accelerating a coal renaissance in Asia as LNG alternatives become unavailable, while simultaneously generating a meaningful uplift in EV demand—UK registrations hit a record 86,120 units in March, and similar trends are visible in Thailand and the US used-car market. The IEA has described the supply disruption as the largest in market history, and OPEC+ has warned of prolonged output impacts even after hostilities end. The dollar has risen roughly 2.2% since hostilities began, partially reversing an 8%-plus decline in 2025, though analysts including Robin Brooks of Brookings argue the move reflects safe-haven flows rather than a structural shift and project a further 10% trade-weighted decline this year driven by political pressure on the Fed to cut rates despite elevated inflation. Foreign central bank holdings at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York have fallen approximately $82 billion since late February to $2.7 trillion—the lowest since 2012—as Gulf producers liquidate Treasuries to defend their currencies and fund wartime expenditures, a structural erosion of the petrodollar recycling mechanism. The UN Security Council was due to vote on a softened Hormuz resolution hours before Trump's deadline, with Russian and Chinese vetoes remaining possible.

  • US

    US equity futures edged higher early Monday, gaining around 0.4% on the S&P 500, lifted by an Axios report that the US, Iran, and regional mediators are exploring terms for a potential 45-day ceasefire. Trading remained thin, with European and several Asian markets closed for Easter Monday, and the durability of the move appeared contingent on news flow ahead of a Trump press conference scheduled for 1 p.m. Washington time. The geopolitical backdrop remained deeply unsettled. Over the weekend, President Trump posted an explicit ultimatum demanding Iran "Open the Strait" by Tuesday or face attacks on power plants and bridges — following the destruction of Iran's largest bridge and the rescue of an F-15E weapons officer after two days of special forces operations. Prediction markets on Polymarket marked down the probability of a ceasefire by end-June to roughly 50/50, a significant deterioration from last week's more optimistic positioning. Against this backdrop, Friday's non-farm payrolls print — released during the Good Friday holiday — offered a modestly constructive data point. March payrolls rose 178,000, the strongest gain since 2024, and the Sahm Rule shows no current recessionary signal. However, the report warrants caution: wage growth is slowing across multiple measures, JOLTS data show hiring near post-GFC lows, quit rates are declining, and average unemployment duration has re-extended above six months. The picture is one of a labor market that is softening gradually rather than collapsing — consistent with a pre-recessionary plateau rather than clear resilience. The Federal Reserve's March meeting minutes, due Wednesday, may offer insight into how policymakers are weighing the Iran-driven inflation shock against these mixed labor signals. Economists expect March CPI — also due this week — to show a roughly 1% month-on-month increase, the sharpest reading since 2022, driven primarily by the approximately $1-per-gallon surge in gasoline prices. That combination of slowing labor markets and accelerating inflation creates a genuinely uncomfortable policy dilemma for the Fed, with rate cuts harder to justify and rate hikes difficult to defend absent clearer demand-side inflationary pressure.

    Asia

    Several major Asian markets remained closed Monday for Easter, limiting price discovery, though Nikkei 225 futures indicated modest gains of around 0.5% in early trading. The session's dominant themes were energy market stress and geopolitical uncertainty emanating from Iran. Saudi Arabia raised the official selling price of its main crude grade to Asia to a record high premium, though the magnitude came in at roughly half what surveyed analysts had anticipated — a reflection of the acute difficulty in gauging near-term demand given war-related volatility. The adjustment nonetheless underscores the structural repricing of energy costs being absorbed by Asian importers, most of whom lack the domestic production buffers of Western economies. Japan is among the most exposed, given its near-total dependence on imported energy. Japan's Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi marks six months in office this week, a tenure that has delivered meaningful policy shifts — most notably a credible pivot away from deflation and a sustained move higher in JGB yields.
