Yet the galvanizing effect her election had on Japanese equities has been largely offset by the Iran war shock. The yen continues to weaken despite significantly higher domestic yields, a divergence that appears increasingly unstable and will likely require either policy intervention or a forced adjustment in positioning. Takaichi's political capital remains intact for now, but the energy import burden represents a mounting structural headwind. The Indian rupee extended what the central bank described as its largest rally in 12 years, following the Reserve Bank of India's reinforced stance against speculative positioning. India's equity markets, however, have fared poorly in the year since Liberation Day tariffs and the onset of the Iran conflict, with the "Chindia" trade — once a consensus long for emerging market allocations — having underperformed sharply. The combination of tariff disruption and energy cost inflation has weighed particularly heavily on both China and India. Whether a durable re-entry point is approaching remains to be seen, likely contingent on clarity around the Iran conflict's trajectory.

    Europe

    European markets were closed Monday for Easter Monday, leaving price discovery to thin futures markets. The week ahead carries several significant data releases and policy decisions for the region. Eurozone services PMI data are due Tuesday, euro-area retail sales and PPI figures follow Wednesday, and Germany's industrial production print arrives Thursday — all of which will be read in the context of Iran-driven energy inflation that is already reshaping the political economy across the continent. The most substantive European story of the moment is unfolding at the state level rather than in Brussels. Soaring energy prices are generating meaningful political pressure on climate regulation across both sides of the Atlantic. In Europe, the Iran war is accelerating what was already a visible tension between the energy transition agenda and near-term affordability constraints. The broader debate — whether the Iran conflict ultimately accelerates the shift to renewables by demonstrating fossil fuel vulnerability, or temporarily weakens it by making affordability the dominant political priority — remains genuinely unresolved and is being contested in real time across multiple European capitals. On the corporate side, Unilever's €44.8 billion sale of its food business to McCormick & Co. was among the headline transactions contributing to a record global M&A first quarter. European dealmaking has benefited from the same strategic logic driving activity globally — portfolio transformation and long-term positioning — though the war-related deterioration in earnings multiples over the past month has complicated valuations and may weigh on second-quarter activity. The ECB's path remains contingent on how durably the energy shock passes through to core inflation; rate decisions in Romania this week and New Zealand and India on Wednesday offer secondary reads on how emerging market central banks are navigating the same dilemma.

    Global

    The defining macro development of the moment remains the Iran war and its cascading effects on energy markets. Brent crude's prompt spread — the difference between its two nearest futures contracts — widened above $10 per barrel in backwardation over the past week, the most acute reading since the conflict began and surpassing peaks reached following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Dated Brent, the benchmark for physical market transactions, briefly surged above $140 per barrel ahead of Easter, the highest level since 2008. OPEC+ warned Sunday that damage to Middle Eastern energy infrastructure will produce prolonged supply disruption extending well beyond any eventual ceasefire, a supply-side assessment that should be taken seriously given the group's direct visibility into regional production capacity. The geopolitical situation deteriorated meaningfully over the Easter weekend. Iran downed two US warplanes; US forces rescued the surviving aircrew after a two-day operation; the US destroyed Iran's largest bridge; and Iranian forces escalated attacks on Gulf energy facilities. President Trump's ultimatum — demanding the Strait of Hormuz reopen by Tuesday or face strikes on Iranian power plants — did little to suggest imminent de-escalation. Prediction markets assign roughly even odds to a ceasefire by end-June; a protracted conflict beyond that horizon would present a more severe challenge to global growth and financial stability. Global M&A recorded its strongest first quarter on record at approximately $1.3 trillion in transaction value, roughly 20% above the prior year, suggesting corporate strategic agendas remained intact through the conflict's early weeks. However, deal values have declined around 15% since the war began, indicating a meaningful frontloading effect and growing caution. Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon characterized the outlook as cautiously constructive but explicitly flagged that a protracted war or additional exogenous shock could alter sentiment materially. Private credit markets — a critical funding source for leveraged transactions — are showing signs of stress, adding a further constraint on deal financing conditions if inflation forces the Fed toward tightening. The war's ultimate impact on the global dealmaking cycle, as with so much else, remains contingent on developments in Iran.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    Australian authorities are grappling with a sharp rise in e-bike-related injuries and public safety incidents, reflecting broader tensions between green transport ambitions and the practical challenges of regulating a fast-growing category of micro-mobility devices. The issue gained particular visibility in February when approximately 40 teenage riders on e-bikes disrupted traffic on the Sydney Harbour Bridge, an incident that went viral and intensified political pressure on state and federal governments. Serious injuries involving e-bikes have risen materially over the past two years, with trauma physicians pointing to a "perfect storm" of looser import standards, higher power limits, and surging adoption among younger riders. The regulatory backdrop is instructive. Australia's federal government relaxed import safety standards in 2021, allowing higher-powered models into the market that industry participants describe as closer to motorbikes than bicycles. New South Wales compounded the issue in 2023 by raising the legal power ceiling to 500 watts, putting the state out of alignment with national norms and, critics argue, incentivising further tampering. Both decisions are now being unwound: New South Wales last month restored the 250-watt European standard, and the federal government reintroduced European import standards late in 2025. Minimum rider age requirements, likely in the 12–16 range, are under active consideration. The episode illustrates a regulatory pattern evident across multiple jurisdictions — initial enthusiasm for low-carbon transport alternatives outpacing the governance frameworks needed to manage them safely. Whether Australia's course correction proves sufficient remains to be seen, as enforcement of power limits and age restrictions has historically been difficult in practice.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme in this period's coverage is the intersection of the ongoing US-Iran conflict with energy markets and the accelerating, if uneven, transition away from fossil fuels. Oil prices are described as continuing their "wild ride," with supply shock concerns persisting amid the Middle East conflict. The key strategic question being debated among policymakers and investors is whether an extended period of elevated oil prices will catalyse a durable shift toward renewables and alternative transport, or whether governments will default to demand-side subsidies — fuel tax cuts — that relieve near-term cost-of-living pressure while reinforcing hydrocarbon dependency. Spain, South Africa, and Poland are among countries that have implemented or are considering significant fuel tax reductions, a response that advocates warn could strain both road maintenance revenues and long-term decarbonisation trajectories. Climate and energy transition investors appear to be watching the conflict closely for its second-order effects. Commentary from clean energy nonprofit RMI suggests that sustained high oil prices create a "behavioral opening" for switching, and that city-level infrastructure interventions — bus lanes, cycling infrastructure — can be deployed cheaply and quickly enough to demonstrate results before public support erodes. The historical precedent most frequently cited is the 1973 oil crisis, which redirected Dutch and Danish transport planning away from car-centric expansion and toward cycling infrastructure, a shift now widely credited with shaping those cities' modal mix for decades. On the energy technology side, the US AI infrastructure buildout's dependence on Chinese electrical equipment was flagged as a structural vulnerability, adding a further dimension to the broader US-China technology decoupling narrative. Global air quality worsened in 2025, according to separate reporting, a data point that sits in tension with the volume of clean energy investment flowing into the sector and suggests that emissions reductions have yet to translate into measurable atmospheric improvement at scale.

  • US

    The oil price shock now in its fifth week is beginning to register in US consumer behavior and state-level fiscal policy. Average gasoline prices have topped $4 per gallon for the first time since 2022, prompting Georgia to implement a 60-day gas tax holiday, with several other states exploring similar measures. US public transit agencies are reporting early signs of a ridership uptick, consistent with historical patterns observed during prior oil shocks, though the magnitude and durability of any modal shift remains uncertain. The Trump administration has so far shown limited appetite for demand-side energy conservation measures, declining to revisit its posture on non-gasoline transportation, a stance that places it at odds with IEA guidance encouraging speed limit reductions, car-sharing, and remote work promotion. The divergence between federal inaction and state-level responses may signal an uncoordinated policy environment that could complicate the macro outlook if prices rise further. On the fiscal side, a proliferation of state-level tax holidays, while politically expedient, carries revenue implications that deserve monitoring in the context of already-stretched state budgets. No major Federal Reserve communications or US equity market developments were reported in today's coverage, though the sustained elevation in energy costs is a meaningful input into the inflation trajectory the Fed is navigating. Energy's pass-through to core inflation will depend on the duration of the Strait of Hormuz disruption and the degree to which demand destruction offsets price pressure. The political economy of the conflict — with President Trump offering no firm end date — adds to uncertainty around that timeline and complicates the Fed's forward guidance calculus.

    Asia

    China is emerging as a relative outperformer amid the Middle East crisis, though the picture is nuanced. The export-oriented economy expanded in March for the first time in 2026, Chinese equities have declined approximately 5.5% since the US-Israel assault on Iran began in late February — meaningfully less than the roughly 10% drawdowns recorded in South Korea, Japan, and India over the same period. This relative resilience appears to reflect the structural investments Beijing has made in renewable energy, diversified export markets, and strategic oil reserves, all of which provide a degree of insulation from the crude price shock that peers lack. BYD reported overseas sales of 120,083 vehicles in March, a three-month high, as elevated fuel prices accelerate EV adoption globally; the company has raised its 2026 export target to 1.5 million units from 1.3 million. In a strategic corporate development, TCL Electronics announced it will acquire a 51% stake in a new joint venture absorbing Sony's home entertainment business for approximately $475 million, a move that would position the combined entity above Samsung in global TV market share at roughly 16.7%. The deal exemplifies Chinese manufacturers' strategy of acquiring premium Western brand equity to penetrate higher-margin consumer segments in Europe and the US. Nevertheless, cracks are appearing in China's resilience narrative. A decline in shipping container volumes out of Chinese ports is a leading indicator worth watching. Smaller manufacturers reliant on oil-derived inputs are facing margin pressure, and the manufacturing PMI for smaller exporters slowed in March. Beijing's unusual activism in peace negotiations — including a five-point joint proposal with Pakistan calling for safeguarding Strait of Hormuz shipping — reflects the economic stakes for a country that sourced roughly a third of its growth from exports last year.

    Europe

    European cities and policymakers are navigating the fifth week of elevated energy costs with a mixture of demand-side interventions and fiscal support. EU officials in Brussels are urging residents to work from home and reduce driving, while France has rolled out an aid package that includes fuel subsidies for the transport sector. Melbourne's temporary suspension of public transit fares — though an Australian rather than European measure — illustrates the range of policy responses being deployed across developed economies facing similar pressure. In the European automotive market, the oil price shock is producing a meaningful structural tailwind for Chinese EV manufacturers. Chinese brands' share of fully electric car sales has risen two percentage points to approximately 8% of the total European EV market, nearly double their share from a year prior. Bernstein analysts note that BYD stands to benefit most from an acceleration in EV adoption driven by elevated fuel costs. The trend is strategically significant: it compounds competitive pressure on European legacy automakers already grappling with the EV transition and tightening emissions standards. In the UK, sales of Chinese-made goods grew more than three times as fast in the first two months of 2026 as in the comparable 2025 period, a dynamic that is providing an effective disinflationary offset to energy-driven price pressures. The net effect on UK inflation — a key variable for Bank of England policy — depends on the balance between these two forces, which pulls in opposite directions and may complicate the rate path. No specific ECB communications were covered in today's material.

    Global

    The US-Israel military campaign against Iran, now entering its second month, is the dominant macro variable shaping global energy markets, trade flows, and geopolitical alignments. The Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed to shipping, a constraint that is reverberating across oil-dependent economies in Asia, South Asia, and parts of Europe. Both Pakistan and Thailand, which rely heavily on Persian Gulf oil transiting the Strait, are experiencing direct fuel cost pressures. China and Pakistan issued a five-point joint peace proposal this week calling for the restoration of shipping through the Strait, a signal that Beijing's calculus is shifting from passive beneficiary to active mediator as the economic costs of prolonged conflict accumulate. President Trump has provided no firm timeline for the campaign's conclusion, sustaining the uncertainty premium embedded in energy prices. The carbon markets are experiencing collateral damage from a separate but significant stress event. Koko Networks, a World Bank-backed clean cookstove company serving approximately 1.3 million Kenyan households, filed for bankruptcy in January amid disputed claims over credit issuance accuracy. The collapse has depressed prices on the Corsia aviation offset marketplace from approximately $15 to $12.85, tightened already-scarce supply of high-quality credits, and extended buyer decision-making timelines ahead of mandatory airline compliance beginning in 2027. The episode reinforces persistent credibility concerns about the avoided-emissions methodology underpinning much of the voluntary carbon market, and arrives at an awkward moment given the UN's newly operational country-to-country trading framework. Whether the Koko episode represents an idiosyncratic failure or a systemic indicator of methodological fragility across the sector remains genuinely unresolved.

  • US

    US equity markets are extending their now-familiar pattern of late-week deterioration, with S&P 500 futures down roughly 1.1% as of Thursday morning. Since the Iran conflict began five weeks ago, the index has posted cumulative gains over the first three days of each week before cratering on Thursdays and Fridays — a net loss of approximately 9% on those two sessions alone. The dynamic reflects a straightforward calculus: with weekends offering ample time for geopolitical developments and President Trump historically inclined to announce major moves when markets are closed, investors are increasingly choosing to reduce risk rather than carry positions into a trading blackout. The upcoming long weekend amplifies that anxiety, with the monthly nonfarm payrolls report due Friday when equity markets are shut. Trump's primetime address, in which he pledged to hit Iran "extremely hard" over the next two to three weeks while simultaneously claiming the war is "very close" to completion, generated precisely the mixed-signal reaction that market participants have come to expect. The strategic ambiguity — promising escalation while signaling an endgame — appears to satisfy neither bulls seeking resolution nor bears seeking clarity on duration. Treasury yields rose across the curve as oil surged, with traders rapidly paring expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts. The Fed faces an increasingly uncomfortable configuration: energy-driven inflation that shows little sign of abating against a backdrop of slowing growth and elevated policy uncertainty. On the trade front, the administration is preparing to announce a tiered system shifting steel and aluminum tariffs from metal content to full product value — a structural simplification that has been under discussion since February. Separately, 100% tariffs on pharmaceutical imports are reportedly imminent for companies that have not struck bilateral deals with the White House. Both announcements fall on the one-year anniversary of Trump's original "Liberation Day" tariff declaration. US manufacturing ISM data for March showed the sharpest expansion since 2022, though the prices-paid subindex climbed to 78.3 — its highest since mid-2022 — underscoring that input cost pressures remain acute. SpaceX has also filed confidentially for an IPO that would rank among the largest in history.

    Asia

    Asia is navigating the Hormuz disruption without a unified regional framework, with each major economy improvising its own response. India has been negotiating safe passage arrangements directly with Tehran, while China has floated a diplomatic peace proposal. Japan is reportedly engaging with a 35-country coalition led by London to restore navigation. Trump's instruction for nations to "take the lead" has deepened this fragmentation rather than catalyzing coordination, leaving a quarter of global oil trade in limbo and no multilateral mechanism in sight. China's policy response is revealing in its prioritization of energy security over market efficiency. Beijing has instructed independent refiners — the so-called "teapot" sector — to maintain 2025 output levels or risk losing future import quotas, even as margins turn negative and access to discounted sanctioned crude dries up. The directive makes commercial losses a cost absorbed by smaller private processors, while the state ensures downstream fuel availability. Meanwhile, the People's Bank of China withdrew cash from its financial system for the first time in approximately a year, a cautious move that keeps policy options open without signaling a pivot in either direction. Separately, Zhongji Innolight — an Nvidia optical components supplier — has confidentially filed for a Hong Kong IPO targeting roughly $3 billion, underscoring the continued momentum in AI-adjacent hardware even as broader sentiment remains pressured. Japan's bond auction underperformed on Thursday, adding to the global fixed-income selloff following Trump's address. On a structural note, Japan's labor market is generating what may be its most sustained wage cycle in decades: the Shunto spring negotiations secured average increases exceeding 5% for a third consecutive year, while base pay recorded its largest year-on-year gain in more than 33 years. Notably, total employment is rising despite population contraction, as female participation, immigration liberalization — a legacy of Abenomics — and extended working careers among older cohorts collectively expand the labor supply. South Korea is contending with inflation nudging above the 2% target and President Lee Jae Myung urging energy conservation while deploying a $17 billion support package. India's rupee staged its largest single-day gain in over a decade after the RBI banned key offshore derivatives and forced the unwind of tens of billions in speculative positions, though the structural pressure from elevated energy import costs persists.

    Europe

    Europe's energy stress is intensifying on multiple fronts simultaneously. Diesel futures for the benchmark ICE Gasoil contract broke above $200 per barrel on Thursday — the highest since 2022 — as Hormuz disruptions compress refined product flows globally. The dynamic is counterintuitive on the surface: tankers that loaded diesel in UK waters are diverting cargoes to destinations as distant as Australia rather than discharging locally, while vessels originally heading toward Europe across the Atlantic have turned toward Africa. The explanation is straightforward: Asia and other regions are outbidding European buyers. The market appears to be signaling that if Europe wishes to attract sufficient diesel supply, benchmark prices will need to rise further still. Compounding the imported energy shock is a domestic supply problem. Norway — which under normal conditions functions as Europe's hydropower reserve, exporting roughly 15% of its annual production — is emerging from its driest winter in decades. Snow reserves have fallen to their lowest levels in two decades, leaving an estimated 25 terawatt-hour energy deficit, equivalent to nearly a fifth of Norway's total hydropower output last year. Electricity exports to the UK have fallen approximately 50% year-on-year, and to Germany by around 40%, while Nordic prices have surged to more than four times 2025 levels in parts of the region. The shortfall arrives precisely when European gas markets are already under pressure from Persian Gulf supply disruptions, leaving the continent navigating a dual squeeze. UK businesses are planning larger-than-normal price increases in response to surging energy costs, according to a Bank of England survey. ECB Governing Council member Fabio Panetta noted that the war's economic impact will persist even if hostilities conclude relatively soon, while stopping short of indicating a clear direction for interest rates — a posture that reflects the genuine policy dilemma facing the institution as inflation risks from energy collide with downside growth risks. The broader question of European defense funding also remains live, with NATO allies skeptical that Trump will follow through on withdrawal threats but increasingly concerned that the rhetoric itself is degrading alliance cohesion at a vulnerable moment.

    Global

    The defining market development of April 2 is the Brent crude price breaking above $107 per barrel following Trump's speech, representing a gain of more than 40% since the Iran conflict began approximately five weeks ago. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to normal commercial traffic, and Trump's pledge of fresh military action over the next two to three weeks — absent any defined exit condition — has extinguished near-term expectations for a reopening. Even in an optimistic scenario where hostilities de-escalate, damaged infrastructure and residual security risks would likely delay a return to normal flow for weeks thereafter. Options markets reflect deep uncertainty about the endgame, with positioning spanning downside hedges to speculative bets on oil reaching $450 per barrel — a distribution that speaks to the absence of conviction rather than directional consensus. The IRGC's management of Hormuz has taken on commercial characteristics: tanker operators report receiving offers of safe passage contingent on re-flagging vessels under friendly-nation registries, with payments demanded in yuan or cryptocurrency. The UAE has formally called on the United Nations to authorize measures — including force — to reopen the waterway, reflecting the acute economic pressure on Gulf states whose export infrastructure remains inside the affected zone. Emirates Global Aluminium has halted operations at its Al Taweelah smelter following missile and drone strikes. The global diesel scramble is reshaping commodity trade routes in ways that carry significant second-order costs. US naphtha exports reached a single-month record of approximately 15 million barrels in March, as Japanese buyers substituted US Gulf Coast supply for unavailable Middle Eastern cargoes. UNCTAD now projects global merchandise trade growth of 1.5–2.5% for 2026, a sharp deceleration from 4.7% last year. Iran's President Pezeshkian published an open letter addressed directly to the American public on Thursday, arguing that Iran did not initiate hostilities and questioning whether the conflict serves US or Israeli interests — a move that appears calibrated to exploit divisions within Trump's political base rather than to advance formal diplomatic channels. Iran's strategic posture remains effectively controlled by the IRGC, which has shown no inclination toward ceasefire discussions.

  • US

    US equity markets posted their best session in nearly a year on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 surging sharply after Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian indicated to EU officials that Tehran was "willing to end the war" but required guarantees — the clearest signal from the Iranian side of any appetite for negotiation. The rally was amplified by heavily bearish end-of-quarter positioning rather than a wholesale shift in investor conviction; Wall Street trading desks noted that the magnitude of the move reflected a short squeeze as much as a fundamental reassessment. The S&P 500 still closed March down 5%, its worst monthly performance in a year, and remains roughly 8% below its pre-war peak. President Trump subsequently told reporters he foresaw ending the conflict within two to three weeks, adding that a formal agreement with Tehran was not a prerequisite for US withdrawal and that the administration had "largely accomplished its military goals." He is scheduled to address the nation Wednesday evening. The statement raises significant strategic questions: the administration's own foreign policy document, published four months ago, listed keeping the Strait of Hormuz open as a core US interest. Trump's apparent willingness to leave that issue to other nations represents a meaningful departure from stated policy. The consumer backdrop is becoming a more pressing concern for macro forecasters. Average US gasoline prices crossed $4 per gallon for the first time since the early months of the Ukraine war, following a 30%-plus surge in March. While fuel expenditure as a share of disposable income remains near historic lows at 1.6%, the household savings rate has declined to 4.5% from 6.4% in January 2024, narrowing the buffer. More consequentially, corporate equities now represent over a quarter of household net worth — a record in eight decades of Fed data — meaning that equity market performance may prove a more potent channel for consumer spending than pump prices. February retail sales data due Wednesday will offer a pre-war baseline. Nike shares fell nearly 10% in premarket trading after issuing a weaker-than-expected full-year outlook, complicating CEO Elliott Hill's turnaround effort.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets surged Wednesday, with the Nikkei 225 rising approximately 5% and South Korean stocks posting their strongest session since 2001, driven by a rebound in Samsung and SK Hynix on revived semiconductor demand sentiment. The gains followed Tuesday's Wall Street rally and Trump's comments signaling a potential exit from the Iran conflict within weeks. Brent crude fell below $100 per barrel for the first time in a week on the news, offering some relief to the region's most oil-dependent economies, though the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed to commercial shipping. The divergence in economic resilience across Asia is sharpening. China's official manufacturing PMI returned to expansionary territory in March, and the country's vast strategic oil reserves and diversified energy sources appear to be providing meaningful insulation. However, a private Caixin survey showed factory activity slipping for export-oriented firms as input costs surged, and Alibaba released its new Qwen3.5-Omni multimodal model on a closed-source basis — a departure from its usual practice that may reflect a shift toward commercialization. South Korea's exports continued to expand at a strong pace, with record AI-linked semiconductor shipments providing a buffer against the external shock. The picture deteriorates sharply elsewhere. India was forced to reverse a near-doubling of domestic jet fuel prices within hours of implementation, after State-owned Indian Oil initially raised prices to 207,341 rupees per kiloliter before revising them back to approximately 104,927 rupees. LPG shortages are rippling through India's food service sector, with restaurants from Delhi to Bangalore substituting kerosene or wood-fired stoves. The Philippines has declared a national emergency. Vietnam, Indonesia and the broader ASEAN manufacturing bloc saw PMI readings fall to their weakest since September, underscoring the asymmetric regional impact. The BOJ's quarterly Tankan survey showed large Japanese manufacturers remaining confident for a fourth consecutive quarter, though that data largely predates the conflict's escalation. US Trade Representative Greer said the Trump-Xi summit, delayed from its earlier date, remains on track for mid-May.

    Europe

    European equity futures and the Stoxx Europe 600 rose approximately 2% Wednesday as risk appetite recovered on Trump's Iran comments, though underlying anxiety over the regional energy outlook persists. The EU is actively considering reactivating emergency energy measures last used after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, while the European Commission has proposed limited adjustments to the EU Emissions Trading System to cushion the impact of elevated energy costs on industry competitiveness — a modest concession that stops well short of structural reform. Aviation is emerging as an acute pressure point. Lufthansa is preparing contingency plans to ground aircraft if demand deteriorates, with teams assigned to develop crisis scenarios of varying severity. Europe is expected to avoid jet fuel shortages in April, but market participants directly involved in aviation fuel supply warn the situation could deteriorate in May. UK factories are experiencing their most intense supply chain strain and sharpest input price pressures since the immediate aftermath of Russia's Ukraine invasion, according to the latest PMI survey. On the corporate side, Meg O'Neill assumes the role of CEO at BP on Wednesday, becoming the first woman to lead a major oil company. She inherits a business already under strategic pressure, now operating in an environment of war-driven price elevation that provides near-term revenue support but deepens long-term questions about capital allocation and transition strategy. LVMH shares fell 28% in the first quarter — the worst start to a year in the company's history — with the luxury conglomerate increasingly serving as a proxy for global confidence. Germany's leading economic institutes cut their 2026 growth forecast to less than half their prior projection, and warned that potential growth may approach zero in the medium term due to demographic decline and falling hours worked, raising questions about whether the government's debt-financed stimulus program can prevent structural stagnation. Greece received a notable upgrade from MSCI to developed-market status, effective during the May 2027 index review — a milestone in the country's recovery from its sovereign debt crisis, during which it was demoted in 2013.

    Global

    The prospective end-state of the US-Iran conflict is generating as much market anxiety as its continuation. Trump's Tuesday comments that the US would exit within two to three weeks, without necessarily reopening the Strait of Hormuz, represent a significant strategic shift. If Iran retains effective control over the Strait as a condition of any peace settlement, it acquires a structural chokehold over global energy flows that no prior adversary has held — functioning, as one analyst noted, less like an oil producer and more like an involuntary central bank for global energy pricing. Brent crude, which reflects non-US supply dynamics, barely moved in response to Iran's peace overture, even as WTI fell sharply, illustrating the divergent exposure. The market's deep contango — futures pricing implies oil will revert significantly from current spot levels — reflects a belief that the supply shock is transitory, but that assumption may prove fragile if the Strait remains restricted post-conflict. Polymarket probabilities now assign a 60% chance that the war concludes in April and only 20% that it extends beyond June, a sharp shift following Pezeshkian's remarks. However, the conditions Iran is understood to require for any settlement — including guarantees of regime survival and continued Hormuz influence — may prove difficult to formalize. Meanwhile, attacks continued across the region even as diplomatic signals multiplied, and US troop deployments to the area are ongoing. Asia continues to scramble for alternative crude sources, with Russian oil seeing increased demand from war-affected regional buyers. Myanmar, operating under a military junta and experiencing civil war, is among the hardest-hit economies as gasoline supplies disappear. Oil futures curve dynamics and the asymmetric regional impact of the Hormuz closure are increasingly defining the macro divergence between the US and the rest of the world. The Russia-Ukraine conflict is also re-intensifying as spring conditions return to the battlefield, with diplomatic attention consumed by the Gulf crisis reducing pressure on Moscow. The Trump-Xi summit, postponed from an earlier date, is now targeted for mid-May; US Trade Representative Greer characterized the bilateral relationship as stable for the near term.

  • US

    US equity markets ended March on a tentative positive note, with S&P 500 futures gaining roughly 0.9% as the Wall Street Journal reported that President Trump is now considering ending the US military campaign against Iran without requiring the Strait of Hormuz to fully reopen, opting for diplomatic pressure instead. The report marked a meaningful shift from earlier signals that Washington was prepared to escalate strikes on Iranian energy and water infrastructure. Trump has oscillated repeatedly since hostilities began — alternating between claims that a deal is imminent and warnings of intensified military action — and the situation remains unresolved. The more significant market development may be occurring in fixed income. US Treasury yields dipped Monday, with Fed Chair Jerome Powell noting that longer-term inflation expectations appear to be in check, and New York Fed President John Williams describing policy as well positioned. Markets appear to be repricing the conflict's growth implications more heavily than its inflation implications — a notable shift from the dynamic that prevailed through most of March. The immediate effect was a brief equity rally, though stocks gave back gains as WTI crude closed above $100 per barrel for the first time since hostilities began. For US consumers, the conflict's costs are now visible at the pump: average gasoline prices crossed $4 per gallon for the first time since August 2022, carrying obvious political risk for the administration in a midterm election year. The S&P 500 is on track to post its worst monthly decline since September 2022, down approximately 7.8%, though forward earnings estimates for the broad market outside the Magnificent Seven have actually risen through March, tempering pressure on valuations. On regulatory policy, the administration proposed allowing 401(k) plans to hold private assets, extending retail investor access to private markets. Nike reports earnings after the close, with investors watching for evidence that CEO Elliott Hill's turnaround strategy is gaining traction.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets closed March under significant pressure. The region's benchmark indices fell roughly 13% over the month — the steepest decline since October 2008 — as surging oil prices, supply-chain disruptions, and deteriorating risk appetite weighed broadly. South Korea, which had been the world's best-performing market entering the year on AI optimism, is now among the hardest hit, having approached bear market territory. Seoul announced a supplementary budget to cushion households and businesses from the oil shock, while the incoming Bank of Korea governor nominee played down concerns over currency weakness. China offered a more nuanced picture. Factory activity returned to expansion in March for the first time this year, supported by ramped-up fiscal spending and continued demand linked to artificial intelligence infrastructure. However, input costs surged sharply, reflecting the upstream pass-through of higher energy prices — a tangible sign that the war's economic spillover is reaching Chinese manufacturing. Meanwhile, Chinese state oil and gas majors are reportedly moderating aggressive expansion plans amid market turbulence, balancing energy security objectives against near-term uncertainty. Separately, Chinese officials are intensifying scrutiny of offshore trusts holding shares in Hong Kong-listed companies, targeting wealth structures used by the country's ultra-rich to invest overseas. In Southeast Asia, Vietnam is emerging as a structural beneficiary of trade diversion away from China. Bloomberg's shipment-level customs data shows the country surpassed China as the leading US supplier of laptops and gaming consoles last year for the first time — though Vietnam's exposure to the very tariff policies driving that shift represents a meaningful vulnerability. In Australia, the online safety regulator launched investigations into Meta, Snap, TikTok, and YouTube over alleged failures to enforce the country's under-16 social media ban, with fines of up to A$49.5 million possible. India faces mounting pressure from soaring crude import costs, with the current account deficit widening in ways that complicate the Reserve Bank of India's efforts to stabilize the rupee.

    Europe

    Euro-area inflation jumped to 2.5% in March, the steepest monthly acceleration since March 2022, when the Russian invasion of Ukraine drove a comparable energy shock. The reading came in below the consensus estimate of 2.6%, providing modest relief, and — importantly — core and services inflation actually softened relative to February. The divergence suggests that the energy pass-through is the primary driver rather than broadening underlying price pressure. Poland's inflation rose to 3%, while Tokyo's gauge cooled, illustrating uneven spillovers across economies. The ECB faces a genuinely difficult calibration. Bank of Italy Governor Fabio Panetta emphasized the need to prevent a wage-price spiral while keeping policy proportionate to the mandate. Bloomberg Economics' assessment is that April rate hike odds remain low given that the data slightly undershot projections, providing cover for the doves on the Governing Council. That said, the ECB's credibility was damaged by its slow response to the 2022 shock, and policymakers appear determined to signal vigilance even if action is not yet warranted. European equity markets have fallen 7.6% in March — the worst monthly performance in nearly four years — with LVMH, SAP, and Novo Nordisk collectively accounting for more than half of the €420 billion in market capitalization erased this quarter. Energy supply disruption is weighing on diesel availability, as tankers divert away from the Persian Gulf. On the corporate side, Unilever announced advanced talks to sell most of its food business to McCormick, with a final announcement potentially imminent. In the UK, house prices unexpectedly rose according to a major mortgage lender, suggesting consumer demand held up at the onset of the conflict, though the UK's structural rigidities — regulated energy pricing and inflation-linked wages — leave it comparatively exposed to energy shocks relative to euro-area peers. A former Deutsche Bank manager, Michele Faissola, filed suit in London for at least £473 million, alleging wrongful blame for the Monte Paschi accounting scandal.

    Global

    The defining story of March 2026 is the US-Iran conflict and its cascading effects on global energy markets. After a month of fighting, the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 135 vessels transited daily under normal conditions — is seeing fewer than six vessels per day on average. Iran's most consequential strategic achievement has not been on the battlefield but in its grip over this chokepoint. An Iranian drone struck a fully laden Kuwaiti oil tanker at anchor near Dubai on Tuesday, one of the most significant vessel attacks of the conflict, temporarily pushing WTI toward $107. WTI has risen more than 50% in March, the largest monthly gain since May 2020, and closed above $100 for the first time since hostilities began. Despite this, benchmark crude futures have not fully priced the physical disruption — jet fuel in Europe has surged to all-time highs equivalent to over $215 per barrel, while crude remains in the low triple digits. The divergence reflects record IEA emergency reserve releases, a well-supplied market entering the conflict, and trader reluctance to hold leveraged positions into weekends when major strikes have occurred. Energy consultancy FGE NexantECA warns that if the near-closure of Hormuz persists for six to eight weeks, Brent could reach $150–$200. A separate threat is materializing via the Houthis in Yemen. Tehran is urging the group to renew attacks on Red Sea shipping; Bloomberg Intelligence estimates oil could surge to $140 per barrel if Houthi strikes resume targeting crude flows bypassing Hormuz. That would constitute what analysts describe as the largest energy disruption in history, compounding an already severe shock. Fuel shortages are spreading to Sub-Saharan Africa — Kenya, Ethiopia, and Zambia are reporting station outages, with prices in some markets set to rise as much as 50%. Geopolitically, Secretary of State Rubio declared NATO's response "very disappointing," calling out allies for denying base access — a statement that may presage a formal reassessment of the US-NATO relationship once the conflict concludes. The WTO's 14th ministerial conference ended without agreement on renewing the e-commerce tariff moratorium, prompting the US Trade Representative to declare the organization's role in global trade policy "limited" and pivot toward bilateral and plurilateral agreements.

  • US

    US equity futures recovered partial ground in early Monday trade, with S&P 500 futures rising 0.3% after an earlier selloff, while the Nasdaq 100 sits roughly 11.5% below its October all-time high and remains under sustained pressure. The slow-moving nature of this correction — quite unlike the sharp sell-off that followed Liberation Day tariffs a year ago — reflects lingering market conviction that either a rapid diplomatic resolution or a decisive US military outcome remains plausible. That conviction is fraying. On the monetary policy front, Federal Reserve Chair Powell is scheduled to speak Monday, with markets attentive to any signal on how the Fed is calibrating the competing risks of oil-driven inflation and decelerating growth. Bond managers at Pimco, JPMorgan Asset Management, and Columbia Threadneedle have begun shifting their focus from the inflationary impulse of higher crude to the growth-shock scenario, arguing the surge in yields has run its course. Two- and five-year Treasury yields have risen by more than half a percentage point since the Iran conflict began; the 10-year briefly traded above 4.4% before firming on Monday. The employment report on Friday — with consensus expecting 60,000 jobs added after a 92,000 decline in February — will provide the first clean read on how the real economy is absorbing the shock. On fiscal and trade policy, this week marks the one-year anniversary of Liberation Day tariffs. The US Supreme Court in February ruled those tariffs illegal, triggering a $170 billion refund process. Bloomberg Economics estimates that 43% of US imports — roughly $1.4 trillion in goods — currently remain tariff-free, a figure frequently lost in the broader narrative. Foreign direct investment into the US totaled $288.4 billion in 2025, a modest decline from 2024 and the lowest of the Biden era, undermining the administration's argument that tariffs are catalyzing a manufacturing investment surge. Aluminum producers Alcoa and Century Aluminum rose 8.7% and 8.5% respectively in premarket trade, following Iran's weekend strikes on Gulf production facilities.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets opened the week sharply lower. Japan's Nikkei fell as much as 4.5% at the open, prompting Japan's top currency official to deliver his strongest warning yet against yen weakness and signal potential bold intervention — remarks that helped stabilize the currency. The broader MSCI Asia gauge declined as investors absorbed the entry of Houthi forces into the conflict and digested the prospect of a prolonged Strait of Hormuz disruption. India remains among the most acutely exposed economies in the region. The Reserve Bank of India's move to cap lenders' open foreign-exchange positions at $100 million per day delivered only a fleeting lift to the rupee, which subsequently weakened to fresh lows. Global funds have withdrawn approximately $12 billion from Indian equities since the conflict began, while index-eligible bonds recorded $1.6 billion in outflows in March alone, a record. India's combination of heavy energy import dependence and proximity to the war zone leaves it with limited natural buffers. South Korea signaled it is considering extending driving restrictions to the general public should Brent crude breach $120 per barrel — a threshold nearly reached on Monday at $117 — the first such potential measure since the 1991 Gulf War. The government has already asked citizens to reduce power consumption and favor public transport. Australia announced a temporary halving of fuel taxes at an estimated cost of A$2.55 billion. BYD offered a more constructive datapoint: management told analysts the company is confident 2026 exports will reach 1.5 million vehicles, 15% above its prior guidance, providing some relief after a disappointing earnings release. On the geopolitical side, President Xi Jinping invited Kuomintang Chairwoman Cheng Li-wun — leader of Taiwan's largest opposition party — to visit China from April 7-12, the first such visit by a sitting KMT leader in a decade. The invitation comes one month before Trump's scheduled summit in Beijing and gives Xi a platform to discuss Taiwan's US weapons purchases directly with a Taiwanese political figure who has publicly questioned the scale of her island's defense spending.

    Europe

    European equity markets showed limited movement, with the Stoxx Europe 600 broadly flat on Monday. The more consequential European developments this week concern the intersection of energy policy, monetary policy, and domestic politics rather than markets per se. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves is scheduled to address G7 partners, urging them to coordinate on boosting domestic energy supplies and warning that the oil and gas price surge is directly benefiting Vladimir Putin — a framing that seeks to link the Middle East crisis to the ongoing Russia-Ukraine dynamic. On monetary policy, the ECB, Bank of England, and Swiss National Bank all held rates at their most recent meetings, each acting unanimously in response to geopolitical uncertainty. ECB Governing Council member Francois Villeroy de Galhau signaled the central bank stands ready to act against rising inflation expectations should they become unanchored. Euro-area CPI data is due Tuesday, alongside German inflation figures on Monday, which may show a notable jump reflecting fuel costs. The ECB's Cipollone is scheduled to speak Wednesday. The UK faces a separate set of pressures. London's youth unemployment has reached a rate of approximately one in four 16-to-24 year-olds actively seeking work, the highest in the country. Graduate job postings in London fell from roughly 13,000 in 2019 to just over 2,000 at the start of 2026, according to Adzuna — a decline attributed to AI-driven hiring retrenchment compounded by broader economic uncertainty. The trend poses a political risk for Keir Starmer's Labour government ahead of municipal elections in just over a month. In Paris, French authorities detained two additional suspects linked to a foiled bomb plot near a Bank of America office, an incident officials believe is connected to the Middle East conflict.

    Global

    The dominant global theme is whether the Iran-US war, now in its second month, will prove more enduring and economically destructive than financial markets have priced. Brent crude extended its advance above $116 per barrel on Monday — heading for its largest-ever monthly gain — after Houthi forces formally entered the conflict over the weekend and Iran struck aluminum production facilities in the UAE and Bahrain. The latter sent aluminum prices up as much as 6%, highlighting how the energy shock is beginning to transmit into industrial metals and broader supply chains. The strategic picture has shifted materially. President Trump's earlier five-day and ten-day extensions of his ultimatum on Iranian energy infrastructure — both of which prompted no discernible Iranian concession — appear to have revealed the limits of rhetorical escalation as a market management tool. Iran's new leadership, having already absorbed significant military damage, retains low incentive to capitulate: the ability to selectively restrict tanker passage through the Strait of Hormuz imposes severe costs on others while leaving Iranian exports less impaired than an outright mining campaign would. Approximately 20,000 seafarers remain stranded in and around the Strait. The nuclear proliferation implications deserve attention. From Germany and Poland exploring deeper integration with France's nuclear umbrella, to rising public support in South Korea and renewed debate in Japan, US allies are reassessing deterrence postures in ways that may prove structurally significant regardless of how the current conflict concludes. The Trump administration is simultaneously considering resuming nuclear testing after more than three decades and potentially sharing enrichment technology with Saudi Arabia, according to documents reviewed by Bloomberg. On the anniversary of Liberation Day tariffs, the global trading system remains in flux. China has initiated two fresh investigations into US trade practices ahead of an expected May presidential summit. The WTO failed to extend its 28-year moratorium on e-commerce tariffs. Fertilizer prices are rising sharply — urea and ammonia particularly — with near half of global urea supply originating in the Persian Gulf region, raising concerns about food security in sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia.

  • US

    US equity markets enter the week's final session absorbing the compounding pressures of elevated energy costs and geopolitical uncertainty stemming from the now month-old US-Israel military operation against Iran. The airline sector is particularly exposed: United Airlines' CEO publicly warned of potential airfare increases of up to 20% as jet fuel costs rise. The warning is consistent with broader consumer price pressures building across the economy, though the magnitude and duration of any inflation pass-through remains contingent on how the conflict evolves. Energy Secretary Chris Wright has been at the center of a separate but related policy debate, having signaled prior to the Iran conflict that the US might withdraw from the International Energy Agency unless the body curtailed its net-zero modeling work. That posture now faces a more complicated political environment, as surging oil prices are reinforcing, for many allied governments, the strategic rationale for energy transition investments — even as Washington remains at odds with that consensus. On fiscal and political dynamics, the administration continues to navigate the foreign policy costs of the Iran engagement. Trump's war has generated notable friction with key allies, several of which have declined to express public enthusiasm for the military campaign and have been publicly rebuked by Washington in response. The domestic political calculus remains unclear: rising energy prices and allied estrangement represent real second-order risks, while the administration continues to send mixed signals about the conflict's intended scope and duration. Markets appear to be pricing some probability of further escalation, though options markets have not yet moved to levels consistent with acute crisis pricing. The interaction between energy inflation and the Federal Reserve's policy path is a mounting concern — any sustained commodity-driven inflation re-acceleration would complicate the rate trajectory at a time when the Fed has limited room to accommodate both growth support and price stability. ---

    Asia

    The economic shockwaves from the US-Iran conflict are landing with particular force across Asia, where dependence on Gulf energy supplies is structurally acute and fiscal buffers vary widely. South Korea has moved to implement crisis-level measures including a fuel-price cap, and its prime minister has explicitly invoked the need to prepare for "worst-case scenarios" — language that signals genuine institutional concern rather than routine contingency planning. Compounding Seoul's calculus is the reported redeployment of US air defense assets from the Korean Peninsula toward the Middle East, raising questions about the credibility of American security commitments in Northeast Asia at a moment of elevated regional tension. The Philippines presents a starker case. President Marcos has declared a national energy emergency, citing the risk that domestic aviation could be grounded entirely due to jet fuel shortages — a materially different order of concern from the airfare increases flagged by US carriers. The contrast is instructive: richer economies face price shocks, while more vulnerable ones face supply disruption. Marcos's response is geopolitically significant as well. Despite deepening military and diplomatic ties with Washington since 2022 — positioning Manila as a key US partner in the South China Sea — the energy crisis is now prompting Manila to explore collaboration with Beijing on oil and gas development in disputed waters, alongside a broader diplomatic re-engagement with China. This realignment, however tentative, reflects a wider pattern across the Indo-Pacific. The Iran war appears to be accelerating a reassessment among US allies of the costs and benefits of Washington alignment, particularly given Trump's simultaneous trade pressure, reduced regional security presence, and diplomatic abrasiveness. Hong Kong's re-emergence as a wealth management destination and China's positioning as a stable counterweight to US unpredictability — framed by some observers as Beijing casting itself as the "adult in the room" — suggest capital and political flows in the region may be gradually, if cautiously, reorienting. ---

    Europe

    Europe enters the weekend confronting a familiar but newly intensified energy vulnerability. The continent's previous exposure to Russian gas, substantially reduced since 2022 through a painful and costly transition toward US LNG and other alternatives, has inadvertently created a new form of dependence — this time on Washington's goodwill. Trump's envoy to the EU, Andrew Puzder, made that leverage explicit this week, warning that American LNG could be redirected to other buyers if Brussels fails to accelerate progress on a trade deal, or that terms "may not be as favorable." The European Parliament approved the trade agreement on March 26, though the longer-term implications of energy supply being instrumentalized as diplomatic leverage remain unsettling for European policymakers. Goldman Sachs Chief Economist Jan Hatzius quantified the inflation risk in a "severely adverse" oil scenario, estimating that euro area headline inflation could rise by nearly 3 percentage points over the next year — the largest projected impact among major economies, driven by the dual exposure to crude oil and natural gas prices. The OECD separately concluded that the UK faces the largest output hit among G7 nations this year, a sobering assessment for a government already managing sluggish growth. Hatzius's analysts added a notable caveat: their estimates "may underestimate the nonlinear impact" of a severe gas supply shock — an acknowledgment that tail risks are not fully captured in central forecasts. Germany is responding with a characteristically structural approach, drafting contingency plans to counter US pressure — including the potential targeting of White House-aligned tech firms in any future trade dispute — while simultaneously implementing domestic energy support measures. German economist Isabella Weber has drawn a pointed historical parallel, comparing the current moment to the early weeks of 2020, when the Wuhan outbreak had signaled catastrophe but its full dimensions remained unclear. Whether other European economies have sufficient fiscal capacity to replicate Germany's support measures is an open and consequential question. ---

    Global

    The US-Iran conflict, now one month old, is functioning as a stress test for the post-Cold War architecture of US-led alliances, global energy markets, and the political economy of the energy transition simultaneously. Oil prices have surged globally — the commodity's fungibility means no economy is insulated — but the distribution of harm is highly unequal, shaped by import dependence, fiscal capacity, and currency strength. The gap between a US airline CEO warning of 20% fare increases and a Philippine president warning of grounded aircraft captures that distribution vividly. In energy markets, the conflict is reinforcing a structural argument that had been gaining traction before the war: that dependence on fossil fuel imports from politically volatile regions carries security costs that renewables and electrification can partly mitigate. European Commission Executive Vice-President Teresa Ribera made the point explicitly this week, arguing that the disruption of global fossil fuel markets would prove "a very relevant driver of the green economy." Early data suggests the conflict is accelerating consumer uptake of electric vehicles, solar panels, and other low-carbon technologies in several markets — a feedback dynamic that, if sustained, could compress the long-run demand outlook for Gulf producers even as near-term prices remain elevated. Geopolitically, the war is reshaping alliance calculus in ways that may prove durable. Nations that had pivoted away from China and Russia toward deeper US alignment are now reassessing that strategy under the combined pressures of trade tariffs, energy disruption, and reduced security commitments. Turkey's $8 billion drawdown of gold reserves has added a further wrinkle to commodity markets. Forecasters acknowledge significant uncertainty: the conflict's trajectory remains genuinely unclear, Washington continues to send mixed signals about scope and duration, and the nonlinear risks — to energy infrastructure, shipping chokepoints, and confidence — are by definition difficult to model. The honest assessment is that the range of outcomes remains wide, and the current market pricing may not fully reflect the left tail.

  • US

    US equity markets remained under pressure as S&P 500 futures extended their losing streak into a fifth consecutive week, the longest such run since 2022, reflecting the compound strains of Middle East-driven inflation anxiety, a weakening labor market, and fiscal uncertainty. The index would need to fall an additional 7.7% to reach the 50% Fibonacci retracement level from April's low, a level technical analysts are watching closely. On the monetary policy front, three Federal Reserve officials expressed growing concern over the economic outlook, with at least one policymaker explicitly characterizing the oil price spike as elevating inflation risks above labor market deterioration in the hierarchy of concerns. That framing matters: it signals the Fed may be moving toward a posture where easing is harder to justify even as growth softens — a stagflationary bind the institution has limited tools to resolve cleanly. Weekly jobless claims fell to their lowest level in nearly two years, offering a modest counterweight, though February's 92,000 decline in nonfarm payrolls remains an overhang. The housing market is exhibiting visible stress heading into the spring selling season. Thirty-year fixed mortgage rates have climbed back to 6.4%, a six-month high, reversing the sub-6% readings seen as recently as late February. KB Home lowered full-year guidance, citing the conflict as a contributing factor, and industry contacts described conditions at a JPMorgan homebuilder conference as "fragile." The deterioration reflects both the mechanical channel — Treasury yields rising on inflation repricing — and the confidence channel, as households facing rising energy costs and equity portfolio losses pull back. On the fiscal front, the Senate passed legislation to fund most of the Department of Homeland Security, advancing a resolution to the partial government shutdown that has produced some of the worst airport congestion in recent memory. The bill must still clear the House and receive presidential signature, leaving the outcome uncertain. Trump separately indicated he would issue an executive order to pay TSA officers, though the legal authority to circumvent congressional appropriations remains questionable.

    Asia

    China's economic and geopolitical positioning was the dominant theme across Asia this week. The White House confirmed a Trump-Xi summit for May 14-15 in Beijing, providing the clearest diplomatic signal yet that the US sees the Iran conflict winding down — though this outcome remains far from assured. Beijing, characteristically, has not confirmed the dates, preserving flexibility should conditions shift. China simultaneously launched retaliatory trade investigations into US supply chain and renewable energy practices, staking out negotiating leverage ahead of the summit rather than signaling a détente. The economic read on China is mixed. Container throughput at Chinese ports remained firm in the first three weeks of March, up over 6% year-on-year, suggesting trade disruption from the war has not yet materialized at scale. However, ground-level conditions in export hubs like Yiwu are deteriorating, with manufacturers facing rising input costs for oil-linked materials and Middle Eastern buyers pulling back on orders over shipping uncertainty. A factory manager in Jiangsu described the environment as the most difficult in over a decade, and the risk of demand-driven overcapacity translating into domestic price wars appears real if the conflict persists. The energy dislocation is reshaping asset prices regionally. BYD's Hong Kong-listed shares gained 12% in March, on track for their best month in over a year, as surging fuel prices drive consumers toward electric vehicles across Southeast Asia. Queues at EV dealerships in the Philippines and Indonesia have been reported. Japan, however, faces a more complex dynamic: rising oil prices combined with yen weakness are stoking stagflation concerns, prompting the Finance Minister to flag potential currency intervention. Japanese super-long bond yields also rose amid the uncertainty. In the Philippines, President Marcos is reportedly considering setting aside South China Sea territorial disputes with Beijing to explore cooperative oil exploration — a striking illustration of how energy stress is reshaping regional geopolitical alignments.

    Europe

    Europe is absorbing the Iran war's economic consequences at a moment of pre-existing fragility. The bloc had only recently begun shaking off the growth drag of the Ukraine conflict, and energy-driven inflation now threatens to reinstate the stagflationary pressure that defined 2022-23. European economies are revising output expectations downward while simultaneously bracing for a renewed inflationary impulse, a combination that complicates ECB policy sequencing significantly. UK retail sales declined for the first time in three months, with consumers pulling back even ahead of the more recent escalation in oil prices. In London, the housing crisis deepened. Ground broken on residential homes fell to just 5,547 units last year — down more than 75% from a decade earlier and the lowest in at least fifteen years — while London's population has grown by over half a million in the same period. The collapse of a contractor in Ealing has left over 100 affordable homes unbuilt, illustrative of a broader pattern of cancellations and indefinitely paused projects. The supply-demand gap appears set to widen further. On the corporate side, Pernod Ricard is in preliminary discussions to acquire Brown-Forman, owner of Jack Daniel's, with Brown-Forman's Class B shares rising as much as 21% on the news while Pernod fell 5.7% in Paris. The potential deal reflects consolidation pressure across the global spirits industry amid a downturn in demand. The UK's flood insurance program came under scrutiny for channeling subsidies from lower-income northern households to wealthy southern property owners through a structurally flawed council tax calculation dating to 1991 — a distributional distortion the program's own CEO acknowledges cannot be corrected independently. UK Prime Minister Starmer signaled publicly that he will not be pressured by Trump, as the Iran war continues to strain the bilateral relationship. Trump's criticism of NATO's refusal to support US military operations — warning he would "remember" the alliance's response — adds a further layer of institutional tension across the Atlantic.

    Global

    The Iran war remained the organizing fact of global markets this week. Trump extended his pause on strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure by ten days, to April 6, citing progress in negotiations — though the talks appear to lack clarity on interlocutors, given the deaths of multiple senior Iranian government and military officials. Iran and Israel exchanged missile fire, and Tehran targeted several Gulf states, underscoring that the conflict retains significant escalation potential despite the diplomatic pause. Brent crude traded at $110 per barrel, and Macquarie warned of a path to $200 if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed through June — a scenario that would represent a supply shock with no modern precedent. The dollar's behavior has confounded pre-war consensus. The Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index is on track for its largest monthly gain since July, an approximately 2% advance that reflects haven demand and the repricing of Fed easing expectations. The move complicates the global inflation picture: a stronger dollar raises the local-currency cost of oil and food for importing nations, compounding the energy price shock. JPMorgan strategists have turned bullish on the dollar for the first time in a year; speculative positioning has flipped from the most net-short in five years to net-long. Gold markets face a structural challenge. Turkey has sold approximately $8 billion — around 60 tons — of gold reserves this month to defend its currency and meet dollar demands, a pace of selling larger than concurrent ETF outflows. Should other energy-importing central banks follow suit, the narrative of central banks as consistent net buyers of gold, which has underpinned the metal's more-than-doubling since 2022, would face meaningful revision. Gold has already fallen roughly 18% from its peak above $5,000 per ounce. Reports that Iran is permitting Hormuz passage for vessels paying oil in yuan has prompted Deutsche Bank to flag the conflict as a potential "catalyst for erosion in petrodollar dominance." The geopolitical and monetary ramifications of that scenario, while uncertain in magnitude, are significant and warrant continued monitoring.

  • US

    US equity markets remained defensive, with S&P 500 futures falling roughly 0.7% as conflicting signals over US-Iran ceasefire prospects sapped risk appetite. The S&P 500 has now surrendered less than 5% since hostilities began nearly a month ago, a relative resilience that many on Wall Street attribute less to fundamental optimism than to the memory of April 9, 2025, when the index surged 9.5% on tariff relief — keeping would-be bears cautious about committing to the short side. The dominant posture across institutional desks is cash accumulation rather than directional conviction: Bank of America's latest fund manager survey recorded the largest jump in cash allocations since March 2020, with holdings at 4.3%, while Nomura's macro traders report having been instructed to stand down. Goldman Sachs has raised its 12-month US recession probability to 30%, citing surging oil prices, and projects unemployment climbing to 4.6% by year-end from 4.4% in February. The OECD now sees US inflation reaching 4.2% in 2026, up from 2.6% last year and 1.2 percentage points above its December projection. Trump administration officials are separately stress-testing scenarios involving oil at $200 a barrel, an exercise framed as contingency planning rather than a forecast. The cost of US imports rose in February by the most in nearly four years, suggesting inflationary pressure was already building before the Middle East conflict began. On the corporate side, a jury found Meta Platforms and Alphabet liable for damages in a social media addiction case — a ruling that may signal broader litigation risk — though both stocks edged higher, consistent with markets treating the decision as bounded rather than existential. Memory and storage stocks fell in premarket trading after Google researchers disclosed a compression technique that could reduce AI memory requirements, with Micron down 2.8% and Sandisk off 4.1%. Federal Reserve nominee Kevin Warsh's ambitions to shrink the Fed's $6.6 trillion balance sheet face a structural constraint: analysts note the task would require more than a single term to execute meaningfully.

    Asia

    Asia is bearing the sharpest economic costs of the Strait of Hormuz disruption. Saudi Aramco has been forced to reroute some flows through its Red Sea pipeline, a partial workaround that still leaves China and India facing reduced crude shipments from the kingdom next month. India, where over 330 million households rely on LPG for cooking and roughly 90% of supply transits the Middle East, has seen delivery times stretch from five to 25 days in some regions, triggering a run on electric induction stoves with online sales rising as much as 30-fold. Across Southeast Asia, consumers are shifting toward BYD electric vehicles, while Pakistan stands to save an estimated $6.3 billion this year by substituting solar for oil and gas at elevated prices — a structural response that the war may be accelerating beyond what the energy transition alone would have achieved. Governments across the region have shifted to emergency energy footing, cutting work weeks, switching off street lighting, and limiting petrol station hours. The IMF is actively modelling which economies may require fresh financing if the conflict extends, with energy-dependent emerging Asia the primary area of concern. China's small and medium-sized enterprises are already reporting surging input costs, with some reducing headcount. BlackRock President Rob Kapito warned that oil could still spike toward $150 a barrel even if the fighting stopped tomorrow, while Apollo's Jim Zelter flagged rising recession risk and credit cycle strain from a prolonged conflict. On a more constructive note, the Trump-Xi summit has been confirmed for May 14-15 in Beijing, a development that provides some stabilization to US-China relations after weeks of additional uncertainty. Meanwhile, China's semiconductor ambitions advanced: ChangXin Memory Technologies reportedly more than doubled revenue to approximately $8 billion in 2025, driven by AI chip demand, as Beijing continues its push toward domestic self-sufficiency in advanced semiconductors. Moonshot AI, maker of the Kimi chatbot, is exploring a Hong Kong IPO alongside up to $1 billion in private funding, reflecting continued investor appetite for Chinese AI assets.

    Europe

    Europe is absorbing a simultaneous growth and inflation shock from the Iran war, with Italy now preparing to cut its 2026 GDP forecast to as low as 0.5%. UK consumer confidence fell to its weakest reading since the British Retail Consortium's tracker began in March 2024, as households anticipate an inflation surge. The Stoxx Europe 600 fell 1.0%, tracking the broader deterioration in risk sentiment. Airfare disruption adds a further consumer cost: ticket prices on major Asia-Europe routes have risen as much as 560% this month and are likely to remain elevated through summer, according to Alton Aviation. British Airways is offering pilots financial incentives to reduce fuel consumption, reflecting the pressure on airline economics. Germany is advancing contingency planning against potential US coercion, with officials mapping vulnerabilities in American supply chains — spanning Big Tech, AI infrastructure, and pharmaceutical flows — that the EU could exploit in a future confrontation with the Trump administration. The exercise reflects a broader shift in Berlin's strategic posture, treating economic leverage as genuinely bilateral rather than asymmetric. Separately, EU lawmakers approved a long-delayed trade deal with the United States, a constructive development at the margins of the WTO's 14th ministerial conference opening in Yaoundé, Cameroon. In the UK, the Green Party under new leader Zack Polanski has moved ahead of both Labour and the Conservatives in some polls, a development worth monitoring as energy costs continue to drive voter behavior. On the pharmaceutical side, Novo Nordisk's market capitalization has declined by nearly $500 billion over 21 months, with patent expirations in India and China — markets representing roughly 40% of the world's population — enabling generic GLP-1 drugs at prices as low as $14. Henkel reached a deal to acquire hair-care brand Olaplex for $1.4 billion, with Olaplex shares surging nearly 50%.

    Global

    The defining macro tension of the moment is the simultaneity of an acute energy supply shock and unresolved geopolitical uncertainty. Brent crude traded near $108 a barrel on March 26 and is on pace for its largest monthly gain since 1990, with the near-total shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly one-fifth of global oil supply transits — driving what the International Chamber of Commerce described as potentially "the worst industrial crisis in living memory." BloombergNEF's base case sees Brent averaging $106.70 in April; a delayed Hormuz reopening until mid-June would push that average to $154.40. Iran, meanwhile, appears to be a net financial beneficiary: as the only major exporter currently able to utilize the strait freely, Tehran has reportedly earned hundreds of millions of dollars in incremental oil revenue since hostilities began, effectively converting the disruption into strategic and fiscal leverage. Diplomatically, the gap between Washington and Tehran remains wide. The White House describes Iran as seeking an exit ramp; Iran publicly rejects US overtures and is drafting legislation to impose passage fees on vessels transiting the strait. Former MI6 chief Alex Young assessed that the US "lost the initiative to Iran about two weeks ago." Prediction markets place the probability of a ceasefire by end of April at no better than 50-50. A suspected sea-drone attack on a crude tanker in the Black Sea added a further dimension to energy market risk. The OECD, the first major institution to formally revise its forecasts, left its 2026 global growth projection unchanged at 2.9% while trimming 2027 by 0.1 percentage point to 3.0%, noting the conflict arrived just as AI investment, tariff normalization, and accommodative policy were generating genuine upside. G20 average inflation is now expected at 4.0% versus 2.8% in December. Central banks are pivoting away from rate cuts, and the OECD warned that "monetary policy adjustments may be needed if price pressures broaden." The structural response from consumers — accelerating EV adoption, rooftop solar, and electrification of cooking in India — may prove durable, though it is unlikely to offset the near-term coal reversion across energy-stressed Asia.

  • US

    US equity futures climbed Wednesday, with S&P 500 futures up roughly 0.9% and Nasdaq 100 futures gaining 1.1%, as diplomatic signals around a potential US-Iran ceasefire allowed markets to partially unwind recent risk-off positioning. The rally followed news that the Trump administration has drafted a 15-point peace plan, delivered to Tehran via Pakistan, though Iran's armed forces publicly dismissed the overture and continued missile and drone attacks on Gulf states and Israel. The market's relief trade remains conditional and fragile. The Federal Reserve remains caught in a genuinely difficult position. Fed funds futures have briefly reflected a non-trivial probability of a rate hike at the April meeting — the first time since 2023 that a hike was priced as more likely than a cut at the next meeting.
