However, most economists close to the Fed, including Citigroup's Veronica Clark, characterize a near-term hike as highly unlikely. Chair Powell has made clear that the Fed will not even fully engage the question of whether to "look through" the energy inflation shock until the tariff impact has been separately assessed. The dual headwinds of an oil-driven inflation surge and a prospective growth slowdown leave the committee in a holding pattern. Fed Governor Michael Barr signaled rates may need to remain steady "for some time." Adding a further layer of complexity, the anticipated arrival of Kevin Warsh as the next Fed chair is beginning to influence market pricing. New Fed chairs are typically tested for hawkish resolve, which arguably tempers the "debasement" narrative that had underpinned gold's January surge. Retail investor fatigue is also becoming apparent: Vanda Research data show Monday marked the first net selling day for individual stock buyers since 2023, with Citadel Securities' gauge of retail risk appetite declining sharply from February highs — a notable development given retail's stabilizing role through this bull market cycle. ---

    Asia

    Xi Jinping's studied silence on the Iran conflict, now nearly a month old, is becoming a substantive strategic posture rather than mere diplomatic reticence. China's relative insulation from the energy shock — rooted in an accelerating renewables buildout, electric vehicles comprising roughly half of new car sales, and continued access to discounted Russian and Iranian crude as US sanctions ease — means Beijing faces fewer immediate economic pressures than its regional peers. Chinese port throughput through the first three weeks of March ran more than 6% ahead of year-ago levels, with AI-driven electronics exports sustaining momentum even as oil-related supply chain disruptions mount. South Korea's exports to China surged 69% in the first 20 days of March, with semiconductor shipments abroad up 164%, reflecting the deep integration of regional supply chains around AI infrastructure demand. The picture is considerably less benign elsewhere in Asia. South Korea has stood up an emergency economic task force and its prime minister is pushing for a supplementary budget to cushion the energy shock. The Philippines has declared a national energy emergency, with Philippine Airlines warning of potential jet fuel rationing, and Vietnam's aviation authority is cutting flights ahead of anticipated shortages from early April. Japan has begun its largest emergency oil stockpile release on record. Signs of fuel hoarding are proliferating across the region, with China, South Korea, and Thailand all restricting some fuel exports — a dynamic that risks deepening market dislocations rather than alleviating them. India faces a distinct diplomatic dilemma. As current chair of BRICS, New Delhi is under mounting pressure to steer the bloc toward a firmer collective position on the conflict, a task complicated by the group's membership spanning multiple sides of the dispute. BRICS has so far failed to adopt any unified stance. Prime Minister Modi discussed the Strait of Hormuz in a call with President Trump, underlining the degree to which the conflict is forcing India to navigate between its strategic partnerships and its energy vulnerabilities. ---

    Europe

    UK gilt markets remain the most acute stress point among developed-economy sovereign bond markets. Ten-year gilt yields briefly breached 5% earlier this week before partially retracing following Trump's temporary delay of strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure. Yields have now returned to levels last seen before the Global Financial Crisis, exceeding even the extremes of the Liz Truss episode in 2022 in duration if not in the immediate velocity of the move. The driver this cycle is less directly fiscal and more about the Bank of England's constrained policy options: markets have pivoted from pricing at least two 25 basis-point cuts by year-end to pricing at least two hikes, as inflation expectations — already elevated relative to European peers — have surged further in response to the oil shock. UK inflation break-even spreads over US equivalents, which had narrowed as the Labour government stabilized its market credibility, have reversed sharply. Politically, Prime Minister Starmer's position is deteriorating. Prediction markets currently assign a 68% probability that he exits office before year-end, with his most likely successors positioned to his left — a configuration unlikely to reassure gilt investors. The party retains a substantial parliamentary majority, precluding any near-term electoral test, but the political and market pressures are feeding on each other. The ECB, by contrast, is moving more deliberately. President Lagarde used the ECB Watchers Conference in Frankfurt to signal that the bank will not act until it has "sufficient information on the size and persistence" of the energy shock, while also making clear it will not be "paralyzed by hesitation" if a broader inflationary spiral materializes. The contrast with the Bank of England's position is meaningful: the ECB retains more credibility cushion and faces less acute sovereign spread pressure, though vigilance is explicitly warranted. Germany's business outlook deteriorated as higher energy prices threaten what had been a nascent industrial recovery. ---

    Global

    The dominant macro theme of March 25 is the tension between a diplomatic opening on Iran and the continued prosecution of the war. The US 15-point ceasefire plan, delivered via Pakistan, has been met publicly by Tehran with dismissal and continued missile strikes on Gulf Arab states and Israel. Brent crude fell more than 6% toward $97 a barrel on the diplomatic headlines, but the move appears to reflect relief at reduced tail risk rather than conviction that an end to the conflict is imminent. Iran's statement that foreign ships may transit the Strait of Hormuz provided they comply with Tehran's regulations — including, reportedly, transit fees on some commercial vessels — stops well short of a full reopening and amounts to a form of de facto toll extraction on global shipping. The commodity spillovers are broadening. Urea, the world's most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, has risen to its highest level in more than three years as energy-linked production costs surge and export restrictions proliferate. The risk of a feedback loop from energy into food prices is real, with governments across Asia restricting fuel and fertilizer exports in ways that may deepen rather than resolve shortages — a dynamic with precedent in the 2008 rice price crisis. Gold's behavior continues to challenge conventional safe-haven narratives. The metal fell 27% from its January intraday peak to its trough earlier this week — its worst multi-week stretch in over a decade — before recovering modestly on ceasefire hopes. The selloff appears to reflect a confluence of factors: the asset was heavily overbought entering the conflict, the dollar has strengthened as an alternative haven, and the shift in rate expectations has undermined the "debasement" trade thesis. The correlation between gold and real yields that anchored its valuation framework through the post-GFC era has broken down; whether this is a structural regime change or a temporary dislocation remains genuinely uncertain.

  • US

    US equity markets ended Monday modestly higher but well below their intraday peaks, as investors quickly tempered their initial enthusiasm following President Trump's Truth Social post claiming "productive conversations" with Iran had produced progress toward a resolution. The Nasdaq 100 closed roughly flat relative to where it stood Friday morning, an outcome that itself signals something important: the market's ability to respond to presidential jawboning on geopolitical risk appears to be diminishing. As Brad Conger of Hirtle Callaghan observed, investor faith in Trump's capacity to end the conflict the way he might reverse a tariff is likely misplaced — the war involves a motivated adversary with independent agency. The fiscal outlook continued to deteriorate in the background. The Pentagon has requested $200 billion in supplemental funding for the Iran conflict, which economists warn could push the 2026 federal deficit toward $2 trillion, roughly double Treasury Secretary Bessent's original 3%-of-GDP target. The Supreme Court's earlier invalidation of most of Trump's tariff regime has further reduced anticipated revenue, while the oil-driven inflation shock is suppressing the probability of Federal Reserve rate cuts that might otherwise have relieved interest costs. The Treasury paid approximately $970 billion in net interest on federal debt last year; Goldman Sachs economists project the deficit will exceed 6% of GDP through 2029. On the corporate side, Apollo Global Management moved to cap redemptions from its $25 billion Apollo Debt Solutions vehicle at 5% of outstanding shares after clients sought to withdraw 11.2%, highlighting mounting stress in private credit markets as the conflict and rate expectations tighten. Meanwhile, unusual trading patterns prior to Trump's Monday post — with roughly 6 million barrels of oil futures changing hands in the two minutes before publication, against a five-day average of 700,000 — drew regulatory scrutiny and commentary from market participants concerned about information leakage around presidential communications. Airline sector stress is also crystallizing domestically. Bloomberg Intelligence estimates sustained elevated jet fuel prices would raise US carriers' costs by 15–23%, with budget operators most exposed. Delta and Southwest are relatively better positioned given stronger balance sheets, while Frontier and JetBlue face greater vulnerability.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific markets opened Tuesday to a deteriorating security picture that undermined Monday's brief optimism. Overnight, Iran launched missile and drone strikes on the Israeli cities of Eilat, Dimona, and Tel Aviv, as well as US bases across the Middle East. Saudi Arabia intercepted a drone in its eastern region, Kuwait reported power line disruptions following an Iranian attack, and sirens sounded in Bahrain. Counter-strikes attributed to the US-Israeli alliance damaged a gas pressure-regulation facility and struck a pipeline supplying the Khorramshahr power plant in southwestern Iran. S&P 500 futures fell 0.4% in choppy trade; Brent crude rose approximately 2% to $102 per barrel. The conflict's economic footprint in the region is widening rapidly. South Korea's Prime Minister Kim Min-seok cancelled a planned visit to China to manage domestic energy and economic disruption, with Seoul announcing vehicle-usage restrictions in the public sector — a step that may extend to private motorists. South Korean government bond yields rose in tandem with a broader global move higher in short-duration debt spanning Canada, Brazil, and the UK. Meanwhile, the Iran crisis is opening an unexpected commercial opportunity for South Korea's defense industry, with Middle Eastern countries increasingly attracted to its lower-cost air-defense systems. Aviation stress is intensifying across Southeast Asia. Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. warned that grounding planes due to jet fuel shortages remains a "distinct possibility," while Vietnam Airlines has suspended select domestic routes and VietJet has reduced flight frequencies. Qantas subsidiary Jetstar has cut roughly 17.5% of intra-New Zealand services in May as demand softens under higher fares. In a separate development, Japan's Finance Ministry is reported to have made inquiries with market participants about potential intervention in crude oil futures markets, reflecting Tokyo's growing anxiety over energy costs and yen pressure. In corporate news, Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is reportedly developing contingency plans for a potential takeover of Jefferies Financial, with a small internal team monitoring the US bank's declining share price for an opportunistic entry. Nintendo reduced Switch 2 production guidance by more than 30%, citing weaker-than-expected holiday demand in the US and rising memory chip costs, with the reduced output rate expected to persist through April.

    Europe

    Eurozone economic data released Tuesday provided the first systematic evidence of the Iran war's impact on European activity. The S&P Global Composite PMI fell to 50.5 in March from 51.9 in February — the slowest expansion since May 2025 — as energy-driven cost pressures and supply chain disruption weighed on both manufacturing and services. Input prices rose at the fastest pace since February 2023. Germany's composite reading declined more than expected, while France recorded a third consecutive month of contraction in the PMI services component. The data also showed the largest deterioration in future output expectations since Russia's invasion of Ukraine four years ago — a signal that corporate confidence is weakening ahead of any potential resolution. The United Kingdom fared similarly. British businesses recorded near-stagnation in March, with factory input costs rising at the fastest pace in more than three decades as the conflict disrupted energy and supply chains. The Bank of England faces a familiar dilemma: deteriorating growth alongside a renewed inflation impulse driven by external commodity shocks. ECB Governing Council member Boris Vujcic — set to become ECB vice president in June — struck a notably cautious tone, warning that the risk of stagflation is increasing and that the Bank must remain "very agile and vigilant." He declined to provide definitive guidance on rates, acknowledging that "how far we'll get in that direction is very difficult to predict." On the political front, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni suffered a referendum defeat on judicial reform — a self-inflicted setback that has damaged her aura of invincibility ahead of a general election roughly a year away. Simultaneously, Denmark held a general election shaped heavily by concerns over Trump's Greenland threats, with polling pointing toward a vote for stability. The EU and Australia concluded a long-pending free trade agreement, with European Commission President von der Leyen taking a pointed implicit swipe at US tariff policy and Chinese critical minerals restrictions in her remarks.

    Global

    The central tension shaping global markets remains unresolved: whether a diplomatic off-ramp to the US-Iran conflict exists, and if so, how quickly it can materialize. Trump's Monday Truth Social post claiming "productive conversations" briefly sent Brent crude down approximately $17 per barrel to as low as $96 before prices rebounded toward $100–$102 as Iran's denial of any negotiations, combined with continued overnight missile exchanges, reasserted themselves. The episode illustrates a structural vulnerability: markets are now dependent on presidential social media for price discovery in one of the world's most systemically important commodity markets, a dynamic that carries obvious risks of manipulation and error. The Strait of Hormuz question remains unresolved in a way that extends beyond any ceasefire. Roughly a fifth of global oil and LNG transits the waterway; a CBS report on Monday indicated approximately a dozen Iranian mines are now present in the strait, raising questions about the timeline and logistics of any reopening even in a post-ceasefire scenario. Control of Hormuz gives Iran a structural lever over global energy prices that will persist regardless of near-term political outcomes. Saudi tanker freight rates from Yanbu — the Red Sea alternative routing — have eased somewhat as capacity arrives, but remain well above pre-conflict levels. Saudi Arabia and the UAE have reportedly taken concrete steps toward joining the conflict on the US-Israeli side, including Riyadh granting US military access to King Fahd Air Base — a reversal of its earlier position. Pakistan's army chief is simultaneously engaged in mediation efforts. The convergence of these dynamics suggests the conflict is at a genuinely uncertain inflection point: both escalation and de-escalation pathways remain live. Iran stopped natural gas exports to Turkey following an Israeli strike on the South Pars gas field, removing another supply route from global markets. The macroeconomic transmission mechanism is broadening. Oil-driven inflation is now arriving in consumer prices, airline costs, food logistics, and industrial inputs across geographies from Italy to India to South Korea. The IEA has reported record stockpile withdrawals; the US is considering further SPR releases. Central banks globally face a stagflationary configuration — the ECB's Vujcic encapsulated it directly — that limits their conventional toolkit precisely when fiscal positions are also under stress.

  • US

    Equity and credit markets opened the week under pressure as Trump's weekend social media posts delivered conflicting signals on war strategy and the Strait of Hormuz. S&P 500 futures declined 0.9%, though markets partially recovered after Trump postponed military strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure, extending his 48-hour ultimatum by five days following what he described as productive talks with Tehran. The whipsaw in presidential messaging — moving within 24 hours from signaling disengagement to threatening to "obliterate" Iran's power plants, then stepping back — has left investors trying to price an inherently unpredictable decision-making process. Bond markets are absorbing a significant inflation repricing. US 10-year Treasury yields rose to 4.44%, with two-year yields climbing 17 basis points last week to 3.89% — the third consecutive week of losses. Futures now assign a 40% probability to a Fed rate hike this year, a striking development given that cuts were the consensus expectation entering 2026. The dynamic reflects a genuine policy bind: tariff-related inflation was fading and might have opened the door to easing, but the roughly 40% surge in Brent crude has revived stagflation concerns that constrain the Fed's room to maneuver. On energy policy, the administration's "drill, baby, drill" directive is running into practical contradictions. Higher oil prices improve the economics for domestic producers, but US E&P stocks have risen only around 30% against a 40% rise in Brent — and the broader consumer impact, with average US diesel prices surpassing $5 per gallon and retail gasoline up roughly a third from a month ago, complicates the political calculus. Exxon and ConocoPhillips edged higher on the session, while the quarterly earnings outlook for US energy has risen sharply relative to the rest of the world. Elsewhere, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have begun placing sizable orders for mortgage-backed securities following a Trump directive to purchase $200 billion of the bonds, an attempt to reduce borrowing costs that sits in some tension with the broader rate environment.

    Asia

    Asian markets experienced their sharpest single-session decline since the Iran conflict began, with MSCI's Asia Pacific gauge falling 3.7% to near its year-to-date breakeven. South Korea's Kospi tumbled 6.5%, its worst session in years, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng declined 3.5%. Currencies across the region extended their depreciation: the Korean won fell to its lowest level since 2009, the Indian rupee and Philippine peso reached record lows, and the Australian dollar dropped 1.3%. Partial recovery followed Trump's decision to extend his Strait of Hormuz deadline. The IEA's executive director Fatih Birol, speaking in Canberra, framed the supply disruption as equivalent to the two major 1970s oil crises and the 2022 European gas crisis combined — and emphasized that Asia bears disproportionate exposure given its structural dependence on Gulf crude. More than 40 energy assets across nine Middle Eastern countries have been severely damaged. Governments across the region are responding with fuel rationing measures: Myanmar has introduced barcode-based purchase limits; Pakistan has redirected its premier cricket league to a watch-from-home format to conserve fuel. On China specifically, state-owned refiners have begun exploring purchases of Iranian crude following a limited US waiver allowing the sale of oil already loaded onto tankers. Representatives of National Iranian Oil Co. are quietly sounding out Asian buyers, with China the most consequential potential customer. The development reflects Beijing's balancing act between energy security needs and US diplomatic sensitivities. Wall Street banks — Bank of America, Citigroup, and Goldman Sachs — have revised China's inflation forecasts upward for 2026, and BofA has withdrawn its call for two rate cuts totaling 20 basis points. Separately, Bloomberg Economics analysis suggests China's property downturn is approximately 70% complete, with stabilization potentially achievable by 2027 — a timeline comparable to the US housing correction of 2006–2012, and considerably shorter than Japan's two-decade slump. Japan's largest labor union secured average wage increases above 5% for a third consecutive year, keeping the Bank of Japan on a gradual tightening path.

    Europe

    European markets bore a heavier share of the global selloff than US counterparts, consistent with the region's greater energy import dependence. The Stoxx Europe 600 fell 2.3% to its lowest since September, now down 11% from its February peak — technically in correction territory. UK gilts are on track for their worst monthly performance since the Truss-era crisis of autumn 2022, with two-year yields climbing to their highest since February 2024 at approximately 4.7%. Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey attended an emergency cabinet meeting convened by Prime Minister Keir Starmer, underscoring the severity of the dual fiscal-monetary stress the UK faces. The ECB signaled it remains on alert for inflation pass-through from elevated energy prices, with Vice President Luis de Guindos warning of war-related price fallout. ECB officials Lane, Cipollone, and Escriva are scheduled to speak Monday. Business PMI data due Tuesday is widely expected to show synchronized deterioration across the eurozone's manufacturing and services sectors — the first comprehensive economic health check since the conflict began. European political developments were notable on several fronts. In France, Socialist Emmanuel Grégoire won the Paris mayoral race, extending the left's hold on the capital, while Marine Le Pen's National Rally fell short in its bid to take control of major cities — a result that may temper expectations for the RN ahead of next year's presidential election. In Germany, Chancellor Merz's CDU dislodged the Social Democrats from power in Rhineland-Palatinate after 35 years, though the result adds complications for SPD within the governing coalition. Slovenia's Prime Minister Golob secured a narrow victory over nationalist Janša in that country's closest election on record, with coalition negotiations likely to be protracted. On the corporate side, Italy's Poste Italiane made a €12.5 billion offer for full control of Telecom Italia, with Poste shares falling 7% and Telecom Italia rising 4.2%.

    Global

    The dominant global theme remains the Iran war and its cascading economic consequences, now entering a fourth week with the trajectory of hostilities still deeply uncertain. Trump's weekend ultimatum — threatening to bomb Iranian power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz was reopened within 48 hours — was effectively rolled back Monday after markets sold off sharply, with the deadline extended by five days following what the administration described as diplomatic contact. The episode illustrates the feedback loop between market signals and presidential decision-making, though the underlying strategic impasse remains unresolved. The IEA estimates the supply disruption is comparable in magnitude to the combined effect of the 1970s oil shocks and the 2022 European gas crisis. Dallas Fed modeling suggests a Strait shutdown lasting through June would reduce global GDP growth by 2.9 percentage points next quarter. Gold posted its worst weekly decline since 1983 — down 10.5% — a counterintuitive move for a traditional safe haven that reflects a dash for liquidity, expectations of higher real rates, and dollar strength. Spot gold traded around $4,248 at session highs before the ultimatum extension provided partial relief. Brent crude held near $113 per barrel, with the Brent-WTI spread widening to levels exceeding those seen during the Ukraine invasion, reflecting the US's relative insulation as a major domestic producer. Beyond the Strait itself, collateral damage is broadening. Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex — the world's largest — has sustained damage that could take years to repair fully, affecting both near-term LNG supply and long-term investment calculus. Satellite data shows elevated flaring at impacted Gulf facilities, adding an underappreciated emissions dimension to the conflict's footprint. Fertilizer supply chains are under pressure, with both nitrogen and phosphate markets disrupted — a development with meaningful implications for global food production. The EU-Australia free trade agreement talks, being advanced by European Commission President von der Leyen in Sydney, reflect Europe's broader effort to diversify strategic and economic partnerships amid simultaneous pressure from US trade policy, the Ukraine conflict, Chinese export competition, and Middle East energy disruption.

  • US

    Federal Reserve communications remained a focal point this week as policymakers navigate the tension between still-elevated inflation and mounting signs of fiscal drag. No major policy decisions were announced, though the broader backdrop of persistent data-center-driven energy demand and Iran-war energy price pass-throughs continues to complicate the Fed's path to its 2% target. The interplay between energy prices and shelter inflation appears to be extending the timeline for any easing cycle, though the precise magnitude remains difficult to quantify. On the fiscal and policy front, New York Governor Kathy Hochul introduced a proposal to revise the state's Climate Leadership and Community Protection Act, seeking to push its 40% emissions reduction target back from 2030 to 2040. Hochul cited affordability pressures — specifically, rising energy bills linked to data-center demand growth and Iran-war-related fuel price increases — as the rationale for delay. The proposal also includes a provision to defer implementation of a cap-and-invest program to 2030. The political optics are notable: a Democratic governor in a reliably blue state moving to weaken climate legislation at the same moment the Trump administration is rolling back federal emissions rules. Whether the state legislature accepts the revisions ahead of the April 1 budget deadline remains uncertain. Critics, including Earthjustice and several state senators, argue the changes primarily benefit fossil-fuel incumbents rather than consumers. Separately, the robotaxi sector drew attention following Uber's announcement of an investment of up to $1.25 billion in Rivian's autonomous vehicle program. The deal reflects continued capital deployment into the AV space, though analysts have flagged the potential congestion externalities of large-scale AV deployment — particularly deadheading miles — as an underappreciated urban policy challenge. The absence of road-pricing frameworks in most US cities may amplify rather than resolve traffic dynamics as the technology scales. ---

    Asia

    Japan and South Korea appear to be consolidating a diplomatic and economic rapprochement that would have seemed implausible as recently as a few years ago. Current South Korean President Lee Jae Myung and Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi — the latter a conservative nationalist by background — have prioritized pragmatic cooperation over historical grievances, a posture that analysts describe as driven by a convergent set of external pressures. China's economic coercion, including restricted Japanese access to rare earths and other critical inputs, has reinforced the logic of bilateral solidarity. The growing China-Russia-North Korea military alignment poses overlapping security challenges for both capitals. The Trump administration has also served as an indirect catalyst. Washington's transactional posture — including tariffs on both allies, large investment pledge demands ($550 billion from Japan, $350 billion from South Korea), and public questioning of US security commitments — has, somewhat paradoxically, pushed Seoul and Tokyo closer together. As one analyst framed it, a more unpredictable United States in the Indo-Pacific has made an active Korea-Japan partnership a strategic necessity rather than a political choice. Last week, the two countries' finance ministers agreed to expand bilateral economic cooperation, including a review of their currency swap arrangement, currently sized at $10 billion. Public opinion appears to be supportive of the new trajectory. Korean favorability toward Japan recently surpassed 56%, described as a record high, while a majority of Japanese hold positive views of South Korea. That symmetry reduces the domestic political cost of sustained engagement for both governments. Separately, China began 2026 with its fastest pace of government spending growth since 2022, suggesting Beijing is leaning on fiscal stimulus to sustain economic momentum. Samsung meanwhile announced plans to deploy approximately $73 billion this year across chip capacity and research, underscoring continued investment intensity in the semiconductor sector even as the broader technology supply chain remains subject to geopolitical disruption. ---

    Europe

    Germany's ongoing military buildup is generating tangible economic reverberations across the domestic economy. While details in the available coverage remain limited, the scale of the fiscal reorientation — driven by NATO pressure and the changed European security environment — appears sufficient to reshape industrial demand patterns, labor markets in defense-adjacent sectors, and potentially medium-term fiscal trajectories. The Bundesbank and European fiscal authorities will need to assess how defense spending interacts with the ECB's policy stance, particularly if it proves inflationary at the margin. No significant ECB policy decisions were reported in this period, though the broader European energy cost environment — elevated in part due to the Iran conflict's effect on shipping fuel availability and crude markets — continues to bear on the inflation outlook. The spillover from African shipping fuel shortages, noted in context of the Iran war, may affect European supply chains that transit those routes, though the direct magnitude is unclear from available reporting. No major UK-specific developments or individual European corporate stories were prominent in the source material for this period. ---

    Global

    The Iran war is emerging as a persistent cross-regional supply shock rather than a discrete geopolitical event. Shipping fuel shortages are now appearing in Africa, a consequence that illustrates how the conflict's disruptions are propagating beyond the immediate theater. Energy price pressures are being cited as contributing factors to consumer affordability stress in markets as disparate as New York and South Asian economies, while Pakistan's accelerated solar adoption is being interpreted partly as a coping mechanism for surging oil prices — an unintended demand-destruction dynamic for conventional fuels. The US-Iran conflict is also reshaping alliance behavior more broadly. Washington has reportedly pressed both Japan and South Korea to contribute to the Iran effort, adding another layer to an already complex alliance management picture. The transactional character of these asks — coming alongside tariffs and investment demands — has accelerated the formation of alternative bilateral and multilateral arrangements among US allies seeking to reduce dependency on American security guarantees. On the climate and energy transition front, the IPCC faces an institutional challenge following US withdrawal of funding for scientific participation. The body's ability to maintain credibility and completeness in future assessment reports is now an open question. Meanwhile, the policy environment for emissions reduction is softening even at the sub-federal level, with New York's proposed rollback of its 2030 climate targets illustrating the political difficulty of sustaining ambitious decarbonization commitments when energy affordability pressures are acute. EVs are estimated to be displacing approximately 2.3 million barrels per day of oil demand globally, a data point that contextualizes the structural headwinds facing crude markets even as near-term supply disruptions support prices. The interaction between long-run demand erosion and short-run geopolitical supply risk remains one of the more consequential tensions in global energy markets.

  • US

    US equity futures were modestly lower Friday, with S&P 500 futures off 0.6%, as the Iran war's implications for monetary policy continued to weigh on risk appetite. The most significant development for US markets was the Federal Reserve's posture: Chair Jerome Powell offered no indication that rate hikes are on the table but gave equally little comfort to those expecting cuts, emphasizing that the inflationary impact of tariffs needs to dissipate before the Fed can ease. Futures markets have now fully repriced the 2026 path, eliminating expectations for any rate reductions this year after previously pricing two cuts. Separately, Fed policymakers revised their median year-end headline inflation estimate to 2.7% from 2.4%. On the legal and institutional front, top Justice Department officials are rallying behind a US attorney's challenge to Fed Chair Powell's authority, with the White House declining to oppose the effort — a development with potentially significant implications for central bank independence that warrants close monitoring. In corporate news, FedEx climbed roughly 10% in premarket trading after raising its earnings forecast, providing a modest counterpoint to the broader market anxiety. Super Micro tumbled 22% after federal prosecutors charged a co-founder with illegally diverting Nvidia-powered servers to China, a high-profile case that highlights ongoing concerns around AI technology export compliance. Options expiration Friday threatened to amplify volatility in an already turbulent tape; Goldman Sachs's trading desk noted that clients who had anticipated a quick resolution to the Iran war are increasingly doubtful, with some now anticipating either a market correction or a prolonged 2022-style grind lower. The US 10-year Treasury yield rose to approximately 4.30%, with the bond selloff reflecting a global reassessment of the rate path as energy-driven inflation concerns mount. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent floated the idea of lifting sanctions on Iranian crude already at sea — roughly 140 million barrels by his estimate — as the administration sought tools to cap oil prices. The practical impact appears limited, however, as virtually all Iranian crude is purchased by Chinese refiners, and broader sanctions infrastructure remains intact.

    Asia

    Asian markets were broadly weaker Friday, with the MSCI Asia benchmark off 0.4% and the Hang Seng declining 0.9%, as investors awaited clarity on the war's trajectory over the weekend. The most consequential regional development came from China, where the deflationary outlook is shifting materially. With Brent crude above $100 per barrel, economists at Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, and Macquarie now project that Chinese producer prices could turn positive as soon as March, potentially ending a deflationary streak that began in 2022 — one of the longest experienced by any major economy since World War II. The key uncertainty is whether the resulting inflation reflects genuine demand recovery or purely cost-push dynamics; the latter would offer companies little pricing power and may compress margins further rather than restore them. Alibaba and Tencent combined lost approximately $66 billion in market value over roughly 24 hours after results from both companies disappointed investors seeking a clear path to AI monetization. Alibaba's quarterly net income fell 67%, its worst performance since early 2024, partly reflecting heavy promotional spending. Xiaomi fell 8.6% in Hong Kong on concerns about EV profitability. Meanwhile, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang confirmed the company is ramping production of H200 AI accelerators for Chinese customers following the Trump administration's reversal on tech export controls — a meaningful shift that reflects the broader improvement in US-China relations. The Trump-Xi summit, originally planned for later this month, has been postponed to approximately mid-May. Beijing appears quietly relieved, given concerns that hosting Trump while the Iran war continues could be construed as implicit endorsement of US military action against a Chinese partner. The trade truce remains intact, and a Paris meeting between senior officials last weekend appeared constructive. However, a pending $14 billion US arms package for Taiwan — potentially one of the largest ever — represents a significant latent irritant. India's rupee weakened past 93 per dollar to a record low, reflecting vulnerability to elevated crude prices given the country imports approximately 90% of its oil. Nomura projects the currency could reach 96 per dollar by end-June. The Reserve Bank of Australia delivered a split-decision rate hike, citing the inflation risks from the energy shock.

    Europe

    European sovereign bond markets came under significant pressure this week, with UK 10-year gilt yields rising to their highest level since 2008 following the Bank of England's Thursday statement that it "stands ready to act" against rising inflation. The BoE voted unanimously to hold rates, but the hawkish shift in tone — Governor Andrew Bailey explicitly noting that the prior expectation of scope to cut has changed — drove a sharp repricing. UK gilt volatility is now a focal point for global fixed income investors, compounded by February borrowing data that came in above forecast due to a surge in debt-interest costs. The European Central Bank's posture has shifted similarly. Governing Council member Joachim Nagel told Bloomberg Friday that a rate hike as early as April is conceivable if energy-driven inflation risks materialize, and the ECB has dropped language describing policy as being in a "good place." Money markets are now pricing two quarter-point ECB hikes this year. The policy pivot echoes the post-Ukraine inflation episode of 2022, which Nagel explicitly referenced. Bloomberg Economics estimates March inflation across advanced economies is running approximately 0.4 percentage points above pre-conflict projections. At the EU Brussels summit, leaders called for a moratorium on strikes against energy infrastructure, with Italian Prime Minister Meloni described as particularly animated about the severity of the energy situation. European officials are simultaneously navigating fatigue from US-related crises, and there are signs that governments are reconsidering their posture toward China as a more predictable trade partner — a strategic reorientation that is still nascent but appears to be gaining momentum. In corporate news, Unilever confirmed it has received an offer from McCormick for its food division, valued by Barclays at approximately €28-31 billion. The deal, if completed, would represent the largest restructuring in Unilever's near-century history, pivoting the group toward beauty and personal care.

    Global

    Three weeks into the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran, the conflict has entered a potentially pivotal moment. Israel's strike on Iran's South Pars gas field — the largest natural gas installation in the world — prompted Iran to inflict significant damage on a Qatari LNG hub, with Qatar indicating two affected trains could remain offline for up to five years. That escalation appears to have prompted a degree of recalibration on all sides. Prime Minister Netanyahu stated Israel would no longer target energy infrastructure and said the war would end faster than people expect; Iran's foreign minister signaled that recent restraint was deliberate. Whether this constitutes a durable de-escalation or tactical repositioning remains to be seen. Brent crude, which surged to $119 per barrel on Thursday, pulled back to approximately $108-110 on Friday following Netanyahu's remarks, still well above $100. The Strait of Hormuz remains closed to commercial shipping, and Europe's largest naval powers stated that reopening the waterway during active hostilities would require a deeply complex multinational capability that is not currently feasible. The Pentagon is reported to have requested an additional $200 billion from Congress to fund ongoing operations. Gold fell approximately 7% on the week — the largest weekly decline since March 2020 — and is on pace for its worst monthly performance since 2008. The move reflects the global monetary policy repricing, as higher-for-longer rates erode the appeal of non-yielding assets. ETF outflows have exceeded 60 tons over three weeks. In Southeast Asia, the oil shock is accelerating EV adoption, with BYD and VinFast reporting surging demand in the Philippines and Vietnam respectively. Asian buyers are also absorbing record volumes of US crude, with April loadings reaching approximately 60 million barrels — the most since 2023 — as buyers seek alternatives to disrupted Persian Gulf supply. US officials have ruled out banning oil and gas exports, and the administration's Iran sanctions relief proposal is widely viewed as operationally complex and unlikely to provide near-term price relief at scale.

  • US

    The Federal Reserve's March meeting delivered no policy change — the FOMC voted 11-1 to hold the benchmark rate at 3.5%-3.75% — but the press conference that followed generated meaningful market volatility. Chair Jerome Powell made clear that rate cuts remain contingent on demonstrated progress on inflation, a bar that appears increasingly distant given both the persistence of tariff-driven price pressures and the rapidly escalating Middle East energy shock. The Fed's preferred core inflation gauge ran at an estimated 3% in February, a full percentage point above target. February producer price data reinforced the concern: core PPI rose to a three-year high, with goods prices continuing to climb since Liberation Day. As Carl Weinberg of High Frequency Economics noted, the pipeline was already accelerating before the Strait of Hormuz blockade. The signal from futures markets is stark. Expectations have shifted from three cuts over the next 18 months to one, and that single cut is now essentially a coin flip. Powell acknowledged "meaningful" internal movement toward fewer cuts, and explicitly noted that colleagues had raised the possibility that the next move could be a hike. Bond yields rose and equities sold off as he spoke, with the S&P 500 recording its largest Fed-day decline since 2024. Powell's remarks on his own tenure compounded market uncertainty. He confirmed that if Kevin Warsh is not confirmed before his term expires in May, he will remain as chair pro tempore. More significantly, he stated he will not vacate his board seat — which runs to January 2028 — until the Justice Department investigation into the Fed's building renovation project is "well and truly over." The succession is now a prolonged open question, with Senator Thom Tillis blocking Warsh's confirmation vote until the probe concludes and prosecutor Jeanine Pirro having signaled she will not abandon the inquiry. Markets appear increasingly uncomfortable with the prospect of a protracted, politically charged transition at the central bank.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets declined sharply on Thursday, with the Nikkei 225 falling 3.4% as the surge in oil prices and tightening global monetary policy expectations weighed on regional sentiment. The Bank of Japan kept its policy rate unchanged at its March meeting, with Governor Kazuo Ueda citing uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict. The BOJ maintained its conditional tightening bias, pledging to raise borrowing costs if its inflation outlook materializes, but the conflict has materially clouded the timing of that adjustment. The most acute near-term pressure in Asia is on energy-importing economies. Asian buyers are heavily reliant on Persian Gulf crude, and the near-complete closure of the Strait of Hormuz since late February has forced a significant reorientation of supply chains. Asian purchases of US crude have surged to approximately 60 million barrels for April loading — the highest single-month volume since April 2023 — as buyers scramble for alternatives. Emergency stockpile releases are expected to benefit Western markets first, meaning the supply shortage remains most acute in Asia. The Philippine peso fell past 60 per dollar as energy costs weighed on the country's outlook. Thailand's parliament reconfirmed Anutin Charnvirakul as prime minister, providing some political stability as the country navigates the economic fallout. China stands as a notable exception to the regional distress. Jefferies' Christopher Wood argued that Chinese mainland equities are the most defensible in the current environment, citing the country's relative energy insulation — comprising robust domestic renewable capacity, battery storage, and reserve buffers — as well as potential support from state funds and easing factory deflation. The AI dimension also skews positively for China: OpenClaw, an open-source agentic AI platform generating significant excitement, has integrated with Chinese platforms, with two recently listed Hong Kong companies surging 19% on its momentum. Beijing's Five-Year Plan emphasis on low-cost, open-source AI deployment aligns structurally with this development, though Bloomberg Intelligence notes that high computational costs associated with agentic functions may compress API margins at firms like Alibaba, SenseTime, and Baidu. Samsung separately announced a record ¥110 trillion capital expenditure plan targeting AI semiconductor leadership.

    Europe

    European markets bore significant pressure on Thursday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 falling 2.0% as the combination of surging energy costs and a hawkish repricing of central bank expectations weighed on equities. European natural gas futures jumped as much as 35%, pushing benchmark prices to more than double their pre-war levels following Iranian missile strikes on Qatar's Ras Laffan complex. The energy shock is feeding directly into inflation expectations across the region. The ECB held its key rate at 2.0% at its March meeting, in line with consensus expectations — the rate has been at that level since last June. The policy decision itself was largely mechanical given the circumstances, but the forward guidance has shifted materially. Traders are now fully pricing two ECB hikes this year, a dramatic reversal from the easing trajectory markets anticipated before the conflict began. The Bank of England also held rates, with markets having abandoned prior near-certain expectations of a cut and now pricing a hike as the most likely next move. Sweden's Riksbank similarly stood pat. The direction of travel across European central banks is now unmistakably toward tighter policy, a consequence of an energy shock whose inflationary pass-through the region has historically absorbed more acutely than the US. In the UK, the labour market showed some resilience ahead of the BOE decision, with unemployment steady and employment modestly higher. The UK government separately announced higher tariffs on steel imports and reduced import quotas, framing the measure as support for the domestic industry amid global competition — a decision with some political resonance given the broader trade environment. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces mounting pressure from Labour's left flank ahead of anticipated losses in May's local elections, with Nigel Farage's Reform UK continuing to lead in national polls. HSBC's announcement that it is weighing cuts to approximately 20,000 roles as part of an AI-driven restructuring of its middle and back offices is among the first concrete signals of how the technology may reshape large financial institution workforces.

    Global

    The most consequential development across global markets on March 19 is the escalation of direct attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure. Israel struck Iran's South Pars gas field — the first attack on Iranian energy production facilities — and Iran responded by striking Qatar's Ras Laffan Industrial City, home to the world's largest LNG export facility, causing "extensive damage" and sparking fires. Additional strikes hit a gas facility in Abu Dhabi, set ablaze two Kuwaiti oil refineries, and caused damage near a Saudi refinery. Tehran explicitly designated energy facilities in Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE as "legitimate targets." Brent crude rose above $118 per barrel on the day, a gain of approximately 63% since the conflict began in late February. Three-month Brent futures have returned to $100, signaling the market is pricing a more prolonged disruption. President Trump sought to contain the escalation, stating the US was not involved in the South Pars strike and warning Iran that further attacks on Qatari LNG would prompt the US to "massively blow up" the entire South Pars field. He simultaneously waived the Jones Act for 60 days, allowing foreign-flagged vessels to transport commodities between US ports — a logistical measure to ease domestic energy distribution. The gap between WTI, trading near $96, and Brent at $115, and Middle Eastern grades above $150, reflects a fragmented supply landscape in which the shortage is most acute for Asian importers dependent on Gulf barrels. The LNG disruption carries particularly broad consequences. Ras Laffan supplied roughly a fifth of global LNG before the war; a restart now expected to take months rather than weeks is pressuring gas prices globally, with downstream effects on fertilizer production, power generation, and semiconductor manufacturing costs. Many African economies are reportedly running on weeks of refined fuel. The Strait of Hormuz remains near-completely closed, with approximately 40,000 seafarers stranded on either side — trapped between drone attacks and electronic warfare. Saudi Arabia has reportedly been able to reroute some oil exports to above half of normal levels, suggesting contingency infrastructure is partially functional, but the broader disruption to global energy flows remains severe and its duration uncertain.

  • US

    The Federal Reserve's policy decision dominated market attention on March 18, with the FOMC widely expected to hold rates steady for a second consecutive meeting. Chair Powell's press conference carried unusual weight given the conflicting signals the central bank must now navigate: energy prices rising sharply on the back of the Iran conflict, a softening consumer backdrop, and an administration publicly at war with itself over the economic response. President Trump called for an emergency rate cut — a move associated historically with acute crises — while National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett struck a notably more sanguine tone on CNBC, saying a prolonged war's impact on consumers was "really the last of our concerns right now." A survey of 28 former Fed policymakers and staff, conducted ahead of Wednesday's meeting, projected PCE inflation of 3% for 2026 and year-end unemployment of 4.6%, with a majority expecting rates to remain on hold throughout the year. The consumer outlook continues to deteriorate at the margin. Average gasoline prices have risen roughly 27% since hostilities began on February 28, reaching levels last seen in 2023, while diesel surpassed $5 per gallon for the first time since 2022. The mechanism differs from the post-Ukraine shock: households no longer hold pandemic-era savings buffers, and January consumer spending was essentially flat even before the energy spike hit. SMBC Nikko Securities estimates the current gasoline price increase could reduce the savings rate by 60 basis points to approximately 3.9%. University of Michigan consumer sentiment fell to a three-month low in the March preliminary reading. On corporate news, Lululemon fell approximately 2% in premarket trading after forecasting a second consecutive year of profit declines, while DocuSign rose after beating earnings expectations. General Mills was set to report before the open, with Micron Technology scheduled after the close — the latter carrying particular macro relevance given its memory chip exposure and a market capitalization that has risen from roughly $100 billion at end-2024 to approximately $520 billion. Pending home sales rose 1.8% in the latest reading, partially reversing a prior decline, though Oxford Economics cautioned that higher energy costs threaten to suppress household spending decisions in the months ahead.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets extended their rally on March 18, with the MSCI Asia-Pacific index rising approximately 2%, led by memory-chip names perceived as relatively insulated from the Middle East conflict. The standout performance came from South Korea, where the Kospi surged 5% after authorities moved to restrict publicly listed companies from listing certain subsidiaries — a practice long associated with shareholder value dilution and the so-called "Korea discount." Samsung Electronics rose 7% after signaling it is exploring a shift toward multi-year memory chip contracts, a structural change that could help stabilize supply dynamics. The Kospi now trades above 5,900, and the governance reform agenda under President Lee Jae Myung appears to be regaining momentum following a brief interruption caused by the Iran conflict. In Japan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi characterized her upcoming White House meeting with President Trump as "extremely difficult." Japan's pacifist constitution constrains overseas military deployment, making Trump's earlier request for warship contributions to Strait of Hormuz operations untenable. Though Trump has since withdrawn the demand, the encounter carries considerable stakes: Japan is seeking US support in its standoff with China, and was hoping to position Takaichi's visit ahead of a planned Trump-Xi summit in Beijing — a trip now postponed by five to six weeks. Japan's February export data added to the complex backdrop, with shipments to the US falling 8% year-on-year and car exports by value down 14.8%, though overall exports beat expectations at +4.2%. Takaichi is expected to offer pledges of additional US investment, potentially in nuclear energy, as a diplomatic offset. China faces mounting energy pressure, with industry consultant FGE NexantECA indicating Beijing is close to drawing on its estimated 1.4 billion barrels of commercial oil reserves as Strait of Hormuz disruptions persist. Alibaba raised AI computing and storage prices by as much as 34%, reflecting surging demand. The AI theme also drove significant market moves in Hong Kong, where LLM providers MiniMax and Zhipu gained approximately 20% and 19% respectively following Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's endorsement of OpenClaw, an agentic AI platform. Goldman Sachs projected the Indian rupee could slide to 95 against the dollar over the next year due to Iran conflict fallout, with pressure on the Reserve Bank of India to respond if inflation accelerates. Separately, Pakistan's airstrikes on Kabul prompted global alarm from the EU and China, raising concerns about a second concurrent conflict igniting alongside the Gulf war.

    Europe

    European markets were constructive on March 18, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 0.6% as oil prices eased and investor sentiment stabilized ahead of a week dense with central bank decisions. Iraq's agreement to resume pipeline exports via Turkey — bypassing the Strait of Hormuz — contributed to the softer oil tone, though the underlying supply disruption remains unresolved. Germany took targeted domestic action in response to war-driven fuel price increases, restricting petrol stations to one price hike per day and tightening antitrust oversight — a measure reflecting political sensitivity to consumer cost pressures rather than a substantive market intervention. The Bank of England's positioning is drawing increasing scrutiny. According to Bloomberg Economics analysis, the BoE's internal models may lead it to respond more aggressively than markets currently expect to the energy price shock, given assumptions about the large and persistent inflationary pass-through from oil. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer navigated continued diplomatic pressure, with Irish Taoiseach Micheál Martin defending him after Trump criticized the UK for declining to join the war effort. Commentary from Rosa Prince in Bloomberg Opinion argued Starmer's refusal was justified but that the harder challenge will be managing the economic damage attributable to the conflict — a framing likely to define UK political discourse through the autumn. On the corporate side, Unilever confirmed it is in the early stages of evaluating a separation of its food assets, a move consistent with the ongoing portfolio simplification strategy the company has pursued since divesting its tea business. Prudential Plc reported an increase in new business profit and announced an additional $1.2 billion share buyback. Logitech fell 5.7% in Zurich after UBS downgraded the stock on weaker gaming market signals, while HelloFresh dropped 9.6% in Frankfurt after issuing guidance materially below analyst expectations. Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel offered a relatively sanguine view on AI's labor market implications, suggesting the technology may prove beneficial for European companies rather than destructive for employment.

    Global

    The central macro reality on March 18 remains the Strait of Hormuz, now effectively shut for three weeks since the US-Israeli strikes on Iran began February 28. Iran continued missile and drone attacks across Israel and Gulf states following the killing of senior security figure Ali Larijani — a pragmatist whose death leaves Iran's wartime leadership increasingly concentrated among hardliners, materially reducing the probability of a negotiated off-ramp in the near term. The USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier withdrew from operations after an onboard fire, a logistical complication for US force projection. Trump, who called the war's end imminent, simultaneously requested NATO allied military support and was rebuffed — a sequence that led him to publicly abandon the request and criticize allies. The episode has exposed meaningful fissures in the Western alliance architecture. Oil markets are beginning to show modest differentiation. Brent edged toward $100 on the session, easing slightly from recent highs, as Iraq's Turkey pipeline deal offered one workaround to Hormuz closure. Notably, Iran itself has been transiting its own crude through the strait at broadly pre-war volumes, exploiting its control of the waterway. BofA's global fund manager survey found institutional investors rotating overweight commodities — the biggest such shift since the 2022 Ukraine invasion — while projecting Brent at approximately $76 by year-end, close to futures pricing of just over $80 nine months out. The implied scenario is a meaningful but not catastrophic supply disruption: inflation modestly higher, growth modestly lower, with full-scale stagflation not yet the base case though increasingly prominent as a tail risk. The Reserve Bank of Australia became the first of eighteen central banks meeting this week to move, hiking rates in response to the inflationary impulse from energy prices — a signal that at least some inflation-targeting frameworks are treating the oil shock as a demand-side tightening event rather than a supply disruption to be looked through. The Fed and Bank of Japan decisions follow, with markets watching closely for any shift in forward guidance. Meanwhile, BloombergNEF data showed oil and gas majors cut low-carbon energy investment by 33% in 2025 to just over $25 billion, the lowest since 2020 — a retrenchment that preceded the current crisis but may complicate medium-term energy transition trajectories if elevated oil prices simultaneously incentivize fossil fuel investment and depress clean energy capital allocation.

  • US

    US markets experienced modest volatility on March 17 as renewed Iranian strikes on Gulf energy infrastructure pushed Brent above $100 a barrel, though the S&P 500 managed a 1.01% gain in the prior session before S&P 500 futures slipped 0.2% in early trading. The more consequential story remains the Federal Reserve's policy calculus heading into this week's FOMC meeting — one that is unusually complicated by the confluence of an oil-driven supply shock, a weakening labor market, and an imminent leadership transition. The bond market's reaction has been notable for its restraint. Five-year/five-year inflation breakevens have barely moved despite a roughly 46% surge in US gasoline futures since hostilities began on February 28, and two-year real rates are declining rather than rising. Markets appear to be treating the oil shock as a growth headwind rather than an inflation catalyst — consistent with historical central bank practice of looking through energy price moves until they feed into core inflation. Fed funds futures have nonetheless repriced, with expectations for two 25-basis-point cuts this year falling to one, reflecting the deteriorating growth outlook rather than inflation fears. The political backdrop adds further complexity. February payrolls unexpectedly declined, reinforcing the dovish case. Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh has signaled a preference for easing, and President Trump — watching gasoline prices rise toward the November midterms — has strong political incentives to push for cuts. Jerome Powell chairs only one more FOMC meeting after this week. The Federal Reserve is also expected to launch revised bank capital proposals that would free approximately $200 billion in capital for the largest lenders, a potentially stimulative move that carries its own overheating risks at an uncertain moment. US diesel topping $5 a gallon for the first time since December 2022 adds to consumer price pressures that complicate any simple policy narrative. Nvidia's annual developer conference provided a constructive counterpoint: CEO Jensen Huang projected that the company's AI processors would help generate $1 trillion in chip sales through 2027, though his commentary favoring copper interconnects over fiber optics weighed on photonics names in premarket trading.

    Asia

    China remains the central geopolitical pivot of the Iran conflict, facing competing pressures that make a clear policy response difficult. Trump's request to delay his planned March 31 summit with President Xi Jinping — ostensibly to remain in Washington to oversee the war — is widely interpreted in Beijing as a tactical maneuver. Chinese state media have responded with undisguised scorn to US appeals for help reopening the Strait of Hormuz, and the structural leverage Washington once wielded through tariff threats has diminished, partly due to US Supreme Court rulings and partly due to China's commanding position in rare earth mineral supply chains. Beijing appears likely to engage on its own terms and timetable, if at all. Chinese economic data released since the National People's Congress offers modest reassurance. Domestic demand rebounded in January and February, and fixed investment — which had been contracting — turned positive. Chinese equities have regained some footing, though they remain below their pre-2021 highs. The risk, as Gavekal Research notes, is that rising commodity prices compress margins for manufacturers unable to pass costs through to consumers, potentially triggering further investment pullback and forcing additional policy stimulus. China's 2026 growth target of approximately 5% relies more heavily on exports to non-US markets than on domestic consumption, making an oil-driven global slowdown a material threat. Elsewhere in the region, Australia's central bank raised its benchmark rate for a second consecutive meeting, standing out as one of the few economies tightening policy amid the global uncertainty. Indonesia's central bank delivered a hawkish hold while simultaneously tightening foreign-exchange regulations to defend the rupiah — the benchmark index is down approximately 14% in March. South Korea has followed China and Thailand in capping refined fuel exports to protect domestic markets. Japan faces a specific industrial supply chain vulnerability from tightening naphtha supplies, with multiple petrochemical firms announcing production cuts, and the Bank of Japan may consider lifting borrowing costs as soon as April. Pakistan's airstrikes on Kabul, which the Taliban government says killed at least 400 people at a drug treatment hospital, represent a separate and serious escalation risk in the region.

    Europe

    European markets are navigating the Iran shock from a position of genuine but incomplete resilience. The Stoxx Europe 600 gained 0.3% on the day, masking significant divergence: France's CAC is down 7.6% in March, Switzerland's SMI off 8.1%. The European Central Bank meets this week against a backdrop where market expectations have shifted sharply toward rate hikes — a marked reversal from pre-war positioning — reflecting the continent's acute energy import dependence. Natural gas prices in Europe have risen more than 60% since hostilities began, and Dubai crude contracts for Gulf-loaded oil are up over 80% this month. One cushioning factor is Europe's expanded renewable fleet. Rabobank estimates that electricity prices would be approximately one-third higher absent the growth in solar and wind capacity since the 2022 energy crisis, with France's nuclear fleet also operating more reliably than during that earlier episode. Evening price spikes in several countries nonetheless point to ongoing vulnerability, and analysts warn that seasonal support from solar output will fade heading into autumn. On the fiscal front, Germany's €600 billion defense spending program is beginning to generate tangible economic stimulus, with defense suppliers reporting record order books across artillery shells, drones, and related equipment. However, Chancellor Friedrich Merz's political standing remains precarious: recent polling shows fewer than a quarter of Germans hold a positive view of him, and critics argue his broader reform agenda has stalled. The European Commission is reported to be in the final stages of concluding a long-delayed trade agreement with Australia, with Commission President von der Leyen potentially traveling to sign the deal as early as this weekend. In Switzerland, a People's Party initiative to cap the national population at 10 million ahead of a June referendum retains 45% support — a level the government views with concern.

    Global

    The defining macro development of this period is the Iran war's uneven transmission through global energy markets, and the growing evidence that the conflict is reshaping monetary policy divergence across major economies. Brent crude rose to $104 a barrel on Tuesday, with the more severe dislocations appearing downstream: Goldman Sachs analysts noted that refined products have rallied far more than crude, reflecting the disruption to medium-heavy crude supplies that feed diesel, jet fuel, and fuel oil production. Jet kerosene in Singapore is up more than 60%, and in parts of Asia fuel costs have approximately doubled. Iran's intensified attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure — including setting the Shah natural gas field in the UAE ablaze and temporarily shutting the Fujairah port — represent the first confirmed damage to upstream oil and gas facilities in the UAE since the war began. Iran has explicitly rejected ceasefire overtures, and there is no clear pathway to resolution. Vessel-tracking data suggest Tehran is consolidating rather than losing control over Strait of Hormuz transit, with tankers navigating through only by hugging the Iranian coastline with apparent approval from Tehran. Insurance premiums for vessels transiting the strait have reached approximately 5% of hull value — roughly five times the level seen at the conflict's outset — and at least 20 security incidents have been recorded in and around the Persian Gulf since March 1. Trump's appeals for allied support have produced little tangible commitment. The UK, Japan, Germany, and South Korea have each indicated they will not participate militarily, and China's state media have been openly dismissive. Finland's President Stubb has been among the more forthcoming NATO voices, urging allies to take Trump's warnings seriously. Global shipping disruption continues to mount, with more than 25,000 flights canceled by major Middle Eastern carriers and air freight rates on Asia-Europe routes up 45-80%. Russia, notably, has benefited: Moscow has accelerated crude loading to capture elevated prices while a US sanctions waiver window remains open, with revenues from its key export blend hitting a record for weekly income since the Ukraine invasion began.

  • US

    US equity futures edged higher on Monday, with S&P 500 contracts up 0.6%, as investors weighed surging energy costs against the prospect that the Federal Reserve and other major central banks will hold rates steady this week. The Fed meets Wednesday in what may be its most consequential policy gathering since the post-pandemic inflation cycle, convening for the first time since US and Israeli strikes on Iran began roughly three weeks ago. Market expectations have shifted materially. Bets on Fed easing in 2026 have eroded substantially, and while investors still lean marginally toward cuts later in the year — making the US the relative dovish outlier among G7 peers — the near-term path has narrowed. As Bloomberg Economics' Tom Orlik summarized the likely message from policymakers: "Stay on hold, signal vigilance, and hope the Iran war ends before it lands them with another inflation problem they're ill equipped to solve." Goldman Sachs economists offered a somewhat reassuring counterpoint, arguing that the inflation risk remains "mostly confined to energy" and that the conditions for a 2021-22 style broad supply-chain shock are absent. The political economy of the conflict is becoming increasingly uncomfortable for the administration. US gasoline prices averaged $3.70 per gallon on Sunday, up 26% from a month ago — a level that historical precedent suggests carries meaningful electoral consequences. The administration faces the compounding challenge of simultaneous tariff pressure and energy inflation, with Supreme Court-directed tariff refund processes still unresolved and new tariff investigations ongoing. A parallel domestic story: the Trump administration directed Sable Offshore to restore oil production off California's coast as part of efforts to ease supply tightness. In corporate news, Meta shares rose roughly 3% in premarket trading after reports of a $27 billion cloud infrastructure deal with Nebius and a planned workforce reduction of over 20%, while Nvidia gained ahead of its four-day AI conference.

    Asia

    China's January-February economic data released Monday surprised to the upside, offering an encouraging snapshot before the Iran conflict's full weight can register. Industrial production rose 6.3% year-on-year — the fastest pace since September — while retail sales and fixed-asset investment both exceeded forecasts, the latter expanding unexpectedly. The rebound suggests domestic momentum was building heading into the crisis, though analysts cautioned that if the Strait of Hormuz closure persists and stalls export activity, sustaining this trajectory will be difficult. The geopolitical pressure on Asia is intensifying. China, India, Japan, and South Korea collectively account for roughly 70% of crude shipments transiting Hormuz under normal conditions, making the region the most acutely exposed to the supply disruption. Standard Chartered's Eric Robertsen identified stagflation — not inflation alone — as the primary risk. Asian currencies are under significant pressure, with Indonesia, India, the Philippines, and Taiwan all intervening in foreign-exchange markets over the past two weeks. The yen has approached its 2024 all-time low despite meaningfully higher interest rates, and Japan's finance minister signaled readiness to respond. Japan also began releasing oil reserves. India finds itself in a particularly difficult position. Prime Minister Modi secured safe passage for two LPG tankers through Hormuz — placing India among a small group of nations, alongside China and Russia, with ships transiting the strait — while simultaneously managing US relations. India takes 90% of its LPG imports from the Middle East, and alternatives from the US would take at least 40 days to arrive at higher cost. State elections in April will test Modi's handling of the energy crisis. Alibaba separately plans to announce an enterprise AI agent product based on its Qwen model this week, integrating with Taobao and Alipay.

    Europe

    European markets reflected the strain of the energy shock on Monday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 slipping 0.2% as bond yields continued rising across the eurozone and UK — reversing the prior trend and signaling market concern about both inflation and potential monetary policy tightening. The Bank of England and ECB both meet this week, with traders now pricing in possible rate hikes later in the year, a marked shift from the easing trajectory that prevailed just weeks ago. The more likely near-term outcome is a hold accompanied by cautious language, but the direction of risk is clearly shifting. UK inflation dynamics are being assessed against the 2011 energy shock precedent rather than 2022, which analysts suggest may imply a more measured BoE response. The conflict is also exposing tensions within European political structures. Trump explicitly warned that the US "will remember" whether NATO allies support efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, adding that insufficient response would be "very bad for the future of NATO." European governments are weighing these demands cautiously, with several years of post-Greenland skepticism about Washington's reliability. In UK domestic politics, Nigel Farage's Reform UK appears to be losing ground to two newer far-right parties — Restore UK (backed by Elon Musk) and Advance UK — suggesting fragmentation on the right fringe may complicate the political landscape ahead of future elections. In European corporate news, UniCredit launched a $40 billion bid for Commerzbank that would allow the Italian lender to increase its shareholding beyond the 30% threshold, easing the path toward a potential full acquisition. In Italy, Giorgia Meloni's government is consulting on restarting nuclear energy production, positioning atomic power as a long-term solution to Italy's elevated energy prices — among the highest in Europe.

    Global

    The defining development across global markets this week is the continued effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, now entering its third week, with no credible near-term resolution in sight. Brent crude is above $105 per barrel, up more than 40% since the conflict began. The IEA has characterized the disruption as the largest oil supply shock in recorded history by volume, though longer-dated futures — Brent for five years hence remains below $70 — suggest markets still price the closure as temporary. That view may be increasingly difficult to sustain. The game theory has shifted against a quick resolution. Prediction markets now put the probability of a ceasefire by May 31 below 50%. Iran appears committed to maintaining the Strait's closure as its primary leverage instrument, and Trump's public demands that NATO allies and China contribute naval assets to reopen the waterway have so far been rebuffed: Australia ruled out deploying vessels, Japan's defense minister said there are no plans to send warships, and China — whose carriers appear to still be transiting selectively — has condemned the war without committing to intervention. Trump threatened to delay his end-of-month summit with Xi Jinping if Beijing does not assist. The macro consequences are broadening. Goldman Sachs economists assessed that broader supply-chain disruption comparable to 2021-22 is unlikely, as non-energy Gulf trade represents only 1% of global commerce. However, the energy shock alone is sufficient to materially complicate central bank deliberations across 18 policy meetings this week, covering approximately two-thirds of the global economy. The week's central bank calendar is historically dense: Australia on Tuesday, the Fed and Brazil on Wednesday, the ECB, Bank of Japan, Bank of England, and several others on Thursday. All are expected to hold, but the tone will be notably more cautious. Tungsten prices have more than doubled this year amid Chinese export controls and rising military demand, illustrating how the conflict is amplifying pre-existing commodity tensions.

  • US

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Asia

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    Two weeks into the US-Israel military campaign against Iran, the conflict's secondary consequences are becoming clearer — in economic dislocation, environmental damage, and geopolitical realignment — even as the primary military situation remains fluid. The Gulf Cooperation Council economies face what may prove to be a structural setback to their diversification ambitions. The implicit bargain underpinning the GCC's relationship with Washington — large investment pledges and increased oil output in exchange for regional stability — has effectively collapsed. Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, and Kuwait have each absorbed Iranian drone and missile strikes on oil and gas infrastructure, representing the first such attacks in their histories. Energy revenue has been curtailed by Tehran's ongoing interdiction of Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic, which Iran has applied with apparent effectiveness despite degraded military capacity. Bloomberg Economics analyst Ziad Daoud notes that the financial arithmetic is straightforward: less revenue means less available for the outward investment commitments made to the Trump administration, which now appears unlikely to reach the headline figures announced during Trump's May 2025 Gulf tour. The ambition to position the Gulf as a hub for global AI infrastructure appears particularly vulnerable.
