The UAE and Saudi Arabia had leveraged their cheap energy and permissive regulatory environment to attract large-scale data center investment, with G42's divestiture from Chinese assets positioning the region as a conduit for American technology's global expansion. Iranian strikes have now targeted AWS facilities in the UAE and Bahrain, exposing a physical security assumption that underwrote the entire partnership. As Bloomberg Economics technology analyst Michael Deng observes, the Gulf states satisfied Washington's chip security demands but left physical infrastructure exposed. Separately, the environmental toll of strikes on Iranian oil infrastructure is emerging as a significant humanitarian concern. Israeli drone strikes on refineries and fuel depots on Tehran's outskirts on March 8 triggered large-scale fires, releasing toxic particulates that mixed with rainfall to produce contaminated black rain over a metropolitan area of approximately 18.5 million people. Tehran's geography — situated at the base of the Alborz mountains, which create thermal inversions trapping pollutants — amplifies the risk of prolonged exposure. Environmental analysts identify over 300 incidents carrying environmental risk from the conflict to date, with the Tehran refinery strike the single largest. The health legacy, including heavy metals and hydrocarbons absorbed into soil, water, and human tissue, may extend for decades.

  • US

    US equity futures were modestly higher on Friday, with S&P 500 futures up 0.13% to 6,686, though the index has declined roughly 3% in March as the Iran conflict has reframed the macro outlook. The Magnificent Seven gauge is nearing correction territory, down nearly 10% from its peak, with the group trading mixed. The session's most notable corporate story was the departure of Adobe CEO Shantanu Narayen after 18 years, with shares falling approximately 8.5% in pre-market trading following a lackluster revenue outlook and deepening investor concern about Adobe's competitive positioning in the AI era. The macro backdrop for the Federal Reserve has grown meaningfully more complex. Economists have pushed back expectations for the next rate cut to June, and Goldman Sachs revised its forecast further to September. With oil above $100 per barrel, the inflation-growth tension has sharpened: elevated energy prices are simultaneously stoking price pressures and eroding household purchasing power, limiting the policy space typically available in a slowdown. The ICE BofA MOVE Index, a measure of Treasury market volatility, climbed to its highest level since June, as traders dialed back 2026 rate-cut expectations materially. Goldman also raised its 12-month US recession probability by 5 percentage points, to 25%. TD Securities quantified the threshold at which demand destruction might outweigh inflation concerns for the Fed at roughly $150 per barrel of Brent, a scenario under which it expects the Fed to ease by more than a percentage point to address recession risk. A more severe scenario involving material damage to Middle East energy infrastructure, with oil reaching $200, could see GDP contract and rates fall below 2%. On the fiscal front, American officials disclosed that the first six days of the Iran war cost over $11.3 billion, with the Financial Times reporting the US has burned through "years" of critical munitions. The financial and strategic costs of the campaign are accumulating without a discernible endgame, a dynamic that is beginning to register in market pricing and economic forecasts.

    Asia

    Asian markets declined on Friday, with the Hang Seng falling 0.98% as risk appetite remained subdued ahead of a potentially volatile weekend in the Middle East. Regional currency markets were notably stressed: the yen weakened to its softest level since 2024 at approximately 159.5 against the dollar, South Korea's won traded near its lowest since 2009, and India's rupee and the Philippine peso reached historic lows. The Thai baht dropped as much as 1.8% intraday. A gauge of dollar strength has risen 2.2% in March, reversing four months of depreciation, as hedge funds accumulate short-dated dollar call options to hedge weekend Iran-related risk. Southeast Asian governments moved to impose emergency energy conservation measures. Thailand ordered most civil servants to work from home full-time; the Philippines shifted to a four-day government work week; Malaysia is expected to announce similar arrangements next week. Philippine President Marcos framed the situation plainly: "We are victims of a war that we didn't choose nor want." Geopolitically, the Trump-Xi summit framework is taking shape. US Treasury Secretary Bessent, Trade Representative Greer, and Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng will convene in Paris over the weekend to map deliverables for the March 31–April 2 summit in Beijing. Key agenda items include tariffs, fentanyl, Taiwan, potential Chinese investment in the US, and semiconductor export policy. Beijing has condemned the Iran strikes but shows no indication of withdrawing from the summit, prioritizing near-term stabilization of the bilateral relationship. Domestically, China's consumer spending may have recorded its worst start to any year outside the pandemic, according to economists surveying ahead of Monday's official release. The divergence between China's export strength — a $1.2 trillion trade surplus last year, with exports surging in the first two months of 2026 — and deteriorating domestic demand and wages for low-skilled workers in provinces like Guangdong remains a structural challenge for Beijing's rebalancing agenda. Worker protest activity rose 44% last year, with Guangdong the primary hotspot.

    Europe

    European equities declined modestly, with the Stoxx Europe 600 off 0.22% as concerns about energy costs and growth weighed on sentiment. The euro fell to its lowest level since August against the dollar, reflecting the continent's acute exposure to elevated oil prices through both import costs and supply chain disruption. The European Central Bank is now expected to hold rates unchanged through 2027, a marked shift from earlier dovish expectations, as inflation risks resurface from energy. The UK delivered a downside surprise on growth, with GDP flat in January against expectations for a 0.2% gain — weaker than every forecast in the analyst survey. The data predate any meaningful impact from the Middle East conflict, suggesting the UK economy was already losing momentum before the current energy shock. The figures add pressure on the government, which faces a deteriorating fiscal backdrop and limited room to respond. Germany's chemical industry association VCI warned that supply chain disruption and surging energy costs are beginning to constrain output across Europe's largest economy. The VCI's director general described "a spiral spinning in the wrong direction," with raw material shortages compounding the direct effect of higher energy prices. This dynamic raises the prospect of a fresh industrial slowdown in Germany, which was only beginning to stabilize after a prolonged contraction. In French politics, Marine Le Pen's effort to broaden National Rally's appeal to corporate and establishment elites is generating internal friction, with some party officials describing a new circle of elite advisers as alienating the working-class base that drove the party's rise. The tensions bear monitoring given Le Pen's proximity to the French presidency in current polling.

    Global

    The defining development of the period remains the Iran war, now approaching its third week, with no credible path to de-escalation visible. Brent crude held above $100 per barrel, with Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei explicitly vowing to keep the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed and threatening to open additional fronts if US-Israeli strikes continue. Approximately a fifth of global oil and natural gas flows through the strait; Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE have all been forced to curtail production. Trump responded with a stark social media threat, language that markets interpreted as signaling no near-term easing of hostilities. The strategic picture is deteriorating in ways beyond the energy market. Russia is providing Iran with satellite imagery and drone-targeting intelligence, according to multiple sources, complicating the US campaign while simultaneously benefiting from the Strait's closure. With the discount on Russian Urals crude nearly eliminated relative to Brent, Russia stands to capture a windfall comparable to its 2022 energy revenues. The US has responded by issuing a second authorization to ease Russian oil sanctions, allowing buyers to take cargoes already at sea — a decision that has drawn particular resentment among European allies. The traditional 60/40 portfolio hedge is breaking down as bonds and equities move in tandem, driven by stagflation concerns. Government bond yields have risen across the US, UK, Germany, Canada, and Australia by more than 25 basis points since the conflict began, eroding bonds' conventional cushioning role. Chinese assets, gold, the dollar, and buffer ETFs are emerging as the practical havens of the moment. US shale producers have not yet publicly committed to expanding output despite WTI touching its highest level since August 2022, though early signals — rising rig counts and government production forecasts revised upward for 2027 — suggest the industry's capital discipline may be tested. The White House has limited tools to suppress prices, and Trump, who campaigned on low energy costs, may move to pressure producers directly if prices remain elevated.

  • US

    US February CPI data arrived broadly benign, with core inflation holding at 2.5% year-on-year — the lowest since the 2021 inflation breakout — as shelter costs rose just 0.1% from January, the slowest pace in five years, and used-car prices continued declining. The data would ordinarily support a more accommodative Federal Reserve posture, but it is being overshadowed by a more concerning divergence: the core PCE is on track to print at approximately 3.1% for January, which would represent the widest gap between the two inflation gauges in decades. Economists at Barclays, Morgan Stanley, and Bank of America project core PCE goods prices advancing at least 0.8% in February alone. The implication, as Citigroup noted, is that PCE data should keep Fed officials cautious and on hold, even as the Trump administration cites CPI prints to argue rates should be cut. Fiscal pressures are mounting independently of the monetary debate. The 30-year Treasury yield approached 4.90%, its highest level in a month, as investors price in the cost of military operations and potential household energy subsidies. The US budget deficit narrowed in February but at a slower pace than the prior month as tariff revenue continued to decelerate from late-2025 peaks. Morgan Stanley and Cliffwater LLC separately capped investor withdrawals from multi-billion-dollar private credit funds after redemption requests materially exceeded permitted limits — a reminder that liquidity mismatches in private markets become visible precisely when macro stress is elevated. On trade policy, the US Trade Representative's office filed sweeping Section 301 investigations against more than a dozen economies — including China, the EU, Mexico, India, and Taiwan — marking the first formal step to reconstruct a tariff wall following last month's Supreme Court ruling that struck down prior levies estimated at $170 billion. A public hearing is set for May 5th. The move risks complicating a planned Trump-Xi summit and further straining USMCA renegotiation efforts with Mexico.

    Asia

    China's footprint across the current crisis is expanding on multiple dimensions simultaneously. Beijing has adopted legislation cementing Xi Jinping's push to assimilate ethnic minorities, a further departure from Beijing's long-standing practice of affording such groups nominal autonomy. Separately, Chinese oil refiners have begun canceling agreed refined-fuel export cargoes — including gasoline and diesel — as Beijing tightens controls to manage the domestic impact of the Iran conflict. The People's Liberation Army resumed aircraft incursions into sensitive airspace near Taiwan following a brief unexplained pause, with Xi having told Trump that Beijing would never permit Taiwan's separation. The combination of economic self-insulation, military signaling, and diplomatic positioning reflects a posture of strategic opportunism. Hong Kong regulators raided the offices of two leading Chinese brokerages and arrested eight individuals on allegations of insider dealing and corruption — a notable enforcement action in a market already navigating external capital-flow pressures. Japan's Nikkei 225 declined 1.04% as global risk sentiment deteriorated, and more than a third of surveyed economists now expect the Bank of Japan to raise rates in April, a view reinforced by the global bond selloff that is pushing yields higher across the Asia-Pacific. Australian officials are seen potentially hiking rates next week. Turkey's central bank paused its rate-cutting cycle. India is in direct negotiations with Iran to secure safe passage for more than twenty tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, an attempt to protect energy supply that also illustrates New Delhi's continued transactional engagement with Tehran. South Korea's parliament approved the special legislation required to implement its $350 billion US investment pledge, even as Washington simultaneously launched a trade investigation targeting Seoul. The juxtaposition captures the difficulty of navigating the current US policy environment, where bilateral deal-making and adversarial trade actions can proceed on parallel tracks.

    Europe

    European bond markets are absorbing overlapping pressures. Yields have risen across the UK, Germany, and Australia as investors price in both the fiscal cost of military spending increases and the probable need to shield households from an energy shock. The European Union has warned that inflation could exceed 3% this year if oil remains near $100 per barrel and gas prices stay elevated — a scenario that would substantially complicate ECB rate decisions in an environment where the central bank had been cautiously easing. The EU's inflation warning is particularly resonant given that Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex, which produces roughly 20% of global LNG supply, has been affected by the regional conflict, driving European and Asian gas prices to their highest levels since early 2023. The Iran war is creating political fractures within the bloc. EU officials have acknowledged fears that economic pressure from elevated fuel prices may deepen disagreements between member states, potentially giving Moscow leverage to erode European solidarity over Ukraine. Kremlin envoy Kirill Dmitriev met senior Trump officials in Florida to discuss the energy crisis and prospective US-Russia economic cooperation — a development that adds to European anxiety about the durability of Washington's NATO commitments. In France, Marine Le Pen's National Rally is contesting municipal elections in Marseille, testing whether the party's appeal can extend beyond its provincial base into a cosmopolitan urban setting. Polls indicate crime ranks among voters' top concerns in the port city. Meanwhile, the Swiss franc has continued appreciating as a safe-haven destination, placing the SNB in a difficult position: intervention risks US accusations of currency manipulation, yet inaction compounds pressure on Swiss exporters. Analysts suggest the SNB may be forced to revisit negative interest rates despite significant domestic opposition.

    Global

    The central dynamic in global markets on March 12 is an oil shock of a scale that stress-tests existing crisis management tools. Brent crude briefly exceeded $100 per barrel again in London after Iran escalated attacks on parts of Dubai and struck two oil tankers in Iraqi waters. Oman evacuated ships from one of the last viable regional export ports, and Iraq halted terminal operations. The IEA announced a record 400-million-barrel release from emergency stockpiles — exceeding any prior intervention, including during the 2011 Libyan war and the 2022 Ukraine invasion — yet the announcement provided only temporary relief. The IEA itself estimates the Strait of Hormuz closure has cut the 20 million barrels per day normally transiting the corridor by approximately 90%, forcing Gulf producers to shut in 10 million barrels per day of output. At the higher end of disruption estimates cited by analysts, the released reserves cover roughly 25 days of the supply shortfall, while prediction markets now assign a 52% probability that the conflict extends beyond April 30. Iran's ceasefire conditions — a permanent pledge from both the US and Israel never to strike again — appear incompatible with either government's stated positions, leaving the diplomatic path narrow. Russia is exploiting the impasse adeptly: Putin has maintained a communication channel with Trump while simultaneously, according to reporting, providing intelligence to Iran to help target US forces. The fiscal windfall from elevated oil and gas prices is providing meaningful relief to Russia's war budget, while the diversion of US political attention reduces pressure on the Ukraine front. The global aviation network has absorbed one of its largest shocks since the pandemic, with over 46,000 flights canceled as Asia-Europe routes are severely disrupted. Economy-class round-trip fares from Sydney to London have increased more than 80%. Bond markets globally have surrendered their year-to-date gains, and the selloff is explicitly being described by investors as a fiscal credibility story rather than a purely inflation-driven one — reflecting the intersection of war financing costs, energy subsidies, and already-elevated sovereign debt levels across major economies.

  • US

    US equity futures erased early gains on Wednesday as oil prices climbed anew after UK naval authorities reported three vessels struck in the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf, underscoring the fragility of any market optimism around a near-term resolution to the US-Iran conflict. S&P 500 futures settled around 6,775, down modestly on the session, while Treasuries held broadly steady ahead of February CPI data — a release that retains significance even as the oil shock increasingly dominates the inflation conversation. On the policy front, Kevin Warsh met with Senator Thom Tillis to discuss his Federal Reserve chair nomination but failed to secure a clear path forward, leaving the succession question unresolved at a moment when the central bank's credibility and independence are under considerable scrutiny. A separate Bloomberg Opinion piece warned that a compromised Fed risks precipitating a fiscal crisis — a concern that may gain traction should energy-driven inflation force difficult monetary decisions in the months ahead. In corporate news, Amazon raised $37 billion in what ranks as the fourth-largest US corporate bond sale on record, with a further $11.6 billion sought in European markets, as the company funds accelerating AI infrastructure investment. Oracle shares surged approximately 10% in extended trading after reporting strong AI cloud demand, reinforcing the thesis that hyperscaler capital expenditure remains robust. On the credit side, JPMorgan Chase moved to restrict lending to certain private credit funds after marking down the value of loans to software companies — a development PIMCO's Christian Stracke described plainly as "a crisis of really bad underwriting." The $1.8 trillion private credit market is showing mounting stress as high-profile corporate blowups surface. Pre-conflict consumer data offered a counterpoint to the gathering macro gloom: Bank of America card data showed spending rose 3.3% in February year-on-year, the strongest growth in over three years, with tax refunds providing a visible boost across income cohorts, though the K-shaped divergence between higher and lower earners remains pronounced.

    Asia

    China's National People's Congress concluded its annual budget deliberations this week with the 15th Five-Year Plan (2026–2030) signaling policy continuity rather than transformation. The 2026 draft budget prioritizes advanced manufacturing, semiconductor development, and artificial intelligence — the so-called "new-productive forces" — while keeping the fiscal deficit at 4% of GDP. Analysts at Oxford Economics and Gavekal Research noted that the plan's twenty main indicators are broadly unchanged from those of the 14th Plan, and the absence of meaningful household consumption stimulus disappointed investors hoping for a demand-side rebalancing. Whether perpetuating the investment-heavy model will prove sustainable in a structurally weaker global trade environment remains an open question. China's resilience amid the oil shock has drawn notice. Chinese equities have declined less than global peers since the conflict began, the yuan has held broadly steady against the dollar, and government bond yields have barely moved. Beijing's years of investment in renewables, electric vehicles, and strategic oil stockpiling — now at a record 851 million barrels in onshore commercial inventories — have provided a tangible buffer. However, Bloomberg Economics cautions that the primary risk for China is not domestic inflation but an export demand shock: a 5% decline in external demand would subtract 0.16 percentage points from GDP growth, while a 10% drop would widen the drag to 0.31 percentage points. With oil briefly clearing $100 per barrel during the week, the threshold at which secondary global demand effects meaningfully pressure Chinese exports — estimated by Bloomberg Economics at a sustained $108/bbl — appears less remote than it did two weeks ago. Separately, Chinese authorities moved to restrict state enterprises and government agencies from deploying OpenClaw AI applications on office computers, citing cybersecurity concerns over the platform's unusually broad data access requirements. The move caused immediate market reaction: Minimax fell 6.5% in Hong Kong and Zhipu dropped 6.1%. India's situation deteriorated further amid the Gulf crisis, with the rupee under pressure, capital outflows accelerating, and the Reserve Bank of India intervening in currency markets. Indian equities have underperformed EM peers significantly since Modi's 2024 coalition government took office, even as real GDP expanded 7.8% last year and inflation remains within target.

    Europe

    The European Central Bank moved to the forefront of the monetary policy debate as the Iran conflict reshapes the eurozone's inflation and growth calculus. ECB Governing Council member Peter Kazimir stated explicitly that the war risks forcing the central bank to raise rates sooner than previously anticipated, while colleague François Villeroy de Galhau struck a slightly more measured tone, noting that next week's meeting is not the right moment for action but that policymakers will not permit inflation to become entrenched. ECB President Christine Lagarde separately pledged that the bank would act to prevent the conflict from inflicting the same inflationary pain on the eurozone as Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine — a signal that the institution is monitoring energy pass-through effects closely, even as the precise monetary response remains undetermined. The Stoxx Europe 600 fell 1.13% as markets processed renewed disruptions to Middle Eastern shipping. Rheinmetall shares declined 4.5% after the German defense contractor guided below analyst sales expectations for 2026, a notable miss given the broader European defense spending cycle. In the automotive sector, Porsche rose 2.1% after incoming CEO Michael Leiters outlined plans to introduce models above the 911 to support margins — though the broader picture for German automakers in China remains challenging, with Porsche's China sales slumping as domestic electric vehicle manufacturers capture an increasing share of the premium segment. Mercedes-Benz separately unveiled a luxury electric van targeting Chinese consumers, illustrating the competitive pressure incumbents face in their most important growth market. In the UK, London Underground commuters face twelve days of strike action beginning later this month, with the RMT union opposing Transport for London's proposal to allow drivers to volunteer for a four-day working week. Chancellor Rachel Reeves declined to speculate on the fiscal implications of the Iran war, while the Bank of England confirmed that historical figures on banknotes — including Winston Churchill and Jane Austen — will be replaced by native British wildlife.

    Global

    The defining development of March 11 in global markets was the IEA's announcement of an emergency release of 400 million barrels of oil from member-country reserves — the largest such action in the agency's history. Executive Director Fatih Birol characterized it as "an emergency collective action of unprecedented size," and the decision was unanimous. Japan separately announced a unilateral release of approximately 80 million barrels, beginning as early as March 16. The announcement temporarily capped Brent crude below $90 per barrel, though analysts were quick to note that execution details — pace, duration, and logistics — will determine market impact. Some traders remain skeptical that reserves can be deployed rapidly enough to meaningfully offset the roughly 7 million barrels per day of supply disrupted by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The geopolitical picture remains fluid and the risk of a prolonged closure is arguably underpriced. UK naval forces reported three vessels struck in the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf on Wednesday, and Dubai International Airport briefly halted operations following drone strikes that injured four people. Analysts at BCA Research raise the uncomfortable possibility that even a ceasefire may not reopen Hormuz, as remnants of Iran's regime — or factions beyond Tehran's control — could sustain drone harassment of shipping indefinitely. Oil briefly traded above $100 during the week, and at $88 Brent, the market appears to embed a meaningful probability of conflict resolution that the military and diplomatic evidence does not yet support. The dollar has strengthened since hostilities commenced, driven by the US's position as the world's largest crude producer and the currency's role in global oil trade — a dynamic that partially reverses the roughly 6% decline in the Bloomberg dollar index over the past year. LNG supply flows are also shifting, with at least nine Europe-bound cargoes diverting to Asia, tightening availability in both regions simultaneously. Pakistan is navigating acute pressure, having raised fuel prices by a record 21% and suspended gas supplies to fertilizer companies following the suspension of Qatari gas flows, while trying to balance obligations to Saudi Arabia against domestic Shia sympathies and historic ties with Iran.

  • US

    US equity markets staged a partial recovery on Tuesday, with S&P 500 futures adding 0.36% and the Nasdaq turning positive for the month, as President Trump signaled the Iran conflict could end "very soon" and suggested he was weighing sanctions waivers and US Navy escorts for tankers through the Strait of Hormuz. The late-day recovery on Monday extended into overnight trading, though the broader macro picture remains materially complicated. The most consequential domestic economic development may be the interaction between the Iran shock and Trump's fiscal agenda. Bloomberg Economics estimates that average US households stand to receive approximately $800 in additional tax refunds from the "One Big Beautiful Bill" this year — but crude oil settling around $80 per barrel would impose an estimated $650 energy cost increase, effectively neutralizing most of the benefit. At $83 per barrel the OBBB gain is entirely erased, and at $110 it represents a net household loss of roughly $1,960. The distributional concern compounds this: energy costs are regressive, disproportionately affecting lower-income households, and the top 20% of earners already account for over 59% of personal consumption. Evercore ISI's Krishna Guha flagged the risk of amplified K-shaped dynamics as the conflict prolongs. Credit markets registered additional stress, with the Markit CDX North American Investment Grade Index posting its largest spread widening since last May, partly catalyzed by a weak February payrolls report. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index fell 0.95% last week, pushing investment-grade yield-to-worst to 4.9% and leaving year-to-date returns barely above zero. Private credit faces compounding pressure, with Blue Owl Capital's redemption difficulties still fresh and alternative asset managers navigating competing obligations to investors seeking exits. The Federal Reserve is in its pre-meeting quiet period ahead of next week's FOMC, but the committee's reaction function has become the pivotal near-term variable. Whether the Fed frames the oil shock primarily as inflationary or as a growth headwind will carry immediate market implications. Deutsche Bank's framework suggests a sustained bear market requires a large and lasting price spike, a hawkish policy pivot, and sufficient macro damage to tip an already-softening economy into recession — none of which is yet confirmed, but none of which can be dismissed.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets suffered significant losses when oil briefly surged toward $120 per barrel during Monday's session — the region's exchanges were open at the worst of the spike — before recovering Tuesday as Brent retreated toward $90. The Nikkei 225 rebounded 2.88% on Tuesday, partially recouping Monday's damage, while broader Asian indices bounced in sympathy with easing crude prices and Trump's signals on a possible near-term resolution to the Iran conflict. China's trade data, released Tuesday, provided a striking counterpoint to the broader anxiety: exports surged 21.9% year-on-year in the first two months of 2026, well above the 7.2% median estimate, generating a record trade surplus of $214 billion for the period. Imports rose nearly 20%. The strength was broad-based geographically, with shipments to the EU and UK holding up despite trade friction concerns, though exports to the US fell 11% reflecting tariff effects. The juxtaposition between China's export machine and domestic labor conditions is notable: workers in Guangdong province report falling wages and disappearing jobs, with automation and AI increasingly substituting for human labor. Citigroup economists project nearly a third of Chinese jobs could eventually be affected by AI-driven displacement. India presents a more nuanced energy picture. New Delhi had been reducing Russian oil purchases under US pressure since December and pivoting toward Saudi and Iraqi barrels — precisely the supply chains most disrupted by the Hormuz closure. Washington's announcement last Thursday of a one-month sanctions waiver allowing India to resume Russian crude purchases provided material relief, with Indian refiners immediately absorbing available Urals spot cargoes. Tankers carrying over 20 million barrels of Russian crude were already within a week's sailing time of Indian ports. Retail fuel prices have been held steady, and India appears unlikely to need to draw on strategic reserves in the near term, though LPG and LNG supply stress persists. Across the broader region, Asian airlines are raising fares — Indian carriers hiked long-haul ticket prices 15% — while Thailand has mandated government work-from-home arrangements to curb energy demand. Apple's continued production diversification into India, where iPhone output rose 53% to approximately 55 million units in 2025, illustrates the ongoing supply-chain reconfiguration away from China, independent of the current crisis.

    Europe

    European equity markets recovered on Tuesday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 2.21% as oil prices retreated and Trump's remarks on a potential near-term end to the Iran conflict improved sentiment. The rebound followed a sharp session Monday when Brent briefly approached $120 during European trading hours, prompting a pause in primary bond issuance as borrowers stood down from planned debt sales. European credit markets remain under visible strain. The iTraxx Europe investment-grade index reached its highest level since May, while the Crossover index of sub-investment-grade credits crossed 300 basis points for the first time since June. Bank of America analysts describe liquidity as "broadly supportive" but note a rising share of respondents reporting deteriorating conditions. An analysis by Goldman Sachs shows that nearly 150 European companies have already ceded control to direct-lending funds after failing to service their debts — a measure of how private credit stress is materializing on the continent. With corporate issuance delayed last week and again Monday, a pipeline of borrowers is waiting for a sufficiently stable window to access markets. UK consumer sentiment fell to a four-month low in March according to Barclays data, reversing gains made at the start of the year as Middle East developments weighed on household confidence, even as British Retail Consortium figures showed modest retail sales growth in February. On a longer-duration European policy question, EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen made pointed remarks calling nuclear energy abandonment a "strategic mistake," a thinly veiled reference to Germany's reactor closures, as energy security anxiety intensifies across the bloc. Marcus Ashworth's analysis highlights that global deleveraging since the Iran war began has been particularly damaging for the euro, with markets now pricing the possibility of ECB rate tightening as early as July — a notable shift in expectations that reflects how quickly the inflationary oil shock is reshaping European monetary policy assumptions. Whether that repricing is durable depends heavily on how long supply disruptions persist.

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme remains the US-Iran conflict and its effects on oil markets, supply chains, and the global growth outlook. Brent crude's intraday range on Monday — a swing of approximately $36, the widest on record and surpassing even the moves seen at the onset of the Ukraine invasion — illustrated the degree of market stress, even as prices ultimately settled near $90. The swift reversal from near-$120 to below $90 in a single session reflects both genuine residual optimism about conflict duration and the extreme sensitivity of sentiment to any signal from Trump or the White House. Six-month Brent futures are pricing approximately $80 per barrel, roughly 10% above pre-conflict levels — meaningful, but not yet the sustained $110-plus scenario that historical analysis suggests is necessary to generate a full macro regime shift. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively at a near-standstill, with at least twelve vessel clusters identified near the waterway, indicating elevated electronic interference and GPS jamming consistent with active military operations. Oxford Economics data show trade through the strait has dried up materially. Deepening Middle East production cuts are estimated to have removed approximately 6% of global supply. Qatar's LNG export facility, the world's largest, has suffered a war-related outage, tightening global gas markets and leaving some Asian buyers struggling to source prompt cargoes. G-7 energy ministers are meeting virtually on Tuesday to coordinate responses. Finance ministers have stated readiness to release strategic petroleum reserves, though that threshold has not yet been reached. French President Macron is separately pursuing a joint maritime escort mission for container vessels once initial hostilities subside. Russia's posture adds complexity: President Putin has instructed domestic energy companies to exploit elevated prices to pay down debt, and Moscow is reportedly considering ending most gas sales to the EU, while also holding what the Kremlin described as constructive discussions with Trump on both Iran and Ukraine. The broader macro framework for assessing duration and damage is reasonably clear among analysts: Societe Generale and Deutsche Bank both identify the shock's persistence and central bank reaction functions — particularly the Fed's — as the two decisive variables. A hawkish Fed pivot in response to oil-driven inflation, layered on an already softening US labor market and strained credit conditions, would constitute a materially more adverse outcome than current market pricing implies. That question is unlikely to be resolved before next week's FOMC meeting.

  • US

    The dominant story for US markets on March 9 is the cascading economic fallout from the US-Israeli war against Iran, now in its tenth day, compounded by a dismal February employment report that preceded the conflict's intensification. Brent crude surged as high as $119.50 per barrel intraday before partially retracing after reports of a prospective G7 coordinated strategic reserve release, but remained firmly above $100. S&P 500 futures declined 0.9%, a relatively contained move compared with broader global equity markets, though approximately $6 trillion in global market capitalization has been erased since hostilities commenced. The February payrolls report, released Friday, delivered an unambiguous negative surprise: private sector payrolls recorded their first decline in five years, while the unemployment rate rose. Under normal circumstances, such data would reinforce expectations for Federal Reserve easing and satisfy political pressure from the Trump administration to cut rates. Instead, the implied fed funds rate in overnight index swap markets has risen over the past week, as oil-driven inflation expectations overwhelm the labor market signal. Oxford Economics maintains its baseline of Fed inaction until June, with two cuts thereafter, but acknowledges the war materially clouds the path. The February CPI report due Wednesday is expected to show benign core readings of approximately 0.2% month-on-month — a pre-war snapshot that will offer limited forward guidance given oil's near-30% surge in March alone. The stagflationary tension is the central macro challenge: deteriorating labor markets argue for accommodation while an energy price shock argues against it. ING's Carsten Brzeski characterized this as a second supply-side inflation shock arriving while tariff pass-through remains unresolved. Consumer balance sheets were already showing stress — New York Fed data on credit card delinquencies indicated elevated household financial pressure — before gasoline prices reached their highest level in more than 18 months. President Trump dismissed the oil price surge as "a very small price to pay" for safety and peace, though midterm elections in November create political sensitivity around an affordability squeeze that the administration had previously sought to contain. On the corporate side, Hims & Hers Health surged 49% after Novo Nordisk agreed to distribute its weight-loss drugs on the telehealth platform, ending a prolonged commercial dispute. Live Nation is reportedly close to settling its DOJ antitrust case on terms that would preserve the Ticketmaster business. Airline stocks fell more than 3% across American, Delta, and United on fuel cost exposure.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets absorbed the sharpest session losses of any region on Monday. The MSCI Asia-Pacific index fell 3.7%, extending its weekly decline to nearly 10%. Japan's Nikkei 225 dropped 5.2%, with the index now in correction territory from its recent highs, as the oil shock interacts with a structurally weak yen to amplify Japan's import cost burden — raising the risk of stagflation in an economy where activity was already sluggish. South Korea's Kospi fell 6%, with Seoul reporting that US military transport aircraft, including C-17 and C-5 variants, had flown out of Osan Air Base in recent days amid discussions around potential redeployment of American assets as the Iran conflict broadens. Gauges in Pakistan and Vietnam fell more than 6%. The macro stress on Asia's energy-importing economies is acute. Fitch Ratings identified India and Pakistan as particularly exposed to deteriorating credit metrics from sustained high oil prices. India imports approximately 90% of its oil needs; a widening trade deficit and currency pressure compound an already fragile external position. South Korean President Lee Jae Myung called for fuel price caps, while the Philippines ordered government offices to operate four days weekly to reduce energy consumption and initiated talks with suppliers in the Americas, Australia, and Africa. Taiwan set a weekly cap on oil price increases. Vietnam faces potential airfare increases of up to 70% as jet fuel costs have tripled. China presents a more buffered picture. As the largest single buyer of Hormuz-transiting oil, China holds substantial strategic petroleum reserves that analysts believe can absorb near-term supply disruption. Separately, Chinese consumer price growth accelerated to its fastest pace in over three years. On the diplomatic front, Foreign Minister Wang Yi described 2026 as a potentially defining year for US-China relations, signaling Beijing's intent to insulate the bilateral relationship from the Iran conflict despite maintaining an upgraded partnership with Tehran. China simultaneously warned Japan of unspecified consequences after Tokyo allowed a visit by Taiwan's Premier Cho Jung-tai — a reminder that regional geopolitical tensions remain layered beneath the Iran-dominated headlines. Taiwan's dollar fell 0.8% on the session.

    Europe

    European markets are confronting a dual shock: soaring energy prices threatening to reignite inflation, and a deteriorating growth backdrop that was already fragile. Interest rate markets moved sharply; swaps now imply two full 25 basis point ECB rate hikes in 2026, compared with one priced as recently as Friday. The first hike is fully priced by June. For the Bank of England, money markets shifted from pricing cuts to a 70% probability of a hike by year-end, as estimates from ING and RSM UK suggest UK inflation could rise to more than double the 2% target if current energy price levels persist. The ECB and BOE face a genuinely uncomfortable position: easing into an energy-driven inflation shock risks credibility loss, while tightening risks amplifying a growth slowdown. German economic data released Monday added to the difficult backdrop. Both industrial production and factory orders declined at the start of the year, tempering expectations for a cyclical recovery in Europe's largest economy. Germany's chemical industry faces a structural crisis that predates the Iran conflict — higher energy costs following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, rising carbon prices, new Chinese capacity, and softening demand have already forced plant closures — but the war materially intensifies pressure. European chemical producers are estimated to pay approximately €3 billion more annually in natural gas costs if the post-war price surge persists. The Leuna chemical park in eastern Germany, exposed through its dependence on energy and raw material imports, has become a test case. In politics, Chancellor Friedrich Merz suffered a notable setback as his CDU lost the Baden-Württemberg regional election to the ruling Greens — a state the CDU had been projected to reclaim after 15 years of Green governance. The result compounds Merz's domestic political difficulties and reflects broader voter unease. In the UK, Prime Minister Starmer pledged support for households facing energy cost pressures. German bund futures slid to an almost 15-year low, with Italian fiscal credibility also identified as at risk from a prolonged Middle East conflict by Scope Ratings.

    Global

    The defining development of March 9 is Iran's selection of Mojtaba Khamenei — the 56-year-old son of the assassinated Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, with deep ties to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps — as the new supreme leader. The appointment, ratified by Iran's Assembly of Experts, signals continuity of hardline governance rather than any movement toward negotiated resolution. Prediction markets place a 48% probability on no ceasefire occurring before the end of April. President Trump described himself as "not happy" with the selection and is reportedly weighing deployment of special forces to seize Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium, amid US official concern that enriched stockpiles may have been relocated since UN inspectors last verified their position nine months ago. Tehran has stated it can sustain high-intensity conflict for at least six months. Brent crude surged intraday to $119.50 — the largest intraday move in percentage terms since April 2020 — before retreating to near $104-105 after the Financial Times reported G7 finance ministers would convene to discuss a coordinated strategic petroleum reserve release. OECD nations collectively hold over 1.2 billion barrels in emergency reserves; the announcement provided meaningful short-term relief, though Citigroup estimates the Hormuz closure is removing approximately 16 million barrels per day of crude and refined products from global markets — roughly 16% of world supply — a disruption that dwarfs the 2022 Russian shock. Saudi Arabia has begun reducing output as Hormuz closure fills storage tanks; Iraq, the UAE, and Kuwait have similarly curtailed production. QatarEnergy has delayed a major LNG expansion project to at least 2027 following drone attacks on the Ras Laffan facility. Beyond energy, the war is disrupting approximately one-fifth of global air freight, compressing Asia-Europe container traffic, and creating acute stress in fertilizer markets. Iran accounts for approximately 5% and 11% of global urea and ammonia trade respectively; urea prices have risen 25% since hostilities began, reaching levels last seen during Russia's Ukraine invasion. Crop prices are rising in parallel, threatening a global food inflation impulse that will materialize with a lag. The IMF has urged policymakers to prepare for a prolonged conflict scenario. European natural gas futures jumped as much as 30% on Monday. Aluminum briefly hit a near four-year high, and coal prices reached their highest level in over a year as gas substitution effects propagated through power markets.

  • US

    US electric vehicle market dynamics present an apparent contradiction: new EV sales fell nearly 40% year-over-year in Q4 2025, yet public charging infrastructure continues to expand and utilization is rising. ChargePoint Holdings posted a 7% increase in quarterly revenue despite the sales decline, while EVgo plans to add up to 1,650 new charging slots in 2026, a 38% increase over last year. The resolution of this paradox lies in the cumulative installed base — approximately 5.8 million EVs now operate on American roads — which sustains charging demand even as new purchase rates soften. Ultra-fast charging capacity expanded roughly 48% in 2024, with nearly one in four new chargers installed in Q4 capable of delivering 250 kilowatts or more. Analysts at Needham characterize the weak new-car sales trend as an overhang, and shares of both ChargePoint and EVgo remain near historical lows despite the constructive operating data, reflecting investor uncertainty about the medium-term trajectory. Two factors may yet shift the demand calculus. Elevated oil prices driven by the US-Iran conflict are providing an indirect subsidy to the economics of electric and hybrid ownership, and a cohort of new affordable electric SUVs entering the market could reignite consumer interest. Hybrid vehicle sales have risen since federal EV incentives expired, with S&P Global Mobility noting continued demand momentum in that segment. Charging infrastructure operators appear to be building for a 2035 demand horizon rather than optimizing for current conditions, suggesting capital deployment will continue even if the pace moderates near term. On the broader industrial policy front, the Trump administration's push to secure critical minerals — copper and cobalt in particular — through direct investment in the Democratic Republic of Congo adds a supply-side dimension to the US energy transition narrative, though execution timelines remain uncertain.

    Asia

    China set its official GDP growth target at approximately 5% for 2026 while deliberately avoiding a large-scale fiscal stimulus package, signaling that policymakers remain cautious about the economy's structural trajectory. The decision reflects a familiar tension: acknowledging near-term headwinds — including export exposure to US tariffs and subdued domestic consumption — while resisting the balance-sheet expansion that larger stimulus would entail. Markets had watched the target-setting closely for signs of a more aggressive demand-side response; the absence of one may temper near-term risk appetite for China-exposed assets. India moved to insulate its economy from the spillover effects of the US-Iran conflict, with policymakers focused on the dual exposure of elevated oil import costs and potential disruption to trade routes through the Persian Gulf. India's dependence on Gulf energy supplies makes it structurally vulnerable to any sustained increase in crude prices or Strait of Hormuz disruption, and the government's defensive posture reflects that sensitivity. The precise policy measures remain to be detailed, but the signal of active contingency planning is itself notable. South Korea's ongoing equity market reform initiative — the so-called "Korea Discount" program aimed at improving corporate governance and shareholder returns — faces a significant test as global risk sentiment becomes more complex. The program has attracted investor attention since its inception, but execution risk remains elevated given the country's chaebol-dominated corporate structure and the tendency for reform momentum to stall. Separately, geopolitical currents, including the minerals competition in Africa and the Iran conflict's effect on energy prices, create second-order headwinds for Asia's manufacturing exporters broadly, as input costs rise and trade route uncertainty increases.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    The US-Iran conflict has emerged as the dominant macro variable across multiple dimensions this week. Oil markets are pricing in a meaningful risk premium as the conflict raises questions about potential disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, through which a substantial share of global crude flows. The energy policy implications are genuinely uncertain: the conflict could accelerate the clean-energy transition by reinforcing the strategic case for domestic renewable capacity, or it could prompt fossil-fuel-dependent economies to extend hydrocarbon infrastructure investment in the name of energy security. Both dynamics may operate simultaneously in different regions, creating divergent policy trajectories rather than a clean directional shift. The US decision to go to war with Iran, while simultaneously pursuing Venezuela ties and sanctioning Rwanda's military, illustrates the breadth of the Trump administration's geopolitical activism and its underlying logic: securing physical access to critical resources — energy and minerals — as a strategic priority. The minerals competition with China is the clearest expression of this, with the US moving to acquire stakes in Congolese copper and cobalt assets while Beijing retains commanding market shares across sub-Saharan African commodity supply chains. China conducted roughly $200 billion in annual trade with sub-Saharan Africa in 2024 — approximately four times the US figure — and controls an estimated 80% of Congo's copper output. Closing that gap through deal-making alone, without the decades of relationship capital China has accumulated, appears a formidable task. The energy transition's near-term trajectory faces genuine uncertainty. Surging gasoline prices may accelerate hybrid and EV adoption in consumer markets, but simultaneously strengthen the political economy arguments for fossil fuel expansion in producing nations. This tension is unlikely to resolve cleanly in the near term, and investors with exposure to energy transition assets should treat the conflict's impact as a source of volatility rather than a directional catalyst.

  • US

    Financial markets entered the week's final session under pressure from a confluence of geopolitical and monetary policy concerns. S&P 500 futures traded 0.44% lower as of early Friday, with WTI crude at $84.46 — up over 4% on the day — deepening concerns about the inflationary trajectory. The setup is politically awkward for the Trump administration: prior to the Iran conflict, the president appeared to be achieving his stated market preferences of lower oil, lower yields, and a weaker dollar. All three have now reversed. The dollar has reclaimed safe-haven status, Treasury yields edged higher to 4.163% on the 10-year, and gasoline pump prices reached their highest level under Trump's presidency at $3.32 per gallon. The Federal Reserve faces a familiar dilemma. With Jerome Powell at the helm, the institution's institutional instinct appears to be patience — the same posture it adopted during the tariff-driven inflation episode last year. Bloomberg Economics' Anna Wong argues the oil shock will create an "asymmetric easing bias": near-term headline inflation rises, but consumer spending on non-energy goods contracts, ultimately pushing unemployment higher. For now, hawkish voices appear to be prevailing within the FOMC. The monthly US jobs report released Friday provided an additional data point for policymakers to assess, with a stronger reading potentially further deferring rate cut expectations. On the fiscal side, the Supreme Court's invalidation of Trump's IEEPA-based tariffs continues to reverberate. The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget estimates the ruling reduces government revenue by $1.7 trillion through 2036. The administration's replacement 10% levy would recoup approximately $925 billion, leaving a meaningful gap even if escalated to 15%. Federal debt accumulation continues on any modeled trajectory. On the corporate side, Marvell Technology surged 12% in premarket trading on accelerating data center demand, while Gap fell 6% on a disappointing Old Navy performance. Trump also dismissed Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, naming Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin as her replacement, raising questions about FEMA's leadership continuity ahead of the spring disaster season.

    Asia

    China's annual National People's Congress dominated regional attention this week, with Premier Li Qiang delivering a government work report that set the growth target at its lowest level since 1991. While the headline figure signals diminished ambition, Chinese officials framed the target as more credible and achievable than prior years, reflecting a deliberate pivot toward "quality" growth and deflation elimination — officials made their strongest commitment yet to ending deflationary conditions in 2026. Defense spending rose 7%, the slowest pace since 2022. The work report's characterization of US-China trade relations was notably upbeat, suggesting Beijing's leadership enters the upcoming Trump visit in Beijing with confidence in their negotiating position. Preparatory meetings between He Lifeng, Scott Bessent, and Jamieson Greer are expected in Paris next week, with potential deliverables including Boeing aircraft purchases, soybean commitments, and the fentanyl tariff framework. The Iran conflict's regional spillover is generating significant stress in Asian markets. Global funds sold a net $11 billion of emerging Asian equities excluding China this week — the largest outflow since March 2022 — with Taiwan absorbing $7.9 billion in withdrawals, South Korea $1.6 billion, and India approximately $1.3 billion. The US Treasury's temporary waiver allowing Indian refiners to resume Russian oil purchases provided some relief to New Delhi, though it simultaneously highlighted India's strategic exposure: a US submarine sank the Iranian frigate IRIS Dena off Sri Lanka after it had participated in an Indian Navy-hosted exercise, placing Modi in a diplomatically untenable position between Washington and Tehran. On China-Taiwan dynamics, Beijing has not dispatched military aircraft near Taiwan for seven consecutive days — the longest such absence since Taiwan began regular disclosure of PLA activity — a development whose significance remains unclear. Meanwhile, three Filipino nationals were detained for allegedly leaking sensitive South China Sea operational data to Beijing, heightening regional security concerns. The Bank of Japan remains on a rate-hiking path, with a former official placing April move odds at 50/50, even as the Iran conflict injects additional uncertainty into the global outlook.

    Europe

    European markets remained under mild pressure, with the Stoxx Europe 600 declining 0.28% amid the broader risk-off tone generated by the continuing Iran conflict. The most consequential regional development lies not in equities but in monetary policy signaling: the European Central Bank's policy trajectory appears to be shifting, with traders now fully pricing in a 2026 rate hike as the oil price surge fans inflation concerns. Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel was unambiguous on Thursday, stating that the inflation impact of the Iran war currently outweighs the growth drag — a view that, if shared by the broader Governing Council, would represent a meaningful pivot from the easing posture of recent quarters. Euro-area fourth-quarter GDP was simultaneously revised lower than initially reported, with trade weighing on output, creating a stagflationary tension that policymakers will need to navigate carefully. Germany provided a rare constructive energy datapoint: unusually strong solar generation in March, with installations feeding over 40 gigawatts into the grid for five consecutive days, has helped cap domestic electricity prices even as global energy markets surge. The episode illustrates the buffering capacity of domestic renewable generation during external supply shocks — a dynamic likely to reinforce policy arguments for accelerated energy transition investment across the continent. Bloomberg Opinion's Marcus Ashworth separately argued this environment represents an opportune moment for the EU to pursue joint bond issuance. The UK confronted a longer-term structural concern: London's child population is declining at the fastest pace in Britain, with the under-10 cohort falling by 99,100 between 2013 and 2023 even as total population rose by 506,000. The capital's fertility rate has dropped below 1.2 births per woman, well below the England and Wales average of 1.41. The demographic implications for school infrastructure, public services, and long-run fiscal capacity are material. Spain also emerged as a flashpoint in the Iran war's diplomatic fallout, with the Trump administration threatening to cut off LNG exports to Madrid after Spain denied access to military bases — a threat that, if executed, would expose Spain to higher marginal gas procurement costs.

    Global

    The US-Israel military campaign against Iran entered its seventh day with no evident de-escalation, reshaping energy markets, financial flows, and geopolitical alignments simultaneously. Iranian missiles and drones struck at least five countries across the Gulf region, including Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Bahrain, while Israeli airstrikes continued on Tehran. The Strait of Hormuz has effectively ceased functioning as an oil transit corridor — only two commercial vessel transits were recorded in the preceding 24-hour period and no oil shipments passed through at all. Goldman Sachs flagged the plausibility of Brent crossing $100 per barrel if low flows persist for another five weeks; Brent has already rallied approximately 18% on the week, with US diesel futures on track for a weekly gain exceeding 40%. The Trump administration has ruled out deploying Treasury to trade oil futures to suppress prices, at least for now, while the temporary waiver on Indian purchases of Russian crude represents a tactical release valve that simultaneously benefits Moscow's war finances at a moment when sanctions were beginning to bite meaningfully. The conflict is forcing central banks globally to confront another supply-side inflation shock in what has become a structurally shock-prone decade — Covid, the Russia-Ukraine war, mercantilist trade disruptions, and now a Middle East regional war have each required difficult monetary policy judgments under uncertainty. The IMF's Kristalina Georgieva warned that a sustained 10% increase in energy prices would push global inflation up 40 basis points and shave 0.1-0.2% from growth. Traditional safe-haven assets have behaved anomalously: gold has declined and is trading at a steep discount in Dubai, while Treasuries have also sold off, leaving the dollar as the primary haven vehicle. Geopolitically, Russia has emerged as the clearest early beneficiary of the conflict. India's return to Russian crude purchasing — with buyers paying premiums for stranded cargoes — replenishes the Kremlin's revenue stream at a critical juncture. The UAE and Gulf financial hubs face reputational and operational pressure as their image as stable commercial anchors is tested. Meanwhile, a new scientific study published in Geophysical Research Letters provides the first statistically significant evidence of an acceleration in global warming, with the planet warming at approximately 0.35°C per decade since 2015, nearly double the average rate between 1970 and 2015 — a finding that adds long-term structural urgency to energy transition debates even as immediate policy attention is consumed by the conflict.

  • US

    US equity markets are showing tentative signs of stabilization following sharp early-week declines, with S&P 500 futures trading near 6,871 — down marginally on the session. The recovery in US stocks relative to the rest of the world reflects a familiar safe-haven dynamic: the dollar is on track for its largest weekly gain in over a year, Treasury yields have partially reversed their sharp pre-war decline (the 10-year trading around 4.12%), and US money-market funds have swelled to a record $8.27 trillion as investors seek refuge. Assets in money-market funds at record levels suggest the flight-to-safety impulse remains intact even as equities stabilize. The conflict's inflationary implications are now complicating the monetary policy outlook in a meaningful way. Gasoline prices have risen 25 cents to $3.25 per gallon in a single week, diesel has surged nearly 30% since hostilities began, and market-implied estimates for the fed funds rate at year-end have risen to their highest level in nine months. That trajectory makes it substantially harder for an incoming Fed chair — presumably Trump's reported nominee Kevin Warsh — to pursue the rate cuts that markets had been pricing earlier this year. The tension between geopolitical inflation risk and any easing ambitions remains unresolved. In corporate news, Morgan Stanley announced it is cutting approximately 3% of its global workforce, or roughly 2,500 employees across all business lines, citing individual performance. Private credit stress continues to intensify: semi-liquid funds including Blackstone have raised their quarterly redemption caps to 7% from 5%, Fitch reported a trailing 12-month default rate of 5.8% in January — the highest since tracking began in 2024 — and short interest against Blue Owl Capital has reached an all-time high. Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon acknowledged watching the sector for "frothiness" while characterizing broad portfolio performance as "reasonably well." The private credit complex's heavy exposure to software, a sector now down roughly 30% against the broader market over six months amid AI disruption fears, remains the central vulnerability. Systemic risk appears contained for now given relatively modest bank leverage, but the asset class's role as the de facto arbiter of corporate lending standards will face a meaningful test this cycle.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific markets are navigating both the immediate shock of a widening conflict and China's annual policy setting, producing a complex and unsettled backdrop. Asian equities recovered partially on Thursday after sharp Wednesday losses, though the rebound was uneven. The most dramatic single-session move came in South Korea, where the Kospi surged 10% after a two-day decline of 18%, supported by President Lee Jae Myung's invocation of the country's 100 trillion won ($68.2 billion) market stabilization program. The swings reflect Korea's acute vulnerability as a major energy importer with elevated retail investor participation. The conflict's escalation into Indian waters commands particular attention. A US submarine sank the Iranian warship IRIS Dena off Sri Lanka's coast — the first US submarine attack on a surface vessel since World War II — as the ship returned from an Indian-hosted multilateral fleet review. The Indian rupee fell to a record low against the dollar, and Indian equity benchmarks have in dollar terms erased all gains since last April's Liberation Day market turmoil. The incident has created a delicate diplomatic position for Prime Minister Narendra Modi, who called for "dialog and diplomacy" while facing uncomfortable questions about sovereignty in India's own maritime neighborhood. China's National People's Congress delivered the session's most consequential policy signals. Premier Li Qiang set a GDP growth target of 4.5% to 5% — the lowest since 1991 — in an explicit acknowledgment that the country's economic model is under strain. Li described the transition to new growth drivers as "formidable," citing an imbalance between strong supply and weak demand. Fiscal stimulus is being modestly dialed back. Notably, Beijing is doubling down on technology: the accompanying five-year plan emphasizes AI, brain-computer interfaces, pharmaceutical innovation, and technological self-reliance, signaling that the rivalry with the US in advanced sectors will intensify. China also instructed its largest oil refiners to suspend diesel and gasoline exports immediately, reflecting precautionary energy conservation as Gulf crude flows are disrupted. On Hong Kong markets, mainland Chinese investors sold a record HK$27.7 billion worth of shares in a single session, the largest net selling on record, underscoring diminished risk appetite for assets perceived as geopolitically exposed.

    Europe

    European markets are absorbing a renewed energy shock with uncomfortable echoes of 2022. European natural gas futures have risen approximately 60% since hostilities began, breaching €50 per megawatt-hour, while the euro has declined roughly 2% against the dollar since the US-Israeli campaign commenced. The currency move is consistent with a well-established dynamic: Europe's structural dependence on imported energy means commodity price surges translate directly into deteriorating terms of trade, lower purchasing power, and growth headwinds. It was precisely this mechanism that drove the euro below parity during the 2022 gas crisis, and the conditions for a repeat are present, though the severity will depend on conflict duration. Politically, the energy price surge arrives at a precarious moment for European governments that have barely recovered from the 2022 shock and are already managing historically elevated debt levels. The European Central Bank's Bundesbank chief indicated the institution is "carefully monitoring" the impact of surging energy costs — language that falls short of signaling a policy response but acknowledges the dilemma between growth support and inflation containment that a sustained energy shock would impose. In the UK, domestic politics are generating their own friction for Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Over 100 Labour MPs wrote privately to Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood urging a rethink of proposed immigration reforms that would tighten residency pathways for low earners. The scale of internal dissent — arriving before Mahmood's planned speech to persuade colleagues — suggests the government's parliamentary management is under strain on multiple fronts. Separately, the Bank of England is planning to war-game a full-blown AI economic shock, including potential incorporation into its banking stress tests, reflecting growing institutional concern that AI-driven job displacement could produce a wave of household and corporate loan defaults material enough to threaten financial stability.

    Global

    Six days into the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran, the conflict has acquired genuinely global characteristics. The geographic footprint has expanded markedly: NATO air defenses downed a ballistic missile over Turkey, Arab Gulf states intercepted Iranian drones and missiles overnight, Israel is conducting waves of airstrikes on Tehran, and a US submarine conducted the first American submarine attack on a surface warship since the Second World War — sinking the IRIS Dena off Sri Lanka in Indian Ocean waters. Iran has vowed escalating retaliation, and US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth estimated operations could last three to eight weeks. The endgame remains opaque. Energy markets are the clearest transmission channel to the global economy. Brent crude has gained approximately 12% in the first three days of the week and continued higher Thursday. European natural gas is up roughly 60% on the week. Persian Gulf airspace and airport closures have disrupted roughly 13% of global air freight capacity — the three Gulf hub carriers alone — with Kuehne + Nagel estimating an 18% loss in global air cargo capacity. The Strait of Hormuz, handling 2-3% of global container volumes, has approximately 100 vessels stranded in the Gulf range. Container shipping surcharges are being applied, and supply chain stress is spreading from Southeast Asia toward Chinese and European ports. Importantly, Iran's military commander stated Tehran "does not believe in closing the Strait of Hormuz at all" — a signal worth monitoring as it would be the most severe escalation scenario. The macro divergence story that defined early 2026 — the "Sell America, Buy Asia" trade — has sharply reversed. The dollar's safe-haven demand, the US's relative energy self-sufficiency, and a pre-existing positioning setup in which non-US assets had been outperforming all contributed to a rapid reversion. Emerging market currencies and equities have given back recent gains at speed. Russia, notably, stands to benefit economically from elevated energy prices the longer the conflict continues. The IMF's Kristalina Georgieva warned that the war will test global resilience and that shocks "will keep coming" — an assessment consistent with the OECD's concurrent warning that sovereign debt markets, with their shortened duration profiles and reduced central bank ownership, are structurally more vulnerable to sudden shocks than surface stability implies.

  • US

    US equity markets displayed notable divergence from global peers on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 futures pointing to a modest gain of roughly 0.3% even as Asian markets collapsed and European indices remained choppy. The benchmark has declined less than 1% since hostilities in Iran began — a resilience that Goldman Sachs chairman David Solomon, speaking at a conference in Sydney, described as "benign" and somewhat surprising. The structural explanation is straightforward: the US is a net energy exporter, making it substantially less exposed to a sustained Strait of Hormuz disruption than European or Asian economies. As Barclays' Ajay Rajadhyaksha noted, the strait's closure is a far more acute problem for large Asian importers than for the American economy. That insulation is not unlimited, however. Fed funds futures markets are pricing out rate cuts at an accelerating pace as traders weigh the inflationary implications of sustained high oil prices. Bloomberg Economics has modeled scenarios in which a prolonged Hormuz closure pushes Brent toward $108 per barrel — a level that would simultaneously push inflation higher and slow growth, placing the Federal Reserve in an awkward position between a president demanding lower rates and an economy facing a supply-side price shock. The 10-year Treasury yield edged up to 4.075%, a modest move that nonetheless reflects the repricing of the easing path. In corporate news, Blackstone came under scrutiny as more than 25 senior executives collectively contributed approximately $150 million to support redemption payments from the firm's flagship private credit fund amid a record withdrawal wave. Separately, executives at the Bloomberg Invest conference, including Apollo's Marc Rowan and Soros's Dawn Fitzpatrick, warned of "a painful 18 to 24 months" ahead for private credit and private equity as default risks rise and asset sales remain difficult. Moderna gained 5.8% after settling Covid delivery-technology litigation for $950 million, while cryptocurrency-linked equities rallied as Bitcoin recovered ground lost earlier in the week.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets suffered their most severe week in nearly a year, with the region bearing a disproportionate share of the global fallout from the US-Iran conflict. South Korea's Kospi index fell 12% on Wednesday — the largest single-day decline in the index's history — compounding a 7.2% drop the prior session. The severity reflects both fundamental vulnerability and the unwinding of an overleveraged rally: the Kospi had risen roughly 50% this year, fueled by AI-driven optimism around chipmakers, retail participation on margin, and active encouragement from President Lee Jae Myung's administration, which had made crossing the 5,000-point threshold a political objective. Samsung Electronics fell 12%, SK Hynix declined approximately 10%, and Hyundai Motor lost 16%. Bank of Korea Governor Rhee Chang Yong delayed travel to Bangkok to convene an emergency meeting, while President Lee, abroad in the Philippines, announced an extraordinary cabinet session. The political consequences for Lee appear significant — his identification with the rally has turned a market rout into a direct test of his credibility. Thailand's benchmark fell as much as 8.6%, and Taiwan's main index declined over 4%. The Indian rupee fell to a record low. Asia's vulnerability is structural: the region depends overwhelmingly on imported oil and gas, meaning a prolonged Hormuz disruption transmits directly into energy import bills, inflation, and current account deterioration. Capital is also flowing out of the region and into dollar assets, with the Bloomberg dollar index up 1.4% on the week. China's National People's Congress convenes Thursday, where Xi Jinping's government is expected to unveil the 15th Five-Year Plan. The plan is anticipated to include a commitment to raise household consumption from roughly 41% of GDP toward 46% by 2030 — a more explicit pivot than prior plans. Fixed-asset investment contracted in 2025 for the first time in over three decades, and official PMI data released Wednesday showed factory activity contracting more than forecast. Chinese financial institutions are meanwhile scaling back exposure to Middle Eastern debt at regulatory direction.

    Europe

    European equity markets offered a partial counterpoint to the global selloff, with the Stoxx Europe 600 rising 1.3% on Wednesday, partly on reports of indirect Iran-US contact toward a ceasefire. However, the underlying macro picture deteriorated sharply. European natural gas prices surged more than 50% on the week — their highest level since Russia's invasion of Ukraine — reflecting the continent's substantial reliance on Qatari LNG at a time when the Ras Laffan facility has been shuttered by drone attack and Hormuz transits remain effectively halted. Bloomberg Economics estimates that sustained high energy prices could push Europe to the brink of recession. The week's most politically consequential European story involved Spain. President Trump threatened to "cut off all trade" with Spain after Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez denied Washington access to Spanish military bases for its Iran bombing campaign. Sánchez declined to yield, stating Spain would not "be complicit in something harmful to the world." Spanish equities fell to their lowest in nearly three months. Trump's trade threat faces legal complications given that bilateral trade is governed by EU-level agreements, and Banco Santander executive chair Ana Botín sought to publicly downplay the rift. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves stated Britain would not sacrifice its principles for favorable US trading terms, declining to endorse the American-Israeli operation. The Office for Budget Responsibility separately indicated that meeting Labour's commitment to raise defence spending to 3.5% of GDP by 2035 would require approximately £40 billion in additional outlays. In corporate news, Adidas fell 7.6% in Frankfurt after its profit forecast missed expectations due to US tariffs and currency headwinds, while Novo Nordisk rose 3.5% after US regulators moved against copycat GLP-1 drugs.

    Global

    The dominant global development remains the US-Iran conflict, now entering its fifth day with no material sign of military de-escalation. Brent crude extended its gains to roughly 13% since hostilities began, settling above $82 per barrel after briefly reaching toward $84. European natural gas prices are up more than 50% on the week. The Strait of Hormuz — through which approximately one-fifth of global oil supply and a comparable share of LNG transits — remains effectively closed to commercial shipping, with Iraq already cutting output as onshore storage fills and Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility offline following drone strikes. Aluminum prices rose to their highest since 2022 after Aluminium Bahrain declared force majeure on some deliveries. Airlines have now canceled over 12,300 flights, stranding crews and equipment across the region. President Trump announced the US would provide naval escorts and insurance guarantees for tankers transiting the strait. The shipping industry regards this as a partial solution at best: ING analysts warned US Navy escorts risk becoming targets, Iran's Revolutionary Guards claim "complete control" of the waterway, and ultimately it is individual ship captains — not desk-bound executives — who determine whether to sail. Saudi Aramco is exploring rerouting cargoes through Yanbu on the Red Sea. A New York Times report that Iranian intelligence officials had made indirect contact with Washington to explore an end to the conflict prompted an equity rebound and modestly pared oil gains, though the situation remains unresolved. Bloomberg Economics has modeled a severe scenario in which prolonged closure lifts Brent to approximately $108 per barrel, generating a material inflation shock across all major economies.
