For the Federal Reserve and other central banks, the dynamic is particularly uncomfortable: an oil-price spike pushes inflation higher while simultaneously depressing growth, complicating any easing cycle. Russia's government, meanwhile, is reported to see potential benefit in Gulf states diverting air-defense missile procurement away from Ukraine.

  • US

    US financial markets entered a period of significant stress on Tuesday as the fourth day of the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran concentrated investor attention on the intersection of geopolitical risk and domestic inflation dynamics. S&P 500 futures fell 1.88%, extending Monday's session in which the main indexes had held roughly flat — a relative calm now appearing fragile. The more durable market signal came from the bond market, where the 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.104%, climbing six basis points on the session, and briefly recording its largest single-day decline in price since the tariff-driven turmoil of last April. The catalyst for renewed rate anxiety predates the conflict but is now amplified by it. The ISM manufacturing survey for February showed the prices-paid component surging to its highest level since 2022, a reading that surprised to the upside by the widest margin on record. Respondents were pointed in their frustration, with one manufacturer noting that domestic steel and aluminum tariffs were simultaneously raising input costs and compressing demand — precisely the opposite of their intended effect. Former Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, speaking at an industry conference, said the Iran conflict leaves the Federal Reserve "even more on hold," a view that aligns with market pricing, which has continued to pare rate-cut expectations. JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon captured the prevailing mood among institutional leaders, cautioning that market exuberance does not match what he described as an economy that is merely "fine," and flagging higher inflation as a potential spoiler. The Trump administration indicated it has no immediate plans to tap the Strategic Petroleum Reserve in response to surging crude prices, despite WTI rising more than 8% to nearly $77 a barrel. On the corporate side, MongoDB shares sank 27% following a weaker-than-expected revenue forecast, dragging down enterprise software names broadly. Blackstone's flagship private credit fund faced a record redemption request equivalent to 7.9% of shares, approximately $3.8 billion, the latest indicator of mounting stress in that asset class.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets bore the sharpest losses of any region on Tuesday, with MSCI's Asia-Pacific benchmark declining approximately 3% as the Iran conflict entered its fourth day with no visible path to de-escalation. The standout move was in South Korea, where the Kospi fell 7.2% as markets reopened following a long weekend — a severe repricing that nevertheless leaves the index up 37% year-to-date, a performance driven largely by semiconductor exporters Samsung and SK Hynix benefiting from AI-driven demand. The magnitude of Tuesday's decline may partly reflect catch-up selling, but it underscores the vulnerability of tech-heavy markets to a sharp reversal in risk appetite. China's response to the conflict has been notably calibrated. Beijing issued its most direct call to date for all parties to ensure safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway through which nearly half of China's crude imports transited as recently as December. Chinese solar companies — beneficiaries of renewed energy-security sentiment — were among the few gainers in the region, with some inverter manufacturers rising more than 10% on the week as investors reconsidered the strategic value of domestically produced clean energy. On the diplomatic front, US and Chinese trade negotiators are reportedly scheduled to meet in Paris in mid-March, with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, USTR Jamieson Greer, and Vice Premier He Lifeng expected to discuss the framework for a potential Trump-Xi summit. The meeting's continuation despite the Iran crisis appears to signal that both sides view the bilateral economic agenda as distinct from the geopolitical environment. Separately, Australia's Reserve Bank Governor Michele Bullock signaled that the central bank's patient approach to inflation has limits and that a rate hike remains possible this month — a reminder that the Iran-driven energy shock compounds pre-existing inflation management challenges across the region.

    Europe

    European markets absorbed some of the heaviest losses outside of Asia, with the Stoxx Europe 600 falling 3.16% and the Stoxx 50 declining 2.5% the previous session. Banks and insurers led the selloff, with major names including Société Générale, UniCredit, Santander, and BNP Paribas each declining more than 4%, pushing both sectors into negative territory for the year. The selloff reflected a combination of higher sovereign yields, deteriorating growth expectations, and uncertainty over the conflict's duration. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves delivered her spring fiscal statement against an unusually turbulent backdrop. The statement was designed to be deliberately low-key, consistent with the government's pledge to confine major tax-and-spend decisions to a single annual event and avoid the market disruption that characterized recent fiscal communications. The conflict overshadowed the occasion, and Prime Minister Keir Starmer sharpened his distancing from US military action, stating that the UK does not support "regime change from the skies" and questioning the legal basis for the strikes. President Trump publicly criticized Starmer for refusing a US request to use British military bases for operations against Iran, adding bilateral friction to an already complicated alliance dynamic. On the economic outlook, analysts have framed the conflict's consequences for Europe in binary terms: if hostilities conclude within roughly a month, the energy price shock may amount to a manageable speed bump for a continent that had been showing signs of modest recovery, supported by increased German fiscal spending. A prolonged disruption would constitute a materially more serious challenge, given Europe's dependence on Middle Eastern fuel — approximately 30% of EU diesel and kerosene imports originate from the region, with roughly two-thirds of those volumes transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

    Global

    The defining market dynamic of March 3 was the collision between a genuine geopolitical shock and an already-complicated global inflation and monetary policy outlook. With the Strait of Hormuz effectively halted for tanker traffic, Brent crude rose nearly 5% on Tuesday to top $80 a barrel, and has now reached levels last seen in July 2024. European natural gas prices surged more than 20% for a second consecutive session following Iranian drone attacks on Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility — the world's largest, accounting for approximately one-fifth of global LNG supply. QatarEnergy has provided no timeline for restart, and the uncertainty is acute: if the outage extends for weeks, Europe and Asia will compete for a finite pool of alternative supply, pushing prices materially higher. The 2022 energy crisis offers a reference point, though current price levels remain far below that episode's peak. The monetary policy implications are increasingly difficult for central banks to navigate. A sustained $20-per-barrel oil shock could add up to one percentage point to global inflation, according to Société Générale, at a moment when most major economies have not yet returned inflation to target. Traders are unwinding rate-cut expectations across the UK, Turkey, Hungary, and potentially the US, producing the counterintuitive result that sovereign bonds — normally a haven — are selling off alongside equities. Gold wavered after earlier gains, while the US dollar extended its advance, with the DXY index rising almost 1% on Monday and extending gains Tuesday, breaking above its 200-day moving average. Emerging markets face a differentiated but broadly negative picture. The FTSE EM index fell 2% Monday, its worst session in eleven months, with EM currencies under pressure as the dollar strengthened. Oil-exporting EMs such as Brazil and Colombia stand to benefit from elevated prices, while net importers including Mexico, Chile, and much of Asia face headwinds. The container shipping industry, gathering for its major annual conference in Long Beach, California, effectively abandoned its pre-conflict agenda of managing overcapacity, with leading voices warning that the logistics disruption could define 2026 as completely as prior crises shaped recent years. Global supply chains, only recently recovering from successive shocks, appear to be entering another period of elevated uncertainty.

  • US

    US equity markets opened under significant pressure on Monday, with S&P 500 futures declining approximately 1.0% as the US-Israeli strikes on Iran upended investor sentiment that, until the weekend, had sat at its most optimistic since the pandemic. Defense names proved a clear exception: Lockheed Martin rose 7.1%, RTX gained 6.5%, and Northrop Grumman advanced 5.2% in premarket trading. Airlines fell sharply — United Airlines down 5.7%, Delta off 5.1% — on the combination of higher fuel costs and regional travel disruption. Oil majors advanced, with Exxon Mobil up roughly 5% and Conoco gaining 6%. The Treasury market behaved unexpectedly for a risk-off session, with the 10-year yield rising around 3 basis points to approximately 3.97%, rather than declining as a traditional haven bid would imply. The most plausible explanation is the inflationary signal embedded in an oil spike: rising energy prices reduce the probability of near-term Federal Reserve rate cuts, and money markets scaled back easing wagers accordingly. Société Générale's historical analysis of five oil supply shocks over the past 50 years found the 10-year Treasury consistently weaker at one-week, three-month, and six-month horizons. Gold, rising to $5,385 per ounce, appears to be the haven of preference in the current environment. Politically, the conflict raises a distinct domestic variable. The Trump administration's decision to target Iran's supreme leader represents a departure from decades of US foreign policy convention, and the president's stated objectives have remained notably fluid — ranging from a deal with new Iranian leadership to a campaign lasting four to five weeks. With midterm elections in November, the inflationary consequences of a sustained oil price elevation carry political weight, as consumer affordability concerns remain elevated. The Federal Reserve transition, with a new chair assuming the role in roughly two months, adds a further layer of institutional uncertainty at a sensitive moment. Berkshire Hathaway's Class B shares slipped 1.7% after the conglomerate reported a near-30% decline in operating profit in what was Warren Buffett's final quarter as CEO, with analysts flagging insurance business weakness.

    Asia

    Asian markets registered broad declines on Monday, with the MSCI Asia benchmark retreating 1.6% as the implications of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran reverberated across regional financial centers. The session's most acute stress emerged in Pakistan, where the KSE-30 Index plunged 9.6% in early trade to trigger an hour-long trading halt — a reflection of Pakistan's geographic proximity to the conflict and domestic instability, with at least ten killed in clashes linked to pro-Iran protests and a concurrent declaration of "open war" against Afghanistan following cross-border strikes. Dubai's status as the pre-eminent financial hub for high-net-worth individuals fleeing earlier geopolitical episodes made the Gulf city a focal point of risk. Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Citigroup, and Nomura instructed regional employees to work from home. The Fairmont The Palm Hotel in Dubai sustained damage in an explosion. Emirates halted all operations until Tuesday afternoon with disruption warnings extending through Thursday; Etihad and Qatar Airways followed suit. Private jet charters to Europe were reportedly reaching $350,000 as affluent travelers sought overland routes to Riyadh. China's position warrants particular attention. Beijing called for an immediate ceasefire and is assessed to be among the most economically exposed nations given that it purchases an estimated 99% of Iranian crude exports. A complete Iranian supply outage could lift oil prices by around 20%, while a Strait of Hormuz closure poses broader LNG supply risks. Asian buyers were already contacting non-Middle Eastern suppliers for early LNG deliveries to cover potential March gaps. China's annual National People's Congress is due to convene later this week, where the economic consequences of the conflict are likely to feature prominently in policy discussions. In Japan, the Toyota group reached a landmark settlement with Elliott Investment Management over the privatization of Toyota Industries, with the group offering ¥20,600 per share — a 9.6% increase from the prior bid — in a deal valued at approximately ¥6.7 trillion ($42.8 billion). A Bank of Japan deputy governor declined to signal any imminent rate increase. Separately, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney's visit to India produced a C$2.6 billion uranium supply agreement with India and agreement on terms of reference for a bilateral trade deal, reflecting both nations' effort to reduce US dependency.

    Europe

    European equity markets declined on Monday, with the Stoxx Europe 600 falling 1.45%, though the regional picture was uneven. The UK's FTSE 100 outperformed materially, down less than 1%, benefiting from its structural tilt toward energy, commodities, aerospace and defense, and defensive sectors — a composition Citigroup described as an effective geopolitical hedge, upgrading UK equities to overweight from underweight. UK bases, including Diego Garcia and RAF Fairford, were granted to the US for destroying Iranian missile depots following discussions with Prime Minister Keir Starmer, though Britain did not join the strikes directly. A suspected drone attack on the UK's Cyprus base occurred hours after Starmer's announcement, underlining the costs of British association with the campaign. The UK's fiscal backdrop shifted notably ahead of Chancellor Rachel Reeves' Spring Statement scheduled for Tuesday. Tax receipts came in approximately £22 billion ahead of Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts, driven by stronger-than-expected self-assessed income tax and VAT revenues. The outperformance raises the prospect of reduced gilt issuance and relieves some pressure on UK yields, which remain the highest in the G7. Reeves' fiscal headroom against the rule requiring current spending to be covered by taxation by decade's end will be closely scrutinized, though the statement was broadly anticipated to be a measured affair before this weekend's events added an external complication. European natural gas futures surged as much as 52% on Monday — the largest intraday gain since the 2022 energy crisis — as Qatar shut down the world's largest LNG export facility following an Iranian drone attack. Goldman Sachs estimates a month-long Hormuz shipping halt could cause European gas prices to more than double. Italian fiscal credibility received a modest setback after data showed it failed to meet the EU's 3% deficit ceiling last year. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz is due in Washington on Tuesday, where clarity on tariff policy for EU goods will be a central agenda item, alongside the broader geopolitical situation.

    Global

    The killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in a US-Israeli strike marks the most consequential geopolitical rupture since Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and arguably the most direct US targeting of a foreign head of state in the modern era. Oil markets reacted with Brent crude surging approximately 12.5% before partially retracing, trading above $80 per barrel. The Strait of Hormuz — through which approximately 20% of global oil exports transit — has effectively closed, with tanker traffic all but halted and more than half the world's largest maritime insurance clubs announcing withdrawal of war-risk coverage for Persian Gulf vessels starting Thursday. Saudi Arabia suspended operations at its 550,000 barrel-per-day Ras Tanura refinery following a drone strike, and QatarEnergy shut LNG production at the world's largest export plant. ICE gasoil futures surged more than 20% intraday, the largest gain since March 2022. The range of oil price scenarios remains wide. A brief conflict and rapid regime transition might return Brent toward $60 per barrel; a full Hormuz disruption could push prices to $125 or beyond; a middle scenario of constrained but continuing flows might see an $80 ceiling. The binding near-term constraint appears to be the insurance market rather than an explicit military blockade, as commercial shipping has self-suspended pending war-risk coverage restoration. OPEC+ announced a modest April production increase on Sunday, but additional volumes are of limited utility if tankers cannot safely transit the strait. Trump's objectives have remained notably fluid — variously described as taking four to five weeks or ending within days, seeking regime change on a "Venezuela model" or opening negotiations with new Iranian leadership. Iran's own signals are contradictory: President Pezeshkian vowed retaliation while security chief Larijani publicly rejected US talks even as the Wall Street Journal reported renewed nuclear negotiation overtures through Omani intermediaries. The Assembly of Experts must now choose Khamenei's successor, with the choice between a continuity hardliner and a more pragmatic figure carrying profound implications for the conflict's duration and the region's stability. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, whose influence expanded substantially under Khamenei, represents a significant institutional actor unlikely to relinquish leverage without resistance. Markets, for their part, are trading probabilities rather than resolved outcomes — a posture that may persist for some time.

  • US

    The defining domestic policy dimension of Saturday's joint US-Israel strikes on Iran is the political risk they carry for President Trump ahead of November midterm elections. Trump, who campaigned on an America-first, anti-interventionist platform, has now presided over the ousting of Venezuelan President Maduro, strikes on Iran's nuclear facilities in June, Christmas Day strikes on Nigeria, and now a direct assault on Iran itself. The cumulative weight of these actions represents a substantial departure from the MAGA orthodoxy that propelled him to power, and polling already suggests Republicans face a difficult path to retaining both chambers of Congress. The immediate domestic economic concern is energy prices. Oil has already risen nearly 20% this year on US-Iran tensions, and any further spike will translate directly into higher gasoline prices at a moment when many American households are already feeling financially pressured. Trump acknowledged in his overnight video address that US casualties are possible — an admission that, if casualties materialize, will intensify pressure from a public that has historically grown weary of open-ended military commitments. The fiscal and monetary policy implications remain secondary to the geopolitical shock for now, though sustained energy price elevation would complicate the Federal Reserve's effort to maintain its current rate trajectory. On corporate news, Berkshire Hathaway's PacifiCorp faces approximately $55 billion in wildfire-related claims, with the utility's credit rating already sitting one notch above junk at S&P. Greg Abel, penning his first annual letter as Buffett's successor, inherits a materially complex liability picture as climate-driven wildfire risk intensifies across the American West. PacifiCorp has reached roughly $2.2 billion in settlements to date and is actively lobbying multiple state legislatures for liability caps, following the legislative template established in California and Utah.

    Asia

    The Iran strikes carry immediate implications for Asian economies through two channels: energy import costs and regional security spillovers. Asia's largest economies — China, Japan, South Korea, and India — are all major importers of Middle Eastern crude, much of which transits the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has threatened to close the strait, and Bloomberg Economics estimates that a full closure could push oil to $108 per barrel. For Japan and South Korea, which have limited domestic energy production, such a scenario would represent a significant terms-of-trade deterioration. India, which had resumed meaningful purchases of discounted Iranian crude, faces both supply disruption and price risk simultaneously. China's position is particularly complex. Beijing is Iran's largest oil trading partner and has maintained economic ties that circumvent Western sanctions. The US-Israel strikes place China in a diplomatically awkward position: condemnation risks straining relations with Washington at a moment when trade tensions are already elevated, while silence signals acquiescence to unilateral military action that Beijing has consistently opposed on principle. The People's Bank of China this week was reportedly taking steps to stem yuan appreciation, a reminder that China's record $1.2 trillion trade surplus continues to generate currency pressure even as President Xi publicly advocates for a more prominent global role for the renminbi. On the reserve currency question, Xi's reiterated ambitions for the yuan as a global reserve asset face structural barriers that the Iran shock does nothing to resolve. Foreign ownership of Chinese government bonds has declined to just under $400 billion, compared with $9.3 trillion of foreign-held Treasuries, reflecting the persistent impediments of capital controls, limited market liquidity, and a banking sector that holds rather than trades fixed income. The ECB's new repo facility for foreign central banks and euro-zone governments' rhetorical support for a broader euro role similarly face fragmentation constraints, with German Bund annual trading volumes representing less than two weeks of equivalent US Treasury turnover.

    Europe

    European airspace was disrupted materially on Saturday as Israel and Gulf states closed their airspace following the onset of US-Israel strikes on Iran, with Emirates suspending all Dubai flight operations. The immediate European economic exposure runs primarily through energy: the eurozone is a large net importer of oil and gas, and a sustained rise toward $108 per barrel — the Bloomberg Economics estimate under a Hormuz closure scenario — would exert renewed inflationary pressure on an economy the ECB has only recently guided toward its 2% target. The ECB's strategic positioning is consequently complicated. Having spent the past two years managing the inflation shock partly attributable to energy price volatility following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, policymakers now face the possibility of another energy-driven cost-push episode. Whether that would alter the ECB's rate path depends heavily on the duration and severity of the oil shock — a point Christine Lagarde and her colleagues will be acutely aware of as they assess incoming data over coming weeks. On the reserve currency front, the ECB this month unveiled a repo facility extending euro liquidity to most foreign central banks, representing the institution's most concrete step yet toward promoting the euro's international role. The initiative has drawn support across euro-zone governments and the European Parliament, and Italy's central bank governor Fabio Panetta and even Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel have indicated openness to a European sovereign bond that would create a liquid pan-European benchmark asset. However, the structural barriers remain formidable: fragmented national bond markets mean German Bund trading volumes in all of 2024 amounted to roughly €6.74 trillion, less than two weeks of US Treasury turnover. The EU's economy commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis also cautioned that a stronger international euro role carries exchange rate appreciation implications that European exporters would need to absorb.

    Global

    Saturday's joint US-Israel strikes on Iran represent the most consequential geopolitical development in recent memory for global energy markets and supply chain stability. Iran produces approximately 5% of global oil supplies, and roughly one-fifth of global oil flows transit the Strait of Hormuz. Tehran responded rapidly by launching missiles at US military bases in Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE, closing Iranian airspace, and prompting Gulf states and Israel to do the same. Emirates suspended all Dubai operations. Bloomberg Economics estimates that a Hormuz closure could push oil to $108 per barrel from levels already up nearly 20% year-to-date on US-Iran tensions. OPEC+ is reportedly considering a larger-than-expected supply increase when key members meet Sunday, which may represent a partial offset, though the group's capacity to compensate for Hormuz disruption is limited by logistics rather than production. Risk assets responded defensively. Bitcoin fell as much as 3.8% to $63,038 and Ether declined 4.5% to $1,835, erasing approximately $128 billion in digital asset market value in the immediate aftermath. The broader implication for global monetary policy divergence is that a sustained energy price shock complicates the easing cycles underway or anticipated across multiple central banks, including the ECB and several Asian central banks, by reintroducing an inflationary impulse that policymakers had largely treated as resolved. The dollar's reserve currency status appears further entrenched by the episode, at least in the near term. Despite sustained "sell America" rhetoric, global investors continue to accumulate dollar-denominated assets, and the structural barriers facing both the euro and yuan as alternatives — capital controls, market fragmentation, and inadequate liquidity depth — remain unresolved. As US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has noted, reserve currency status carries the burden of currency appreciation that neither Beijing nor Brussels has demonstrated willingness to fully absorb.

  • US

    US equity markets are heading into the month-end under pressure, with S&P 500 futures off 0.35% as February draws to a close. The benchmark is on course for its largest monthly decline since April, down roughly 0.4% on the month, a striking contrast to European and emerging-market outperformance. The underperformance reflects the twin concerns that have defined February: stretched valuations in AI-adjacent equities and growing recognition that AI's disruptive potential may compress margins and pricing power across the economy. Among individual names, Block surged as much as 27% in extended trading after Jack Dorsey's fintech announced it would cut approximately 4,000 employees — nearly half its workforce — framing the reduction as an AI-driven productivity bet. Dell gained 9.8% on an outlook for AI server sales that exceeded estimates, underscoring that infrastructure demand remains robust even as software sentiment wavers. CoreWeave tumbled 11% after reporting a larger-than-expected loss alongside elevated capital expenditure guidance. Duolingo fell 24% in premarket after a below-consensus full-year outlook triggered downgrades from Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan, and Evercore ISI. The most notable market development in February may ultimately be the Treasury rally. US government bonds returned 1.5% for the month, with long-dated securities gaining 4% — their best monthly performance in roughly a year. The flight-to-quality bid reflects elevated geopolitical uncertainty and AI-related equity volatility, and appears to confirm that the $30 trillion Treasury market retains its role as the primary haven asset despite periodic debate about US fiscal sustainability. On the trade policy front, reporting reveals a $112 billion gap between Chinese export data and US import data for 2025 — suggesting that up to a quarter of goods shipped from China may have circumvented existing tariffs through fraud, shell-company routing, and customs undervaluation. This has material implications: compliant US manufacturers face a structural pricing disadvantage, and the revenue and reshoring rationale underlying the tariff regime is partially undermined.

    Asia

    China's yuan ended its longest winning streak since 2010 after the People's Bank of China moved to temper the currency's appreciation by removing the 20% reserve requirement on foreign-currency forward contracts, effective March 2. The adjustment lowers the cost of bearish derivative positions, signaling that authorities view excessive yuan strength as a risk to exporters and a potential aggravant of already-entrenched deflationary pressures. Currency-option markets nonetheless continue to price in further yuan gains, with traders positioning for a move toward 6.50 per dollar by year-end — suggesting the PBoC's signal has not fully dislodged bullish conviction. Domestic consumption data from the Lunar New Year holiday offered a mixed signal. China recorded 596 million tourist trips over the nine-day break, 95 million more than a year earlier, and total domestic tourism spending reached a record. However, per-trip expenditure of 1,348 yuan was marginally below the prior year. The divergence between volume and per-capita spending is consistent with a consumer that is mobile but cautious — and with the deflationary environment reflected in unusually cheap airfares and hotel rates even in popular destinations like Harbin. It would be premature to interpret the holiday data as a consumption inflection point. Japan's political economy is drawing increasing scrutiny. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi this week nominated two reflationist academics to forthcoming Bank of Japan board openings, reinforcing concerns that she intends to constrain the pace of rate normalization. The yen and JGBs fell on the announcement. Notably, former BOJ Governor Haruhiko Kuroda — himself the architect of Japan's decades-long easing experiment — has called publicly for rate hikes and warned that the yen may be "somewhat too weak." The tension between Takaichi's fiscal and monetary preferences and the BOJ's tightening trajectory represents a developing risk for Japanese markets. Separately, South Korea's National Pension Service posted an 18.82% return in 2025, its strongest ever, driven by semiconductor and AI-related equity gains, though foreign funds sold a record net $4.7 billion of Kospi stocks on Friday after the benchmark's near-50% year-to-date run.

    Europe

    European equities continue to outperform their US counterparts in what has become the dominant cross-regional market theme of early 2026. The Stoxx Europe 600 rose 0.18% Friday and is set to close February with an 8th consecutive monthly gain — the longest winning streak since 2013 — ending the month up 3.7%. The region's relative insulation from AI disruption risk, alongside a cyclical recovery that some argue is underappreciated, appears to be drawing capital from US equity allocations. European companies in the Stoxx 600 have announced €85.7 billion in share buybacks year-to-date through February, the highest ever for the January-February period, according to Barclays data. The collapse of London-based Market Financial Solutions has created an acute problem for several Wall Street institutions. Barclays has approximately £600 million in exposure, Atlas roughly £400 million, and Jefferies around £100 million. The situation adds to a broader narrative of stress in parts of the private credit and non-bank lending ecosystem, consistent with the Apollo Global Management fund that separately cut its dividend and marked down asset values this week. On the UK political front, the Green Party won the Gorton and Denton by-election in Manchester with 40.7% of the vote; Reform UK placed second with 28.7%, and Labour trailed third with 25.4%. The result illustrates the two-front pressure on Keir Starmer's government from both the progressive left and the nationalist right. Chancellor Rachel Reeves is expected to deliver a deliberately low-key economic update next week, with gilt issuance projected to be reduced — an outcome markets are likely to receive constructively given recent concerns about UK fiscal sustainability. Inflation data from the eurozone's second and fourth largest economies surprised to the upside. French inflation quickened more than anticipated in February, and Spanish inflation accelerated unexpectedly, complicating the ECB's path toward further easing and reinforcing the case for caution at forthcoming policy meetings.

    Global

    The most consequential cross-regional dynamic remains the state of US-Iran relations and its implications for oil markets. US and Iranian officials concluded a further round of nuclear talks in Geneva on Thursday, agreeing to reconvene as soon as next week. The diplomatic channel remains open, but the context is extraordinary: Iran's nuclear facilities were struck by the US last June, a second US carrier strike group is reportedly already in Israeli waters, and Tehran is simultaneously preparing for a potential war while attempting to negotiate. Oil markets appear to price a deal as the less likely outcome, with Brent futures around $70 per barrel — up roughly 19% year-to-date — reflecting a meaningful geopolitical risk premium. OPEC+ is set to meet Sunday, and delegates indicate the group will likely agree to resume the output increases it suspended in Q1. The group's ability to consider this option reflects a combination of factors: supply disruptions in Kazakhstan, North America, and Russia have trimmed the theoretical glut, and Chinese strategic reserve purchases have absorbed additional barrels. Whether the supply overhang is as large as the IEA and JPMorgan project remains genuinely uncertain, with several US shale executives arguing the surplus will arrive later — or not at all. Pakistan declared "open war" with Afghanistan after cross-border strikes killed over 130 people, adding a further geopolitical fault line in a region already under strain. China meanwhile removed nine military lawmakers from its National People's Congress, shrinking the military delegation to its smallest size since at least 1974, in an apparent continuation of Xi Jinping's defense establishment purge. On energy transition, US renewable power generation hit a record 1,162 terawatt-hours in 2025 — a 10% year-on-year increase representing 26% of total US electricity output — despite the Trump administration's sustained policy headwinds for the sector. The economics of wind and solar continue to drive deployment independent of subsidy status, with Wood Mackenzie projecting renewables will account for nearly one in three US electrons by 2030.

  • US

    Nvidia's earnings release dominated pre-market attention, with the chipmaker delivering a revenue forecast of approximately $78 billion that beat consensus expectations yet failed to ignite a sustained rally. Shares slid as much as 1.5% in after-hours trading before recovering marginally, a reaction that encapsulates the increasingly demanding bar set by AI-adjacent equities. The muted response extends a pattern visible across the software sector: Salesforce fell roughly 4% after its full-year outlook was judged underwhelming, while C3.ai tumbled 24% following a revenue guidance cut, and Trade Desk dropped 17% in pre-market on a weak forecast. The common thread is investor anxiety that AI infrastructure spending may crowd out legacy software revenues rather than lifting all boats — a tension that remains unresolved. The broader S&P 500 has been range-bound for four months. Options market structure is now reflecting elevated caution, with the put-call skew on the index rising to a two-year high last week and normalized two-month skew approaching the upper end of its five-year range. Some strategists interpret extreme positioning as a contrarian setup, though the fundamental picture — tariff uncertainty, the aftermath of the Supreme Court ruling on global levies, and a Fed leadership transition — provides ample reason for the hedging. On monetary policy, discussions at the National Association for Business Economics conference centered almost entirely on AI's labor market implications. Fed Governor Lisa Cook offered a sober assessment, suggesting the economy may be approaching the most significant reorganization of work in generations, and that the Fed may lack the usual tools to offset AI-driven unemployment since elevated joblessness would not necessarily signal conventional economic slack. Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin echoed similar caution, noting that benchmark rate policy cannot resolve the structural adjustments AI will impose. Separately, Deutsche Bank analysts sketched potential reforms under incoming Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh, including revisions to the dot plot framework and a possible shift toward rolling 12- and 24-month rate horizons rather than year-end projections.

    Asia

    China's trade posture hardened ahead of a planned Trump-Xi summit, with Beijing warning against further tariff increases even as Washington reaffirmed its commitment to maintaining elevated levies on Chinese goods. Trump's State of the Union address notably omitted any direct mention of China — the first such omission by a US president in two decades — leaving the bilateral relationship in an ambiguous posture: confrontational on trade, yet diplomatically active at the leadership level. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz's concurrent visit to Beijing with a delegation of German business leaders underscores how other major economies continue to engage China commercially even as Western political rhetoric remains cautious. Japan's corporate governance reform narrative advanced with Reuters reporting that Toyota is preparing to unwind approximately ¥3 trillion ($19 billion) in cross-shareholdings with banks and financial institutions, potentially as early as this year. The move would represent a meaningful step in Japan's multi-year effort to improve capital efficiency and shareholder returns. Separately, the Bank of Japan's most hawkish board member renewed his call for higher interest rates, a signal consistent with the yen outperforming G10 peers following reports that sustain rate hike expectations. South Korea's central bank held its benchmark rate at 2.5%, upgrading both growth and inflation forecasts while publishing new dot-plot projections. India presented a more nuanced picture. Prominent investor Raamdeo Agrawal cautioned that the post-pandemic equity rally has largely bypassed the broader population, with financial asset ownership concentrated in just 5% to 7% of households, raising questions about the sustainability of consumer demand. Indian regulators simultaneously tightened bank lending to brokers and raised margin requirements on derivatives in an explicit effort to reduce systemic risk, pressuring exchange and brokerage stocks. On the corporate side, Apple is in discussions with major Indian lenders and card networks for a potential mid-2026 Apple Pay launch integrating with the Unified Payments Interface — a strategically significant move given UPI's dominance. In Hong Kong, Jimmy Lai's 2022 fraud conviction was overturned by the Court of Appeal, a narrow legal reprieve that leaves his 20-year national security sentence intact.

    Europe

    Two UK parliamentary by-elections in Gorton and Denton drew significant attention as a test of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's Labour government. What might otherwise have been a safe Labour hold appears to have become a three-way contest among Labour, Reform UK, and the Greens — a result that, if confirmed, would expose the breadth of political dissatisfaction with the governing party from both its right and left flanks. With November's general election still distant, the immediate consequence is reputational rather than structural, but the signal for Labour's governing coalition is uncomfortable. European credit markets presented a more constructive picture. US borrowers have been issuing record volumes of "reverse Yankee" bonds denominated in euros, attracted by yields approximately 170 basis points below comparable US-denominated issuance. The spread differential reflects both the ECB's more accommodative stance relative to the Fed and residual demand for euro-denominated paper, and represents a tangible capital flow toward European debt markets. The ECB itself reported another operating loss, though President Christine Lagarde noted the institution is monitoring for productivity gains from AI while remaining attentive to any associated job displacement. Italy's industry minister escalated pressure on the EU's Emissions Trading System, calling it "ineffective and harmful" and urging the European Commission to suspend the program pending a thorough overhaul. The intervention follows an 8% decline in European carbon prices after Chancellor Merz indicated openness to revising or delaying the program, and aligns with French President Macron's acknowledgment that the ETS "is not functioning well for some countries." The Commission is due next week to unveil a "Made in Europe" industrial act, with the latest draft setting strict criteria favoring European firms in public procurement while requiring some foreign competitors to share technology and hire locally — a meaningful shift from the free-trade framework that has governed EU industrial policy for decades.

    Global

    The dominant global risk theme remains the US-Iran nuclear standoff. A third round of Geneva talks began on February 26, with President Trump's March 1-6 diplomatic window — and his threat of military action — framing the negotiations. Trump's assertion that the US would "easily" prevail in any armed confrontation sits in tension with satellite imagery showing Iranian engineers actively reinforcing and concealing nuclear and missile sites damaged during last June's conflict, hardening tunnels and burying facilities against future strikes. The IAEA reports Iran has accumulated enough highly enriched uranium for roughly a dozen nuclear devices, though its precise whereabouts have not been independently verified in over eight months. Critically, US air power can destroy physical infrastructure but cannot eliminate scientific knowledge, stockpiled material, or political will — a constraint that military planners and energy markets are pricing accordingly. Oil supertanker rates have surged to levels not seen since early 2020, with very large crude carrier day-rates on the Middle East-to-China benchmark route exceeding $200,000 — a rise of more than 600% year-to-date. Two factors are driving the spike: elevated volumes of oil at sea, partly reflecting sanctioned Russian crude struggling to find buyers alongside increased Saudi exports, and a highly concentrated market position being built by South Korean shipowner Sinokor Group, which is acquiring enough tonnage to potentially control roughly 40% of the global VLCC fleet. The resulting cost pressure is already affecting crude price differentials, with West African barrels being offered at deeper discounts to Asian buyers. Any Strait of Hormuz disruption — through which approximately 20% of global oil flows — would layer a supply shock onto an already tight tanker market. Global debt reached $348 trillion at end-2025 per the Institute of International Finance, a fresh record driven by a $29 trillion annual increase — the fastest accumulation since the pandemic. The figure underscores the fiscal vulnerability of sovereign borrowers in an environment of persistently elevated interest rates. Russia's war in Ukraine entered its fifth year with the battlefield broadly stalemated, Putin facing growing economic strain, and US diplomacy increasingly focused on arranging a leaders-level summit between Trump and Zelenskyy, with Geneva talks between US and Ukrainian officials on February 26 representing the next procedural step.

  • US

    Donald Trump's State of the Union address, delivered Tuesday evening, offered a defiant defense of his economic record while introducing limited new policy substance. The president declared the US is "back bigger, better, richer and stronger than ever before" at a moment when public skepticism of his economic stewardship is rising and consumer sentiment has softened. Notably, Trump made no direct mention of China — the first time in two decades a sitting president has omitted the country from the annual address, a striking rhetorical choice given the centrality of trade policy to his agenda. He was also measurably milder in his criticism of the Supreme Court justices who recently struck down many of his signature tariffs, with several present in the chamber. On the policy margin, Trump proposed new tax-advantaged retirement savings accounts for workers without employer-sponsored plans, with a government match of up to $1,000 annually — a modest fiscal gesture unlikely to move macro aggregates materially. In equity markets, US futures were marginally higher as investors awaited Nvidia's quarterly results after the close, the most consequential single earnings event in the current AI-valuation debate. S&P 500 fourth-quarter earnings growth came in at approximately 13%, roughly six percentage points above expectations, and yet the index has declined 1.7% over the six-week earnings window — tied for its worst earnings-season performance in ten quarters — suggesting valuation and policy uncertainty are dominating fundamental signals. Workday fell 9.2% in premarket trading on a subscription revenue miss, HP dropped 5% on a disappointing profit outlook citing tariffs and rising memory-chip costs, while Cava gained 9% on above-consensus 2026 guidance. First Solar slid 14% on a sales shortfall. The Warner Bros. Discovery-Paramount media consolidation dynamics evolved further, with Warner Bros. signaling a new $31-per-share Paramount Skydance offer could produce a superior outcome to its existing Netflix arrangement, without withdrawing that recommendation. Stripe is separately reported to be exploring an acquisition of some or all of PayPal, a combination that would reshape the payments landscape significantly if pursued. Bitcoin's price structure remained technically fragile, with Glassnode data showing 19 of the first 22 days in February recorded net realized losses. Approximately 45% of circulating supply sits underwater relative to acquisition cost, creating a persistent ceiling on rallies as distressed holders sell into any recovery.

    Asia

    Asian equity markets posted strong gains, with the Nikkei 225 rising 2.20% to 58,583 and the South Korean Kospi advancing 1.91% to 6,084. The session reflected a broader rotation dynamic that has defined 2026 year-to-date: investors are concentrating capital in Asian hardware manufacturers perceived as infrastructure beneficiaries of AI, while retreating from US-listed software and services names exposed to AI disruption risk. Taiwan Semiconductor is up 30% year-to-date, Samsung Electronics has surged 70%, and SK Hynix has gained 57%. Overseas investors purchased a net $2.77 billion of Taiwan equities on Tuesday, the largest single-day foreign inflow since December 2005, extending a six-day buying streak. South Korea's total equity market capitalization has risen to $3.76 trillion, surpassing France to rank ninth globally — having already overtaken Germany in late January. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz arrived in Beijing on his first official visit since taking office last May, framing the trip around balanced engagement with a country that serves simultaneously as strategic rival and indispensable trading partner. "It would be a mistake for us to seek to decouple ourselves from China," Merz said before departure. The visit carries a note of economic asymmetry: Germany's trade dependence on China has deepened, German investment in China reached a four-year high in 2025, and Berlin is increasingly reliant on Chinese critical materials for industrial production. Merz will hold separate meetings with President Xi Jinping and Premier Li Qiang. Both sides signaled an appetite for improved relations, though trade frictions and mineral supply chains remain unresolved structural tensions. Hong Kong's Financial Secretary Paul Chan delivered the city's annual budget, reporting a consolidated surplus of HK$2.9 billion — a material turnaround from the HK$67 billion deficit originally forecast — enabling pledges of technology spending and tax relief for over two million residents. Singapore banks drew $61 billion in new wealth inflows from Asian high-net-worth clients, underscoring the city-state's continued role as a regional wealth management hub. China separately warned it will take "all necessary measures" if the US proceeds with fresh tariffs tied to an ongoing probe into a 2020 trade deal. Meanwhile, a record $112 billion discrepancy between Chinese reported export data and US Customs arrival figures for 2025 suggests that as much as a quarter of China-origin shipments may have bypassed US tariff controls — complicating both revenue collection and any analytical assessment of the bilateral trade relationship. South Korea's fertility rate rose for a second consecutive year, a rare positive signal for a country with the world's lowest birth rate.

    Europe

    HSBC reported full-year pretax profit of $29.9 billion, surpassing analyst expectations and sending shares up 5.4% in London trading. The result was underpinned by strong performance in the wealth division, consistent with CEO Georges Elhedery's strategic pivot toward asset and wealth management as part of a broader institutional overhaul. The bank's bonus pool rose 10% to $3.93 billion, the highest in at least a decade, reflecting both the earnings outperformance and Elhedery's deliberate move toward a more performance-differentiated, Wall Street-style compensation structure. HSBC derives the bulk of its earnings from Asia, and its shares reached a record following the results. Diageo fell 5.8% after the spirits group cut its full-year organic net sales guidance for the second time this fiscal year, now projecting a decline of 2-3%. The company continues to face demand weakness in both the US and China, illustrating how exposure to discretionary consumer spending in two of the world's largest economies creates compounding risk in the current environment. Aston Martin separately announced plans to reduce its workforce by approximately 20%, citing Trump's tariffs as a complicating factor in an already-difficult operational turnaround. Germany's fourth-quarter GDP data showed a long-anticipated recovery taking hold, driven by domestic spending, though trade remained a drag — a pattern consistent with the broader structural challenge Merz is navigating. Factory orders have shown a distinct improvement in recent months, jumping in December by the most in two years, and business confidence as measured by the Ifo institute rose more than expected this month. Merz's government loosened Germany's constitutional debt brake earlier in his term to fund a substantial defense and infrastructure investment program, and early indicators suggest some economic benefit is beginning to materialize, though the trajectory remains uncertain. Spain's housing situation deteriorated further into structural dysfunction, with government eviction protections due for another one-year extension. Critics argue the measures have created perverse incentives for tenants to halt rent payments, stranding tens of thousands of property owners in illegally occupied homes with limited legal recourse. A secondary market of specialist firms promising rapid removal of occupants has emerged, charging several thousand euros per engagement. French President Macron accepted the resignation of Louvre president Laurence des Cars following October's high-profile museum heist.

    Global

    President Trump's Iran warnings dominated geopolitical attention on Wednesday. In his State of the Union address, Trump accused Iranian officials of pursuing "sinister ambitions" to reconstitute their nuclear program following US airstrikes last year, while simultaneously reiterating interest in a diplomatic resolution. The dual messaging — military threat and negotiating posture — reflects a deliberate ambiguity, though it adds to mounting evidence of a US military buildup in the Middle East. NATO's AWACS surveillance aircraft stationed in Turkey have reportedly shifted their operational focus from Russia to Iran. The prospect of an armed escalation appears to be influencing energy markets: supertanker earnings have risen to their highest level in years, with traders pricing a non-trivial risk that conflict could disrupt oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump's special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are scheduled to hold fresh talks with Iranian officials in Geneva on Thursday, keeping a diplomatic channel open even as military preparations appear to be advancing. On the energy transition, Bloomberg Opinion's Javier Blas argued that "net zero" has effectively been abandoned as a political commitment, noting that mentions have collapsed in rich-nation energy communiqués while global demand for oil, gas, and coal stands at all-time highs. His projection — that emissions may stabilize in the 25,000-30,000 million metric ton range by 2050, well above zero — represents an implicit acknowledgment that the 2050 target is no longer operationally credible under current policy trajectories. Renewable electricity capacity is nonetheless expected to continue expanding; the divergence between clean power growth and overall emissions reduction illustrates the core tension in the transition narrative. China's Belt and Road Initiative appears to be entering a new expansionary phase after a period of retrenchment. Data cited by advisory firm Teneo shows total BRI-related engagements reached an all-time high of $214 billion in 2025, with both average deal size and geographic reach at record levels, concentrated in central Asia, energy, metals, and technology. The "small-but-beautiful" era declared in 2021 appears to have been a Covid-era artifact rather than a structural recalibration. UBS raised its worst-case private credit default scenario to 15%, up from 13% issued less than a month ago, citing "rapid, severe AI disruption" as a clearer catalyst than previously modeled. The revision underscores the degree to which AI disruption risk has migrated from equity market narratives into credit analysis, with implications for the $1.8 trillion private credit complex.

  • US

    Equity markets steadied early Tuesday, with S&P 500 futures rising 0.2% after a broad selloff the prior session driven by renewed anxiety over AI's disruptive potential. The catalyst was a weekend report from Citrini Research outlining AI-related risks across multiple sectors, which, combined with a warning from Nassim Taleb about escalating volatility and potential software-sector bankruptcies, sent IBM to its steepest single-day decline in 25 years — triggered specifically by Anthropic's announcement that its Claude Code tool can modernize COBOL, the dated programming language central to IBM's mainframe business. Indian IT exporters Infosys, TCS, and Wipro fell 3–4% in Mumbai as the selloff propagated globally. Elsewhere in corporate news, Paramount Skydance raised its offer for Warner Bros. Discovery, and PayPal was reported to be attracting acquisition interest after losing roughly half its market value. Against this backdrop, Jamie Dimon offered a sobering assessment at an investor event, drawing parallels to the pre-2008 period when competitive pressure to generate net interest income led banks toward poor underwriting decisions. "I see a couple people doing some dumb things," he said, a remark notable for its directness even if the systemic parallels remain contested. Bitcoin extended its February decline to approximately 19%, consistent with its behavior as a risk asset rather than the safe-haven narrative some proponents advance. On fiscal and trade policy, President Trump's 10% global tariff entered into force at 12:01 a.m. Washington time Tuesday — a replacement measure following the Supreme Court's invalidation of his original IEEPA-based duties. The administration is preparing a formal directive to raise the rate to 15%, though timing remains unfinalized. Separately, the administration signaled it intends to slow-walk refunds on the overturned tariffs, with Treasury Secretary Bessent framing repayments as "corporate welfare." Legal challenges are accumulating, with more than 1,500 refund lawsuits already filed. Bloomberg Economics estimates roughly $170 billion in contested duties. The macro backdrop ahead of Trump's State of the Union address was notably mixed: 2025 GDP growth came in at 2.2% — the slowest since the post-COVID expansion began — while core inflation ended the year at 3%, a full percentage point above the Fed's target. January payrolls of 130,000 represented a significant rebound from the meager 15,000 monthly average through 2025, and unemployment edged down to 4.3%. Housing, however, remained sluggish, with new home starts posting a fourth consecutive annual decline.

    Asia

    Two developments dominated Asian market attention Tuesday: renewed AI-driven risk aversion and a sharp move in the Japanese yen. The yen fell approximately 1% to around 156 per dollar following a Mainichi newspaper report that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi expressed apprehension over further Bank of Japan rate increases during a recent meeting with Governor Kazuo Ueda — taking what the newspaper described as a "tougher stance" than at their November meeting. The report appeared to revive concerns that political resistance could constrain the BOJ's policy normalization path. Markets had broadly anticipated that Takaichi, whose election victory earlier this month had been welcomed by investors, would adopt a more accommodative posture toward monetary tightening. The Mainichi report, if accurate, suggests that expectation may have been premature. Japan also raised concerns directly with Washington, with Trade Minister Ryosei Akazawa calling Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to request that the new 10% global tariff not leave Tokyo in a worse position than the terms agreed under last year's bilateral trade deal. China's escalating pressure on Japan deepened further as Beijing blacklisted 20 Japanese entities — including affiliates of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries — on its export control list, effective immediately. This marks the first addition of Japanese firms to the list since its inception in January 2025. An additional 20 entities, including Subaru and Mitsubishi Materials, were placed on a monitoring list subjecting them to heightened scrutiny on dual-use export applications. The moves appear calibrated to signal continued displeasure following Takaichi's landslide election victory. Separately, Shein announced plans to invest over 10 billion yuan ($1.45 billion) in intelligent supply chain infrastructure in Guangdong province — a notable strategic pivot for a company that had previously sought to distance itself from its Chinese origins, relocating its headquarters to Singapore and initially pursuing a New York listing. The announcement, made by founder Xu Yangtian at a Guangzhou conference, signals a tactical reorientation toward Beijing as Shein's Hong Kong IPO remains stalled and external pressures mount. In North Korea, Kim Jong Un's sister Kim Yo Jong was elevated to department director within the Workers' Party Central Committee as the party's first congress in five years convenes in Pyongyang.

    Europe

    European markets absorbed a modest decline, with the Stoxx 600 off 0.12% as the AI-driven selloff that battered US software and payments stocks rippled through European equities. Teleperformance, among the earliest European casualties of AI disruption fears, saw short sellers renewing bets against the stock, which has shed roughly €20 billion in market value since January 2022. Goldman Sachs offered a partial counternarrative, promoting a basket of capital-intensive companies — utilities, miners, some industrials, and luxury goods — under the label HALO (Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence), arguing these sectors are structurally insulated from AI displacement. The basket has outperformed capital-light peers by 35% since early 2025. On trade, an EU assessment confirmed that Trump's new 10% global tariff, layered on top of existing levies, will push the cumulative rate on certain EU exports — including cheese and agricultural products — above the 15% ceiling permitted under the EU-US trade agreement. The European Commission, which presented the assessment to lawmakers, faces pressure to respond without triggering further escalation. Separately, the US warned the EU against advancing "Made in Europe" local-content rules, with Brussels caught between the need to rebuild industrial competitiveness following Chinese pressure and Trumpian tariffs on one side, and the risk of retaliatory friction with Washington on the other. In the UK, the political environment around Prime Minister Keir Starmer grew more complicated as two public figures — Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and former US ambassador Peter Mandelson — were arrested on alleged misconduct charges. With Starmer registering as the most unpopular UK prime minister in modern times by some polling measures and local elections approaching, the episode adds unwanted turbulence. On a brighter note, retail crime — which surged in the post-pandemic period — appears to be easing, with the British Retail Consortium reporting a fifth reduction in daily incidents through August 2025, reflecting over £5 billion in security investment over five years.

    Global

    The most consequential cross-cutting theme Tuesday was the formal activation of Trump's 10% global tariff, a reconstituted trade barrier following the Supreme Court's invalidation of his original IEEPA duties. While the rate is lower than the tariffs it replaced in aggregate, Bloomberg Economics notes it creates a more complex set of winners and losers — with some adversarial nations faring relatively better and US allies generally worse. The White House has signaled intent to raise the rate to 15% via formal executive order, though the timeline is unset, leaving market participants uncertain about the trajectory of the broader trade regime. On the Iran front, US special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were preparing to travel to Geneva for a further round of nuclear talks, even as Trump publicly reserved the right to strike militarily if diplomacy fails. Oil prices steadied near multi-month highs as markets weighed these competing signals — a potential deal implying resumed Iranian supply, military action implying Strait of Hormuz risk. Diamondback Energy separately pushed back against glut concerns, arguing that resilient demand is more durable than consensus forecasts assume. The Ukraine conflict marked its fourth anniversary with no resolution in sight. Peace talks remain deadlocked, the front line is broadly static, and senior European and NATO officials say there is no indication Putin is prepared to accept terms short of his maximalist demands. The US has been working toward a deal ahead of July 4th Independence Day celebrations, but the self-imposed deadline appears increasingly aspirational. Germany's defense chief publicly criticized Trump's negotiating approach. On energy, a decade of US LNG exports has materially reshaped global gas markets, with the US now the world's largest LNG exporter. Approximately 25% of global LNG supply now originates in the United States, a share projected to reach one-third by 2030. The flexibility of US cargoes — sold free-on-board and therefore tradeable across global destinations — has progressively eroded Russia's historical ability to leverage gas supply as a geopolitical instrument against Europe. Saudi Arabia's fiscal deficit widened to a five-year high as lower oil prices pressured revenues, forcing a deceleration of some Vision 2030 megaprojects.

  • US

    The dominant US macro development of the day is the aftermath of the Supreme Court's ruling invalidating President Trump's IEEPA-based tariffs, and the administration's rapid pivot to replacement levies. Trump signed an executive proclamation imposing a 10% global tariff under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, subsequently raising it to 15% over the weekend. The legal foundation appears fragile: Section 122 permits duties only in response to "fundamental international payments problems," a threshold most economists do not believe the US currently meets. The IMF's former chief economist Gita Gopinath stated plainly that the US does not face such a problem, and former Treasury official Jay Shambaugh noted that a genuine balance-of-payments crisis would be reflected in dollar weakness and capital flight — neither of which is occurring. Legal challenges appear likely, though courts may move too slowly to constrain tariffs that expire in 150 days. S&P 500 futures fell approximately 0.3% on the day, with market participants pricing in continued policy uncertainty rather than relief from the court ruling. Treasury Secretary Bessent characterized the IEEPA tool as "instantaneous" leverage now lost, while flagging what he termed a "draconian alternative" — the option of complete trade embargoes. Analyst commentary emphasizes that Trump retains an array of coercive instruments beyond formal tariff authority, including sector-specific duties, regulatory threats, and financial access. A major winter storm named Hernando has paralyzed the US Northeast, with 41 million people facing blizzard conditions. More than 10,000 flights were canceled through Tuesday, with Delta suspending operations at LaGuardia, JFK, and Boston Logan. US natural gas futures jumped nearly 7% as heating demand surged. The storm is among the most severe on record for the New York area and will likely weigh on near-term regional economic activity. Kevin Warsh, Trump's expected nominee for Federal Reserve chair, continues to attract attention; Bloomberg Economics analysis of his public statements confirms a marked shift from hawkish positioning in 2009–2011 toward dovish messaging since 2025.

    Asia

    The Supreme Court's invalidation of Trump's IEEPA tariffs has materially shifted the regional trade calculus. China emerges as the clearest beneficiary: Morgan Stanley estimates average US levies on Chinese goods fall to 24% from 32%, while the Asia-wide weighted average tariff rate drops to approximately 17% from 20%. Chinese equities in Hong Kong rallied 2.65% on the session, with the Hang Seng China Enterprises index among the stronger performers globally. Mainland China and Japan markets were closed for holidays, limiting broader regional price discovery. The geopolitical significance extends beyond the tariff arithmetic. President Xi Jinping is scheduled to host Trump in Beijing beginning March 31, and the court ruling has meaningfully altered the negotiating dynamic. Xi enters those discussions with Chinese export volumes running at full capacity, average levies reduced, and a US counterpart stripped of his most flexible coercive instrument. Whether Beijing opts to capitalize on this position or maintains its measured posture to avoid fresh friction ahead of the summit remains to be seen. India presents a more complicated picture. New Delhi had been advancing toward an interim trade deal with Washington, but Indian officials postponed the relevant trip to the US following the court ruling, citing uncertainty over the new tariff framework's legal durability. South Korea, by contrast, is demonstrating resilience: the Bank of Korea told parliament that the semiconductor cycle is exhibiting unusual strength, and exports jumped 47% in the first 20 days of February on a working-day-adjusted basis. The central bank expects "significantly higher" GDP growth in 2026 relative to 2025. South Korean President Lee Jae Myung's market reform agenda continues to attract attention, with the Kospi having gained 115% since he took office in June, supported by corporate governance improvements that have broadened shareholder returns.

    Europe

    European markets opened softer, with the Stoxx Europe 600 declining 0.24%, as the continent grappled with the implications of a reshuffled US tariff regime. The European Parliament's trade chief Bernd Lange moved to freeze ratification of the EU-US trade agreement pending clarification from Washington on its revised policy framework — a significant step that signals the fragility of arrangements negotiated under the now-invalidated IEEPA tariffs. ECB President Christine Lagarde warned that Trump's latest moves risk disrupting the EU-US trade "equilibrium" and could pose a fresh headwind to the eurozone economy. German business confidence brightened more than anticipated, providing some offset to the broader uncertainty. Chancellor Friedrich Merz is visiting China, where he signaled a notably sharper line than his predecessors, warning German business leaders against deepening investments there and indicating that government backing would not be forthcoming if those investments soured. The shift reflects a broader reassessment of Germany's China exposure, though it sits in some tension with the economy's ongoing need for export markets. In France, President Macron appointed David Amiel as budget minister in a cabinet reshuffle that drew criticism from opposition parties. Macron separately wrote to Trump requesting the lifting of sanctions on French and EU officials, including former Commissioner Thierry Breton, while France summoned US Ambassador Charles Kushner over comments related to a domestic violent incident. On corporate news, Johnson Matthey shares fell 17% after Honeywell reduced its offer price for the company's Catalyst Technologies business. JD Sports rose 4.4% after announcing a £200 million shareholder buyback. A significant governance failure at the VZB pension fund — a Berlin-based professional fund for dentists — has drawn scrutiny to Germany's Versorgungswerke system, which manages roughly €300 billion in assets, after an estimated €1.1 billion in losses from concentrated illiquid exposures.

    Global

    The overarching theme across global markets is the uncertainty created by the collapse of Trump's IEEPA tariff framework and the contested legal legitimacy of its Section 122 replacement. The practical consequence is a reconfiguration of relative tariff burdens: countries such as China, India, and Brazil, which faced elevated reciprocal levies, now converge toward the new 15% global rate, while prior beneficiaries — notably the UK and Australia — lose their preferential 10% arrangements. The British Chambers of Commerce estimates UK exporters face up to £3 billion in additional costs, affecting some 40,000 companies. Indonesia signed a bilateral deal with the US just last Thursday, leaving its status uncertain within days of the agreement. Oil markets remain focused on the Iran risk premium. Brent crude has gained approximately 18% year-to-date, trading above $70 per barrel, driven in part by fears of US military action against Iranian nuclear infrastructure. Trump stated last week that Iran had 10–15 days to reach a deal, while also noting he is considering limited strikes. US-Iran nuclear talks are scheduled to resume Thursday in Geneva, with Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi meeting US envoy Steve Witkoff. Analysts and traders caution that a sustained oil price rally requires an actual supply disruption — prior episodes of elevated tension, including last June's Israeli strikes, produced short-lived price moves. US forces in the region are at their largest deployment in over two decades, widening the range of potential outcomes, but the base case among commodity market participants remains diplomatic engagement rather than kinetic escalation. Gold moved toward its highest level of the month as investors sought haven assets amid trade and geopolitical uncertainty. Bitcoin fell more than 2%, trading near $66,000, extending a decline of more than 40% from its peak and raising questions about the asset's investment thesis at a time of otherwise supportive institutional and regulatory conditions. Private equity returned distributions equivalent to just 14% of net asset value in 2025 — the second-lowest since 2008 — with $3.8 trillion in unsold assets weighing on the industry, according to Bain. Blue Owl Capital's decision to gate one of its private credit funds has intensified scrutiny of the $1.8 trillion sector, with some observers drawing comparisons to pre-2008 credit market dynamics.

  • US

    Federal climate and energy policy developments dominated the domestic agenda on February 21. The Environmental Protection Agency moved to roll back the Biden administration's 2024 strengthening of the Mercury and Air Toxics Standards for power plants, reversing rules requiring reduction of mercury, arsenic, and lead emissions from coal-fired facilities. EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin framed the action as relieving undue burden on the coal sector, consistent with the administration's broader posture of loosening environmental constraints in pursuit of baseload power expansion. The regulatory reversal is the latest in a sequence of actions benefiting coal producers, though the sector's structural economics — including cheap natural gas and declining capital investment — remain a headwind regardless of the regulatory environment. On the energy infrastructure front, a proposed 9.2-gigawatt gas-fired power plant in Ohio, envisaged as a SoftBank-led development with a reported cost of approximately $33 billion, continued to attract scrutiny for its potential emissions footprint. Analysts at BloombergNEF estimated the facility could emit roughly 19.4 million metric tons of CO₂ annually assuming a 65% capacity factor with combined-cycle gas turbine technology, which would make it among the largest single point sources of carbon emissions in the US electricity sector. The project, backed by the Trump administration's Commerce Department, highlights the tension between the administration's energy security objectives and its stated ambition to grow the economy while protecting public health — goals that, in this instance, pull in opposite directions. The US-Indonesia trade deal, described as lowering tariffs between the two economies, was reported as complete, adding a modest but symbolically notable win for the administration's bilateral trade agenda in Southeast Asia. Separately, reports indicated the administration is planning a revamp of the Peace Corps with an AI-competitiveness dimension, framing the initiative as a tool to extend US soft power and counter Chinese influence in developing markets.

    Asia

    India occupied the foreground of Asia-Pacific developments this week, hosting two major international gatherings that together illustrated the country's growing ambitions — and the tensions embedded within them. The fourth annual global AI summit concluded in New Delhi, the first such event held in the Global South. With attendance from OpenAI's Sam Altman, DeepMind's Demis Hassabis, and French President Macron, among others, the summit underscored India's convening power and its attractiveness as a market, given that more than 100 million Indians use ChatGPT weekly, second only to the United States. Prior to the summit, India had secured approximately $5 billion in venture capital commitments and $140 billion in AI infrastructure pledges; by its conclusion, those figures had risen to at least $17 billion and $270 billion respectively — a material step toward the government's AI sovereignty agenda. India's AI sovereignty strategy, articulated by Electronics Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw, rests on several pillars: development of small language models calibrated to the Indian market, construction of shared compute facilities for startups and students, domestic semiconductor design and eventual manufacturing, and active attraction of foreign capital for data center infrastructure. The underlying concern driving these ambitions is strategic dependency — the risk that foreign control over cloud and AI infrastructure could be interrupted, whether by corporate decision or government directive. Separately, India held its first major national climate conference in Mumbai, where former US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton urged the country to assume greater global leadership on emissions reduction in the absence of US federal engagement under the Trump administration. The call comes at a moment of genuine ambiguity: India remains the world's third-largest emitter, coal accounts for nearly three-quarters of power generation, and the Modi government missed its 2025 UN deadline to submit updated emissions-cutting plans. Investment in renewables is accelerating, but policy clarity remains elusive, and proposals to sanction new coal plant construction through 2047 sit uneasily alongside the government's 2070 net-zero target.

    Europe

    No significant coverage for this period.

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme this week was AI sovereignty — the question of whether nations outside the US and China can meaningfully insulate themselves from dependency on a small number of platforms and infrastructure providers. India's AI summit crystallized this concern, with delegates from across Asia, Africa, Europe, and Latin America confronting a common structural reality: the most consequential AI systems are controlled by US and Chinese entities, and access to them can, in principle, be withdrawn. The 2024 UniSuper incident — in which a Google cloud misconfiguration cut Australian pension fund members off from their accounts — served as a reference point for how technology dependency can manifest in practice. Rishi Sunak, now a Goldman Sachs adviser, offered a framework for sovereign resilience: secure a niche position in the AI supply chain, diversify providers to avoid vendor lock-in, and build switching capabilities through partnerships. These are practical, if difficult, prescriptions. India's approach — combining domestic small language models, shared compute, semiconductor ambitions, and capital attraction — reflects a more state-directed variant of the same logic. On climate and energy, the week produced a clear illustration of divergence between the US federal trajectory and the rest of the world. While the Trump administration rolled back EPA emissions standards and advanced plans for a large new gas plant, former US officials were in Mumbai urging developing nations to accelerate green transitions without waiting for Washington. Hillary Clinton's framing — that the Global South must lead because US policy change is "a few years off" — reflects a broader recalibration among climate advocates who are repositioning emerging economies as the primary site of ambition.
