Whether India can fulfill that role while simultaneously planning new coal capacity through mid-century remains an unresolved tension that no summit rhetoric has yet bridged.

  • US

    The Supreme Court delivered the most consequential domestic policy ruling of the Trump presidency on Friday, striking down in a 6-3 decision the administration's "Liberation Day" reciprocal tariffs imposed under emergency powers. The ruling invalidates duties on goods from dozens of countries and could cut the US average effective tariff rate by more than half. The court left unresolved whether importers are entitled to refunds, which Bloomberg Economics estimates could total as much as $170 billion — a messy legal fight now remanded to lower courts. The White House indicated tariffs remain available under alternative legal authorities, suggesting the administration will seek to reconstruct its trade architecture on different statutory grounds. Market reaction, while notable, was not a complete surprise given legal challenges had been widely anticipated. The ruling lands against a backdrop of a broader market debate over Federal Reserve policy. A growing minority of money managers are skeptical of consensus expectations for two or more rate cuts in 2026. Invesco's Rob Waldner identifies one cut as his base case, with "no cuts" an increasingly plausible scenario given robust employment data and the surge of AI-driven capital expenditure. Carmignac's Guillaume Rigeade holds a short US Treasury position and projects the 10-year yield drifting toward 4.5% from roughly 4.07% currently. The dollar is on track for its best week in four months as rate-cut expectations are pared. Complicating the outlook further: Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term expires in May, adding institutional uncertainty to an already complex policy calculus. On fiscal dynamics, new CBO projections show net interest payments rising from $1 trillion this year to $2.1 trillion by 2036, reaching 4.6% of GDP — a ratio that alone exceeds Treasury Secretary Bessent's 3% total deficit target for 2029. The CBO does not anticipate a disorderly bond market response, projecting 10-year yields moving from 4.1% to only 4.4% over the decade, though analysts note the sheer scale of outstanding debt may constrain future administrations' fiscal flexibility. S&P 500 futures were little changed heading into the session. ---

    Asia

    Asian equity markets edged lower in early trading, with the Hang Seng declining 1.1% as the broader risk environment was weighed by rising geopolitical tensions and uncertainty around Iran. Against that backdrop, a notable bifurcation emerged within Chinese equities: shares of generative AI startups Zhipu and MiniMax Group soared as Hong Kong markets reopened after the Lunar New Year holiday, with investors rotating into pure AI plays and out of traditional internet giants. Robotics firms also attracted buying interest following their prominent showcase during China's Spring Festival gala, though the broader market remained under pressure. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi used her parliamentary address to set out a broad economic agenda, pledging to "hit the button" on growth through strategic investment, active fiscal policy, and assertive diplomacy. Her comments reflect the difficult balance she must strike: stimulating growth without triggering the bond market volatility that has historically accompanied aggressive Japanese fiscal expansion. Separately, Japan's manufacturing PMI remained in expansionary territory for a third consecutive month, suggesting firmer corporate sentiment. Japan's cultural economy also drew attention, with Bloomberg identifying how intellectual property rights tied to Hello Kitty, Demon Slayer, and other franchises have minted a new class of billionaires — with pop culture now the country's second most valuable export category. India presented several constructive signals. HSBC's flash survey showed economic activity accelerating in February, partly attributed to improved sentiment following US tariff reductions on Indian goods. Prime Minister Modi's hosting of OpenAI's Sam Altman and Anthropic's Dario Amodei at the India AI Impact Summit catalyzed a roughly $4 billion combined rally in data center-related stocks. India also formally joined a US-led critical minerals supply chain initiative. Elsewhere in the region, Malaysian exports rose at their fastest pace in over three years in January, driven by electronics shipments. The US also finalized a trade agreement with Indonesia, with Jakarta committing to facilitate an estimated $33 billion in American goods purchases in exchange for lower US tariffs — a significant development for Southeast Asian trade architecture. ---

    Europe

    European equities outperformed on the day, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 0.5% to 628, near the all-time high of 630 reached Wednesday. Strategists are already signaling caution, however: the median of 17 Bloomberg survey forecasts suggests European stocks will finish 2026 little changed from current levels, as eight consecutive months of gains and valuations at five-year highs leave limited room for further multiple expansion. Luxury names were a notable bright spot, with Moncler rallying 13% on results described by Barclays as significantly ahead of estimates, pulling LVMH and Hermès higher. The euro area flash PMI surprised to the upside, with manufacturers recording their best performance since 2022, driven by an unexpected improvement in Germany. Q4 negotiated wages rose 3%, a data point the ECB will monitor carefully as it balances disinflation progress against residual services-sector price pressures. Separately, the UK recorded its largest budget surplus on record, boosted by surging capital gains tax receipts and lower debt servicing costs — a brief fiscal respite for a government navigating difficult spending decisions ahead. The arrest of Prince Andrew on suspicion of misconduct in public office — on his 66th birthday — marks a significant escalation in the broader Epstein fallout, with the investigation set to scrutinize years of alleged ties to the convicted sex offender. The development adds pressure to the monarchy at a moment when public confidence in institutions is already fragile. Separately, Primark faces structural headwinds as sales flatlined at £9.5 billion and the company remains without a permanent CEO; parent AB Foods is weighing a potential spin-off, though analysts caution this would require performance improvement first. On climate policy, UK Energy Secretary Ed Miliband defended his clean energy agreement with California Governor Newsom despite criticism from President Trump, framing it as consistent with British national interest. ---

    Global

    The dominant cross-cutting theme remains the US-Iran standoff and its implications for energy markets. President Trump stated Iran has 10 to 15 days at most to reach a nuclear agreement, with the US having assembled a substantial military presence across the Middle East. The Wall Street Journal reported the administration is evaluating a limited early strike designed to pressure Tehran toward negotiations. Oil rose to near a six-month high on the back of these developments, with WTI trading around $66 per barrel. The situation remains fluid: the UK has reportedly held off granting the US access to Diego Garcia — a critical Indian Ocean base — for potential operations against Iran, complicating military planning. How Iran responds to the deadline, and whether diplomatic channels remain viable, is the single most consequential near-term variable for global energy markets. The fracture within the International Energy Agency crystallized this week at its Paris ministerial summit. US Energy Secretary Chris Wright assailed the IEA's climate focus, arguing that emissions policies have suppressed European growth and energy access. The US threatened to exit if the agency does not abandon net zero framing — a significant escalation given the IEA was co-founded by Washington in the 1970s. The meeting ended without the customary joint ministerial communiqué, with only a Netherlands-authored summary released. Members did find limited common ground on critical minerals cooperation and energy security, but the institutional fracture appears genuine and its longer-term ramifications remain unresolved. The Supreme Court's tariff ruling carries significant global trade implications. By invalidating the emergency-powers legal basis for reciprocal tariffs, the decision removes — at least temporarily — the single largest source of trade policy uncertainty for US trading partners. The US-Indonesia trade deal, the US-Taiwan import shift, and India's tariff concessions all reflect ongoing reconfiguration of supply chains that will continue regardless of the court's ruling. Separately, the EPA's own cost-benefit analysis of its endangerment finding rollback suggests costs could exceed $1.4 trillion under higher-end scenarios — materially complicating the administration's $1.3 trillion savings claim and likely to figure in ongoing legal challenges.

  • US

    Federal Reserve officials were notably reluctant to ease monetary policy when they convened last month, with several members suggesting rates may need to rise if inflation remains persistently elevated, according to FOMC minutes released Wednesday. The hawkish tone came as a surprise relative to market expectations — traders had priced in a more neutral posture — and prompted a reassessment of the near-term rate path. Separately, National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett drew scrutiny after publicly attacking a New York Fed study concluding that US companies bear the primary burden of existing tariffs, calling it "an embarrassment" and suggesting its authors should be "disciplined." The episode highlights ongoing friction between the administration and independent economic research institutions. On the capital flows front, Treasury Department data showed overseas investors purchased a net $1.55 trillion of long-term US financial assets in 2025, up from $1.18 trillion the prior year. Equity allocations accounted for $658.5 billion of the total, with Treasuries absorbing another $442.7 billion — a result that appears to undercut the narrative of a sustained "Sell America" rotation, though dollar weakness may have amplified the attractiveness of US assets to foreign buyers rather than reflecting unambiguous confidence in US policy. US industrial production rose 0.7% in January, the largest monthly gain in nearly a year, with manufacturing output up 0.6%. The White House cited the figures as evidence that its trade agenda is strengthening domestic industry, though the data predate the full effect of tariff escalations implemented in subsequent weeks. In corporate news, AI-adjacent names were mixed: Figma jumped 17% after revenue guidance exceeded estimates, and DoorDash gained 14% on a strong order forecast, while Carvana fell sharply on margin pressure. Bitcoin continued its retreat, declining more than 40% from its October peak as US institutional flows — which had become the marginal price-setter via spot ETFs and CME futures — remain in withdrawal.

    Asia

    Former South Korean President Yoon Suk Yeol was sentenced to life in prison after a Seoul court found him guilty of leading an insurrection following his declaration of martial law in late 2024. Prosecutors had sought the death penalty, arguing the act posed an existential threat to South Korea's constitutional order. Yoon denied wrongdoing and retains the right to appeal, but the verdict formally closes one chapter of what was the country's most severe political crisis in decades. The ruling carries significant implications for South Korea's institutional trajectory and near-term political calendar. In Japan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi faces an early test of her intentions toward monetary policy, with nominations for two open Bank of Japan board seats expected as soon as February 25. The seats — those of dovish member Asahi Noguchi and consensus voter Junko Nakagawa — give Takaichi, historically associated with reflation, an opportunity to shape the board's composition at a delicate moment. Inflation running above target and the yen's sensitivity to rate expectations mean the appointments will be closely parsed by bond and currency markets. A like-for-like swap may not dramatically alter the balance; a hawkish deviation would carry more signal. Bank Indonesia held its benchmark rate at 4.75%, a widely expected pause as the rupiah hovers near record lows. Governor Warjiyo signaled stepped-up currency stabilization efforts while urging banks to pass through prior rate reductions — a tension that reflects the difficult position facing several Southeast Asian central banks caught between currency defense and growth support. India's AI Impact Summit in New Delhi saw significant capital commitment announcements. Mukesh Ambani pledged up to 10 trillion rupees ($110 billion) over seven years through Reliance Industries and Jio, following rival Gautam Adani's comparable pledge. OpenAI formalized a partnership with Tata Group. The summit's ambitions were briefly overshadowed by Bill Gates's abrupt withdrawal from a keynote appearance amid renewed scrutiny over his past ties to Jeffrey Epstein.

    Europe

    Christine Lagarde is reportedly considering stepping down ahead of the official conclusion of her ECB term in October 2027, a move that would preserve outgoing French President Emmanuel Macron's ability to influence the choice of successor before a potential hard-right electoral outcome in France next year. The report has not been confirmed but has accelerated a succession debate that carries both policy and political dimensions. The hawkish Bundesbank-aligned governor Isabel Schnabel stated she intends to serve out her term, while Spain is actively campaigning for its own candidate. Dutch governor Klaas Knot appears the most plausible consensus choice. The process threatens to expose the fault lines — hawks versus doves, national versus supranational interests — that European institutions routinely struggle to manage. The euro's recent strength provides some context for Lagarde's potential legacy. In real effective terms, the currency has reached its strongest level in over a decade, reflecting both the weak dollar environment and a partial recovery of French sovereign spreads. The OAT-Bund spread has retraced much of its post-2024 snap-election widening, suggesting that financial markets have become more comfortable with France's political situation than the underlying fiscal dynamics strictly warrant. Airbus shares fell 6.7% in Paris after the company disclosed that engine supplier Pratt & Whitney has failed to deliver engines in sufficient quantities, forcing a reduction in aircraft output. The development is a material setback for a company managing record order backlogs, and shifts some accountability to the supply chain rather than demand. Nestlé gained 4.5% in Zurich after new CEO Philipp Navratil signaled the turnaround is on track and guided for 3-4% organic revenue growth in 2026. Novo Nordisk's approximately 5,000 job cuts — among the largest layoffs in Danish history — are beginning to weigh on consumer sentiment in Denmark ahead of a general election due before October.

    Global

    The most consequential single variable in global markets at present may be the trajectory of US-Iran tensions. Brent crude surged 4.3% on Wednesday to re-cross $70 per barrel and extended gains Thursday, reflecting growing concern that a military confrontation is no longer a remote possibility. Prediction market participants now assign roughly 70% probability to a US strike. President Trump stated the US is "ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary"; Iran responded it would "defend itself and respond like never before." US Vice President Vance described the latest round of nuclear talks as having gone "well in some ways" while acknowledging Iranian negotiators had not accepted the red lines set before them. The US also announced visa restrictions on Iranian officials for suppressing domestic protests and restricting internet access. Iran's capacity to administer a meaningful oil supply shock — through the Strait of Hormuz or direct targeting of regional energy infrastructure — is not in question, and the risk of a financial accident remains material even if broader equity markets appear, for now, to be pricing the situation with unusual equanimity. The IMF released its annual Article IV assessment of China, calling once again for a rebalancing toward domestic consumption and away from investment and export-led growth. The Fund noted China's large current-account surplus has produced "adverse spillovers to trading partners." The prescription is identical to guidance the IMF has issued for two decades — Chinese household consumption remains near 40% of GDP, essentially unchanged since 2005 — though officials have recently signaled the forthcoming 15th five-year plan may give consumption a more prominent role, driven in part by the realization that trade barriers make continued export reliance increasingly untenable. OpenAI is close to finalizing the first phase of a funding round exceeding $100 billion, which would value the company above $850 billion — among the largest private financings on record. Amazon, SoftBank, Nvidia, and Microsoft are expected to anchor the round, with sovereign wealth funds and venture capital to follow. The scale of capital deployment reflects a broader dynamic in which AI infrastructure spending has become a geopolitical as well as commercial competition, with India's AI summit serving as the most recent venue for governments and corporations to signal strategic commitment.

  • US

    US markets returned from the Presidents' Day holiday to a broadly quiet session, with the S&P 500 gaining 0.1% and the 10-year Treasury yield adding one basis point to sit near 4.07%. Beneath the calm surface, however, a meaningful structural rotation appears to be accelerating. Bank of America's latest global fund manager survey reveals that, for the first time in a generation, a majority of money managers are concerned about corporate overinvestment rather than underinvestment — a direct consequence of the AI capital expenditure cycle. The clearest market expression of this anxiety is the divergence between hardware and software: chipmakers have surged while software companies face growing fears that AI will commoditize their core products. Nvidia rose 1.2% in premarket trading after Meta agreed to deploy millions of its processors, while Palo Alto Networks fell 7.5% on a weaker-than-expected earnings outlook. Apple, conspicuously absent from the AI spending bonanza, saw its 40-day correlation with the Nasdaq 100 fall to around 0.2 — its lowest since 2006 — effectively functioning as a haven within the tech sector. The macro backdrop carries an internal tension worth flagging explicitly. The economy appears firm, fiscal stimulus from last year's One Big Beautiful Bill is filtering through to consumers — average tax refunds are running roughly $200 above last year's pace — and capex spending is providing an additional impulse. Yet markets are simultaneously pricing two Fed rate cuts within a year as near-certainties, with roughly 50/50 odds on a third. Fund managers surveyed by BofA now believe a "no landing" scenario — in which the economy runs hot, inflation re-accelerates, and the Fed is eventually forced into a sharp response — is more likely than either a soft or hard landing. Bond yields are broadly expected to rise, and investors appear to be positioning accordingly: rotating toward non-US equities, value stocks, and away from the Magnificent Seven. US corporate bond daily trading volume hit a record $61 billion in January, reflecting the intensity of repositioning in credit markets as well.

    Asia

    Japan dominated the Asia newsflow, with Tokyo announcing it will invest up to $36 billion in US oil, gas, and critical minerals projects — the first concrete tranche of the $550 billion framework agreed with the Trump administration. The most prominent component is a large natural gas facility in Ohio. The announcement arrives weeks before Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, formally re-elected today following her strong electoral showing earlier this month, is set to visit Washington. The investment serves multiple strategic functions: it provides visible progress on the trade deal, reinforces energy security ties, and may help Tokyo navigate the tariff environment. The Japan-US trade deficit, notably, has remained largely static in nominal terms for three decades — partly because Japanese manufacturers shifted production to America, a model that Trump officials are now reportedly exploring with Chinese automakers. The Ford-China joint venture concept, in which US firms would hold controlling stakes, represents a meaningfully different structure from either the Japan precedent or existing Chinese investments elsewhere in the world such as BYD's Hungary facility, and faces material political headwinds at the state level even if Washington proves receptive. Indonesia presents a contrasting picture within the region. MSCI has placed the country's emerging-market classification under review, and FTSE Russell has followed suit, citing governance deterioration under President Prabowo Subianto. The rupiah has weakened to levels last seen nearly three decades ago, Moody's cut the credit outlook to negative, and market capitalization fell nearly $80 billion from peak levels following the abrupt dismissal of the heads of the Financial Services Authority and the Indonesian Stock Exchange. The structural concerns are layered: Prabowo's replacement of technocrats with loyalists, the appointment of a family member as Bank Indonesia deputy governor, and a flagship school lunch program costing an estimated $28 billion annually that strains an already narrow fiscal base. Central bank rate cuts — warranted by benign inflation — remain politically constrained by currency weakness, illustrating the compounding nature of the credibility erosion.

    Europe

    The most consequential European development of the day was the Financial Times report that ECB President Christine Lagarde is expected to depart before her mandate expires in October 2027, with the timing calibrated around the French presidential election in April 2027. An ECB spokesperson confirmed that Lagarde "has not taken any decision regarding the end of her term," preserving optionality, but the market implications of an early transition are nonetheless worth considering. An earlier-than-scheduled departure would allow French President Macron and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz to coordinate on her successor — a politically significant dynamic given the ECB's evolving role in an environment of fiscal expansion, German rearmament, and ongoing disinflation. The identity and orientation of the next ECB president will bear directly on the pace of any further easing and on the institution's longer-term credibility framework. European equities set fresh records, with the Stoxx Europe 600 gaining 0.97% to 627.32, continuing a run that reflects a meaningful reallocation away from US assets. Fund manager positioning data from BofA shows the largest-ever overweight on the euro versus the dollar, and the shift appears driven by a combination of dollar weakness, relative valuation, and investor hedging against a potential AI-related correction in US tech. On the corporate side, Bayer agreed to pay more than $7 billion to resolve Roundup cancer litigation, a settlement that may finally close a years-long legal overhang. Carrefour fell 5% after disappointing full-year results, while Raspberry Pi surged 33% on AI agent-driven demand expectations. UK unemployment reached its highest level since the pandemic and youth unemployment rose above the EU average for the first time, adding to pressure on the Bank of England ahead of its next policy decision.

    Global

    The US-Iran nuclear negotiations in Geneva concluded on a cautiously constructive note, with both sides describing the second round of talks as positive. Iran's delegation is expected to return with detailed proposals within two weeks, after which a third round would address outstanding sticking points. The White House, however, was explicit that military strikes remain on the table, with Vice President Vance noting that Iran has not yet acknowledged core US red lines. President Trump is deploying a second aircraft carrier to the region, maintaining coercive leverage even as diplomacy proceeds. The dynamic reflects a deliberate two-track strategy, though the timeline for resolution is compressed: Trump faces midterm elections and has signaled limited tolerance for protracted negotiations. Supreme Leader Khamenei retains the capacity to threaten regional escalation, and oil markets remain sensitive to any deterioration — traders are already pricing a risk premium given the proximity to the Strait of Hormuz. On energy and trade, the divergence in US tariff outcomes sharpened. Port of Los Angeles export volumes fell 8% year-on-year in January, with soybean shipments to China down 80% and South American competitors — Argentina and Brazil — having locked in multi-month supply contracts that effectively displace US exporters for at least another agricultural cycle. Against that, Japan's $36 billion investment commitment represents a tangible win for the administration's bilateral deal framework. The US also renewed threats to withdraw from the International Energy Agency unless the organization reduces its climate advocacy focus. Meanwhile, Ethiopia's EV adoption offers a case study in policy-driven energy transition: a fossil fuel import ban and tariff cuts on electric vehicles have pushed EV penetration from below 1% to over 6% in under two years, primarily through inexpensive Chinese models, driven by fiscal necessity rather than climate ambition — a dynamic likely to repeat across other fuel-importing emerging economies.

  • US

    Markets digest soft equity tape despite supportive macro

    US technology shares were positioned for further declines as trading resumed after the Presidents' Day holiday, with Nasdaq 100 futures down 0.75%. The S&P 500's 14% gain since election day represents the weakest start to a presidential term in decades on a comparable basis, and the rotation underway — consumer discretionary lagging consumer staples — suggests a market reassessing AI infrastructure risks rather than embracing macro tailwinds. That weakness has come despite Q4 corporate earnings growth of 14.4% (the strongest in four years), Treasury yields running below Election Day 2024 levels, and inflation swaps little changed. Some strategists frame the AI-driven selloffs in adjacent sectors — logistics declined nearly 7% Thursday on disruption fears before partially retracing — as offering selective entry points, though the broader signal of markets shrugging off favorable data warrants caution.

    Inflation trajectory remains constructive heading into Friday's PCE

    January CPI data showed continued disinflation across the Fed's preferred underlying measures: the trimmed mean and median CPI sit at their lowest since 2021, supercore services continues to decline, and the Atlanta Fed's sticky-price gauge fell to 3%. The market is pricing roughly two cuts in the back half of 2026, with little expectation of action at the March meeting. Friday's PCE release — delayed since November owing to last year's shutdown — carries unusual weight; PCE had been stuck near 3% and risen above core CPI. A failure to resume its descent would complicate the easing path. One caveat: new car prices posted their largest January jump in years, masked by falling used car prices, suggesting tariff pass-through to consumers may only now be materializing.

    Private equity strains

    With roughly $7 trillion in AUM, holding periods at a 20-year high of 4.4 years, and LPs increasingly vocal about distribution shortfalls, the asset class faces mounting pressure. A pickup in IPOs and strategic deals through 2026 appears plausible but unlikely to clear the backlog quickly.

    Asia

    Asia Daily Briefing — February 17, 2026 Trading across much of Asia remained subdued with several markets closed for the Lunar New Year holiday, leaving regional benchmarks thinly traded. The Nikkei 225 declined 0.42% to 56,566, and Nasdaq futures pointed lower, signaling continued pressure on technology names as the AI-related rotation that has gripped US markets bleeds into Asian sentiment. Indian IT stocks have been a notable casualty: an index of names including Tata Consultancy and Infosys has shed roughly $56 billion in market value since Anthropic released a tool perceived as encroaching on the sector's outsourcing business model. In Australia, BHP delivered earnings at the upper end of expectations, with copper contributing more than half of group profit for the first time, offsetting weakness in iron ore. Shares closed at a record in Sydney. Separately, an Australian court dismissed the long-running greenwashing case brought against Santos by the ACCR, removing — at least at first instance — a significant legal overhang for the energy producer, though written reasons are not due until February 23. The Reserve Bank of Australia's February minutes confirmed that this month's rate hike reflected a downgraded view of productivity and potential output rather than excess demand, making the RBA the first major central bank to tighten in 2026 and a potential outlier — or forerunner — among DM peers. In China, AI startup Moonshot is targeting a $10 billion valuation in a new funding round, more than doubling the $4.3 billion mark set only weeks ago in a round backed by Alibaba and Tencent — an indication that domestic AI capital formation remains robust despite the US tech selloff. On Taiwan, President Trump indicated future US weapons sales are under discussion with Xi Jinping, characterizing his recent call with the Chinese leader as "excellent"; Beijing's readout urged "prudence." Karishma Vaswani argues Belt and Road is not retrenching but evolving into a longer-horizon development partnership model, which may prove harder for Western initiatives to counter. In India, Modi used the AI summit, alongside Macron's fourth state visit, to press global ambitions; Adani announced a $100 billion data-center buildout through 2035. January's trade deficit widened ahead of an interim tariff pact with Washington. In Japan, JGBs extended their rally through a smooth auction, while Mizuho fell 3.8% in Tokyo after disclosing a regulatory probe into suspected insider trading at its brokerage unit.

    Europe

    Eurozone macro signals were mixed today. German investor optimism, as measured by the ZEW survey, unexpectedly declined, tempering hopes that Europe's largest economy is on the cusp of the strong recovery markets had begun to price in. Set against that, eurozone finance chiefs used the day to push for a larger global role for the euro — a theme gaining traction as US policy unpredictability weighs on dollar credibility, though concrete steps remain limited. Bloomberg Economics flagged a broader concern relevant to ECB watchers: Australia's surprise rate hike, premised on downgraded productivity and a higher neutral rate, may foreshadow a similar reassessment in Europe, where weak productivity has so far been acknowledged but not yet operationalized into policy. For now, the ECB's framing remains anchored on demand and disinflation. In the UK, the macro picture tilted decisively dovish. Unemployment rose to its highest level since the pandemic and wage growth eased further, prompting traders to fully price half a point of Bank of England rate cuts by year-end. BBVA's Roberto Cobo Garcia suggested a March cut now appears increasingly likely and that two cuts may prove insufficient given the deteriorating labour market. Politically, Prime Minister Keir Starmer reversed plans to cancel some May local elections following backlash, the latest U-turn under his premiership, and sought to steady his government after the Mandelson-Epstein scandal that briefly threatened his position. On the corporate side, Germany's Hapag-Lloyd agreed to acquire ZIM Integrated Shipping at a valuation of roughly $4.2 billion, sending ZIM shares up 35%. Qiagen fell nearly 5% in Frankfurt after reports that Danaher is nearing a roughly $10 billion deal for Masimo, reducing the probability of a Qiagen takeout. Novo Nordisk confirmed expanded investment at its Athlone, Ireland facility to produce Wegovy in oral form for non-US markets, reinforcing Ireland's pharmaceutical export base even as Trump continues to single out the country's $112 billion goods surplus with the US. In Denmark, Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen is weighing a snap election to capitalise on her recovered standing following the Greenland dispute with Washington. The Stoxx Europe 600 closed roughly flat.

    Global

    US-Iran talks reopen as Hormuz sees scheduled disruption

    US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi are holding indirect nuclear talks in Geneva, with President Trump saying Tehran "wants to make a deal" and that he will be involved indirectly. Secretary of State Marco Rubio struck a cautiously optimistic tone, expressing hope that years of hostility can be bridged. The diplomatic opening coincides, however, with Iranian naval exercises that will close parts of the Strait of Hormuz for "several hours" today, according to Iranian state media — a calibrated reminder of Tehran's leverage over the world's most important oil chokepoint. Crude prices initially declined before recovering as the drills drew attention, leaving the market to weigh the prospect of de-escalation against the continued risk premium embedded in any Hormuz disruption. The combination of talks and exercises suggests both sides are negotiating from positions they wish to reinforce militarily; an agreement remains far from assured.

    Monetary policy divergence sharpens

    UK unemployment reached its highest level since the pandemic alongside easing wage growth, prompting traders to fully price half a point of Bank of England cuts by year-end. In contrast, the Reserve Bank of Australia's February minutes confirmed its rate hike was driven by a downgrade to productivity and potential output, with policymakers concluding the neutral rate is higher than previously assumed. The RBA's Andrew Hauser openly questioned whether Australia is a "first mover" on a constraint other advanced economies will face, or an idiosyncratic case. If productivity-constrained potential growth proves a broader phenomenon, markets pricing aggressive global easing may be underestimating where neutral now sits. US disinflation, by contrast, continues gradually, with markets discounting two Fed cuts in the second half of 2026.

    Russia's African pivot

    Lacking the fiscal capacity of China or the US, Moscow is leveraging the Russian Orthodox Church, now present in at least 34 African countries, alongside cultural centers and scholarships. Reporting suggests the soft-power push is increasingly tied to military and industrial recruitment as Ukraine attrition continues — an inexpensive substitute for the economic statecraft Russia can no longer afford.

  • US

    Markets closed for Presidents' Day

    With cash equity and Treasury markets shut, today's briefing focuses on positioning into the week ahead and weekend developments.

    Rates and Inflation

    Treasuries posted their best week in months as benign January CPI data reinforced expectations that the Fed will deliver rate cuts this year. Granular detail from Bloomberg Economics underscores the disinflation: across the 12 large US metros tracked by the BLS, January CPI averaged 2.16%, below the national print. Annual inflation has dropped below 1% in the West South Central region, with Dallas registering its first deflationary reading since the pandemic. Chicago, Boston, and Minneapolis all printed sub-2%. Whether this geographic breadth persists into the PCE release on Friday — alongside Q4 GDP and personal income — will shape the March FOMC debate.

    Equities and the AI Contradiction

    A tension at the heart of US equity positioning is becoming harder to reconcile: bearish AI-disruption narratives have driven punishing selloffs in real estate services, wealth management, insurance, and logistics, even as Microsoft and Amazon shares have been hit on capex concerns suggesting an AI bubble. Both views cannot fully hold. Hyperscaler capex from Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Alphabet is projected to exceed $600 billion in 2026, transforming cash-rich balance sheets into more leveraged ones — a shift now visible in credit derivatives, where single-name CDS on Meta and Alphabet have become actively traded contracts. Goldman has rolled out a software pair-trade basket favoring infrastructure names (Cloudflare, CrowdStrike, Oracle) against application software shorts (Salesforce, Monday.com, DocuSign).

    Memory Chip Squeeze

    DRAM prices rose 75% from December to January as AI accelerator demand crowds out conventional buyers. Cisco's weak guidance last week — and its worst share loss in nearly four years — was attributed partly to the memory squeeze; Qualcomm and Arm have flagged similar pressure. The pass-through to broader tech margins remains to be seen.

    Week Ahead

    Q4 GDP, PCE, housing starts, and Walmart earnings headline. Friday brings a potential Supreme Court ruling on Trump's tariffs.

    Asia

    Coverage across the region was constrained by Lunar New Year holidays, with mainland China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, South Korea and several Southeast Asian markets either closed or operating on shortened sessions. Activity nonetheless centered on a handful of consequential developments.

    China policy and US tech tensions

    Xi Jinping called for anchoring China's economic growth around domestic demand as the "main driver" — a notable rhetorical pivot that reinforces Beijing's pivot toward consumption-led rebalancing, though concrete fiscal follow-through remains the test. Separately, the Pentagon briefly added Alibaba, BYD and Baidu to its list of companies deemed to be aiding the Chinese military, only to withdraw the designation minutes later without explanation. The episode triggered modest selling — Alibaba slid more than 3% in Hong Kong, BYD and Baidu fell roughly 1% — and underscores the unpredictability of US-China tech friction even absent formal policy shifts. Alibaba simultaneously unveiled a major upgrade to its flagship AI model, positioning ahead of DeepSeek's anticipated next release. Chinese equities' bull run has, however, begun to falter as the earnings season approaches with subdued expectations.

    Japan: weak growth complicates BOJ path

    Fourth-quarter real GDP expanded just 0.2% annualized, far below the 1.6% consensus, with consumer spending up only 0.1%. The print strengthens the case for Prime Minister Takaichi's expansionary fiscal stance, and a former currency chief argued the BOJ is "behind the curve." Separately, Mizuho Financial shares fell about 5% after reports that employees are under investigation for suspected insider trading.

    Southeast Asia and Australia

    Thailand's Q4 GDP rose 2.5% year-on-year, exceeding all forecasts and prompting an upward revision to 2026 growth projections on stronger exports and tourism. Malaysia's king publicly placed the MACC anti-graft agency on his "radar" following allegations against its leadership, with street protests in Kuala Lumpur. In Australia, a Macquarie-led consortium's A$11.7 billion bid for Qube revived dealmaking momentum after a thin 2025. India tightened equity trading curbs amid ongoing efforts to cool speculative activity.

    Memory chips

    DRAM prices rose 75% from December to January, with Asian suppliers Samsung and SK Hynix among the principal beneficiaries.

    Europe

    ECB signals global euro ambitions amid succession questions

    The European Central Bank said it stands ready to provide euro liquidity to monetary authorities globally, an attempt to forestall market stress and broaden international use of the single currency. The move fits a wider European push to assert monetary autonomy as the transatlantic relationship is recalibrated — US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said over the weekend that the US-Europe alliance "has to change." Separately, succession planning at the ECB is gaining urgency. François Villeroy de Galhau's surprise resignation from the Bank of France hands the appointment to outgoing President Macron rather than a potential National Rally successor, and with Christine Lagarde's term expiring end-2027, Philip Lane departing in May 2027, and Isabel Schnabel's board seat opening in December 2027, governments may pursue a package deal. Whether Macron's preference for an accelerated, bundled appointment finds support among other capitals remains to be seen.

    EU carbon market faces political stress test

    EU carbon permits fell to their lowest since July as governments openly debate weakening the Emissions Trading System. Slovakia's Fico has called for suspension, Germany's Merz floated revision before softening his stance, while Macron defended the program. Commission President von der Leyen pledged a reform proposal for Q3. JPMorgan analysts noted the lack of visibility is impeding long-term green capex planning — a tension worth monitoring given Europe's broader industrial competitiveness debate.

    UK: BOE in data-dependent mode; Starmer turns to AI

    A heavy UK data week — labor market Tuesday, CPI Wednesday, retail sales and public finances Friday — follows a 5-4 BOE hold this month, with rates tied with the Fed's upper bound at the G-7's highest. UK house prices stalled in February with buyers enjoying the widest choice in a decade, consistent with a softening demand backdrop. Prime Minister Starmer is set to outline regulation requiring AI chatbots to comply with child online safety laws.

    Periphery and corporates

    Sweden's establishment is tentatively reopening the euro adoption debate, framed increasingly through geopolitical rather than purely economic lenses. Switzerland's economy rebounded in Q4 despite outsized US tariffs. The Stoxx 600 rose 0.32% in quiet trade with US markets closed. Notable single-name moves: Norsk Hydro fell 4.4% on downgrades from Goldman and RBC following Friday's weak guidance; Pinewood Technologies tumbled 32% after Apax abandoned its bid citing market conditions; KGHM declined 3.3% on a Citi downgrade.

    Global

    Oil markets in suspended animation ahead of US-Iran talks

    WTI traded narrowly around $63 as participants awaited the resumption of US-Iran negotiations Tuesday, with directional conviction notably absent. Javier Blas characterized last week's International Energy Week debate as a narrow win for bears, though "whispers about an actual surplus are getting louder." More striking was activity in tanker markets: South Korea's Sinokor group has accumulated roughly 120 VLCCs over the past two months, helping push benchmark VLCC earnings above $120,000 per day—a more than fourfold increase in a month. Separately, the US military boarded a tanker in the Indian Ocean over the weekend, alleging it sought to evade Trump's Caribbean energy blockade against sanctioned vessels, while Netanyahu reiterated that any Iran deal must strip nuclear capabilities—a maximalist position that may complicate the diplomatic track.

    Monetary policy divergence widens, with the ECB making a strategic move

    The European Central Bank announced it stands ready to provide euro liquidity to monetary authorities globally, an effort to mitigate market stress and expand the single currency's international role. The initiative fits a broader European recalibration in response to a shifting US posture. Sweden's tentative reopening of the euro debate—unthinkable a few years ago—reflects the same dynamic, with geopolitical exposure rather than monetary mechanics now driving the conversation. Speculation has also intensified around an accelerated, package appointment of Lagarde's successor at the ECB, partly to insulate the choice from a possible National Rally victory in France in April 2027.

    Europe's carbon market faces its sternest political test in years

    EU allowances fell to their lowest since July as leaders openly clashed over ETS reform at last week's summit. Slovakia's Fico called for suspension; Merz floated revision before walking it back; Macron defended the system. The Commission has committed to a reform proposal in Q3, but JPMorgan notes the uncertainty is already impairing corporate green capex planning—a tension that may persist regardless of where reforms ultimately land.

    Climate tools under pressure elsewhere

    The collapse of Kenya's Koko Networks, after authorities delayed compliance-market authorization, has damaged Kenya's standing as an offsets destination and may trigger a World Bank insurance payout near $180 million.

  • US

    Power Costs Emerge as Midterm Liability for GOP

    A Goldman Sachs study released this week by economists Manuel Abecasis and Hongcen Wei projects that data centers will account for roughly 40% of new US power demand over the next five years, with electricity-price inflation holding near 6% through 2027. The analysis points to a politically sensitive dynamic heading into November's midterms: cost of living, and electricity bills specifically, are shaping up as a defining voter concern. The administration has responded with an initiative pressing hyperscalers to fund the generation capacity their facilities require — a structure the Goldman authors flag as material, warning that if non-AI ratepayers absorb a disproportionate share of capex, headline electricity inflation could run higher still. The IEA's latest outlook reinforces the demand picture, projecting global power consumption growth roughly 50% faster over the next five years than the prior decade, decoupling from GDP in a manner that marks a structural break.

    AI Competitive Position vs. China

    The power constraint is increasingly framed as the binding variable in US-China AI competition. While US firms retain leadership across semiconductors, cloud, models, and applications, Chinese generation capacity additions are outpacing the US by a wide margin — a concern voiced publicly by Elon Musk and consistent with Jensen Huang's Davos framing of electricity as the foundational layer of the AI stack. The implication for investors: utility capex, grid interconnection timelines, and behind-the-meter generation deals warrant closer attention as gating factors for hyperscaler buildouts.

    Used EV Market Diverges from New

    Cox Automotive data show used EV sales rose 13.5% year-over-year in Q4 2025 to nearly 89,000 units, with inventory turning in roughly 50 days. New EV sales fell 36% over the same period following the expiration of federal purchase incentives — a bifurcation relevant to OEM residual value assumptions and dealer floor-plan economics. Coverage of Fed policy, fiscal developments, and credit markets was thin in today's source material.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific Daily Briefing — February 14, 2026

    Coverage relevant to the region today is relatively thin, with the day's Bloomberg dispatches focused largely on global thematic pieces. The most consequential threads for Asia-Pacific investors center on electricity demand, AI competitiveness, and India's deepening air pollution crisis.

    China's structural edge in the AI power race

    The International Energy Agency's annual outlook, released this week, projects global electricity demand will grow roughly 50% faster over the next five years than in the prior decade — outpacing GDP growth in what the agency terms an emerging "Age of Electricity." China remains the clear pacesetter, adding generation capacity at a rate well beyond US build-out and leading globally in electrification of industrial machinery. This positions Chinese AI infrastructure on a more favorable cost curve than US peers, where Goldman Sachs economists Manuel Abecasis and Hongcen Wei this week forecast data centers will drive 40% of incremental US power demand through 2030 and keep electricity-price inflation near 6% through 2027. The implication for capital allocation is meaningful: power-cost differentials may increasingly shape where the marginal data center is sited, though US export controls on advanced chips continue to constrain China's position higher up Jensen Huang's "five-layer cake."

    Taiwan upgrade

    Bloomberg references a substantial upward revision to Taiwan's growth outlook tied to what is described as "relentless" AI-related demand, reinforcing the island's centrality to the global semiconductor supply chain and supporting the case for continued strength in TWD-denominated tech exporters.

    India — US trade signals and pollution politics

    The US has altered its public statement on the India trade deal, generating confusion over the terms — a development worth monitoring for INR and Indian equity sentiment, though specifics remain unclear. Separately, public anger in New Delhi over hazardous winter air quality (AQI averaging 307–349 in December and January) appears to have reached a tipping point following November protests, raising the prospect of more assertive regulatory intervention on emissions, construction and crop burning. No material flow from Japan, Korea, Australia or ASEAN in today's dispatches.

    Europe

    Coverage note

    Today's source material is thin on Europe-specific news, with most content focused on global thematic stories (AI power demand, climate, EV markets). The most relevant European threads are extracted below.

    Germany signals pragmatic shift on EU climate policy

    Chancellor Friedrich Merz this week indicated the European Union should be prepared to rework its flagship carbon-market plan if compliance costs begin to weigh materially on business competitiveness. The remarks reflect mounting concern in Berlin about the erosion of Germany's industrial base, with elevated power prices identified as a central factor. Merz's tone marks a notable departure from the environmentalist orthodoxy that drove Germany's nuclear exit — a decision that, in retrospect, appears increasingly to be viewed within the governing coalition as a strategic miscalculation. Any meaningful revision to the EU Emissions Trading System would carry significant implications for European industrials, utilities, and carbon-price trajectories, though the political pathway to such reform remains uncertain given likely resistance from other member states.

    European energy security calculus continues to evolve

    The post-2022 substitution of Russian pipeline gas with American LNG has left Europe exposed to a different form of geopolitical leverage, with US reliability now viewed with greater skepticism in European capitals. This dynamic intersects with the broader competitiveness debate and may sustain political momentum behind both domestic generation buildout and renewed engagement with nuclear power across the continent.

    Europe's AI lag carries an inadvertent benefit

    Europe's position well behind the US and China in data-center buildout — generally regarded as a strategic weakness — has at least spared the continent from the acute incremental power-demand pressures now confronting US grids, where the IEA projects global electricity demand growth roughly 50% faster over the next five years than the prior decade. Whether this translates into durable industrial advantage or simply compounds Europe's technology gap remains to be seen. No material UK political, eurozone macro, ECB, or single-name European corporate developments featured in today's source set.

    Global

    The Electricity Constraint Tightens

    The IEA's latest annual power report frames the coming half-decade as the "Age of Electricity," forecasting global electricity demand growth roughly 50% faster than the prior decade and more than double the pace of overall energy demand. Critically, electricity consumption is now expected to outpace GDP growth — a structural break from the historical relationship that carries meaningful implications for capex cycles, utility regulation, and inflation dynamics. Goldman Sachs economists Manuel Abecasis and Hongcen Wei project data centers will account for 40% of incremental US power demand through 2030, with electricity-price inflation holding near 6% through 2027. That print, if realized, would complicate the Fed's disinflation narrative and sits awkwardly against the cost-of-living politics shaping November's US midterms.

    Divergent National Responses

    China continues to add generating capacity at a pace that materially exceeds the US build-out, reinforcing what Elon Musk and others have flagged as a structural disadvantage in the AI infrastructure race. The Trump administration's response — pushing hyperscalers to internalize power-generation capex alongside data-center investment — addresses the optics but, per the Goldman analysis, the inflation outcome hinges critically on cost allocation between commercial and residential ratepayers. In Europe, Chancellor Merz signaled openness this week to reworking the EU's flagship carbon-market framework if it continues to weigh on industrial competitiveness, a notable softening reflecting persistent concerns over German deindustrialization. Europe's lag in the AI buildout, while a competitive liability, has incidentally reduced strain on its grid — a modest offset to an otherwise unfavorable position. The substitution of US LNG for Russian gas, meanwhile, has traded one form of geopolitical energy dependence for another, with reliability concerns around Washington now part of the European calculus.

    Coverage Note

    Today's source material is thin on US-Iran developments, Strait of Hormuz dynamics, and oil-market specifics; we would not infer quiescence from absence of reporting.

  • US

    Markets: AI disruption trade broadens into a cross-sector repricing

    US equities extended losses, with the S&P 500 down 1.6% on Thursday and futures off another 0.3% overnight, as fears that newly released models from Google and Anthropic threaten incumbents spread well beyond software. Commercial real estate stocks slid sharply on concerns AI-driven productivity gains will erode office demand, while insurance brokers, wealth managers, private credit firms and logistics names also came under pressure. The episode that crystallized the mood: Algorhythm Holdings, a $5 million micro-cap former karaoke importer, touted an AI freight platform — sending the Russell 3000 Trucking Index down 6.6%, with C.H. Robinson off 15% and Landstar 16%. Treasury volatility, by contrast, sits at a five-year low, suggesting the rotation remains an equity-market phenomenon rather than a broader risk-off event.

    Inflation print in focus; Fed policy debate widens

    January CPI lands today against a backdrop of bond-market conviction that the Fed will resume cuts later this year. Some desks are pushing back: Citi recommends five-year, five-year inflation forwards near 2.5%, while JPMorgan suggests tactically shorting 2-year Treasuries on resilient growth. Separately, Judy Shelton — whose 2020 Fed nomination failed in the Senate — has re-emerged in policy discussions, meeting privately with Treasury Secretary Bessent on central bank reform, including a supply-side rate framework and redeployment of the $2.9 trillion in bank reserves parked at the Fed. Implementation would require buy-in well beyond the administration.

    Trade and corporates

    The administration is working to narrow the 50% steel and aluminum tariffs, which have proven difficult for importers to calculate and remain a sticking point in EU negotiations. Washington also finalized a Taiwan trade deal featuring $44 billion in US LNG and crude purchases plus $15 billion in aircraft orders. In single names, Goldman Sachs general counsel Kathy Ruemmler will depart this summer following renewed scrutiny of her Epstein ties; Pinterest fell 20% premarket on a weak sales outlook, DraftKings declined 14%, while Applied Materials, Roku, Rivian and Airbnb rallied on results.

    Asia

    Markets: AI-driven selloff reaches Asia

    Regional equities slipped roughly 1% from a record after Wall Street's tech-led decline, with the Hang Seng down 1.7% as concern spread that newly capable AI tools from Google, Anthropic and others threaten incumbents across software, logistics, insurance and commercial real estate. Asia has so far weathered the rotation better than US markets, but the MSCI All Country World Index is on track for back-to-back weekly losses. Separately, OpenAI told US lawmakers that DeepSeek has used increasingly sophisticated distillation techniques to extract output from leading American models — a claim that, if it gains traction in Washington, could harden the policy backdrop for Chinese AI champions just as Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance and Baidu are collectively spending roughly $720 million on Lunar New Year red-packet promotions to seed their new AI shopping tools.

    China–Japan tensions deepen in consumer behavior

    Following PM Takaichi's comments that a Taiwan contingency could trigger a Japanese military response, Beijing's travel advisory is translating into measurable economic effects: bookings to Sapporo are projected to fall 62% in January–February, while domestic resorts such as Jilin's Beidahu have seen reservations climb up to 70%. With Takaichi's mandate secure, a near-term détente appears unlikely.

    Capital flows and outbound M&A

    Chinese regulators have instructed banks with concentrated US Treasury exposure to pare positions, framing the move as risk diversification rather than a geopolitical signal. The market response has been muted — Treasury volatility sits at a five-year low — and the guidance extends a multi-year trend rather than marking a rupture. Concurrently, Greater China outbound M&A approached $12 billion in January, the strongest start since 2017, with targets spanning Puma and Allied Gold. The pattern is consistent with Beijing's tacit blessing for strategic acquisitions amid soft domestic demand.

    Other developments

    The US and Taiwan finalized a trade pact including $44 billion in LNG and crude purchases, $15 billion in civil aircraft, and roughly $25 billion in US power-generation equipment by 2029. Hong Kong is weighing expanded confidential IPO filings beyond tech and biotech. Sanrio surged 15% on raised guidance. A hawkish BOJ member signaled conditions for the next hike could align by spring.

    Europe

    European Morning Briefing — February 13, 2026

    European equities edged lower in early trade, with the Stoxx 600 down 0.35% as the AI-driven selloff that battered US megacaps overnight rippled across global markets. Sentiment remained fragile ahead of the US January CPI release, though by mid-morning the selloff was losing momentum. Treasury volatility has paradoxically fallen to a five-year low, suggesting the rotation reflects sector-specific repricing of AI exposure rather than broader macro stress.

    L'Oréal

    led losers after the French beauty group reported disappointing sales growth, weighed down by its luxury division and continued softness in the Greater China region — a reminder that European consumer exposure to Chinese demand remains a meaningful drag even as broader trade flows stabilize. The print extends a run of mixed results from Europe's luxury complex and will reinforce questions about the timing of any China consumer recovery. On the policy front, the ECB fined Crédit Agricole €9 million for failing to adequately manage climate-related and environmental risks — a modest sum but a notable signal of supervisory follow-through on the ECB's climate framework, and one that peers will likely study closely. Separately, the EU continues to press Washington to narrow the scope of US steel and aluminium tariffs as part of pending trade negotiations; the USTR is reportedly working to address calculation complexities that have frustrated European exporters since the 50% levy took effect. In Hungary, opposition leader Péter Magyar, whose Tisza party leads in polls ahead of this year's election, disclosed that he was secretly recorded in 2024 in what he characterised as a "Russia-style" blackmail attempt. The pre-emptive disclosure appears designed to neutralise the material's political impact; whether it succeeds remains to be seen, but Tisza's polling lead over Fidesz makes the episode consequential for Hungarian political risk. Finally, at the Munich Security Conference, European officials are reportedly reassessing the continent's nuclear deterrent posture following what attendees described as a sobering recalibration of US security commitments — a discussion with potentially significant long-term implications for European defence spending trajectories.

    Global

    Oil markets caught between glut narrative and geopolitical tail risks

    Brent traded around $70 per barrel and headed for back-to-back weekly declines on broader risk-off sentiment, concerns over a global crude surplus, and the prospect of drawn-out US-Iran nuclear talks. At this week's London oil trading summit, sentiment was conflicted: bears point to current price levels as evidence of ample supply despite ongoing tensions, while bulls highlight the accumulation of sanctioned Russian and Iranian crude on tankers, arguing that unsanctioned barrels show little sign of the much-discussed surplus. Pimco's Greg Sharenow captured the prevailing mood: "The uncertainty band is wider than we would have thought six or 10 weeks ago." Cross-currents include US military activity near Venezuela and Iran, Kazakh supply disruptions, and a US-India trade arrangement that appears to be stranding Russian crude at sea. Separately, Reliance Industries received a US general license to purchase Venezuelan oil directly, a notable carve-out within the broader sanctions architecture.

    Monetary policy divergence broadens

    Russia's central bank delivered an unexpected rate cut, while Peru held steady; economists now anticipate three further cuts from the South African Reserve Bank. A senior BOJ hawk signaled that conditions for the next rate hike could be in place by spring, while the PBOC's growing focus on the overnight rate is fueling speculation of a formal shift in its policy target. In the US, markets await January CPI for cues on the Fed's resumption of cuts, though Citi and JPMorgan strategists separately warn of complacency on the inflation trajectory.

    Climate-energy split-screen

    Initial CREA analysis suggests Chinese carbon emissions fell 0.3% in 2025—the first non-pandemic decline—as EV sales and clean generation absorbed incremental power demand. The US moved in the opposite direction, with the EPA rescinding the 2009 endangerment finding that underpins federal greenhouse gas authority, alongside the repeal of Biden-era vehicle emissions standards. The policy divergence between the two largest emitters appears increasingly structural.

    Treasury stewardship

    China's holdings fell to $683 billion in November, the lowest since 2008, with Beijing reportedly advising banks to trim US sovereign exposure on concentration grounds. European and EM official holders have also pared positions, though Treasury volatility sits at a five-year low—suggesting reallocation remains gradual rather than disorderly.

  • US

    Labor Market Surprises, Complicating the Easing Path

    January nonfarm payrolls expanded by 130,000 — the strongest monthly gain in over a year — while the unemployment rate ticked lower. The print upended expectations of continued labor market softening and prompted futures markets to push the next anticipated Fed cut to July from June. Treasury yields rose in response, though pricing still embeds two cuts for the full year. That conviction may prove optimistic: with prime-age employment ratios suggesting the economy is operating near full capacity and the available labor supply revised lower, additional stimulus risks feeding through to inflation rather than activity. The January CPI release, expected to show core inflation easing to a post-pandemic low of 2.5%, will be the next critical data point. Markets appear to be assuming Kevin Warsh, the president's nominee to chair the Fed beginning in May, will deliver the priced easing — an assumption that may face tests later in the year if inflation proves sticky.

    House Republicans Break with Trump on Canada Tariffs

    In the most significant legislative pushback to the administration's trade agenda to date, the Republican-led House passed a bill terminating tariffs on Canadian imports, with six Republicans crossing the aisle. The measure is unlikely to survive a presidential veto, but the vote exposed GOP fissures heading into midterm primaries and reflects growing concern among swing-district members about affordability pressures. Trump's economic approval ratings have weakened as Democrats have outperformed in recent special elections.

    Equity Underperformance and Sector Rotation

    The S&P 500 is up just 1.4% year-to-date, ranking 69th among 92 global indices tracked by Bloomberg and trailing world ex-US equities by roughly 7.7 points. Capital is rotating both abroad — EM portfolio inflows reached nearly $100 billion in January, the second-highest on record — and within US markets toward economically-sensitive names. The Dow Jones Transportation Average has outpaced the S&P 500 by 13 percentage points over the past six weeks. In single-stock news, Cisco fell roughly 8% premarket on a soft outlook tied to rising memory costs, while Nuveen agreed to acquire Schroders for $13.5 billion.

    EPA Rescinds Endangerment Finding

    The administration moved to rescind the 2009 endangerment finding underpinning federal greenhouse gas regulation, setting up litigation likely to reach the Supreme Court and potentially constraining future climate rulemaking regardless of political control.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific Daily Briefing — February 12, 2026 Asian equities extended a powerful run, with the MSCI Asia Pacific Index closing at a record and posting its strongest start to a year versus the S&P 500 this century — up roughly 13% year-to-date against 1.4% for US stocks. South Korea's KOSPI led the move, gaining another 3.1% to bring year-to-date gains near 30%, supported by AI hardware exposure, shareholder-return reforms, and relatively cheaper valuations. January portfolio inflows into emerging markets approached $100 billion, the second-strongest reading in two decades per IIF data, with allocations broad-based across China and the rest of EM. Whether this rotation reflects a durable re-rating or a tactical underweight unwind remains to be seen — EM equities are no longer convincingly cheap on price-to-book terms. In China, regulators moved to end the prolonged auto price war, with the State Administration for Market Regulation banning below-cost sales and tightening oversight of rebate programs and digital platforms. The measures address Beijing's concerns over deflationary competition, though enforcement efficacy is uncertain given ongoing discounting. Separately, CREA analysis suggests China's CO2 emissions fell 0.3% in 2025 — the first non-Covid decline — as renewables absorbed all incremental power demand. Inbound tourism is on track to surpass 2019 levels, providing a modest offset to weak retail sales, which in December marked their softest reading since reopening. Japanese assets rallied as markets digested Prime Minister Takaichi's decisive election win. The yen strengthened to ¥153 from near ¥160 in January, 30-year JGB yields fell roughly 40bp from January highs, and the Topix advanced. Washington and Tokyo are reportedly narrowing the first projects under the $550 billion investment vehicle tied to last year's trade deal, with candidates spanning SoftBank-led data centers, a Gulf of Mexico oil terminal, and synthetic diamonds for semiconductors. SoftBank separately posted a profit on OpenAI valuation gains. In India, GQG is adding to a $24 billion position despite foreign outflows, while farmer protests against the US trade deal underscore domestic political constraints on Modi's tariff concessions. Singapore's Wong unveiled a budget framed around a "more dangerous" geopolitical backdrop.

    Europe

    Europe's competitiveness debate sharpens ahead of EU summit

    French President Emmanuel Macron revived his "Made in Europe" agenda ahead of a key EU meeting, pressing for local-content priority in strategic sectors. Chancellor Friedrich Merz pushed back, warning against excessive protectionism, exposing a familiar Franco-German split over how to lift European productivity. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves argued for the inclusion of partners such as the UK, Norway and Canada in any industrial framework. Separately, ECB Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel called for accelerated structural reforms, underscoring official concern that monetary policy alone cannot close the growth gap with the US.

    UK macro remains soft

    Fourth-quarter GDP barely expanded, with services stagnating — adding to political pressure on Prime Minister Keir Starmer. A RICS survey nonetheless pointed to the first projected rise in London house prices in more than a year, though overall transaction activity remains subdued following tax uncertainty. The political tone hardened as Jim Ratcliffe told Sky News the UK has been "colonized" by immigrants, sharpening an already contentious debate over benefits and migration.

    European energy security back in focus

    At the E-world conference in Essen, traders flagged that the continent will exit winter with gas inventories more depleted than in 2025, with German storage only roughly a quarter full. Summer-winter price spreads offer little incentive to refill, and Uniper and Bayernugs have already sought to close loss-making facilities. EU carbon prices fell sharply after Merz suggested the bloc should be open to revising or delaying its emissions market — the steepest drop since 2022.

    Corporate

    Nuveen agreed to acquire Schroders for $13.5 billion in cash at a roughly 29% premium, ending more than two centuries of independence for the UK's largest standalone asset manager while preserving the brand and London headquarters. Sanofi fell 6% in Paris after abruptly replacing CEO Paul Hudson with Merck KGaA's Belén Garijo. Adyen sank 20% in Amsterdam on a softer revenue outlook, and Magnum Ice Cream dropped 16% on weak operating profit. The Stoxx Europe 600 nonetheless rose 0.4%, supported by a broadly constructive earnings tape.

    Global

    Oil and Iran diplomacy in uneasy equilibrium

    Crude edged higher as traders weighed President Trump's statement to Prime Minister Netanyahu that he will pursue talks with Iran and prefers a deal, even as Israel signaled discomfort with Washington's diplomatic opening. The market remains caught between a fundamental oversupply — which has kept a lid on prices and pressured upstream margins while delivering a windfall to US refiners — and tail risk around any disruption to Gulf flows. Traders also note that this year's price firmness has been aided by major refiners turning away barrels from sanctioned origins, a quieter but consequential structural shift. For now, the conflict premium remains contained; whether it stays that way depends on whether the diplomatic track holds.

    Monetary policy divergence widens

    A stronger-than-expected US payrolls print (+130,000) and a falling unemployment rate pushed the projected timing of the next Fed cut from June to July, lifting Treasury yields and tempering the two-cut consensus for 2026 — though Kevin Warsh's anticipated May arrival as Fed chair complicates the picture. The dollar nonetheless slipped, supporting a broad rotation: the MSCI Asia Pacific Index hit a record and is outpacing the S&P 500 by roughly 12 percentage points year-to-date, with Latin American equities up 21% and Korea's KOSPI up 30%. Non-resident EM portfolio inflows reached nearly $100 billion in January, the second-highest monthly print in two decades. The rally appears broad-based across debt, equity, China and ex-China, though absolute EM valuations are no longer cheap on price-to-book.

    European gas and carbon signals diverge

    Continental gas storage is heading into spring more depleted than a year ago, with little financial incentive to refill given summer prices trading above next winter's. Roughly 130 additional LNG cargoes may be needed to close the gap. Separately, EU carbon prices fell sharply after Chancellor Merz indicated openness to revising or delaying the bloc's emissions market — a notable softening of climate commitment from Europe's largest economy.

    China emissions inflect

    CO2 emissions fell 0.3% in 2025, the first non-pandemic annual decline, as renewables covered all incremental power demand. Whether this marks a durable peak remains to be seen; March's five-year plan will be the key tell.

  • US

    Macro and the Fed

    The delayed January employment report, postponed by the recent brief shutdown, arrives this morning against a backdrop of softening data. Consensus looks for roughly 65,000 jobs added, with the unemployment rate holding at 4.4%. December retail sales unexpectedly stalled, the Q4 employment cost index posted its smallest gain since 2021, and the NFIB survey showed the share of small firms reporting hard-to-fill openings at a post-pandemic low. Markets have re-priced accordingly: traders now lean toward two Fed cuts this year under incoming Chair Kevin Warsh, partially reversing the hawkish drift that followed his January 30 nomination. The 10-year Treasury yield fell six basis points to back below its 200-day moving average, and the MOVE index continues its steady decline from last April's Liberation Day peak. Powell's framing of a possible "zero job growth, stable unemployment" equilibrium — a function of restricted immigration flows — complicates how a soft headline print should be read. A weak number that depresses consumption is a different signal than one driven by labor-supply contraction.

    Equities and the AI rotation

    With roughly 75% of S&P 500 names reported, Q4 blended earnings growth has reached a four-year high of about 14.5%, with eight of eleven sectors positive. The Russell 1000 Value has opened a meaningful lead over Growth since mid-December, consistent with a broadening that the extreme concentration of recent years — the top 10 names now 39% of the S&P 500 — had set up. At the same time, a more uncomfortable subplot is developing: investors are pre-emptively selling firms perceived as vulnerable to AI displacement. An Altruist tax-strategy tool drove Schwab, Raymond James and LPL down 7%+ Tuesday, the sharpest declines since April. Software, insurance brokers, and legal/financial information providers have all been hit in recent sessions. JPMorgan strategists view the software reaction as overblown; the dispersion bears watching.

    Corporate

    Ford guided to higher 2026 profit despite a $900 million Q4 tariff charge. Mattel (-32% premarket) and Lyft (-17%) disappointed on guidance; Cloudflare rose 14% on a strong outlook.

    Asia

    Regional equities extend outperformance

    The MSCI Asia Pacific Index rose 1% to a fresh record, widening its lead over US and European benchmarks as a softening dollar and rising conviction in Fed easing drew flows toward tech-heavy markets including Taiwan and South Korea. The regional gauge is now up 39% since the start of 2025. A weak US December retail sales print and a cooling employment cost index have shifted rate-cut expectations back toward two reductions this year under Fed chair-designate Kevin Warsh, supporting risk appetite across the region ahead of the delayed US payrolls release.

    China — disinflation easing, but recovery uneven

    January factory-gate deflation moderated more than expected, though the improvement leaned heavily on the global metals rally rather than a durable pickup in domestic demand. Morgan Stanley's Robin Xing cautioned there is "no decisive reflation yet." Consumption fragility was underscored by the abrupt closure of all Shanghai outlets of premium chain Xiaonanguo days before Lunar New Year. Separately, SMIC warned that the breakneck pace of AI chip spending may be pulling forward years of demand, raising the prospect of underutilized data center capacity — a notable caution from inside the supply chain. Beijing's reported guidance to domestic banks to limit Treasury purchases briefly weighed on US bonds but appears consistent with a decade-long, gradual reduction in holdings rather than a fresh inflection.

    India — trade-deal ambiguity, capital markets maturing

    The White House revised its US-India trade fact sheet, removing references to pulses and softening language on Indian commitments to American goods, compounding confusion already flagged by Indian farmer groups. Separately, Goldman Sachs vaulted to fourth in Indian equity underwriting and fifth in M&A league tables in 2025, overtaking Morgan Stanley in stock sales for the first time in a decade.

    Commodities and policy

    Indonesia cut the production quota at Weda Bay — the world's largest nickel mine — to 12 million tons from 42 million, an aggressive supply curb aimed at lifting prices. The RBA's Deputy Governor Hauser reiterated inflation remains "too high," reinforcing the recent hawkish tilt. Singapore's PM Wong is preparing a budget centered on AI adaptation and trade fragmentation.

    Europe

    UK politics and markets

    Prime Minister Keir Starmer secured a temporary reprieve from the most acute pressure on his leadership, winning public backing from his full cabinet — including Wes Streeting and Ed Miliband, both viewed as potential successors. The dynamic appears to have strengthened Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner's position within Labour's internal hierarchy. However, the relief is likely to prove short-lived: pressures linked to the Epstein files, which reference a key Starmer appointee extensively, continue to weigh, and would-be challengers are widely understood to be biding their time rather than abandoning their ambitions. Separately, Elliott Investment Management has built a stake in London Stock Exchange Group, according to a person familiar with the matter, as the exchange contends with AI-related disruption and a sustained decline in listings — a development that may catalyse strategic pressure on management.

    European corporates

    BP extended yesterday's slump, falling as much as 7.7% after executives offered no timeline for restoring share buybacks, leaving the UK major increasingly isolated from peers. TotalEnergies trimmed but maintained its buyback, while Chevron, Exxon and Shell held theirs steady.
