The buyback question will be among the first challenges for incoming CEO Meg O'Neill, who inherits roughly $60 billion in total debt and financial obligations. Heineken rose 3.6% in Amsterdam after announcing a roughly 7% reduction in its global workforce in response to softening beer demand. Dassault Systèmes fell around 20% in Paris following disappointing results, becoming the latest European software name caught in the broader AI-displacement selloff. Ford flagged a $900 million tariff charge but guided to higher 2026 profits.

    Macro and policy

    Norwegian January CPI surprised sharply to the upside, driving 10-year yields to levels last seen in early 2009 and shifting OIS pricing toward a March hike from Norges Bank — a notable divergence from the broader DM easing trajectory. Eurozone yields fell on the day, suggesting markets view the Norwegian print as idiosyncratic rather than a regional signal. The European Parliament moved closer to ratifying the US trade deal, agreeing a 2028 sunset clause that gives Washington six months to reduce steel and aluminium tariffs from 50% to 15%. European Council President António Costa said EU leaders are finally prepared to unify capital markets to compete globally, though execution risks remain considerable.

    Global

    Cross-currents in monetary policy divergence sharpen

    Norway delivered the day's most striking macro signal, with January CPI printing well above expectations and triggering a steep selloff in NOK government bonds. Ten-year yields climbed to levels not seen since early 2009, and markets now price a March hike from Norges Bank — joining the RBA, whose Deputy Governor Hauser reiterated that Australian inflation remains "too high." The divergence is becoming material: Norwegian-Swedish yield spreads have widened sharply even as euro-area yields drifted lower on the same session. Norway's energy-heavy economy and historically erratic inflation prints argue against extrapolating globally, but the episode underscores how country-specific dynamics are reasserting themselves as the post-Covid synchronization fades. In the US, by contrast, soft December retail sales and a benign employment cost index pushed traders to price two Fed cuts under incoming Chair Warsh, with the 10-year Treasury yield falling six basis points back below its 200-day moving average. The MOVE index continues its steady decline from last April's Liberation Day shock — a notable disconnect given the geopolitical backdrop.

    Iran diplomacy enters a delicate phase

    Netanyahu travels to Washington today to meet Trump, with the explicit aim of pressing for tougher concessions from Tehran. Recent US-Iran exchanges have struck a constructive tone on preliminary talks, though analysts remain skeptical that a substantive agreement is within reach. Oil gained on the session as Middle East tensions outweighed mounting evidence of a building supply glut — a tension that will resolve one way or the other in coming weeks.

    Energy transition and commodity policy

    Indonesia ordered Weda Bay Nickel — the world's largest nickel mine — to cut permitted output to 12 million tons from 42 million, an aggressive supply-side intervention aimed at supporting global prices. Separately, the Trump administration is expected to unveil federal support for coal-fired generation, while the EPA moves to repeal the 2009 endangerment finding underpinning US greenhouse gas regulation. BP halted buybacks to address roughly $60 billion in debt and obligations, diverging from peers Chevron, Exxon, Shell and TotalEnergies, all of which maintained shareholder returns.

  • US

    Markets pause near records ahead of data

    S&P 500 futures were little changed at 6,988, with the index sitting just below an all-time high as traders awaited December retail sales and remarks from Cleveland Fed President Hammack and Dallas Fed President Logan — both 2026 voters viewed as hawkish. Asian indexes set fresh records overnight, led by Japan's Nikkei (+2.3%), but US tech sentiment remains uneven: ON Semiconductor fell 4.3% pre-market on soft guidance, Goodyear dropped 8.8% on a projected 10% Q1 volume decline, and Regenxbio slid 15% after an FDA rejection. Coca-Cola, CVS, Fiserv, S&P Global and Datadog report today, with the latter two under scrutiny following last week's AI-disruption selloff in data and analytics names.

    Alphabet's century bond underscores AI capex scale

    Alphabet launched its first sterling and Swiss franc bond sales, including a rare 100-year tranche — the first century bond from a tech issuer since Motorola in 1997 — following a $20 billion US deal last week. The financing is earmarked for AI infrastructure. While pension and insurance demand for ultra-long duration is real, the structure is unusually sensitive to rate expectations; comparable Austrian century debt trades near 30 cents on the euro after peaking near 140. The issuance highlights how hyperscalers are terming out AI-related capex into the long end of the curve.

    Fed succession rhetoric escalates

    President Trump claimed his Fed chair pick Kevin Warsh could deliver 15% GDP growth — a figure detached from any plausible potential-output estimate, and one that will sharpen questions about institutional independence should Warsh be confirmed. Separately, Fed Governor Waller suggested Trump-induced crypto enthusiasm may be fading; Bitcoin fell 2.2% and ether extended declines as derivatives positioning remained defensive following October's crash.

    Memory chip squeeze bifurcates equity returns

    Sustained DRAM and NAND price increases are compressing margins at PC OEMs and Apple suppliers while lifting memory producers to records. Fidelity International's Vivian Pai expects tightness to persist through 2026, suggesting the dispersion trade has further to run.

    Trade friction

    Trump said he would open negotiations with Canada over the Gordie Howe Bridge, threatening to block its opening absent US compensation and partial ownership — a reminder that USMCA-adjacent frictions remain a recurring tail risk.

    Asia

    China sentiment inflection deepens, with caveats

    The pivot in foreign positioning toward Chinese equities continued to gather pace. Eight Roads, the venture vehicle backed by Fidelity's Johnson family, has reportedly shelved plans to offload stakes in roughly 40 Chinese tech companies for approximately $400 million — a sale conceived last year at a steep discount to a peak combined valuation near $1 billion. The reversal reflects improved valuations and the lower temperature in US-China relations following the Trump-Xi meeting in October. Goldman Sachs and BNP Paribas surveys point to accelerating allocations, and Qube's China long-only fund has grown more than tenfold to $2 billion year-on-year. Set against this, Bloomberg reports Chinese regulators are urging domestic institutions to pare US Treasury purchases — a development that bears monitoring for cross-border flow implications, though its operational scope remains unclear. Separately, BoE's Catherine Mann noted Chinese exporters have raised prices to the UK to offset US tariffs, a modest data point consistent with tariff pass-through rather than deflationary dumping.

    Japan's Takaichi trade extends

    The Nikkei 225 gained 2.8% Tuesday after Monday's 3.9% advance, with JGB yields edging lower — the rare equity-up, bond-stable combination the new prime minister needs. Markets are positioning for fiscal expansion paired with industrial policy directed at semiconductors, AI, defense, and shipbuilding. The constraint is external: a more assertive Tokyo and a weaker yen complicate the China relationship, and Xi faces a genuine dilemma over whether to engage Japan's most popular postwar leader or maintain distance given Tokyo's alignment with Washington.

    Regional and corporate notes

    Asian indices set fresh records overnight on easing AI capex concerns. Memory chip tightness continues to bifurcate Asian tech, pressuring Nintendo and Apple suppliers while lifting Korean and Japanese memory producers; Korean equities have added roughly $1.7 trillion in market cap since the start of 2025 but still trade at a governance discount. Singapore upgraded its 2026 growth outlook on AI-infrastructure demand. Adani Enterprises disclosed it received an OFAC information request on February 4 regarding alleged Iranian LPG imports through Mundra. Qatar's LNG expansion is creating absorption challenges, with stalled India talks a notable Asia data point. Hong Kong single-family offices rose 25% to 3,384 by end-2025, with 19% planning reduced US exposure — among the highest reallocation intentions surveyed.

    Europe

    UK politics: Starmer under siege, gilts wobble

    The Epstein scandal has metastasized into the most acute political crisis of Keir Starmer's premiership, despite no evidence the Prime Minister ever met the late financier. The catalyst remains the 2024 appointment of Peter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington — subsequently dismissed after correspondence with Epstein emerged, and now compounded by disclosures that Mandelson shared market-sensitive policy decisions with Epstein while serving as deputy PM. Starmer's chief of staff and communications head have resigned in the past 48 hours, and Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar has publicly called for him to stand down. Gilt yields moved higher following Sarwar's intervention, though the reaction remains an order of magnitude below the 2022 Truss episode — sterling has held up and the curve has not dislocated. Prediction markets now price Starmer's departure by end-June. The succession question is the more durable concern for fixed income: the most plausible candidates (Rayner, Burnham) sit to Starmer's left and are perceived as more fiscally expansive. As Mizuho's Jordan Rochester puts it, the question of "who's next" is a Damocles sword over gilt traders. Separately, BoE's Catherine Mann flagged that Chinese exporters raising prices to the UK to offset US tariffs is adding to domestic inflation.

    France: Villeroy's surprise exit hands Macron a succession card

    Bank of France Governor François Villeroy de Galhau unexpectedly announced his June departure, roughly 16 months ahead of schedule, citing personal reasons. The timing transfers the appointment from a potential post-2027 Le Pen or Bardella administration to Macron, allowing him to insulate the institution with a six-year term ahead of presidential elections. Given Bardella's stated preference for ECB QE to address French debt, the political stakes around the choice are material.

    Corporates

    BP shares fell 5% after suspending its $750m quarterly buyback and withdrawing capital return guidance, capping a turbulent year that saw CEO Auchincloss ousted. Barclays committed to returning at least £15bn through 2028. Standard Chartered CFO Diego De Giorgi unexpectedly resigned to join Apollo as EMEA head, removing him from CEO succession contention. Kering rose 10% in Paris on a smaller-than-expected Gucci sales decline and a special dividend. Alphabet is tapping sterling and Swiss franc markets, including a rare 100-year bond — the first century note from a tech issuer since Motorola in 1997. The Stoxx 600 was flat into the European afternoon.

    Global

    Energy markets: Qatar's LNG calculus and North Sea decline

    Qatar's aggressive long-term contracting drive — including a 27-year supply deal with Japan and a notable first sale to Malaysia, traditionally an exporter — has locked in roughly 39 million tons since 2022. Yet these commitments cover only about 60% of planned capacity expansion, forcing QatarEnergy toward greater spot-market participation and potentially a slower production ramp. Given Qatar's structural weight in seaborne gas, any strategic shift would reverberate globally. Separately, Carlyle's Jeff Currie argued oil and metals remain underinvested, with glut fears overstated. The contrast with the UK North Sea is stark: no new exploratory well was drilled in Britain last year — the first such pause since the 1960s — as Aberdeen's industry, now roughly 1% of GDP, contracts under the combined weight of fiscal policy and the energy transition.

    Iran sanctions enforcement broadens

    Adani Enterprises disclosed that OFAC has requested information regarding alleged imports of Iranian LPG through its Mundra port, following a WSJ report from June. Separately, the Indian Coast Guard seized three tankers in what appears to be its first material enforcement action against the dark fleet — a potentially meaningful signal given India's role as a residual buyer of sanctioned barrels.

    Defense spending entrenches as a structural theme

    European aerospace and defense has returned 236% since the February 2022 invasion, with US peers also outperforming materially. The pipeline remains supportive: Trump is pushing US military outlays toward $1.5 trillion by 2027, while Takaichi's Japan and Carney's "middle powers" accelerate their own build-ups. Valuations have stretched, and de-escalation episodes — such as Trump's recent softening on Greenland — will likely produce tactical volatility, but the structural thesis appears intact.

    Monetary policy divergence and central bank independence

    Bank of France Governor Villeroy de Galhau announced an unexpected June departure, handing Macron the opportunity to make a six-year appointment ahead of presidential elections in which Le Pen or Bardella are polling competitively. The timing, alongside Trump's continued pressure on the Fed and his claim that Warsh could deliver 15% growth, underscores rising political pressure on G-7 central banks — though Villeroy denies any non-personal motive.

  • US

    Equity market leadership in flux

    Friday's recovery — which lifted the Dow Industrials above 50,000 for the first time — masked a more consequential repositioning underneath. The rotation out of mega-cap software and into chipmakers, equal-weighted indices, small-caps, and value has accelerated, with broader market measures hitting all-time highs even as the S&P 500 and Magnificent Seven sit below recent peaks. The catalyst appears to be Anthropic's release of new AI work-automation tools, which sent the iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF down 8.7% on the week and prompted reassessments of incumbent software business models. Friday's bounce was thin: volume ran roughly 13% below the five-day average, implied volatility remained elevated, and a 9% rally in Goldman's most-shorted basket suggests short-covering rather than fresh conviction. Goldman's trading desk warned of further selling pressure this week from CTA unwinds. S&P 500 futures were down 0.29% in early trading; 10-year Treasury yields edged up 2bp to 4.23%.

    Fed leadership and institutional questions

    Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent urged the Senate to begin hearings on Kevin Warsh's nomination to chair the Federal Reserve, despite a Republican senator's pledge to block all such nominations pending resolution of a criminal investigation involving Chair Powell. Separately, Warsh floated reviving a Fed-Treasury accord to constrain bond-market intervention — a proposal that has stirred debate across rates markets given its implications for Fed independence and balance-sheet operations.

    China trims Treasury exposure; market reaction muted

    Chinese regulators have verbally instructed large domestic banks to pare US Treasury holdings, citing concentration and volatility concerns. The market response was negligible, reflecting that Chinese official and private holdings have declined steadily for over a decade to roughly $683 billion (about 7% of foreign-held Treasuries) and have been more than absorbed by other foreign buyers.

    Week ahead

    The shutdown-delayed January payrolls report lands Wednesday — including the annual benchmark revision, expected to mark down payrolls growth through March 2025 — followed by CPI on Friday. Fed speakers include Waller, Miran, Bostic, Hammack, Logan, Bowman, and Schmid.

    Asia

    Japan: Takaichi's Landslide Reshapes the Outlook

    Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's snap election gamble produced the Liberal Democratic Party's largest postwar single-party victory, delivering a two-thirds lower-house supermajority — an outcome prediction markets had priced at roughly even odds just two weeks ago. The Nikkei 225 surged nearly 4% to a record high, with investors pricing in fiscal stimulus, accelerated corporate governance reform, and the prospect of multi-year policy continuity. The yen and JGBs were notably calmer than the January rout had suggested possible, helped by Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama's assurance that a proposed two-year consumption tax cut on food would not require additional bond issuance. Still, the yen drifted to 157.76 against the dollar, with 160 widely viewed as the intervention threshold. The central tension remains unresolved: Takaichi's fiscal expansion sits awkwardly against Japan's ~230% debt-to-GDP burden, and her well-documented opposition to BOJ rate hikes complicates the inflation response. Evercore ISI frames the risk as a "fat right tail" — a Japanese analogue to the Truss episode — should political authority override fiscal restraint.

    China: Treasury Guidance and Lunar New Year Liquidity

    Chinese regulators have verbally instructed major banks to limit US Treasury purchases and trim concentrated positions, citing volatility and concentration risk. The directive excludes state holdings, and markets shrugged — 10-year yields rose only two basis points — reflecting that Chinese diversification has been gradual and well-absorbed by other foreign buyers. Separately, the PBOC injected $86.4 billion via 14-day repos, with Industrial Securities forecasting over $500 billion in liquidity provision ahead of Sunday's holiday. The PBOC also extended its gold-buying streak to 15 months. In Hong Kong, the 20-year sentence handed to Jimmy Lai under the national security law will test Western capitals' appetite to press Beijing while pursuing stabilization.

    India and Southeast Asia

    Trump formally rescinded the additional 25% tariff on Indian goods tied to Russian oil purchases, cementing terms of last week's trade deal; the Nifty 50 gained 3.5% on the week. The RBI held at 5.25%, citing reduced external uncertainty. Gavekal cautions the deal is necessary but not sufficient given MSCI India earnings growth of 8.3% trails regional peers materially. In Thailand, the Bhumjaithai Party's strong showing — tracking 191 seats — lifted the SET Index as much as 4% and strengthened the baht over 1%, on expectations of policy continuity under PM Anutin Charnvirakul. DBS shares fell as much as 1.9% after Q4 net income dropped 10% to $1.85 billion, missing estimates on comp

    Europe

    UK political crisis intensifies

    Prime Minister Keir Starmer's position has come under serious strain following the resignation of Morgan McSweeney, his chief of staff, amid the fallout from the appointment of Peter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington. People familiar with the matter indicate a group of cabinet ministers may privately urge Starmer to step down, though allies expect him to survive the week. Markets are positioning for further weakness: hedge funds have built short positions in sterling against the euro, citing the combination of political turmoil, expectations of further BOE rate cuts, and elevated options demand. Gilts declined as the political pressure intensified, though separate UK labour market data suggested the jobs sector is beginning to stabilise as firms emerge from a period dominated by tax speculation. UK Q4 GDP and industrial production are due Thursday.

    Iberian political shifts

    Socialist António José Seguro won Portugal's presidency in the first runoff in four decades, defeating far-right candidate André Ventura after a turbulent campaign. In Spain, the Socialists suffered another setback in a snap regional vote, underscoring waning support for Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez.

    Corporate developments

    Swatch Group's defence against activist Steven Wood strengthened materially after last month's upbeat outlook drove the most-shorted stock in Europe to its best day on record; shares are up 15% year-to-date. The company has nominated its own new board member ahead of Wood's second-year campaign for a seat. Novo Nordisk shares rebounded in pre-market trade after Hims & Hers Health withdrew a copy of its Wegovy weight-loss pill. STMicroelectronics gained 6.5% in Paris on a deepening partnership with AWS. InPost jumped 14% in Amsterdam after an Advent/FedEx-led group disclosed buyout plans. Greggs fell 6% in London following a Jefferies downgrade citing GLP-1 headwinds. BP reports Tuesday, with Bloomberg Opinion's Javier Blas arguing the $750mn quarterly buyback is unaffordable given softer commodity prices.

    ECB calendar

    Lagarde, Lane, Simkus and Nagel speak today, with Schnabel, Cipollone and others later in the week alongside euro-area Q4 GDP on Friday.

    Global

    US-Iran Talks Lower Geopolitical Premium, But Not by Much

    The first round of US-Iran nuclear negotiations in Oman concluded on a constructive note, with President Trump describing the talks as "very good" and Iranian President Pezeshkian calling them "a step forward." A second round is scheduled this week. Brent crude opened lower in response, but remains up more than 10% year-to-date, suggesting traders are unwilling to fully discount the geopolitical premium. As SEB's Bjarne Schieldrop noted, "the Iranian risk premium cannot be fully defused as long as US warships are located where they are." Meaningful divergence persists between the two sides: Iran insists negotiations remain confined to its nuclear program, while Secretary of State Rubio has signaled Washington wants a broader framework covering ballistic missiles and proxy financing. Trump has indicated he could accept a narrower deal, which would offer a politically expedient win ahead of midterms — though prior negotiations with Tehran ended with US participation in Israel's 12-day strike campaign, a reminder that the path is not linear. Netanyahu travels to Washington Wednesday to press for tighter constraints on Tehran.

    Monetary Policy Divergence: Japan Takes Center Stage

    PM Takaichi's two-thirds lower-house supermajority — the LDP's largest postwar single-party mandate — reshapes the global fiscal-monetary backdrop. The Nikkei rallied nearly 4% to record highs, while the yen and JGBs held steadier than feared after Finance Minister Katayama clarified that a proposed two-year food consumption tax cut would not require additional debt issuance. The tension remains structural: planned fiscal expansion against a 230% debt-to-GDP backdrop, combined with implicit pressure on the BOJ to delay further hikes, sits awkwardly with a yen trading near 157 and approaching levels markets associate with intervention. Evercore ISI flags a "fat right tail" risk of a Truss-style bond episode should fiscal restraint erode.

    Cross-Currents: China Trims Treasuries, Gold Rebounds

    Chinese regulators have verbally instructed banks to reduce US Treasury holdings on concentration and volatility concerns. Market reaction was muted — 10-year yields rose only two basis points — reflecting that Chinese diversification has been underway for a decade. Gold reclaimed $5,000/oz, recovering roughly half its late-January drawdown, with Bessent attributing recent volatility partly to Chinese trading activity. Separately, Trump removed the 25% tariff surcharge on Indian goods tied to Russian oil purchases, a step that may halve India's Russian crude imports and redirect flows toward US and Venezuelan barrels.

  • US

    US Daily Briefing — February 7, 2026

    Weekend coverage of US-specific developments is limited, with most syndicated content focused on global trade dynamics and climate-related operational risks. The most consequential US threads warrant brief treatment.

    Trade policy and the tariff regime

    The Trump administration's tariff architecture continues to reshape global commodity and capital flows, though Saturday brought no new policy announcements. Notable adjacent developments include "Project Vault," a planned US strategic stockpile in key minerals, which extends the administration's industrial policy footprint into critical-materials security and may have downstream implications for domestic mining equities and processing capacity. Separately, a reported surprise US-India trade framework could mark a partial reset of bilateral ties that had frayed under earlier tariff actions, though terms and durability remain to be seen. The broader pattern—major economies diversifying trade relationships in response to US tariffs, with Gulf-Asia corridors expanding at 15% annually—suggests US exporters may face structurally tougher competition in third markets even as bilateral deals are negotiated.

    Corporate capex and the AI cycle

    Big Tech capital expenditure is tracking toward $650 billion in 2026, an escalation that continues to underpin the equity rally in hyperscaler-adjacent names and power infrastructure. The figure, if realized, reinforces concerns about concentration risk in the S&P 500 and raises questions about return-on-invested-capital trajectories that have yet to be tested through a full cycle. The associated power-demand intensity also intersects with grid-reliability concerns flagged this week.

    Utility sector and wildfire risk

    Western US utilities—PG&E, Edison International, and Xcel Energy—remain in focus as an unusually warm, arid La Niña winter elevates fire risk across Texas, New Mexico, and Colorado. Xcel executed multiple preventative shutoffs in Colorado affecting more than 50,000 customers. Edison faces hundreds of lawsuits tied to last year's Eaton fire. The pattern reinforces a familiar tail-risk overhang for Western utility credit and equity, with implications for ratepayer dynamics and state regulatory posture heading into spring. No material Fed or fiscal developments crossed the wires today.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific Daily Briefing — February 7, 2026

    Weekend flow is light, but several threads carry meaningful implications for the region.

    China-Panama friction escalates

    Beijing is preparing retaliatory measures against Panama following the cancellation of the Canalport contract involving Chinese-linked operators. While details remain limited, the move underscores China's increasing willingness to deploy economic coercion in defense of overseas commercial interests, particularly those tied to strategic logistics infrastructure. The episode adds to the broader contest over global port concessions in which Chinese state-linked operators have come under pressure across multiple jurisdictions since the start of the Trump administration. For investors, it reinforces political risk around Chinese-operated maritime assets and may accelerate divestment discussions at COSCO and Hutchison-related entities.

    US-India reset in view

    Reporting suggests a surprise Trump trade deal could materially reset bilateral ties after a fractious period marked by tariff disputes and friction over Russian oil purchases. The contours remain undisclosed, and execution risk is meaningful given the administration's track record of late-stage renegotiation. Still, a constructive outcome would be supportive of Indian equities, particularly export-sensitive IT and pharmaceutical names, and could ease pressure on the rupee. It would also have implications for Quad dynamics and India's positioning vis-à-vis China.

    Indonesia sovereign tensions surface

    Following its recent downgrade, Jakarta publicly criticized a Citigroup economist, an unusually personal response that may signal heightened sensitivity to external commentary as the government navigates fiscal slippage concerns. The episode bears watching for any chilling effect on sell-side coverage and for what it implies about the Prabowo administration's market communication approach.

    Gulf-Asia trade corridor deepens

    Standard Chartered projects Asia-Middle East trade reaching $1.5 trillion by 2030 from $905 billion in 2024. Chinese vehicle exports to the UAE reached roughly 560,000 units in 2025—the third-largest destination globally—though a substantial share is transshipped onward. The data reinforces the Gulf's emergence as a re-export hub for Chinese manufactured goods seeking to circumvent direct exposure to Western tariff regimes. Japan, Korea, and Australia coverage is thin in today's flow.

    Europe

    European Daily Briefing — February 7, 2026

    Coverage from today's wires offers limited Europe-specific content, with the principal regional threads emerging at the margins of broader global stories. We summarize the most relevant developments below.

    UK trade policy: GCC deal reportedly near

    The British government indicated this week that a trade agreement with the Gulf Cooperation Council is "imminent," according to officials cited in coverage of Middle Eastern trade dynamics. If concluded, the deal would mark a notable post-Brexit win for Whitehall and align with broader UK efforts to deepen commercial ties beyond traditional European partners. The GCC bloc — anchored by Saudi Arabia and the UAE — has seen trade expand at a 15% annual clip from 2021-24, materially outpacing the 9% global rate per Standard Chartered data, making it an increasingly consequential counterparty. Concrete terms and timing remain to be disclosed, and the deal's macro impact will depend on the scope of services access and investment provisions, which have historically been the sticking points in GCC negotiations with Western partners.

    European shipping and ports

    France's CMA CGM has recently joined MSC of Switzerland-Italy as joint-venture partners at Abu Dhabi's Khalifa Port, operated by AD Ports. The arrangement reflects continued strategic positioning by European container lines in Gulf logistics infrastructure, where competition between DP World and AD Ports is intensifying. For European carriers, deeper Gulf terminal integration supports the Asia-Europe trade corridor at a time when Red Sea routing remains a consideration for fleet economics.

    Cortina Winter Olympics underway

    The 2026 Winter Games opened Friday in Cortina d'Ampezzo. Regional snowfall this week improved conditions, though the event has drawn renewed attention to the structural challenges facing Alpine tourism economies as warming trends pressure lower-elevation resorts — a slow-moving but material consideration for Italian, French, Swiss and Austrian tourism-dependent regions. No material eurozone macro data, ECB commentary, or UK gilt market developments appeared in today's wires.

    Global

    Middle East emerges as globalization's bright spot

    While protectionist currents dominate the broader global trade narrative, Gulf Cooperation Council economies expanded trade at a 15% annual clip from 2021-24, outpacing the 9% global rate, according to Standard Chartered. The Asia-Middle East corridor is projected to reach $1.5 trillion by 2030 from $905 billion in 2024, with Middle East-Africa trade expected to roughly double to $260 billion. The UAE, in particular, has positioned itself as an intercontinental logistics nexus: it imported approximately 560,000 Chinese passenger vehicles in 2025—third globally behind Mexico and Russia despite a population of only 11 million—reflecting its role as a transshipment hub rather than purely an end market. Britain signaled a GCC trade deal is "imminent," and competition between DP World's Jebel Ali and ADQ-owned Khalifa Port underscores the depth of regional logistics capacity. For investors, the Gulf increasingly offers diversification optionality as the Trump tariff regime reshapes traditional corridors, though the durability of this trade momentum will depend on whether energy-funded fiscal expansion can be sustained—Saudi Arabia has already scaled back its more ambitious Vision 2030 projects.

    Climate-energy nexus pressures Western utilities

    A La Niña-driven dry winter across the US Mountain West has forced Xcel Energy to implement multiple preventative power shutoffs across Colorado, affecting more than 50,000 customers. The pattern, projected to persist into spring, illustrates the operating cost utilities now bear from elevated wildfire liability—a tail risk made tangible by Edison International's mounting litigation over the Eaton fire and PG&E's 2019 bankruptcy precedent. Capital allocation toward weather monitoring infrastructure, AI-driven risk modeling, and vegetation management is becoming a structural feature of utility cost bases in fire-prone jurisdictions.

    Climate transition meets symbolism

    The Cortina Winter Olympics opened with regional temperatures 7°C above 1956 levels, with athletes increasingly competing on manufactured snow. While anecdotal, the trajectory has implications for tourism-dependent Alpine economies and reinforces the secular case for adaptation-related capex.

  • US

    Markets: Risk Unwind Extends, With Tentative Stabilization

    US equity futures pointed to a modest recovery Friday morning, with S&P 500 futures up 0.55% and Nasdaq 100 futures gaining 0.71%, following a sharp three-day decline that marked the Nasdaq 100's worst stretch since April. The selloff has been broad rather than catalyzed by a single trigger, reflecting an unwind of crowded positioning across megacap tech, precious metals, and crypto. Bitcoin recovered toward $66,000 after touching $60,033 in Asian trading — still roughly half its October peak. Software stocks have been particularly weak, with traders coining the term "SaaSpocalypse" as investors reassess the threat AI poses to incumbent business models. The 10-year Treasury yield was little changed at 4.20%.

    The AI Capex Question Intensifies

    Amazon shares tumbled approximately 8% in premarket trading after the company guided to $200 billion in 2026 AI-related capital expenditure. Aggregate planned spending from Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft now approaches $650 billion for 2026 — a scale of investment with few historical analogs outside the late-1990s telecom buildout. The disclosures are sharpening investor scrutiny of return timelines, and Blackstone-backed Liftoff Mobile postponed its IPO citing the deteriorating tape. Whether this represents healthy repricing or the early stages of a more material correction remains to be seen.

    Fed Politics

    Senate Republicans have raised concerns about the Justice Department's investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, while former Vice Chair Richard Clarida weighed in publicly on Kevin Warsh, whom President Trump has tapped to lead the Fed. The succession dynamic, combined with ongoing questions about institutional independence, has been one factor cited in this year's earlier bid for gold and dollar-diversification trades.

    Corporates and Trade

    Stellantis took $26 billion in charges tied to walking back its EV strategy, with shares down as much as 24% in Milan. Molina Healthcare plunged 33% premarket after guiding 2026 profit to less than half consensus, dragging Centene and Oscar Health lower. French customs data showed US tariffs weighing on European exports: Q4 spirits shipments to the US fell 47%, wine 39%, and cosmetics 25%. Separately, the administration set aside $12 billion to seed a critical-minerals stockpile aimed at reducing reliance on Chinese supply.

    Asia

    Markets stabilize after risk-off rout

    Asian equities steadied Friday as the heavy selling that battered global risk assets earlier in the week showed signs of abating. The recovery followed a punishing three-day stretch in which the Nasdaq 100 fell roughly 4.6%—its worst run since April—on concerns about stretched valuations and the scale of hyperscaler AI capex, which for the four largest US tech names is forecast to approach $650 billion in 2026. Bitcoin, which briefly traded near $60,000 in Asian hours, rebounded more than 3%. Precious and base metals also found a footing after volatile sessions, though direction remains unsettled.

    Chinese hot money amplified the metals cycle

    Last month's surge and subsequent reversal across gold, silver and copper was meaningfully driven by mainland retail flows, with Shanghai copper futures registering record January volumes and China's top four gold ETFs seeing nearly $1 billion of outflows on Tuesday. The episode underscores a persistent challenge for Beijing: with property and equity returns subdued, surplus household savings continue to chase speculative outlets rather than productive investment. Structural drivers—central bank gold buying, dollar diversification, and strategic demand for industrial metals—appear intact, but near-term positioning has clearly overextended.

    EV sector under pressure

    BYD's Hong Kong shares extended a selloff that has erased over $60 billion in market value since May, weighed by softer domestic sales and rising input costs. Separately, Beijing's ban on concealed door handles—effective 2027—will require redesigns across much of the premium EV lineup, with per-model costs reportedly exceeding 100 million yuan. The rule signals China's growing willingness to set global automotive safety standards unilaterally.

    Southeast Asia headwinds; Japan and Thailand vote Sunday

    Moody's cut Indonesia's outlook to negative, citing fiscal risks; Jakarta's finance ministry pushed back. The Philippines posted its weakest non-pandemic growth in 15 years amid a deepening corruption scandal. India's RBI held rates, citing improved visibility from the US trade deal. Thailand's three-way election and Japan's lower house vote—where PM Takaichi's majority is in question—pose meaningful event risk for regional rates and FX into next week.

    Europe

    Markets and macro

    European markets open against a backdrop of a sharp global retreat from risk that has dominated the week, though selling pressure showed signs of easing in Asian trading. The Nasdaq 100 has fallen roughly 4.6% over three sessions in its worst stretch since April, with software ("SaaSpocalypse"), crypto and precious metals all caught in the unwind. Silver swung nearly 10% intraday before stabilizing, and bitcoin briefly traded below $61,000. European equities will likely take direction from whether this stabilization holds into the US open.

    Bank of England

    Governor Andrew Bailey appeared to endorse market pricing of a roughly 50% probability of a March rate cut, following this week's more dovish-than-expected hold. The MPC's growing confidence that UK inflation has been contained now shifts the policy risk toward the labour market, where signs of softening have accumulated. Sterling and short sterling will remain sensitive to incoming wage and employment data.

    ECB and the euro

    ECB Governing Council member Yannis Stournaras downplayed concerns about the euro's recent appreciation, saying the move is being monitored but does not warrant alarm. The comments are consistent with a Governing Council comfortable holding policy steady, though a sustained currency rally would tighten financial conditions at the margin.

    Corporate

    Stellantis fell as much as 24% in Milan — a record intraday decline — after announcing €22 billion (~$26 billion) in charges tied to unwinding its EV strategy, including model cancellations and supplier compensation. The writedown exceeds analyst expectations and follows similar, smaller moves at Ford, GM and Porsche, underscoring the broader recalibration of European auto EV ambitions. Separately, the proposed Rio Tinto–Glencore tie-up collapsed within 24 hours, reportedly over price; Glencore shares fell roughly 11%, leaving questions about strategic direction with Ivan Glasenberg still a key shareholder.

    Trade

    French customs data showed Q4 2025 spirits exports to the US down 47%, wine down 39% and perfumes/cosmetics down 25%, evidence that US tariffs are now materially affecting French luxury and consumer export categories.

    Global

    Global Cross-Currents: Risk Unwind, Energy Geopolitics, and Strategic Materials

    A synchronized retreat from crowded trades dominated global markets this week, with the unwind extending across US equities, precious metals, base metals, and crypto. The Nasdaq 100 logged its worst three-day decline since April, Bitcoin briefly traded below $61,000 — roughly half its October peak — and silver swung nearly 10% intraday amid thin liquidity. The trigger was less a single catalyst than an accumulation of valuation concerns, headlined by hyperscaler capex disclosures: Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft together now guide to roughly $650 billion in 2026 AI-related spending, with Amazon alone at $200 billion. Whether returns justify outlays remains an open question, and the resulting cash-flow implications appear to be reshaping risk appetite across asset classes. Asian sessions showed tentative stabilization on Friday, though it is too early to characterize the move as a durable bottom.

    The metals complex reflects more than speculative excess

    While Chinese retail flows — channeled into gold, silver, copper and tin amid limited domestic investment alternatives — clearly amplified recent volatility, the underlying drivers are structural: continued central-bank gold accumulation, dollar diversification, and the strategic repricing of copper and other inputs tied to grid, renewables, and AI infrastructure. Shanghai copper futures recorded their highest-ever monthly volumes in January. Separately, the Trump administration is establishing a $12 billion critical-minerals stockpile, an explicit response to last year's demonstration of China's rare-earth leverage.

    In energy, Strait of Hormuz risk is back in focus

    Some VLCC operators are reportedly accelerating transits through the chokepoint amid renewed US-Iran tensions, with Tehran signaling that Friday's talks are unlikely to yield rapid resolution. Concurrently, more than a dozen tankers carrying Russian Urals are repositioning toward Asia, as India appears to retreat from the trade following its US arrangement — a meaningful shift in crude flows that should tighten the discount structure for remaining buyers, principally China.

    Corporate strategy is realigning around the energy transition's slower-than-expected pace

    Stellantis took €22 billion in charges unwinding its EV strategy, joining Ford, GM and Porsche in scaling back electrification timelines — a notable counterpoint to Tesla's reported plans to build domestic solar-cell capacity of up to 10 GW to feed AI power demand.

  • US

    Tech Rotation Deepens; Software Bears the Brunt

    US equities are in the midst of a sharp factor rotation rather than a broad selloff. Value outperformed momentum on Wednesday by the largest margin since November 2020, when initial vaccine efficacy data triggered a comparable reversal — a move all the more notable for occurring without a singular catalyst. The S&P 1500 Software index has declined more than 17% year-to-date, with Anthropic's latest tool intensifying concerns that AI is now disrupting, not merely augmenting, incumbent software business models. Thomson Reuters fell as much as 20% on the week, its largest decline on record. AMD slid 17% post-earnings, and an iShares software ETF has shed nearly $1 trillion in market value over seven sessions. Alphabet beat Q4 revenue estimates but guided 2026 capex to as much as $185 billion versus consensus near $120 billion; shares fell 3.3% premarket. Qualcomm declined 9% and Arm 6.6% on softer outlooks. Notably, breadth remains constructive: roughly two-thirds of S&P 500 constituents advanced Wednesday, and the equal-weighted index closed at a record. Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang called the software selloff "illogical," though investors appear unlikely to take comfort absent stronger evidence — Nvidia's earnings later this month loom as the key test.

    Macro Backdrop Remains Supportive

    Q3 GDP was revised to a 4.4% annualized pace, the strongest in two years. Bank of America expects front-loaded 2026 growth, citing roughly $135–140 billion in OBBBA stimulus (about 0.6% of disposable income) and a 0.5–1pp boost from post-shutdown normalization in Q1 — factors not yet reflected in consensus forecasts. Separately, AEI/Brookings researchers estimate the US may have experienced net negative immigration in 2025, with 2026 projections ranging from a 185,000 gain to a 925,000 decline, a development with potential implications for labor supply and trend growth.

    Crypto and Fed

    Bitcoin fell below $70,000, down more than 42% from its October peak, with Strategy expected to report a multibillion-dollar Q4 loss. On policy, President Trump indicated he would not have nominated Kevin Warsh to the Fed had Warsh signaled a preference for hikes; Governor Lisa Cook reiterated the importance of central bank credibility. Treasury Secretary Bessent testifies before the Senate today following a contentious House appearance.

    Asia

    Global tech rout reaches Asia; software, chip names lead declines

    The selloff that erased close to $1 trillion from an iShares software ETF over seven days extended into Asian trading, with Samsung Electronics tumbling and a Hang Seng gauge of Chinese technology shares approaching bear-market territory. The proximate catalyst — concern that AI tools may disrupt incumbent software business models — has now broadened to chipmakers, with AMD down 17% post-earnings and Qualcomm and Arm guiding cautiously, citing potential memory-component shortages. Against this, Baidu announced its first-ever dividend alongside a buyback of up to $5 billion, a notable shift in capital-return posture from China's large-cap internet names. Alphabet's capex guidance of up to $185 billion (versus $119.5 billion consensus) will be read in Asia as supportive for memory and AI infrastructure suppliers, though the broader rotation away from momentum names complicates that read.

    Japan: Takaichi looks set to consolidate her mandate

    Polling ahead of Sunday's lower-house election suggests Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's LDP-led coalition will comfortably clear the 233 seats needed for a majority. The yen has whipsawed on her comments regarding a weaker currency, which she has partially walked back. More structurally, the Topix rally appears increasingly underpinned by genuine governance reform — rising dividends, accelerating buybacks, and a re-rating of long-discounted sectors such as non-ferrous metals (Sumitomo Electric has tripled since the US election). The 30-year JGB yield surge is best characterized as catch-up to G7 peers rather than incipient fiscal crisis, though Takaichi's expansionary instincts warrant continued monitoring.

    China policy and geopolitics

    More than a dozen Chinese provinces have lowered 2026 growth targets, and consensus is coalescing around a national target of 4.5%–5% at next month's NPC — the first downward revision in three years. Citi now treats its previously baseline stimulus package (≈1 trillion yuan fiscal, 20bp rate cut, 50bp RRR cut) as a ceiling rather than a baseline. Separately, Beijing has instructed state firms to halt new project discussions in Panama and is intensifying customs inspections on Panamanian goods, retaliating against the voiding of a Hong Kong company's port contract. Trump and Xi spoke by phone, covering trade and Taiwan; tone appears constructive, though substance remains opaque.

    India and Australia

    India's exports to the US will face an 18% tariff rate once a joint statement is finalized. Indian refiners are seeking clarity on Trump's claim that Modi pledged to halt Russian crude purchases; in practice, flows are likely to taper toward 800k–1mb/d rather than zero, given freight economics, refinery configurations, and persistent discounts. Australia's Treasurer is on the def

    Europe

    Central banks hold the line

    The European Central Bank is expected to leave policy rates unchanged today, with the Bank of England following suit. The BOE decision came down to a 5-4 vote in favor of holding, signaling a more divided committee than the consensus framing implies and leaving open the possibility that the easing cycle could resume in coming meetings. Only one of 32 economists surveyed had expected a cut. The ECB's hold reflects continued patience as policymakers assess whether disinflation is durable enough to justify further accommodation.

    UK political risk reasserts itself

    Prime Minister Keir Starmer's position has deteriorated materially over his ties to Peter Mandelson, with Labour MPs describing a "febrile, mutinous mood" within the parliamentary party. Parliament voted to compel disclosure of documents related to Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador, given his longstanding association with Jeffrey Epstein. The political strain has begun to spill into markets, with sterling under pressure and gilt yields reflecting renewed political risk premium, though rivals have so far refrained from a formal leadership challenge. How long Starmer can persist absent a credible challenger remains an open question.

    Macro backdrop remains constructive

    German factory orders unexpectedly rose at their fastest pace in two years, reinforcing tentative signs of a manufacturing recovery in the euro area's largest economy, which closed 2025 with stronger-than-expected growth. The UK government separately indicated that a free-trade agreement with the Gulf Cooperation Council is "imminent." Beneath the political noise, there are early signs of a long-anticipated UK productivity resurgence, though this remains to be confirmed in the data.

    Corporate

    Shell missed fourth-quarter expectations with adjusted net income of $3.26 billion, down 11% year-on-year, weighed by softer crude prices, weak oil trading and chemicals losses; the $3.5 billion quarterly buyback was maintained. HSBC is preparing minimal or zero bonuses for underperformers across investment banking and wealth as it adopts a more Wall Street-style compensation model. BNP Paribas's CFO described the bank as "strongly delivering" on growth targets. European software names were caught in the broader AI-driven tech rotation, with Thomson Reuters down as much as 20% earlier in the week on concerns Anthropic's new tool threatens its legal workflow franchise.

    Global

    Cross-Asset Volatility Eclipses Geopolitical Headlines

    A pronounced de-risking episode swept across asset classes overnight, with the most dramatic moves concentrated in precious metals and technology. Silver fell as much as 17% intraday to roughly $74/oz, taking its five-day decline to nearly 40% and erasing a brief rebound from last week's rout. Gold declined as much as 3.5%. The unwind exposes the speculative excess that built up after Citigroup's recent call for $150 silver; Chinese trader Bian Ximing, having previously profited from the gold rally, now sits on roughly $300 million in paper gains from the largest net-short silver position on the Shanghai Futures Exchange. Bitcoin slid below $70,000, down more than 42% from its October peak, undermining the safe-haven thesis its proponents had advanced.

    AI-Driven Rotation, Not Wholesale Selloff

    The global technology selloff that began with concerns about AI disrupting incumbent software business models extended into Asia, with Samsung weaker and Hong Kong-listed Chinese tech approaching bear-market territory. An iShares software ETF has shed close to $1 trillion in seven sessions. The dynamic appears to be rotation rather than capitulation: roughly two-thirds of S&P 500 constituents gained Wednesday and the equal-weighted index hit a record. Value beat momentum by the widest margin since the November 2020 vaccine results — a factor move unusual in the absence of a discrete catalyst.

    Central Banks Hold; Iran Diplomacy Resumes

    The BOE held rates with a notably close 5-4 split, and the ECB is expected to stand pat. Both remain in wait-and-see mode on inflation. Oil declined for the first time in three days after Iran confirmed nuclear talks with the US are scheduled in Muscat, even as President Trump warned Tehran amid a continued American military build-up in the region. Separately, Javier Blas notes cracks emerging in the sanctioned-crude black market: Iranian and Russian barrels are accumulating unsold, less from enforcement than from ample availability of legitimate alternatives at competitive prices — a development worth monitoring for its implications on the marginal price of oil.

    Structural Note

    China's power capacity additions — 543 GW in 2025 alone — increasingly look like a durable competitive advantage in AI infrastructure, a point both Musk and Huang have flagged. The US data-center grid-connection backlog stretches to seven years in parts of Virginia.

  • US

    Markets and Tech Earnings in Focus

    US equity futures traded mixed ahead of Alphabet's results after the close, with S&P 500 futures up 0.14% and Nasdaq 100 futures roughly flat. The 10-year Treasury yield ticked up to 4.28%. The session's most consequential moves were idiosyncratic: AMD fell 7.3% in pre-market after a sales forecast that disappointed investors hoping for a clearer AI payoff, while Chipotle declined 6.2% on a weak outlook. On the upside, Silicon Labs jumped 33% on a Financial Times report that Texas Instruments is in advanced talks to acquire the chip designer for roughly $7 billion, and Enphase rallied 21% on a revenue beat. The day's earnings docket also includes AbbVie, Eli Lilly, Uber, Qualcomm, and Snap.

    Value Rotation Gains Traction

    The Russell 1000 Value Index has advanced 8.6% since early November, outperforming its growth counterpart by 14 percentage points as software stocks have come under pressure on concerns AI may erode incumbent business models. Apollo's John Zito framed the question directly: "Is software dead?" The shift bears watching, though prior value-over-growth episodes of comparable magnitude (2022 and 2001) coincided with broader market drawdowns rather than durable regime change. Whether this rotation reflects healthy breadth or defensive positioning remains to be seen.

    Fed Transition and Fiscal Backdrop

    Kevin Warsh's incoming challenge as Fed Chair will be building consensus among colleagues, while Governor Stephen Miran has stepped down from his White House role. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent testifies before the House today. The partial government shutdown ended after President Trump signed a funding deal negotiated with Senate Democrats.

    Affordability Pressures Persist

    Despite real wage gains—average hourly earnings rose 3.8% in December, well above inflation—consumer confidence registered its weakest reading in over a decade. Goldman research highlights housing as the binding constraint: the bottom income quintile now spends 55% of income on rent, and combined down payment plus first-year mortgage costs in major metros have risen to 160-200% of income, from 90-120% in 2000. The disconnect between aggregate income data and household sentiment continues to carry political weight.

    Asia

    Australia leads global tightening cycle

    The Reserve Bank of Australia delivered the first interest-rate increase among major central banks this year, positioning the country at the leading edge of a "splintering global policy outlook." Economists and markets are already pricing in a further hike, suggesting underlying inflation pressures — likely services-driven — have proven more persistent than the RBA had anticipated. The divergence is notable against a backdrop of disinflation in the euro area, where headline prices have sunk further below the ECB's 2% target, and points to potential support for AUD on widening rate differentials, though the implications for housing-sensitive consumption bear watching.

    China — services firm, but tensions accumulate

    Services activity picked up in January, and Beijing reiterated its commitment to building a "unified national market" to unlock domestic consumption — a recurring structural priority that has yet to translate into a durable demand-side rebound. Separately, a Panama court decision voiding CK Hutchison's contract for two ports drew a sharp response from Beijing, which warned of a "heavy price" to be paid. The episode underscores how port and logistics infrastructure has become a contested arena in US-China strategic competition. Domestically, a speculative gold rush — amplified by bullion's move back above $5,000/oz after a sharp recent correction — is testing official tolerance for retail risk-taking, with authorities mindful of any threat to social cohesion.

    Selective decoupling, not full retreat

    New NBER research from Alfaro and Chor finds China's share of US imports has fallen to roughly 9%, back to pre-WTO accession levels, with Vietnam, Mexico and Taiwan the principal beneficiaries. The reallocation has largely been a reshuffling among existing top-20 partners rather than a broader deglobalization — a meaningful nuance for Southeast Asian exporters positioning around tariff arbitrage.

    Australia critical minerals

    Canberra is weighing a price floor for rare earths and other critical minerals to counter Chinese dominance and attract investment — a policy lever whose effectiveness will depend on coordinated demand-side commitments from allied buyers.

    Europe

    Novo Nordisk shock dominates corporate tape

    Shares of the Danish pharmaceutical giant plunged as much as 20% in Copenhagen after the company forecast a steep decline in sales, providing concrete evidence of an intensifying price war in the obesity drug market. The guidance suggests competitive dynamics in GLP-1s are deteriorating faster than the sell-side had modeled, with implications for the broader European healthcare complex and for Denmark's equity index, where Novo carries outsized weight. The scale of the move — among the largest single-day declines in the company's history — points to a meaningful reset of consensus assumptions around pricing power in the category.

    UBS delivers shareholder return signal

    UBS announced a $3 billion buyback alongside an earnings beat, reinforcing the constructive narrative around large-cap European banks. The capital return underscores management's confidence in integration progress following the Credit Suisse absorption, though investors will want to assess underlying revenue quality and the trajectory of cost synergies in subsequent disclosures.

    Disinflation continues to run ahead of the ECB

    Euro-area inflation sank further below the ECB's 2% target, sharpening the policy debate as the Governing Council weighs its next move. The undershoot complicates the recent communication that easing is largely complete, and markets will likely lean toward pricing additional accommodation if the trend persists into the spring projection round. The data sit uneasily alongside firmer activity signals elsewhere, and the Council's reaction function around target undershoots — versus overshoots in 2022-23 — remains an open question.

    Energy diversification and Equinor

    Chancellor Friedrich Merz is courting Middle Eastern suppliers to diversify German energy sources and reduce reliance on US LNG, a notable strategic pivot given recent transatlantic frictions. Separately, Equinor scaled back its buyback program to as much as $1.5 billion this year from $5 billion in 2025 after Q4 profit missed estimates on lower oil and gas prices — a reminder that European energy majors' capital return capacity remains highly sensitive to commodity prints.

    Global

    Middle East tensions keep a bid under crude, but markets remain sanguine

    Brent advanced to around $68 a barrel for a second consecutive session after the US Navy shot down an Iranian drone heading toward an American aircraft carrier in the Arabian Sea. Oil swung between gains and losses through the session as traders balanced geopolitical risk against a reported sharp drawdown in US inventories. Notably, Bloomberg Opinion's John Authers argued that markets are under-pricing the probability of a major oil shock — a view that sits uncomfortably with current term-structure pricing and suggests asymmetric risk should hostilities escalate toward the Strait of Hormuz. Separately, discounts on Russian Urals to Indian refiners have widened to more than $10 below Brent over the past 10 days, testing New Delhi's commitment to a US trade agreement predicated on lower Russian purchases.

    Monetary policy divergence is widening

    The Reserve Bank of Australia has delivered the first rate hike among major central banks in 2026, with markets and economists positioning for a further increase — the first tightening cycle of the year globally. The contrast with the euro area is stark: HICP has sunk further below the ECB's 2% target, sharpening the debate over the next move. In the US, Kevin Warsh's challenge as incoming Fed chair will be building internal consensus, while Governor Stephen Miran has stepped down from his White House role. The splintering policy backdrop argues for sustained FX volatility and continued cross-market dispersion.

    Gold extended its rebound

    above $5,000/oz for a second day as dip-buyers returned following the recent collapse, while Michael Burry warned of a self-reinforcing "death spiral" risk in Bitcoin, down roughly 40% from October highs. The divergence reinforces the thesis that bullion — not crypto — is absorbing safe-haven flows tied to deficit and policy concerns.

    Climate-energy transition

    Germany's Merz is courting Middle East suppliers to dilute LNG dependence on the US, and Australia is weighing a critical-minerals price floor to counter Chinese dominance — both incremental but directionally meaningful for supply-chain realignment.

  • US

    Trump Nominates Warsh to Chair the Fed

    President Trump named Kevin Warsh as his nominee to succeed Jerome Powell when Powell's term concludes in May. Warsh, a former Fed governor passed over for the chairmanship in 2017, has publicly aligned himself with the president's views on monetary policy, though his policy record suggests a more orthodox stance than the dovish posture Trump has demanded. Initial Wall Street reaction characterized the pick as relatively hawkish — a notable tension given the administration's persistent pressure for lower rates. Confirmation by the Republican-controlled Senate appears likely but is not assured. The practical implications for the rate path remain uncertain: Warsh would inherit a committee whose median view he cannot unilaterally dictate, and his historical concerns about asset-price distortions and inflation credibility could constrain any rapid pivot toward easier policy. As Jonathan Levin observed in Bloomberg Opinion, Trump "demanded a dove [and] picked a hawk" — a characterization that, if it holds, complicates the narrative of imminent Fed capitulation to political pressure.

    Markets React to Fed News and Hotter PPI

    US equities declined following the Warsh announcement, with the move compounded by a hotter-than-expected PPI print that reinforces concerns about sticky upstream inflation. The combination — a perceived hawkish nominee alongside firmer producer prices — pressured rate-sensitive sectors and contributed to a sharp reversal in precious metals, with gold and silver both falling sharply after recent record-setting moves. The metals decline marks a notable unwind of the safe-haven trade that had built through January amid geopolitical strains, including renewed friction over Trump's Greenland posture that had earlier revived "Sell America" flows.

    Positioning Backdrop

    Wealth advisers surveyed by Bloomberg generally counsel against elevated cash allocations despite the risk-off tone, with several rotating toward value equities and selective international exposure, including Japan. The advice reflects a view that current volatility, while elevated, does not warrant defensive capitulation.

    Asia

    Asia-Pacific Daily Briefing — January 30, 2026 Coverage of Asia-Pacific developments in today's source material is limited, with the news flow dominated by US-centric stories. We flag two items with meaningful regional implications. The most consequential development for Asian markets is President Trump's nomination of Kevin Warsh to succeed Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve Chair when Powell's term concludes in May. Wall Street's initial read characterizes Warsh as a relatively hawkish choice — a notable interpretation given Trump's repeated calls for lower rates. The announcement coincided with a hotter-than-expected PPI print, and US equities declined while gold and silver sold off sharply after recent record highs. For Asia, the implications are nuanced and the policy trajectory remains to be seen. A hawkish Fed chair, if confirmed, could sustain dollar strength and keep pressure on Asian currencies, complicating the Bank of Japan's normalization path, constraining the PBoC's room to ease further, and tightening financial conditions across emerging Asia. However, the tension between Trump's stated preference for easier policy and Warsh's historical leanings introduces meaningful uncertainty about how the Fed's reaction function evolves through 2026. The pullback in precious metals — which had been fueled by safe-haven demand amid geopolitical tensions including Trump's renewed Greenland rhetoric — may also dampen flows that had supported regional gold-linked equities. Separately, Japanese equities received favorable mentions in Bloomberg's wealth adviser survey on allocation preferences, with managers citing value characteristics amid a broader risk-off tilt. This is consistent with continued foreign interest in Japan as a relative value play versus stretched US mega-cap technology, though it does not constitute a material market-moving development on its own. Today's available material contains no substantive reporting on China economic data, PBoC actions, capital flows, Korean, Indian, Southeast Asian, or Australian developments. Readers should treat this briefing as a partial snapshot pending broader regional newsflow.

    Europe

    European Daily Briefing — January 30, 2026 Coverage of European-specific developments in today's source material is notably thin, with the news flow dominated by US-centric stories. We flag this candidly rather than overstate the European angle. The most consequential cross-Atlantic development for European markets is President Trump's nomination of Kevin Warsh to succeed Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve Chair when Powell's term expires in May. Initial Wall Street reaction characterizes Warsh as a relatively hawkish choice, a read that — if it holds — carries meaningful implications for the ECB and European rate-sensitive assets. A hawkish Fed transition would likely support the dollar against the euro and complicate the ECB's calculus should the Governing Council seek to extend its easing cycle without provoking unwanted euro weakness and imported disinflation reversal. Bund-Treasury spreads bear watching in the sessions ahead as the market digests Warsh's confirmation path through the Republican-controlled Senate. We would caution against premature conclusions: Warsh's stated alignment with Trump's policy views sits uneasily with his historically hawkish instincts, and how that tension resolves remains to be seen. Geopolitical noise around Trump's renewed demand for US control over Greenland has reignited "Sell America" positioning and contributed to gold's recent record run, though metals reversed sharply in today's session alongside the equity selloff that followed the Warsh announcement and a hotter-than-expected US PPI print. For European investors, the Greenland dispute carries direct relevance given Denmark's sovereignty and the broader question of transatlantic alliance cohesion, though today's sources offer no fresh detail on Copenhagen's response or wider EU positioning. On UK politics, eurozone macro data, ECB commentary, and European corporate news, today's material contains no substantive items worth synthesizing. We would note only the passing reference to rising "soak the rich" tax rhetoric spanning London to California, a theme worth monitoring as UK fiscal debate evolves but lacking actionable detail in today's flow.

    Global

    Trump Names Warsh to Succeed Powell at the Fed

    President Trump selected Kevin Warsh as his nominee to chair the Federal Reserve following the May expiration of Jerome Powell's term. The choice is notable for its tension with prior expectations: Trump has consistently pressed for lower rates, yet Warsh is broadly viewed on Wall Street as a relative hawk, having criticized accommodative policy during and after his prior tenure as a Fed governor. Warsh has publicly aligned himself with the President's monetary policy views, though institutional constraints and FOMC composition suggest any pivot would be gradual rather than immediate. Markets responded with a risk-off tone: US equities declined, gold and silver sold off sharply amid broader volatility in precious metals, and the reaction was compounded by a hotter-than-expected PPI print. The juxtaposition — a hawkish nominee against a White House demanding easier policy — leaves the trajectory of US monetary policy unusually difficult to forecast, with implications for global rate differentials, dollar positioning, and the relative attractiveness of duration outside the US. Senate confirmation is expected given the Republican majority, but the substantive question of how Warsh balances political pressure against the Fed's institutional preferences remains open.

    Geopolitical Risk Premium Persists

    Renewed friction over Trump's demand for US control of Greenland has reactivated the "Sell America" trade that surfaced periodically through 2025, contributing to defensive positioning across portfolios. Gold's run to record levels earlier in the week reflected this safe-haven bid, though today's sharp reversal in metals illustrates how crowded the trade had become. Among wealth managers surveyed by Bloomberg, the response to the current environment has tilted toward value equities and selective international exposure, with Japanese equities cited favorably — consistent with a view that US exceptionalism is being repriced at the margin. Coverage of oil markets, the Strait of Hormuz, and the energy transition was absent from today's available material.

  • US

    Coverage for the US macro and policy complex in today's source material is exceptionally thin. The only Bloomberg dispatch available is a weekend lifestyle compilation from the Pursuits desk, focused on consumer trends in wellness (the growth of bathhouse and sauna venues as substitutes for traditional happy-hour socializing), family-oriented luxury travel offerings, wearable health-tracking devices including Oura and Apple Watch, overtourism policy debates in global destinations, and longevity-focused wellness retreats. None of this material bears materially on Federal Reserve policy, fiscal developments, equity or credit market conditions, or US corporate newsflow. To the extent any thread is tangentially relevant to the US consumer discretionary complex, the continued expansion of premium experiential spending — wellness venues, high-end family travel, and connected health hardware — is consistent with the bifurcated consumption pattern that has characterized the post-pandemic cycle, in which upper-income households continue to support services inflation even as lower-quintile cohorts retrench. Apple's wearables franchise and the broader connected-health category remain a structural growth vector within large-cap tech, though today's source provides no incremental data points on unit economics or penetration. No new information is available in today's inputs regarding the FOMC's January meeting trajectory, Treasury issuance, the labor market, earnings season progression, credit spreads, or Washington fiscal negotiations. Readers should treat this briefing as a placeholder; substantive analysis will resume when primary macro and market source material is available. Given that January 24 falls on a Saturday, the absence of hard newsflow is not unusual, and positioning ahead of next week's economic calendar — which typically includes the advance Q4 GDP print and PCE inflation data in this part of the cycle — should be informed by Friday's closing levels and any weekend developments captured in subsequent dispatches.
